```
and if you can get the systems programmers to modify the change management
package being used, a variation of the crossrefence listing in FileAide can
be used to create Sort Symbol Files from every copybook moved into
production. I wrote a similar REXX which read the output from the File Aid
XREF listing and generated properly commented sort cards:

01 COMPANY-RECORD.
    05 COMPANY-NUMBER PIC X(03).
    05 DIVISION                  PIC X(03).

Would generate:
    0001,0006,CH,                   COMPANY-RECORD
    0001,0003,CH,                   COMPANY-NUMBER
    0004,0003,CH,                   DIVISION

However something like this still requires manual restructuring of the sort
cards to handle all the different uses of the sort offsets.


>http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmr14.htm
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler)
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f4ac06.3234478@news.teo-computer.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7sqk9b$82a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Sep 1999 14:45:03 GMT, john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:

>  "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:
>> What do you prefer?  I guess both are useful but I still like the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I agree.  And I'll throw in another good reason for using external
sorts.

COBOL programs tend to last a long time in production, and get
enhanced quite often.  They get FAT.  Some of this FAT can take the
form of Spaghetti.  In large IS shops, a program can get enhanced by a
considerable number of programmers, all with different styles.  A
simple program, isn't so simple after 5yrs, and 8 different
programmers enhancing and maintaining it.

It's a lot easier to keep those programs simple (KISS), when you've
got one program handle pre-sort processing, and another program
handling post-sort processing.

When downtime is critical, this helps quite a bit.  An ABEND in the
pre-sort processing program, immediately elimilates the sort, or the
post-sort processing.  If all of these functions were combined in a
single program, you won't know this until after you investigate.




Tim Oxler
Teo Computer
http://www.teo-computer.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eC=1N5TNfL=VrkDzXT9rJam8tKkm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <7sqk9b$82a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37f4ac06.3234478@news.teo-computer.com>`

```
The venerable tim.oxler@NOSPAMteo-computer.com (Tim Oxler) bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on 01 Oct 1999 06:00:25 PDT these words:

>It's a lot easier to keep those programs simple (KISS), when you've
>got one program handle pre-sort processing, and another program
>handling post-sort processing.

I had a job where most of the programs we wrote could be described as
follows:

1)  Read a sequential file selecting 1-20% of the records.
2)  Sort the file on 4 to 8 keys.
3)  Print a report with control breaks on 2 to 5 keys.

The operations supervisor was talking about forbidding internal sorts.
I warned him that if he did that, lazy programmers who didn't want to
make two separate programs would put the select after the external
sort.  He became an advocate of internal sorts, and at future change
control meetings he would tell the code reviewers (he didn't know
Cobol himself) to be sure the records are selected before the sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** "Solar Systems" <solar@axs2k.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f52cdf.0@news.axs2k.net>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote in message ...
>.... With an internal SORT the
>program has to be recompiled .... Changing a SORT card to change the sort
sequence
>is typically easier

Even if the calling program (Cobol, PL1, whatever) has a hard-coded SORT
statement in it, such as SORT FIELDS=(1,12,CH,A), you can always add an
overriding control statement in the $ORTPARM DD file, such as
// $ORTPARM  DD *
sort   fields=(1,13,ch,a)

without having to change to program.   At least this works with our version
(non-IBM) of the sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F55D1A.BFDF4199@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper> <37f52cdf.0@news.axs2k.net>`

```
Anyone who does this on other than an emergency basis deserves to be
shot.  The advantage of the internal sort is that the fields that you
are sorting on are described by name, not offset and size.  The
overriding of what the program says is the surest way to confusion in
the future.  It may be needed to get out of an immediate problem and may
need to be left in production until the recompile can be tested and
gotten through the quality assurance group.  It definitely should not be
a "permanent" change.

Parenthetically, control card changes probably should be subject to
greater review than they are in most shops.  Some of the more complex
JCL and batch processing scripts on other platforms also might warrant
greater scrutiny depending on the amount of programming that it is
possible to do with these tools.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services

Solar Systems wrote:
> 
   snip. 
> Even if the calling program (Cobol, PL1, whatever) has a hard-coded SORT
> statement in it, such as SORT FIELDS=(1,12,CH,A), you can always add an
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> (non-IBM) of the sort.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <gumbo@please.eatme.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mlEJ3.5633$CV1.25412@viper>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper> <37f52cdf.0@news.axs2k.net> <37F55D1A.BFDF4199@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Further, overriding the sortsequence with a parm card removes the need for a
sort statement in the first place. All you can do with Sort in COBOL anyways
is sort. Why not break the program into 3 parts, input processing, sorting,
and output processing. Leading back to my favorite saying EXTRACT, FILTER,
SORT  ans REPORT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fe2275.13935081@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37f08f2f@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <IGfI3.3052$j4.83678@viper> <37f52cdf.0@news.axs2k.net> <37F55D1A.BFDF4199@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```

I too perfer internal sorts.  I, like many shops, use copybooks for
describing the data.  It's a simple matter for me to copy the copybook
into the SD section, change my prefix copybook code (ex:  k400- to
k400sort-) to the fieldname and there you have it: instant sort
description.  It saves me a lot of time when I change a copybook
image.

Boyce G. Williams, Jr.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zb39N4is4RmYba1qJsx4qzLkBgJf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37fbfa3a.228708089@news.ntplx.net> <37FC121A.7F3B33EB@home.com>`

```
The venerable "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Thu, 07 Oct 1999 03:15:02 GMT these words:

>Let me qualify, as I stated previously, I don't use sorts. From
>following this thread with its ifs, buts, maybes, I think Bob's approach
>makes sense. Either the operating system or the compiler developer
>provides an external sort feature - why convolute the business logic
>with the sort mechanism. 

James, since you don't use sorts, you don't have much experience to
contribute to the subject.  From my years of experience I ask you to
consider this scenario:

The auditor asks for a report showing 25 fields from every savings
account on the database, sorted on four fields.  Because shop
standards require external sorts, the programmer produces one program
to read the database writing selected data, a second program to read
that file and produce a report, and also a sort control record, which
needs to be cataloged somewhere just like a program source.  Never
mind that the programmer gets the runaround for two days finding out
_how_ to put a sort control record in production, this is a story
about maintenance.

The auditor gets four boxes of paper, and decides he only wants 10
fields sorted on two fields, and he only wants every 100th record.  A
different programmer is assigned, and he doesn't even know there's a
select program or a sort, he just knows he's getting a file, and now
he adds code to throw most of that data away before printing.  Maybe
even the same programmer is assigned, but he doesn't want the hassle
of changing the first program and the sort control record.
Programmers are lazy.  I've seen programmers put some very inefficient
stuff in production, because it was more coding to do it right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FDED9F.CE04905E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37fbfa3a.228708089@news.ntplx.net> <37FC121A.7F3B33EB@home.com> <zb39N4is4RmYba1qJsx4qzLkBgJf@4ax.com>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:

> The auditor gets four boxes of paper, and decides he only wants 10
> fields sorted on two fields, and he only wants every 100th record.  A
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> stuff in production, because it was more coding to do it right.

And the lazy way to do this job is to modify the external sort.  It is
also the easiest way to debug, and most importantly, it allows user 1 to
use the same report program as user 2, even though they have different
select criteria.

If a programmer doesn't know what he's doing, he shouldn't be doing it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Alex Flinsch <aflinsch@njeb.att.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FDF09D.CE04F06D@njeb.att.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37fbfa3a.228708089@news.ntplx.net> <37FC121A.7F3B33EB@home.com> <zb39N4is4RmYba1qJsx4qzLkBgJf@4ax.com>`

```


Randall Bart wrote:

>
> The auditor asks for a report showing 25 fields from every savings
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> about maintenance.

Oddball method 1 - No sort required
Create a (secondary index/order by clause or whatever, depends on what kind
of database yer dealing with) that consists of your sort fields. This method
eliminates the need for a sort altogether, but won't win you any friends with
the dba nazis  (unless of course you are the dba).  I actually was at one
installation that had 12 of these on a (very small) IMS database. The
indicies actually took up more space than the whole DB did.

Oddball method 2 - No sort control required
The selection program has the sortcontrol records hardcoded in an internal
table. Said program writes table to temp file. sort reads sort control from
temp file.  I have actually seen this done many times.

Once again, there is more than one way to skin a cat (or sort a file for that
matter)

>
> The auditor gets four boxes of paper, and decides he only wants 10
> fields sorted on two fields, and he only wants every 100th record.  A

Nah... never happens that way. Usually they take the multiple boxes of paper
and throw away everything except the final summary page of the report. Then
they start complaining about costs and and institute "cost saving measures"
like hiding the paper for the lan printers/copiers.

>
> Programmers are lazy.  I've seen programmers put some very inefficient
> stuff in production, because it was more coding to do it right.

Sometimes it's less coding to do it right, but the crap code goes in because
no one can figure out how to get something slightly unusual past the
installation group.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Scott Peterson <scottp4.removethis@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sWQBOF48ui2gBNAf8s5CRnh60UV5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7sp6vd$idp@enews3.newsguy.com> <37fbfa3a.228708089@news.ntplx.net> <37FC121A.7F3B33EB@home.com> <zb39N4is4RmYba1qJsx4qzLkBgJf@4ax.com> <37FDF09D.CE04F06D@njeb.att.com>`

```
Alex Flinsch <aflinsch@njeb.att.com> wrote:


>Oddball method 1 - No sort required
>Create a (secondary index/order by clause or whatever, depends on what kind
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
On a standalone system this is great. Do this on a heavily loaded
DB/DC system and those DBA Nazi's wold be in your office making sure
you don't do it again, possibly wanting valued body parts as insurance
of same.  It's simply a wasteful programming practice if there is a
significant amount of data.  Have you ever traced the disk activity as
you do this? You're effectively reading the entire file randomly. No
buffering or readahead possible.   I would guess that in about 8 out
of 10 cases it's significantly faster to do a full unload of the
pertinent segments of the database and sort them than it is to do a
pass of the same file through the secondary index.

                         
                         		Scott Peterson


All programmers are playwrights 
and all computers are lousy actors.
```

```
>
```

```
news:38022A9A.50E360C2@NOSPAMhome.com...
> I remember a shop which did not have any sort utility.  To sort files
> they created a temporary keyed version of those files then copied that

We use code originally generated by a product called "cscript".  To produce
a sorted report it does the same thing on a temporary indexed file.  The
SORT verb is explained in our AcuCobol manual but I don't ever recall seeing
it used here.
For line sequential files we have the Unix "sort" command.
```

- **From:** lopax@aol.com (Lopax)

- **Message-ID:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
I have a file that contains packed decimal fields that I am ultimately trying
to move to  pic x fields.  I tried doing the following:

05  wkly-sal    pic 9(5)v99 comp-3 value zeroes
05  wkly-sal-r redefines wkly-sal pic x(07).

I was getting a wkly-sal-r redefined a smaller item error.  How can I move that
comp-3 field to a pic x field?  Can I use redefines like I was attempting to
do?  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

```
> 
> I have a file that contains packed decimal fields that I am ultimately trying
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> do?  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.


Do you have a switch where your compiler can show you how large IT
thinks your fields are?  If so, use it.  I don't know if there are other
definitions, but on IBM mainframes, pic 9(5)v99 is stored like in 4
bytes like this: s9 99 99 99
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ sign is leading....

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia>`

```
>Howard, the IBM mainframe is not my forte', but is the sign not in
>the rightmost nibble?  Like this: 99 99 99 9s where 's' = hex 'F'
>because the field is 'unsigned' (otherwise 'C' = + and 'D' = -).


Correct, with a further clarification. The SIGN IS LEADING clause moves the
sign to the leading nibble:
sign is leading s9 99 99 99
sign is trailing 99 99 99 9s
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w2PQ3.26241$Fj2.238229@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper>`

```
No, that's ONLY for COMP-3 / Packed Decimal.
You are thinking of what is known as 'OVERPUNCHED'
a 0 is {
    1 is A
    ..
    9 is I
(Positive)
Negative is
    1 is J
    ..
    9 is R

Also you can code 'SEPERATE [Leading / trailing] CHARACTER, in which case
you count
the Sign as a position and it is a physically seperate byte.


Gumbo wrote in message ...
>>Howard, the IBM mainframe is not my forte', but is the sign not in
>>the rightmost nibble?  Like this: 99 99 99 9s where 's' = hex 'F'
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** Andreas Strzoda <as@as-data.de>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F46E8D.27B743C0@as-data.de>`

```
Lopax schrieb:

> I understand that now, but how can I move comp-3 fields to pic x fields.  I
> tried moving the pic 9(7)v99 comp-3 to pic 9(7) then moving those pic 9(7)'s to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

No, the only way to unpack a Comp-3 field is the MOVE to
a numeric field with usage is DISPLAY (the default), and..
take care of the decimal-point!

so define
01 TO-SHOW PIC 99999.99 [usage is display].
and move your field to this field. If you prefer leading spaces at the beginning
use the Z instead of the 9'th.
greating

AS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t26hb$oqu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7t1c7n$i7o$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <19991001003546.02150.00000534@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991001003546.02150.00000534@ng-cm1.aol.com>,
  lopax@aol.com (Lopax) wrote:

<Snipped>

> >Lopax <lopax@aol.com> wrote in message
> >news:19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com...
> >> I have a file that contains packed decimal fields that I am
ultimately
> >trying
> >> to move to  pic x fields.  I tried doing the following:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> I was getting a wkly-sal-r redefined a smaller item error.  How
can I move
> >that
> >> comp-3 field to a pic x field?  Can I use redefines like I was
attempting
> >to
> >> do?  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.
>
>

If you define your unpacked (target) field as PIC 9(05)V99 and then
redefine it as PIC X(07), you can save yourself one MOVE statement. You
may want to consider defining the target as PIC S9(05)V99 to preserve
the sign, providing whether the value could in fact, be negative.

However, when addressing the PIC X(07), the usage of the implied two
position decimal and the sign-overpunch is not considered. It will just
look like any 7-byte character string. Also, you can't do any
arithmetic (if needed) on the PIC X(07). Note that the last (7th) byte
will include the sign. If you're confident the COMP-3 field will always
be ZERO or GREATER, then eliminate the sign on the target field.

03 WKLY-SAL PIC S9(05)V99 COMP-3 VALUE ZERO.
03 WKLY-SAL-UNPK-NUMERIC PIC 9(05)V99.
03 WKLY-SAL-UNPK-CHAR REDEFINES WKLY-SAL-UNPK-NUMERIC PIC X(07).

MOVE WKLY-SAL TO WKLY-SAL-UNPK-NUMERIC.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t2f1k$if6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7t1c7n$i7o$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <19991001003546.02150.00000534@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
It depends on whether you want to just move them or convert them.  If you
redefine accurately, and move the result to a picture x field, the data will
be there. If you want them *converted* to a straight ASCII field, then you
have to move them to a field defined that way.

You say you want to "move" fields.  If that is all you want, then the MOVE
statement works quite well with any data you want, from any definition you
want to any other definition you want.

>tried moving the pic 9(7)v99 comp-3 to pic 9(7) then moving those pic
9(7)'s to
>pic x(7)'s and that worked.  Is there a more efficient way than that?  Can
I
>redefine the comp-3's as pic 9's then move to pic x's?  Or do I have to
move
>the comp-3's 2 times like I stated above?  Thanks again
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> I was getting a wkly-sal-r redefined a smaller item error.  How can I
move
>>that
>>> comp-3 field to a pic x field?  Can I use redefines like I was
attempting
>>to
>>> do?  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HeHQ3.1370$CS3.43054@viper>`
- **References:** `<7t1c7n$i7o$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <19991001003546.02150.00000534@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
>>> 05  wkly-sal    pic 9(5)v99 comp-3 value zeroes
>>> 05  wkly-sal-r redefines wkly-sal pic x(07).


I am geussing a little bit here about what you want but....

05 wkly-sal pic 9(5)v99 usage packed-decimal value zeroes.
05 wkly-sal-display pic 9(5).99 usage display.

Move wkly-sal to wkly-sal-display

The above will translate the number into a properly displayed number with a
decimal point. However no mathemeatical operations will be able to pe
performed on the -display field. If operations need to be performed on the
number, then defining it as a PIC X as you previously did does not make
sense.
```

#### ↳ Re: packed decimal question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<op2J3.2443$Pt.19729@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
Lopax wrote:
>I have a file that contains packed decimal fields that I am ultimately trying
>to move to  pic x fields.  I tried doing the following:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>do?  Any help would be greatly appreciated.  Thanks.

It is not clear what you mean when you say "move that COMP-3 field to a PIC
X field."  There are several possible meanings to what you ask.

1. Unpack the numeric digits into bytes, for example: 12345v67 -> "1234567"
   (not room for decimal in 7 bytes).  For this, define the receiving field
   as PIC 9(5)V99 DISPLAY, and redefine it as PIC X(7) for reference.

2. Convert the COMP-3 field to a properly formatted edited text field:
   12345v67 -> "12345.67".  For this, define the receiving field as
   PIC 9(5).99 DISPLAY, and redefine it as PIC X(8) for reference.

3. Move the COMP-3 field bit-for-bit into the correct number of bytes in a
   PIC X field.  For this, redefine wkly-sal as PIC X(4) for reference.
```

---

## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 5 this month / 24 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-4305df7e18)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EkH1NyQoo6uFvjafZzwyy2Je36yh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37EEB49A.E85E534E@att.net> <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com> <37f0c05e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <37F0E952.7E1F@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> <37F27EB1.6A074BF2@nsf.gov> <ZajyNy2pwIzlt2NUs+CJQetYa287@4ax.com> <7t0p5p$k1t$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
The venerable "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Thu, 30 Sep 1999 15:45:41 -0700 these words:

>Some clarifications, provided by thanks to a friend and coworker who was on
>the team at the time:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>code.  There was only one assembly/bind of that original compiler.

Thanx, Chuck.  Originally I had understood that the original ALGOL was
cross compiled on a B200 or B300.  Then I heard from a more
authoritative source about the weeks of manually compiling changes and
patching.  Your version resolves the conflict.

>But once the ALGOL-language version of the ALGOL compiler successfully
>"triple-compiled",  the original compiler produced on the B220 was rendered
>obsolete.

And I am sure that was quite a joyous day in Sierra Madre.

I would like to explain at length the reason for a triple compile,
what it demonstrates, and why it doesn't quite demonstrate that.

We start with an old object, and a new source.  We compile the new
source with the old object producing the first new object.  This isn't
the same as the old object (of course, it's from a new source), so the
first (and last) run of the first new object is to compile the new
source producing the second new object.

The first and second new objects should be functionally the same
because they reflect the same source, but since they were produced by
different compilers they may contain different object code to
accomplish the the same function.  So the first (and not necessarily
last, see below) run of the second new object is to compile the new
new source for the third time producing the third new object.  The
second and third new objects were produced from the same source and
functionally identical compilers, so they are compared.  If the second
and third objects are not identical, or if the third object is not
created (due to syntax errors, MCP crashes, etc) the new source has
failed it's triple compile. 

>From that day to this, updated ALGOL compilers shipped to Unisys customers
>are three generations removed from their previous versions;

I was told by someone in Release Control (Pam Whittington's boss, at
the time) that it's a popular misconception that we ship the third
object.  He said we ship the result of the second compile, not that it
makes any diference since the second and third objects are identical.

Triple compile only tells you that a compiler compiles itself.  That
is far from a complete test.  Lex Borger told me we once shipped a
NEWP compiler with a bug in it, which successfully triple-compiled
itself, yet which couldn't triple compile the predeessor source
because that source hit the bug.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Virtual Storage (was: PL/I - COBOL comparison)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Shmuel (Seymour J.) Metz" <nospam@nsf.gov.invalid>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F501C7.CD0580A7@nsf.gov.invalid>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37f0c05e$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <37F0E952.7E1F@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> <37F27EB1.6A074BF2@nsf.gov> <37f3aa00_1@news.wizvax.net> <37f4960f.1208404@enews.newsguy.com>`

```
Clement McGann wrote:
> 
> On 30 Sep 1999 14:20:48 -0400, multics@wizvax.wizvax.net (Richard Shetron)
> wrote:

> >IIRC I thought the Bourroughs machines did segmentation but did not
> >do paging.  The entire segment was swapped in and out.

He's correct, except that Burroughs supported multiple levels of
segments: a descriptor could point to an array of descriptors. Indexing
was automatic.

> >Multics did both segmentation and pageing.  There were no 'files',
> >everything on disk was a segment and mapped into virtual memory when
> >first refrenced.

One of the ways that MULTICS was ahead of its time. IMHO we still
haven't caught up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John W. Kennedy" <rri0189@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FBA5BD.73F8598@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<fndH3.25599$ei1.47531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <37EEB49A.E85E534E@att.net> <37ef2e26.1973620@enews.newsguy.com> <37F1A07A.825134DD@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Clement McGann wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> offered it then, it was still a proprietary language.

The PL/I language was _never_ proprietary in that sense.  It was never
owned by IBM at all, except in the sense that most of the work was done
by IBM and SHARE.

And during most of the specific time cited, all actual IBM software was
free.
```

<a id="t-8528c94344"></a>
## Thread: sct-3-5-6: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: features and fixes — 4 msgs

### sct-3-5-6: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: features and fixes

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F4D7CF.AC19EA83@siber.com>`

```
The new 3-5-6 version of CobolTransformer tools and 
converters is released. It has the following
new features and improvements:

* data2cr: Crystal Reports Reader for Cobol Data Files.
After you install the data2cr software, your 
Seagate Software Crystal Reports report generator
will be able to read Fujitsu and Micro Focus 
Cobol data files directly.

Just imagine: you will be generating elegant and informative 
reports from your Cobol data files directly, using 
powerful graphical user interface of Crystal Reports, 
instead of tediously programming report generation
directly in Cobol language.

For more info look at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

* Data Reader for other Cobol data formats.

Feel free to send us your Cobol data file, so that we can 
add reader for your format to the Data Reader library.
We are interested in seeing data files of Ryan McFarland Cobol,
AcuCobol, and IBM mainframe Cobols -- we want DataReader
to read these formats too.

* You can use repeaters such as X(9) or 9(8) in picture strings 
in data item descriptors in RDD files now. Originally RDD files
were intended to be used only as intermediate format generated 
by cbl2fdd program from the Cobol source. But several users
who did not have the program source and yet need to read the data,
chose to compose RDD files themselves. When you do this, having
repeaters and not just unrolled picture string is convenient.


Future versions
---------------

This improvements are planed for version to be released
in the next 2-3 weeks. They are being implemented and 
tested as you read this message.

* Date and Time recognition in record items.
You will be able to specify special DateTime picture
string that will tell DataReader that certain record 
item should be interpreted as date/time and will specify
format of date/time.

Once data item is interpreted as date/time, you will be
able to reformat it to your liking using data output
facilities such as Crystal Reports generator.

* EBCDIC <-> ASCII translation.
Cobol Data File reader will be able to perform
EBCDIC <-> ASCII translation, so that mainframe
Cobol data files can be read.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

#### ↳ Re: sct-3-5-6: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: features and fixes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t6jn9$bu4@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37F4D7CF.AC19EA83@siber.com>`

```
Curiosity (and totally off-topic),

Did other see the "attached" note as HTML - or something other than "Plain
Text"?

I seem to be seeing Vadim's posts (and only his) this way - but he says that
he is definitely sending it in "plain text".

Anyone else getting the same symptoms?  It may very well be a problem with my
NG reader, but it is odd that it only seems to happen with his posts.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: sct-3-5-6: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: features and fixes

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n8LJ3.1124$Iw5.50846@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37F4D7CF.AC19EA83@siber.com> <7t6jn9$bu4@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
If you are using Microsoft Outlook Express, that happens because of the
"character set" used on the message. Vadim's post includes this option in
the message header (open/properties): charset=koi8-r.

Go to "Tools/Options/Read/Fonts" to set your viewing font based on the
character set in the message header. If this charset is not in the list
(it's not in mine), I think Outlook Express just switches to this charset
and forgets to switch back unless it gets a message to read with a "charset"
option which IS in its list.

The real solution is to ask eveyone who posts to only use a "charset" option
which is supported by Outlook Express.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sct-3-5-6: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: features and fixes

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F803DB.ACA6AE68@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<37F4D7CF.AC19EA83@siber.com> <7t6jn9$bu4@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <n8LJ3.1124$Iw5.50846@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> If you are using Microsoft Outlook Express, that happens because of the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> which is supported by Outlook Express.

I did not use the koi8-r charset intentionally.

This was Netscape Communicator. Apparently
I was reading news article or letter in Russian
before posting to comp.lang.cobol and 
Communicator decided that I want to post in Russian too.

I would say this is a bug in Netscape Comuunicator.
Sending an article that contains no national characters
with national encoding that was previously used for reading 
something else, something unrelated it just plain wrong.

Probably it's time to switch to Outlook Express or 
just go back to my trusted (but rusty) UNIX-based news reader.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
> 
> On Fri, 01 Oct 1999 17:27:00 GMT, "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> download/updates on files this also.

I was hoping Bob would jump in here.  this is my (non-user)
understanding of the SP2 Thin client process.

There is a stub program (about 70 kb) that runs on the windows
client.  This stub program will download screens from the master as
they are accessed.  This means that they are cached and exceptionally
fast.  When you updated the main system the time stamp difference is
noted and a new copy of the screen is downloaded on the next access.

Automated configuration management, no mismatch between versions. 
This is definitely worth a look at IMHO.

Ken
```

### RE: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698513150@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```


> : 	This may look stupid, but we do it all the time.
> : 	There is really no point in distributing a small external table
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
	Okay.
	Let's have a look at how microsoft does it.
	Why not browse the developer network cd set. You will find language
specifice tables at SOURCE level, and burnt-in language specific
EXECUTABLES.

	Am I right or am I right ?





 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express / Dialog System context help

- **From:** "Stephen Dennis" <sdennis@svdltd.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LVZI3.110$YI2.3863@sea-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698513150@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote in message
news:6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698513150@NL-AMB-MAIL01...
>
> Okay.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Am I right or am I right ?

You will find language specific tables, and they are 'burned' into the
executable, however looking at what Microsoft does rather argues against
your own point, and here's why:

In the Windows Program case, every user-visible string in the program is put
into a seperate 'string table' in a seperate text RC source file. The
program that uses those strings does so by reference to a particular entry
in the table....by unique number.

Those string tables are translated seperately into various languages
seperately from the source code. In fact, the only way Microsoft has a
chance of supporting such a broad array of languages is to use this
approach. For most Pan-European languages, no re-compile of the source is
necesary (some German phrases tend to be long-winded and sometimes the
dialogs box need to be made larger...fortunately, the dialogs themselves are
part of the same RC file). Translation into Japanese, Korean, Traditional
and Simplified Chinese can have farther reaching effects on the source code.

The resource compiler takes these strings (for whichever language you happen
to be building for) and creates an in-memory image (RES). The linker pretty
much tacks this in-memory image onto the end of the executable like a
fashion accessory.

These fashion accessories can be stripped off and new ones put in their
place quite automatically. I know the resource tools for 16-bit allowed you
to edit resources in an already-built executable. I'm fairly certain that
this capability exists in the 32-bit tools...as least from a third party.

There are strings which are -not- put into the resource file. FTP/HTTP/HTML
protocol or format-oriented strings that are spelled out in a standard
somewhere that cannot and should not be changed. These are things that the
user will never see.

Having a seperate files has the following disadvantages:

1. Seperate files (particularly a lot of little ones) waste space on the
disk due to fragmentation. Game like Doom, Quake, Half-Life have invented
blob-files as a way of collecting all these little pieces into one big file.
Without a blob file, these games would take 2-5 times more disk space due to
fragmentation of the file system. For example, a little 512 byte image
that's dealth with in the source code seperately becaues it's used on 14
different monsters in 500 different places would still consume an entire
disk allocation unit if stored seperately.

2. Seperate files increase you load time. Another file(s) for the OS to
find, security check, and make available to you.

3. Distribution problem. Having two files that must be installed in sync and
kept in sync in the face of users and later installs. This creates new error
situations and potential support calls.

However, for user-visible strings, the advantages of a seperate file are
clear. And, in the Microsoft case, they solved some of the disadvantages by
having it be seperately tacked onto the end of your program. You
must -still- ask the operating system for access to your own resources.
Those resources must -still- be opened and accessed seperately. They
are -not- typically loaded into memory with the program. Or there may be an
option to do so, but if that option is available, it's probably only the
critical error messages that are loaded at program load time.

Sorry for the long rambling message.
```

```

Clicked in when you said 'chaining'. I've got a COBOL textbook which
describes a chaining concept - not quite sure if it is what you were
alluding to, but from memory it's something like this :-

	Previoous 	Thisrecord	Next
	 000		001       	002
	 001		002		003
	 002		003		004

Gave it a try once using MF COBOL (DOS). More or less got it to work -
but it's tricky to handle all permutations, inclusions, deletions etc.	
The text book was used at a local college. The idiot instructor I phoned
to query about it, dismissed the concept out of hand.  

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37f54e52.0@news2.tm.net.my>`

```
Hello. I am new to Cobol and I need some help. How can I prompt the user for
some alphabets then assign the alphabets to variables and later change it to
another alphabets or numbers, like for example:

A=11

You just need to give me a simple example and I will do the other job.
Please send me the whole source cause I am a total newbie. Please send only
your source codes, and send it to kokliang@mail.flashmail.com. Thanks.

Kok Liang
kokliang@mail.flashmail.com
```

#### ↳ Re: I need some help here!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F55F7A.486E13D4@home.com>`

```


Gin Wen wrote:
> 
> Hello. I am new to Cobol and I need some help. How can I prompt the user for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> A=11

This smells suspiciously like homework - so unless you produce some 
source and send it to this NewsGroup (NOT me), people here are going to
be reluctant to help. Sorry, only know Roman alphabet, so lets take
Arabic and Roman numerals.

01 A1 pic x(30) value "01,02,03,04,05,06,07,08,09,10,".
01 A2 redefines A1.
   05 A3 occurs 10.
      10 ArabicNumber	pic 9(02).
      10                pic x.

01 R1 pic x(50) value
   "   I,  II, III,  IV,   V,  VI, VII,VIII,  IX,    X,".
01 R2 redefines R1.
   05 R3 occurs 10.
      10 RomanNumber	pic x(04).
      10                pic x.

Now you figure the rest; use the table references to get from one to the
other.

Jimmy, Calgary
```

<a id="t-167957dddd"></a>
## Thread: "low-values" — 3 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-167957dddd)._

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20TI3.676$lD.26803@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
sysdev wrote in message <37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>...
>We have been experiencing problems with data containing binary zeros. We
>want to check the data for low values, to exclude the records with this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not that this is a real possibility, but... COBOL 'low-value[s]' is not
necessarily x'00'. It is the first character of the current collating
sequence. If you are using a really weird collating sequence, you may be
looking for the wrong thing.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f42a74.15277197@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
On Thu, 30 Sep 1999 13:50:12 -0500, sysdev <sysdev@mb.sympatico.ca>
wrote:

>We have been experiencing problems with data containing binary zeros. We
>want to check the data for low values, to exclude the records with this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm making a guess and some assumptions here.

You mentioned Oracle.  This makes me think you have NULL values in the
table that you are trying to handle.  These are NOT zeros, or low
values.  This is a BIG SECRET, but I'll let you in on it because it
took me a while to grasp it.

Let's say you have a table and the element is called "Element".

If you do:

Select Element from Table where Element < 1.

It won't find it.

Say you try:

Select Element from table where element not = 1

That doens't return it either even though it's not equal to 1!

Null's are SPECIAL.  

They have NO value not LOW value.

How about:

Select Element from table where element is null;

that returns the record.


So, let's say you want to "assume" a value for the element when it's
not there, or null

Select nvl(element,0) from table where nvl(element,0) = 0;

will return all the "null" or "zero" elements in the table.

Does that help clear up the mystery?
```

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37f42db7.16112193@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37F3B0E3.710C916A@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
On Thu, 30 Sep 1999 13:50:12 -0500, sysdev <sysdev@mb.sympatico.ca>
wrote:

>We have been experiencing problems with data containing binary zeros. We
>want to check the data for low values, to exclude the records with this
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm making a guess and some assumptions here.

You mentioned Oracle.  This makes me think you have NULL values in the
table that you are trying to handle.  These are NOT zeros, or low
values.  This is a BIG SECRET, but I'll let you in on it because it
took me a while to grasp it.

Let's say you have a table and the element is called "Element".

If you do:

Select Element from Table where Element < 1.

It won't find it.

Say you try:

Select Element from table where element not = 1

That doens't return it either even though it's not equal to 1!

Null's are SPECIAL.  

They have NO value not LOW value.

How about:

Select Element from table where element is null;

that returns the record.


So, let's say you want to "assume" a value for the element when it's
not there, or null

Select nvl(element,0) from table where nvl(element,0) = 0;

will return all the "null" or "zero" elements in the table.

Does that help clear up the mystery?
```

<a id="t-36acd85ed2"></a>
## Thread: internal v. external sort-> which do you like? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### RE: internal v. external sort-> which do you like?

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-10-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69851314E@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
I second the idea of using index files. I use it a lot. When using the
logic:

form the key
read temporary file
invalid
   create record for this key
not invalid
   add current value to it
    rewrite record for this key
end read

it dismisses you from writing at least the lowest level in the level breaks
for the output processing.
It may not be as efficient, but there are two huge advantages:
(1) it is extremely easy to write
(2) you yourself are in control. Not some implementation/environment-defined
behind-the-screens probably-optimized misterious routine that is called
'internal sort' or 'external sort'. Especially the latter gives you no
indication of progress whatsoever.


> -----Original Message-----
> From:	James J. Gavan [SMTP:jjgavan@home.com]
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **References:** `<7sglvm$99k$1@talia.mad.ttd.net>`

```


Hello Oscar.

Yes you can change the name of wrun32.exe, just
remember that the license-file wrun32.alc must
have the same prefix as the exe-file.
Example:
wrun32.exe <-> wrun32.alc
wrmod32.exe <-> wrmod32.alc

Just like you I've also been adding c++ functions
to the runtime-module.

Regards

        Anders
```

```
using PC_PRINT and when it's in reasonable shape I'll send that to Jose.

MERANT - HELP ME ! I know Jose is using outdated Visoc - but as a
kindness, is there a Merant contact in Portugal or Spain who can help
the poor sod ? (Failing that, can I get a job with you where people get
paid bucks for technical support).

----------------------------------------------------------------------

exists..." 
----------------------------------------------------------------------
(2) To-day from Nilo :-

 "Subject : CAPUCHO RIDES AGAIN.

(josecapucho@teleweb.pt). He is still waiting some contact with someone.
I said him that Frederico is going to answer him.

Two weeks ago i've received almost the same mail from Jose and i reply
him telling the same that now.

What do you think? I told you that his e-mail address had changed, and i
made him a question about. He didn't answered.

It seems to me that even Jose << doesn't know >> knows the Jose
address...   8o)

anything in Flexus he can use, or should I throw him for a loop and

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 11:28:39 -0600, Howard Brazee
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> But right now everyone (including IBM) does it differently.

Then you can assume I'll beat you to the punch - If you wait for
COBOL-xxx - recall those lamentable tales here of people still using
COBOl-68 because the COBOL-74 compiler was a lousy performer. I would
like to think so - but I doubt that the compromise the Standards people
will arrive at, will actually allow for a "generic" text-book to be
written.

And I mean exactly what I'm writing - You big-iron people should be
insisting NOW, that when COBOL-XXX is officially released, your compiler
developer indicates a target date for implementation of the new
standards. No, not a polite enquiry from the Head of MIS. Take it to the
top of the Xmas tree. A short concise letter to the manufacturer from
your CEO, "If you don't give us COBOL-xxx standards in short-order, then
next time around, your chunk of iron/compiler goes out the door to be
replaced by someone else who can do it for us". We live in the most
consumer-concentric society here in N. America - and given the
informtion explosion of the 'Net - why should you still be accepting
this nonsense.

Hmmmm. Rabble-rouser, says IBM, Unisys etc... Don't give a damn - at my
age it aint gonna blot my resume - other than for entrance to an old
folks home.

For Thane - Your free-advertising agent <G>.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Books on OO COBOL - was Sam's teach yoursel COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCEED2.9B15A685@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8LdJ3.31518$ei1.54191@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <19991006161158.07965.00001399@ngol03.aol.com> <7tglc6$kog@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <37fc1fb0.81087661@news1.attglobal.net> <7tij9p$660@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <37FCD847.20C4F003@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> Every couple of months I check local book stores for a book designed to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> into an old MVS shop.

Howard,

I'll list books below. But you have an initial problem, assuming you are
thinking mainframe. There was a one-page blurb posted saying that Unisys
was 'renting" OO technolgy from Hitachi - but nothing since and no
details. IBM - from what I've gathered so far, the mechanism to write
clases but no pre-packaged classes that give you a kickstart; they pass
you through the gateway to C++ and Smalltalk. See my recent query on
Smalltalk NG  "COBOL and Smalltalk" and the response I got.

To allay one of your concerns, (truly legitimate),the NetExpress source
for ALL classes is available to the developer - you don't need it - but
it gives you a comfort-zone - and of course it gives you ideas on how to
write your own classes.

Now to the books :-

1. objectz.com for Wilson Price's "Elements of OO COBOL" from which I
took my file design (You have my oofiles.zip I believe ? If not mail me
direct and I'll send it). Tutorial style and excellent. (I'm told Will
is currently out there among the Pharisees lecturing to the
unconverted).

2. Arranga & Coyle "OO COBOL" - also excellent - developing step by step
through OO concepts. Pity it didn't have an accompanying disk showing
their source examples.

Both the above used MF COBOL to produce the results. I really fail to
see how you can teach a new topic without examples actually compiled.

3. Useful - Ray Obin's "OO An Introduction for COBOL Programmers" a
paperback quick-reference; he is MF (talk about nepotism <G>) serves on
the standards OO Group. Only available source would be Merant - if still
available.

4. While I'm at it, I'll throw in Adele Goldberg's "Smalltalk-80" - not
for the Smalltalk language per se - but her insight on designing
collections/dictionaries - Merant, (NetExpress)  unashamedly pay
Smalltalk the compliment for their own collection/dictionary classes.

In the Raspberry category :-

4. Doke & Hardgrave "An Introduction to Object COBOL". - Arrived in mail
at 12:00 - finished it 15:00 - sorry guys it didn't tell me a damn thing
that wasn't better covered by 1 and 2. (To be fair to them - they have
also written "COBOL to Java" - looks interesting, but haven't read it in
detail yet).

5. Andrew Topper "OO Development in COBOL". Goes on and on in reams
about theory, lots of diagrams, quasi-code based on that mythical
COBOL-XXX, and quotes 'Standard OO'. Sorry - I'm a meat and potatoes man
- I want concrete examples in source code.

(Wonder if the Standards people, when they meet in Amsterdam next
Spring, might actually get conclusive - perhaps if their Dutch hosts
take them out on the town for Nasi Goreng and a looksee at the
magnificent tulip displays ? Waat sekt U Meneer Frog ?)

Not specifically COBOL - but got the following two as a result of
belonging to one of those damned book clubs for a short while :-

Webster "Pitfalls of OO Development". This gentleman has a computer
without a disk or CD, he doesn't mention data-files at all - Have I lost
it or somethin' - isn't the game all about storage/retrieval of data ?

Coad and Yourdan "OO Design" - YAWwwwwnnnnn . (Ken - you misread me re 
"What did Horace say Winnie ?". You did an excellent job of explaining
in plain Anglo-Saxon Pommie/Ausie speech what this guy Yourdan should
have done - it's no use treating the subject as an abstract - source
'Coad' examples please Mr. Yourdan. (The abstract bit was fine back in
Xerox PARC days when they first invented Simula/Smalltalk).

In my not humble opinion Howard, 1 and 2 will get you flyin'. Who knows,
you might become less "underwhelmed" about OO as a concept <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Books on OO COBOL - was Sam's teach yoursel COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCF162.9D7FFB95@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8LdJ3.31518$ei1.54191@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <19991006161158.07965.00001399@ngol03.aol.com> <7tglc6$kog@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <37fc1fb0.81087661@news1.attglobal.net> <7tij9p$660@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <37FCD847.20C4F003@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FCEED2.9B15A685@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> 1. objectz.com for Wilson Price's "Elements of OO COBOL" from which I
> took my file design (You have my oofiles.zip I believe ? If not mail me
> direct and I'll send it). 

I do.

...

> In my not humble opinion Howard, 1 and 2 will get you flyin'. Who knows,
> you might become less "underwhelmed" about OO as a concept <G>
> 
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

While we have some Java in our shop, it is for screenscraper
applications - the only reason it is OO is because Java is OO, the
applications to not take advantage of OO design.  It would be nice to
become comfortable enough with OO Cobol that I could "sell" it for use
in a project big enough to benefit by its features.  That would be fun
to work on.  Or it would be useful to know enough to know whether my
fears and concerns about OO in general are applicable with OO Cobol.

At any rate, the more I know, the more valuable I am (and the more fun I
get to have).

Thanks for the list!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Books on OO COBOL - was Sam's teach yoursel COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38029D0E.4619B070@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8LdJ3.31518$ei1.54191@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <19991006161158.07965.00001399@ngol03.aol.com> <7tglc6$kog@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <37fc1fb0.81087661@news1.attglobal.net> <7tij9p$660@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <37FCD847.20C4F003@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FCEED2.9B15A685@home.com> <37FF9E5B.12E93B4@IMN.nl>`

```
Just thought I'd pass this one on.  If you haven't already done so,
go to the Fujitsu site http://www.adtools.com/download/index.htm
and download the fourth book in the list :-

	FJ Cobol User's Guide for Windows

It's zipped, so download, unzip and you can read it with Adobe. You want
Chapters 14 - 17. Adobe wont print all in one shot as it runs out of
memory; so I did Page 547 - 647 which gave me Chap 14 thru to the start
of Chap 16. I haven't fully read the text yet, but they have done an
excellent job on the basics of using OO.

No Ken, I don't think they mention de-coupling which you did such a good
job on with - "What did Horace say Winnie ?". Which reminds me of the
following amusing and TRUE story from my days in Taunton, Somerset, some
thirty years ago.

Jack Tar was home on leave from the Royal Navy having done his bit to
protect Her Britannic Majesty's realm. Feeling the urge, Jack found an
accommodating nubile companion. They 'got it on'. How do I put this
delicately ? Jack got stuck. He and his companion had to suffer the
indignity of being carted off on ONE stretcher to a hospital where they
were medically assisted in de-coupling.

Jimmy
```

```
I must agree. I got TYC-21D as a "throw in" with MF Personal COBOL a couple
of years ago.

Now *I* can't give it away, because I'd feel guilty. Any book that teaches
the studuent to convert YYMMDD to MMDDYY by relying on multiplication with
high-order digit truncation just can't earn my endorsement.
```

```
348-S Procedure name 330-Close-Files undeclared, line 190 (first usage).
```

```
>   COPY STDPROC  replacing ==standard-verification== with
> ==special-verification==
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
===> Exactly. In most cases a call could be used, but not always. If
copybooks are standardized with special "macro parameter" names to
substitute as needed every time they're used, along with generating compile
errors if this isn't done right, then they can be used as a limited macro
facility.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
See replies below.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


Boris Nashtut wrote in message <4rTG3.97$e5.4459@newsr1.san.rr.com>...

>When I develop modifications for the system the COPY statement provides
ease
>of implementation. I can change one copybook and it would eventually apply
>to many load modules in many libraries. I can make last minute change and
>start mass recompile.

===> If instead you call a subroutine and you always use dynamic calls (or
whatever appropriate equivalent for your environment), then as soon as you
replace the routine in the load library (and e.g. tell CICS that it's NEW),
all callers immediately call the new version without having to be
recompiled or relinked.

>Debugging:
>When I work on production support and get calls in the middle of the night
>because system is down/abend, the COPY statements caused me allot of
>trouble. I have to constantly switch from 'source' library to 'copy'
library
>just to read code. This is the time when I HATE COPY statement.

===> That's a real pain, and one more reason I don't like having separate
source and copy libraries. DSN's should reflect the language type (using
the standard ones wherever possible) thus supporting edit profiles etc. and
this is more difficult with separate copy libraries. Separate copy
libraries might make sense for multi-platform support but even then
separate subroutine libraries might be better.

>
>Called program would be preferred method for me in both of the above
>instances.

===> Right.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37EC6B1D.FBE8A4C9@melbpc.org.au>`

```
If indeed used with discipline as a macro facility with such safeguards,
these can be useful - but even so, wherever possible, subroutines should be
used instead, especially now that local ones are available (these are
sometimes better).
```

```
"Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
> 
> If indeed used with discipline as a macro facility with such
> safeguards, these can be useful - but even so, wherever possible,
> subroutines should be used instead, especially now that local ones
> are available (these are sometimes better).

One of the best new features in C is the inline routine.  You can call
a subroutine and not have the overhead of all the calls and much
better checking than a macro facility.

Ken
```

```
more unnecessary now that you can have local procedures.
```

<a id="t-5393cafa2e"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Query — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Suppose:
1. User enters data in field "A" and attempts to exit via
Tab, Return, or mouse click, destination: field "B."
2. Program gets control and attempts to
validate user-supplied data in "A". Data are found
to be wrong and (after suitable message)
control is forced to the beginning of field "A"
(via a SetFocus) so user can make corrections.
3. However, this SetFocus to "A" causes loss of
focus from "B"  (where control has apparently moved).
The SetFocus to "A"  triggers a LostFocus for "B,"
which may have validation tests for "B" and a SetFocus
of its own. The whole thing spirals out of control.
Endless loops, dogs marrying cats. It's just awful.

Fujitsu support has mulled, is mulling, will mull.

Any suggestions?


--------------------------------------------------------------------
"I opened my Windows 95 manual and fell into a magical place."
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Yes.  Validate on a screen, not field, basis, then SetFocus to the
first error you find.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia...
> Yes.  Validate on a screen, not field, basis, then SetFocus to the
> first error you find.

That isn't reasonable:
1. One screen has 108 editable fields on it. Others are similarly
packed.
2. The length of time the record would have to be locked while
the user --- well, you get the idea.
3. VB has a control (I'm told) called VALIDATE that gives you
an opportunity to stay in the field.
4. One circumvention we've considered is starting each
LostFocus event with:

    IF ALLOW-FOCUS = 'N'
            EXIT PROGRAM.
    MOVE 'Y' TO ALLOW-FOCUS.

just in case the program got there when it shouldn't.


Then, just before a SetFocus command in field "A," the
statement:

     MOVE 'N' TO ALLOW-FOCUS.

So, then, in the error checking for field "A" we have:

     (error condition detected)
     MOVE 'N' TO ALLOW-FOCUS
     INVOKE FIELD-A 'SetFocus'

And in every other LostFocus possibility;

     IF ALLOW-FOCUS = 'N'
          EXIT PROGRAM.

I'm not looking forward to adding this (in my mind)
klutzy circumvention.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote in message ...
>
>Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>packed.

So what's unreasonable? You take in 108 fields, you edit 108 fields. Seems
fair to me.

>2. The length of time the record would have to be locked while
>the user --- well, you get the idea.

No, I don't get the idea. There is no reason to lock the record while you
edit. You should not procure a lock until the data have been validated and
you are ready to rewrite.

>3. VB has a control (I'm told) called VALIDATE that gives you
>an opportunity to stay in the field.

So use Visual BASIC instead of COBOL for your dialog. Return to your COBOL
program when done. (It's a thought).

>4. One circumvention we've considered is starting each
>LostFocus event with:..klutzy..

Not needed if you follow Judson's sound advice. Besides, I find it extremely
difficult to believe that with 108 fields on a single screen, there is not
at least one 'dependent' edit; that is, the data in field '57' must be
non-blank if and only if the data in field '56' is equal to "Y" or something
like that. Full screen editing makes a lot of sense when you have dependent
edits.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
The views and opinions expressed herein are my own.
As I am self-employed, they also express the views of my employer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Record Locking while doing data validation (was Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
  <snip>
> >2. The length of time the record would have to be locked while
> >the user --- well, you get the idea.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, maybe this shows my "mainframe background" - but I agree with the
concept of "locking a record" while you do the editing.  Otherwise, any edits
that you do against "existing" field data may or may not be "valid" when you
have finished your editing.

NOTE: if your application ONLY "edits" input fields - that don't depend at
all upon existing data (in the "record in storage") then this does become
irrelevant.

The alternative method (that I know of) is to "save" the record - but don't
lock it, then do your editing, then (if everything passes), RE-get the record
(this time with a LOCK), then compare the "new" record with the saved
record - and FINALLY if everything is OK (original edits and no changes to
the record), do the update.

IMHO, if you don't do one of these, then you had better do the other
(unless - of course - you are in a "single user" environment - or use some
other type of "enquing").

Maybe it is simplistic, but I guess that I would recommend the first method -
if your validation - is "relatively" quick and would recommend the second
method if your editing is slow (compared to the multi-user "demand" for
records).  (With the first method, do make certain that you "handle" the case
where the "user" walks away from a terminal for a break, lunch, overnight -
or a 2-week vacation - in the middle of doing some data entry.)

NOTE WELL:  All of the previous assumes that you have a method of doing
"individual record locking".  With some environments, you can only "lock" a
block of records - or worse a full file.  This too can "impact" your
decisions on what/how to lock.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation (was Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6USJ3.2949$mN5.161919@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com...
>
>
> The alternative method (that I know of) is to "save" the record -
but don't
> lock it, then do your editing, then (if everything passes), RE-get
the record
> (this time with a LOCK), then compare the "new" record with the
saved
> record - and FINALLY if everything is OK (original edits and no
changes to
> the record), do the update.

Our plan was:
1. Get the record and release the locks.
2. If the user changes a field, test the new value.
3. If valid, get the record with lock, replace the field, rewrite the
record.
4. If an invalid entry, issue warning, and reset the input screen
to the field being worked on.

It is, of course, this last item that's at issue because the program
does
not get control - or at least we can't find a way to get control -
until after
the field pointer has moved to the start of another field. Our pointer
reset causes an automatic loss of focus from whereever the pointer
has moved. This LostFocus event causes the program to meander
through code in a field the user may not yet have visited.

For example: Suppose Field A: "Is tornado coming (Y/N)?" and
Field B is "Type of warning:" The LostFocus event for Field B is
(after passing evaluation tests)
IF FIELD-A NOT = "N"
     IF FIELD-B = 'A' PERFORM CALL-BOSS END-IF
     IF FIELD-B = 'B' PERFORM (something else) END-IF
    PERFORM SOUND-EVACUATION-WARNING.

And the user enters a "B" in Field-A.

> Maybe it is simplistic, but I guess that I would recommend the first
method -

One disadvantage I can see in editting the whole screen occurs when
each field on the screen consumes significant resources, such as a
file lookup on a part number. Locking a record while (maybe several
hundred) file look-ups take place seems unacceptable compared to
a field-by-field edit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation (was Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F83A70.2D8239F1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <6USJ3.2949$mN5.161919@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```


Jerry P wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> a field-by-field edit.

Intersting topic. Strange how the 'real-world' contradicts the simple
Windows-type examples where you see an empty screen, fill the record,
validate, then file it.

Probably a play on words, but taking Jerry's example, (caveat I'm not
into multi-user record-locking, but same concepts really apply). 

Having a system which has worked in DOS for almost 20 years, my user is
not about to switch to that bloody tab-key so I'm testing if ENTER-key
is used to end-a-fieldentry or is it truly the default <OK> button. So
rather than tabs, I'm using Business-Logic to control "set-nextfocs"

Right or wrong I validate each field by returning the value from Dialog
to Business-Logic when entered, and where necessary do file/collection
'look-ups'.

- If incorrect
     error-messasge
     set CurrentObject = this-field, invoke Dialog            		
	"setfocus-CurrentObject" "clearing" or "emptying"
- if correct, increment this-field 
     set CurrentObject = this-field, invoke Dialog            		
     	"setfocus-CurrentObject"
- if correct and this-field > MaxField
     invoke Dialog "setfocus-DefaultOK"

I take Jerry's point. Read the record to get it into your program and
then unlock. You should not be concerned with the file until the user
clicks on the OK-button, then as necesary you go through your
lock/unllock to write/rewrite the record, and of course don't even
access the file if the user hasn't changed the record.

Coupled with the above I use Level 88s :-

01			pic 9.
  88 RecordFound   	value 1.
  88 RecordNotFound 	value 0.

01 			pic 9.
  88 RecordChanged	value 1.
  88 RecordNotChanged	value 0.

On the question of using tab-key - I find it extremely irritating. I
have one program, (actually it represents 80% of the activity in the
system), which is a 10 x 24 table. Assuming user is entering
'past-history' - given there are only 5 points to be inspected for 3
inspection dates gives me a 'true' data-entry table of 3 x 5  - I
disable all remaining entry-fields. But, I still have to 'guide' the
user down column 1, back up to column 2, then column 3, and at that
stage after (3, 5) has been entered, "setfocs-OKdefault" 

Changes - he randomly selects fields, follows same routine, back to
Business-Logic, which checks, and if correct "setfocus-OKdefault".

Multi-User 

What I've described so far works fine as an outline for both single and
multi-user. But take the situation where user(Bill) accesses Record 123;
while you are playing with it user(Jerry) also accesses Record 123 -
rather than tie-up the file with locks, when you press <OK> you should
do a second read of Record 123, locking the record - do a field-by-field
comparison and display back at you, if necessary, with a message saying
fields have since been changed - you are looking at latest state before
updating. (Rather than the field-by-field comparision, if records had a
time-stamp - this would give an easier approach; another possibility  -
a flag(88s) in the record - RecordBeingUsed = 1, RecordNotUsed = 0).

Two situations can occur - user(Jerry) clicks on <OK>

- if currently unlocked he updates - and if you didn't beat him to the
  punch you're going to get back that "fields-changed" message
- if he attempts to update while you have temporarily got it locked
he's   the one who will get back the "fields-changed" message when you
unlock

What we are talking about, is what in the old days we would have called
'Masterfiles' - it isn't relevant to individual transactions such as
entering invoices into an A/P system is it - or is it ?

Arguably, one could contend that the likelihood of two users changing
the same 'existing' master record are highly unlikely, and it isn't
unreasonable to have that record locked while user(Bill) is editing it.
(You've got this file in i-o mode, and those look-up files Jerry
mentioned are opened as input only with no locks required).
Unless I have a phone in each hand and make two phone calls to Amex
simultaneously, to tell them they've misspelled my last name - not much
likelihood two Amex operators are going to change my name, is there ?

Problem arises if user(Jimmy), three floors down, is entering A/P
transactions and needs to access Record 123 to confirm it exists before
the system will accept an invoice. Such a situation suggests having the
record locked/unlocked ONLY for the duration of your rewrite, triggered
by your <OK>. This situation can only occur with EXISTING records.

Sorry to plug it guys - but a COBOL site with examples of how to do
things in the 'real-world' would have helped here. We are discussing a
topic for which literally thousands of COBOLers have already found a
solution.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
