###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation (was Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yJ1K3.280$01.2947@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>Michael Mattias wrote:
>  <snip>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>have finished your editing.

This is a circular argument. :-)  If the above is true, then how are you
better by editing the fields one at a time as they are entered?  Would
you not need to keep accessing the record for each field validated? ;-)

Anyway, since you are updating an existing record, you must read it first
to display its current contents, or the user is entering the data blind.
Save off that record.  When the changes have been entered, validate them
against the saved data.  If the data is valid, then lock the record for
update.  If the data in the record has changed from the saved data, then
release the record and notify the user.

If you allow a user to modify data in a record, without locking it during
the process, you must allow for the possibility that the data has been
changed during that time.  This is irrelevant of whether the data is
validated by field or by screen.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation (was Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F8CB69.D7B3E037@home.com>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8h11$9pi@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <yJ1K3.280$01.2947@news3.mia>`

```


> William M. Klein wrote: <snip>.......

Have to go out this morning but thought I should add this as a
supplement to what I've already written. Ignore my 'business' about
RecordUse/NotUsed flag - it's a no-brainer and necessitates two
re-writes. It's just as dangerous as the following. 

Editing with Locked Record -

Betsy works for a large corporation; her main job setting-up new vendors
and changing records for same. Diligent worker - on flexi-time comes in
at 7:30 and keeps busy at her keyboard. She brings up the existing
record for Abercromby Corporation, checks her watch - it's 10 am -
coffee time. With Abercromby Corp still staring at you on the screen she
takes off for coffee. Normally she's back at her desk by 10:15 but
to-day she gets into a gab-fest with her co-workers at the coffee
counter. On the way back to her desk she bumps into the deputy
controller. "Oh Betsy come with me, there's something I'd like you to
do....". She gets back to her desk at 11:05 - then actions that
Abercromby record.

Somewhere else in the building at 10:15 somebody was trying to enter a
bunch of invoices for Abercromby.....

-never happened/wont happen - you gotta be kidding.

Record locking for duration of an edit has to be a No-No. The situation
of course, varies depending upon the application - but possibly some
time-out mechanism if somebody has a record displayed for too long.
Again, we need various examples.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Query

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M2TJ3.2987$mN5.162060@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <LdIJ3.8373$Iv5.69013@news2.mia> <QtOJ3.2694$mN5.147328@typhoon01.swbell.net> <q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:q5PJ3.2178$Iw5.61475@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> >3. VB has a control (I'm told) called VALIDATE that gives you
> >an opportunity to stay in the field.
>
> So use Visual BASIC instead of COBOL for your dialog. Return to your
COBOL
> program when done. (It's a thought).

It's not a thought. I mentioned it to illustrate the construct is
available in other languages, not as a thinking-out-loud list
of possibilities. VB is almost totally useless for our purposes.
>
>
> Not needed if you follow Judson's sound advice. Besides, I find it
extremely
> difficult to believe that with 108 fields on a single screen, there
is not
> at least one 'dependent' edit; that is, the data in field '57' must
be
> non-blank if and only if the data in field '56' is equal to "Y" or
something
> like that. Full screen editing makes a lot of sense when you have
dependent
> edits.

To do full screen editing, the record must be locked during the entire
editing process (exception below). It may take a user an unacceptable
amount of time (maybe several hours) to fill the screen, so locking
the record for that duration is out.

One could get a copy of the record,
wait for the user to complete the screen, then get a fresh copy of the
record with lock and compare the recently-edited data with the fresh
copy. Where differences exist, the newly-edited data could be
inserted into the record and the record rewritten.

I'd just rather do it field-by-field and am looking for an easier way
than trapping for do-not-execute-this-event code in each LostFocus
procedure.
```

On Sat, 2 Oct 1999 22:58:57 -0500, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
wrote:

>Suppose:
>1. User enters data in field "A" and attempts to exit via
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-7a7bab612f"></a>
## Thread: "low-values" — 2 msgs

### Re: "low-values"

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991002125941.02146.00000036@ng-fm1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<37f3c25b.15328200@news.mindspring.com>`

```
>You might try defining a working storage field with low-values as:
>
>    01  W-LOW-VALUES           PIC (2)   VALUE X'0000'.

That won't work, will it?  The default USAGE in this case will be DISPLAY. 
Therefore, assigning '0000' will leave in storage the hex values of 'F0F0F0F0'.

Wouldn't you want to use the actual figurative constant, LOW-VALUES?

RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F6C91D.ABF7E0EA@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37f3c25b.15328200@news.mindspring.com> <19991002125941.02146.00000036@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
RoadRaat wrote:
> 
> >You might try defining a working storage field with low-values as:
> >
> >    01  W-LOW-VALUES           PIC (2)   VALUE X'0000'.

The PIC is in error, if you make it "X(2) ..." it'll be fine. The VALUE
clause is invalid for any PIC 9...

> That won't work, will it?  The default USAGE in this case will be DISPLAY.
> Therefore, assigning '0000' will leave in storage the hex values of 'F0F0F0F0'.

He didn't assign a value of '0000'; but X'0000', which is LOW-VALUES in
the IBM mainframe world.

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-c36615dbfc"></a>
## Thread: Where can i find a free cobol compiler — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-c36615dbfc)._

#### ↳ Re: Where can i find a free cobol compiler

- **From:** luvisi@andru.sonoma.edu

## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 1 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-5256e5a514)._

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eql3fLUD$GA.306@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<7scoqn$5or$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`

```
Even with Cobol's recent extensions, I like PL/1 better as a language. It's
much better for bit manipulation, user functions, and probably even local
scopes/procedures and recursion although Cobol lately supports those, at
least some Cobol's support recursion although I'm not sure of the details.
PL/1's macro facility is also nice. And I think it still has the PIC thing
and support for display decimal fields just like Cobol. Other than missing
the Search verbs and maybe a few other things, PL/1 probably does
everything Cobol does and is therefore (with the above) better overall.

I'm much fresher on Cobol and rustier in PL/1 at present, though.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA

<a id="t-c2659a983b"></a>
## Thread: Searching Tables — 7 this month / 28 total

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-c2659a983b)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38039202.89AC376D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <7soht4$cav@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 

Bill,

Can't find it now I want it - but I'm referring to your 'hypothetical'
about using a mechanism to 'halve' the table, also I think given the
name 'binary chop (???). If you are going to 'chop' then it is only
going to work with a sorted table - true/false ?

A bit late in the day having kick-started this with Perform v Search,
but these are the circumstances under which I need to do a 'search' -
and I'm using OO/GUI - playing with objects.

(1) Search table - as in following, where I'm using hard-coded tables to 
generate descriptions :-

  Key: V10BES - gives - V-BAND ON 10 IN. PIPE BOTTOM (east side)

       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "bottom".
       *>------------------------------------------------------------
       Working-storage section.
       01 n                        pic x(4) comp-5.
       78 wt-kount                 value 23.
       01 wt1.
          05 pic x(33) value "B   ,BOTTOM ~      ,            ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BD  ,BEND~         ,            ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BE  ,BOTTOM EAST ~ ,            ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BENE,BOTTOM EAST ~ ,(north end) ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BES ,BOTTOM ~      ,(east side) ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BMD ,BOTTOM MID ~  ,            ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BN  ,BOTTOM NORTH ~,            ~".
          05 pic x(33) value "BNE ,BOTTOM N.E. ~ ,            ~".

SEARCH will work great because I've designed the various tables to be
alphabetical; the tildes shown are for stringing delimited by "~"

The above technique reduces my storage to a 6-character-key from what
was a 40 character description. Initially the re-designed system
contained a 995 generic description list-box. Oooh I thought - supposing
that grows to 1,500 ? So I used the above approach and it works fine. It
isn't even discernible that it goes through its 'machinations' to
produce the description.

(2) Listbox - using a Collection - no problem 'on record select" I have
reference to the PrimeKey

(3) Dictionary - invoke MyDictionary "includesKey" using MyKey
                    returning ls-Bool

   (This lets the class Dictionary do the donkey-work for searching)

(4) Droplists - AHhhhh - I create a sorted collection of objects which
is passed to the control - droplist.  Now I'm trying to compare apples
with oranges. Assuming the droplist contained the following objects :-

		AB Alberta
		BC British Columbia
		MN Manitoba etc......

and my key in the file-record for Province/State is Pic x(02) "BC"; as I
see it, I have no choice but to do it using :-

	Perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size (size of 
						collection)
           'unpack' each object in turn to compare text to text 
           if file-key = text-key I've got description for the display
	End-perform    

If anybody can suggest a better technique than (4) I'd love to hear -
and for the 'SP2-fan', don't forget I'm dabbling with objects not text
as in SP2 or Dialog System tables.(In most situations my droplists are
between 10 - 40 entries - so perhaps no big deal)

Volker - I mislead you a bit - I can use SEARCH with (1); (2) and (3)
are covered - it's (4) which seems to require a perform varying.....

Bob Wolfe - this is what I was alluding to about needing objects.
Another facet - from SP2 or N/E Dialog System you can do a multi-select
from a Listbox. Using OO/GUI I can return that multi-selection as Class
Bag - literally a shopping-bag that you just chuck objects into,
unordered, and the objects could contain bitmaps. Then you have the
flexibility to chug-a-lug that bag through your system using the
individual objects in the bag as you see fit, turning them into an
ordered or sorted collection, if you wish. (I appreciate Bob - you're
watching but until there are more than the three of us here who confess
to using OO - no point you getting excited is there ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

CORRECTION ! (4) Checking Droplists - I put that ass-about-face. If I

When I want to display for an 'Existing' record a description in the

description and turn it into an object for displaying, or

(b) Using the existing droplist collection from which I'm allowing the
user to make subsequent selections, use the perform varying... to check

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard D Riehle <laoXhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
peter dashwood wrote in message <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
>3. COBOL is a self documenting, easily maintainable language.

COBOL has some virtues of a self-documenting language, when 
properly coded by a programmer concerned about such things.  
COBOL is not - never has been - "easily maintainable" compared 
to more modern languages that eliminate dependency on global data
definitions.  

This seems to have been improved in the object-oriented COBOL
standard with its better support for encapsulation.  Now, if we 
could just get programmers to understand how to use this capability,
COBOL would become an "easily maintainable language."  Of course,
the same problem with programmers exists in C++, Ada 95, Modula-3,
and other object-oriented programming languages that still permit
global data.  

As to searching tables, I agree with those who believe the SEARCH
construct is a better alternative.  It is more "self-documenting,"
and definitely more conducive to maintainability.  And it can be
designed as part of an encapsulated COBOL class to make it safer.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t8uj0$gsk@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"Local" versus "global" variables became available with the '85 Standard (and
nested programs). It has nothing to do with the availability of OO constructs
or methodology.  OF COURSE, OO does provide "additional" encapsulation
features, but if programmers don't know how/when to use "local" variables in
COBOL today (14 years after they became "Standard"), then that must mean that

  A) their training has been poor (or)
  B) their '85 Standard compilers have been "undesirable" (or)
  C) the advantages of "local" vs "global" data have not proven themselves
sufficient to justify the paradigm shift in programming methodology - for
COBOL programmers.

My personal guess is that it is (at least) a bit of all 3.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F890B3.C9181D43@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <7t8uj0$gsk@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> "Local" versus "global" variables became available with the '85
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Local storage is as I understand it the storage that is created every
time you enter a particular program element starting with a PROGRAM ID
section.

The concept of local storage is more at a perform level where if you
need a simple loop variable you define one in the perform and discard
it without clashing with other names.  The concept of passed values
into routines also comes under this.

In order to correctly implement it you need many PROGRAM ID section
and multiple calls.  This has a large degree of code overhead to do in
Cobol, if it was short and simple you would see a change.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F81690.548D3F91@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```


Richard D Riehle wrote:
> 
> peter dashwood wrote in message <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com>...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lao Xiao Hai


I would contend that COBOL HAS A great virtue in that it is
self-documenting. What always attracted me to the language was (a)
Its readability - I don't find a great necessity to include comment 
statements too often and (b) because of this readability, and given 
good design concepts, whether you are talking structured or OO - it is
maintainable. (It's when programmers use ambiguous constructs or lack of
Level 88s to indicate what is going on, that we run into trouble. OK for
me, a loner - I'm maintaining programs written in my own particular
style, but for a big shop, as somebody eloquently put it, "if you're the
poor little sod who has to look at the program at 2 am in the
morning...".

As regards maintainability, I don't think those new languages have it
over COBOL. Bill Klein has correctly pointed out that "local-storage"
concepts have been available for some time. Mea culpa; in structured I
confess to not having used 'local'. Why - raises an interesting
observation - I can't recall any texts I've read that help in this type
of area. As a further example - entry-points - never used them. Why ?
Because I couldn't comprehend their construct, from help available with
the compiler. Stephen Gennard (Merant, Newbury) posted source some 2-3
years ago to somebody querying how to use COBOL with VB. Read Stephen's
example and BINGO! - I could understand what entry-points were about.
One simple example illustrated more than reams written in a book. 
I've tried to raise this issue here before, but no takers - we do need a
COBOL site with numerous examples to help COBOLers on their way. (If and
when people do switch to OO - then this area is an obvious candidate for
illustrating with examples).

As an OO user I wouldn't claim that OO has the edge over structured; It
is structured COBOL plus some goodies, e.g. the ability to have a
variable length collection as opposed, within structured, deciding to
have a fixed-length table/collection or one based on OCCURS DEPENDING.

From a recent book I picked up the phrase "three-tiered-design", which I
think describes it well  - business-logic, GUI-interface and DB
Interface, handling(flat or DB files). This concept is just as readily
available in a well-designed structured system. Not many, but some in
the NG here have indicated that they hive-off file handling as a
separate activity - and this is parallel to what I'm doing in OO.

Those other languages you refer to - out of curiosity take a peek at
their NewsGroups - they run into exactly the same sorts of problems -
and it is, I would suggest, a lack of comprehension about good system
design, before you get down to coding.

I take your point about the SEARCH verb - I was the one that
kick-started this topic by asking the question <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO makes maintenance easier? was: Searching Tables

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F8A96D.9F09C57C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <37F81690.548D3F91@home.com>`

```
> COBOL has some virtues of a self-documenting language, when
> properly coded by a programmer concerned about such things.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lao Xiao Hai

Having reusable, relatively hidden objects makes maintenance easier - 
provided the part of your code needing maintenance isn't in those
objects, and provided you can 100% trust those objects.

But trying to trace down errors and maintenance changes to these
objects, and to change them for one application without breaking other
applications makes maintenance much MORE difficult.

As with any choice, there are trade offs.  A simple extract is probably
better off without OO, but an integrated operating system certainly
benefits with OO.

Same thing with global variables.  There are times where I would like to
have local variables with COBOL.  In those cases, I usually just make
UNIQUE variables which aren't used elsewhere.  But having multiple
variables with the same names certainly does not make maintenance
EASIER.  If everything is global, I can have my editor find every
occurrence of a variable and know I'm getting the same thing each time.  

My experience with modern languages is limited to C, C++, Java, & Visual
Basic.  While my expertise in them is limited, my biggest gripe with
them is that it is much harder to maintain a bunch of modules in
different files and libraries than it is to maintain one structured
program.

Of course theoretically, each module and object works exactly as
desired, so they won't be part of the problem...
```

<a id="t-70a07e424c"></a>
## Thread: LE Dump in Sort Output Procedure — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### LE Dump in Sort Output Procedure

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37F78AFB.9E915B18@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
In preparation for giving a brief dump debugging session using the
CEEDUMP, I found an anomaly when the abend occurs in a SORT OUTPUT
PROCEDURE.  The COBOL compiler is COBOL FOR MVS and VM.  LE is the LE
for OS390 release 2.5.  I found that the record area associated with the
SD doesn't appear in the dump and if the SD is not the last descriptor
in the list the BLF numbers for the files after the SD are off by 1 (see
example below).  This is a minor annoyance for the CEEDUMP in R2.5 (LE
1.8 I think) is a vast improvement over that associated with OS390 1.3
(LE 1.5).  You can find the record last read (or to be written) for a
file by doing a find for BLF 1 20.  The indexes are before the first
BLF.  The WORKING STORAGE can be found by doing a find on BLW 1 20 while
the LINKAGE SECTION can be found by doing a find on BLL 1 20.  The
CEEDUMP is manageable even if you blow up in a SORT as was done
intentionally in this example.  

I suspect that the BLF associated with an SD is a pseudo BLF (Base
Locator File) and that LE is unaware of it because there probably is no
associated FIB (File Information Block).  The reasons that this anomaly
concerns me is that for programs with SD statements at other than the
end, the BLF numbers do not match the listing.  This will be even more
confusing if the records on one or more of the files or associated with
the SORT file are over 4K in length thus causing multiple BLF numbers to
be assigned (BLF-n for the first 4K, BLF-n+1 for the second 4K, etc.). 
Given that in general it is easy to correlate the information in the
dump to the COBOL listing, this is a shame.    

The program File Section is as follows where the DDNAME associated with
a file is the same as the name of the file in the FD statement: 

FD  FILEA BLOCK 0.
01  RECORD-A.                    BLF-00
    COPY AREC.
     snip
SD  SORTWK01.
01  SORTWK-RECORD.               BLF-01
    COPY SREC.
    snip
FD  FILE-B BLOCK 0. 
01  RECORD-B.                    BLF-02 
    COPY BREC. 
FD  FILE-C BLOCK 0.
01  RECORD-C.                    BLF-03  
    COPY CREC.                    	

The CEEDUMP has

BLF-00 FILEA

record for FILEA

BLF-01 FILEB

record for FILEB

BLF-02 FILEC

record for FILEC

rest of program.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

---

<a id="t-ba5718b8b0"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL - Loading a List — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL - Loading a List

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t89s4$pmn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7skqu5$579@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com> <19990926133140.08966.00000778@ngol08.aol.com> <7slu1f$ld4@dfw-ixnews14.ix.netcom.com> <37EE9E55.97F17493@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

: Program Customer Edit :- left-side of screen is dialog to display a
: customer record and right-side is a list box of customers. 

: - 	If new customer press 'Add" pushbutton and enter data; on 	
	...
: -	 if delete customer press "Delete" pushbutton; on OKDelete,
	...
: -	if change customer, you select from listbox and display on

: As you can see, you get a 'revised' Listbox from each transaction. Don't

I don't use list boxes or combo boxes for Customers or Products,
there tends to be thousands of them - far too many to load
reasonably.  I use a separate panel with a repeat group into
which I read 10 (or so) at a time and allow paging forwards and
backwards.

In the customer code field they could input the correct code
and have the customer appear, or they could input (for example,
depending on system) a part code or a part alpha key and press
a function key or button.  The program passes this to the
listing program which opens a panel and starts displaying at
that point using the file key as appropriate.  10 are displayed
and the user can move up and down these, as they do so the
full customer details are read and displayed below the
select list.  PageUp/Down gives forward/back.  Function key
used in the select list gives access to the maintenance 
function which opens a new panel on top.  When maintenance is
finished the select panel reloads starting at the key it
last started so new details (even if created elsewhere)
are loaded.

By having a modular design the customer (or product or whatever)
maintenance screen can call the select list, or after calling
the select list from, say, orders, it can call the maintenance
screen.

A mechanism is then required to prevent recursion.  I use a
EXTERNAL COPYed data area with flags indicating which panels
are active so that the flag is checked before calling.

This is done using SP2, mainly in text mode (but works 
identically in Windows).

Richard
```

## Thread: To create a COBOL program from a question: — 4 msgs

### To create a COBOL program from a question:

- **From:** Masood <callmasood@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rvfnvmumom31@corp.supernews.com>`

```
PROBLEM:
     A program is required to validate batches of transactions that are 
held on a disk file.Each batch should consist of up to 100 transaction 
records followed by a batch control record.Each field of the transaction 
record needs to be validated and hash totals of all the numeric fields 
calculated.
     If the accumulated hash totals match those of the batch control 
record AND there are no invalid records AND the number of transaction 
records in the batch does not exceeds 100,then the batch is to be classed 
as valid.All valid records are to be written to a valid file.An erroneous 
batch is to be printed out on an error report that contains appropriate 
headings,with each batch starting on a new page.It is also required that 
the accumulated hash totals for that batch are printed after each invalid 
batch.A separate disk file is to be used as temporary storage of a batch 
until the validity of the batch is ascertained.

Record description is as follows:

INPUT FILE
This file is made up from two types of records,i.e.:-
     i) The actual transaction records
    ii) Batch control records

TRANSACTION RECORDS CONSISTS OF THE FOLLOWING FIELDS:-
     Record Type                             1 alphanumeric "T"
     Part No.                                8 numeric
     Customer/Supplier No.                   6 numeric
     Date supplied/Received                  6 numeric
     Quantity                                5 numeric


BATCH CONTROL RECORDS CONSISTS OF THE FOLLOWING FIELDS:-
     Record Type                             1 alphanumeric "B"
     Hash total of Customer/Supplier No's.   8 numeric
     Hash total of Dates                     8 numeric
     Hash total of Quantities                8 numeric
     Hash total of Part No's                 8 numeric



------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

<a id="t-8560d35505"></a>
## Thread: How do you pass an arry between modules ? — 3 msgs

### How do you pass an arry between modules ?

- **From:** "Liam Tovey" <s347586@student.uq.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t8qqe$sj8$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
I am running acucobol on windows 98. Just wondering how you pass an array of
data between different modules. Is it done via the linkages section, and if
so what is the code used to pass this.

Thanks in advance

Liam
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you pass an arry between modules ?

- **From:** "The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jTJ3.10794$Pt.76502@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7t8qqe$sj8$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
That's what's so wonderful about COBOL: it slices, it dices, it looks at
data any way you want it.

Yes, in the LINKAGE SECTION is where you'd place items that are to be
passed. For example...

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  LS-DATA PIC X(70).
01  LS-ARRAY REDEFINES LS-DATA.
    05  LS-ARRAY-ITEMS OCCURS 10 TIMES.
        10  LS-ARRAY-ITEM-X PIC X(05).
        10  LS-ARRAY-ITEM-9 PIC S999 COMP-3.

There, you've got your mixed strings and packed decimal items defined. Now,
in order to be able to address these data elements, you're gonna need the
following statement...

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LS-DATA.

This makes everything in your LINKAGE SECTION available to your code. Now
you can go about quite happily referencing LS-ARRAY-ITEM-9(WS-ITEM-NBR) with
impunity... assuming that the value of WS-ITEM-NBR always falls between +1
and +10.

Understand?? :)

(::Yeah, I know... I probably just did his homework for him... I'm feeling
generous tonight::)
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you pass an arry between modules ?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t92kj$im1$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7t8qqe$sj8$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
One calls using data in working-storage (usually), and sees the data within
the subroutine as being in the linkage section.   The linkage section, as
you would expect from the name, is really a way of linking the subroutine
procedures back to data that exists in the calling program.

An array is data the same as any other record.  It exists in the calling
program, and is seen through the linkage in the subroutine.  A common way of
handling that, and ensuring that the descriptions are the same in both
modules, is to make it a copied file.

As to actual code, I think perhaps you better do at least some of your
homework yourself.


Liam Tovey wrote in message <7t8qqe$sj8$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>...
>I am running acucobol on windows 98. Just wondering how you pass an array
of
>data between different modules. Is it done via the linkages section, and if
>so what is the code used to pass this.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-b4d4510e22"></a>
## Thread: Is COBOL more powerful... — 1 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-b4d4510e22)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is COBOL more powerful...

- **From:** Richard D Riehle <laoXhai@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t8vvp$dtt@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7stjpk$92h$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <37F25E7C.50ED4DE5@home.com>`

```
In article <37F25E7C.50ED4DE5@home.com>,
	"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>COBOL is the dominant programming language of the corporate world, with
>over 80% of ALL code written in COBOL. For the existing base of
>software, estimates range from 70 billion lines of COBOL code to over
>200 billion. Although the estimates vary, it is fair to say that there
>are billions of lines of COBOL code."

The problem with comparing languages is often one of expressibility
versus expresssivness.  COBOL, by design, is expressive of the kinds
of problems encountered in business data processing.  C++, while it
can be used to express solutions to such problems (expressibility)
is not inherently suited to expressiveness for such solutions. 
Object-oriented COBOL is a good choice where expressivness is more 
important than expressibility for financial and accounting software
solutions.

And for financial applications, it is still the only object-oriented
programming language, with the exception of Ada 95, that has direct
support for monetary data types and a large number of options for
formatting and correctly handling them.  C++ does not have this
capability, although one can design a class to emulate it.  This
is one of those cases where a built-in type is more appropriate 
than depending on a user-defined class.  

C++ is also excessively needy of pointer constructs.  Such constructs
are unnecessary for most business data processing problems.  On the
other hand, COBOL is a little weak in its support for the kind of
sophisticated data structures possible in C++.  For example, COBOL
programmers typically do not have much experience with Linked Lists,
Binary Search Trees, etc.  It is a matter of picking the language
that best expresses solutions to the kind of problems one is 
attempting to solve.  For business data processing, the best choice
would be either COBOL or Ada 95.

Lao Xiao Hai
```

---

<a id="t-153332e4d4"></a>
## Thread: Novice question: What is meaning of this error? — 2 msgs

### Novice question: What is meaning of this error?

- **From:** "B.B. Bigab" <bigab@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7t9agn$ehg$1@vixen.cso.uiuc.edu>`

```
Hi everybody,

I've just starting tinkering around with COBOL one month ago so there's alot
for me to learn. In a program I'm writing, I get the following error when I
run it:

File error 46 on file1
COBOL error at 0000EA in cbl.out

I'm using COBOL 85 and Acucobol-85 complier on a Unix environment.

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Novice question: What is meaning of this error?

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SLWJ3.1542$0l6.2715@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<7t9agn$ehg$1@vixen.cso.uiuc.edu>`

```
A file error with a prefix of 4 means Error in Application Logic.
Specifically File Error 46 means that that a sequential read was attempted
but the end of file was already reached.

You probably need the "AT END" clause on the READ statement.

Example:

READ IN-FILE-RECORD
       AT END MOVE "YES" TO END-OF-FILE.

PERFORM 2000-PROCESS-LOOP
       UNTIL END-OF-FILE  = "YES".
```

information for also *writing* to RM/COBOL files.

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL Index File Structure

- **From:** "S Donleavy" <s.donleavy@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tghoe$ji7$1@nclient11-gui.server.virgin.net>`
- **References:** `<m4s9t7.q1l.ln@bergen.swp-irma.de>`

```
I'm not sure about the existence of any formal documentation on the subject
but it depends upon which version of RM you are running
RM74 used to use 2 Physical files for each indexed file = one for the data
and
one for the index, with the index held as a linked list
RM85 puts both the index and data in the same .dat file with the index at
the
start of the file (minimum size 8k) in the form of a Binary+ Tree and the
data
records to follow - most Computer Science text books describe Binary Trees
quite well

```
Sunshyne wrote:
>
>I just started Cobol a couple weeks ago.  We had to type a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>emailing my instructor to no avail.

You are having serious problems with missing and misplaced periods. :-)
You also misspelled 'PROCEDURE DIVSION'.  Apply the changes indicated
below and the program will compile.

Comparing files ASSIGN1.OLD and ASSIGN1.CBL
****** ASSIGN1.OLD
                           ASSIGN TO "A:EMP.DAT".
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
                           ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
       DATA DIVISION.
****** ASSIGN1.CBL
                           ASSIGN TO "A:EMP.DAT"
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
                           ASSIGN TO PRINTER
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
       DATA DIVISION.

****** ASSIGN1.OLD
       FD  EMPLOYEE-DATA
****** ASSIGN1.CBL
       FD  EMPLOYEE-DATA.

****** ASSIGN1.OLD
       FD  PAYROLL-LISTING
****** ASSIGN1.CBL
       FD  PAYROLL-LISTING.

****** ASSIGN1.OLD
       PROCEDURE DIVSION.
****** ASSIGN1.CBL
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

****** ASSIGN1.OLD
       200-WAGE-ROUTINE
****** ASSIGN1.CBL
       200-WAGE-ROUTINE.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCB0FF.10C87F32@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net> <ukIK3.1695$x4.45017@news2.mia> <37FB750D.870FD155@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FC680E.2F9187D3@att.net> <2e1L3.4285$eM4.345931@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Warren Porter wrote:

> One of the hardest bugs to find involved a nested IF statement with the
> final period in column 73.  As for the next statement, sometimes it would,
> sometimes "it had a headache".

I remember one of those back in the days of poring over printed listings
to find errors.  But when I edit with a 72 column TSO display, this is
no problem at all.  If I can see the period, so can the compiler.

I haven't been convinced of the value of one period paragraphs, even if
I didn't need them to make sure my precompiler expansions get terminated
completely.  Actually, I haven't been even CLOSE to being convinced.  An
explicit full stop is clear and solid.

But it never hurts to add a few END-IFs to old code, just to clarify the
logic.  (or if it does hurt, that hurt is worth while avoiding a bigger
hurt later).  Besides clarifying the logic, this will often catch extra
periods.

Oh, it might cause problems down the road if I put END-IF if there is a
NEXT SENTENCE in the IF code.  The ANSI committee has seen to that, but
I haven't seen a compiler which is that dumb yet.
```

Paragraph-one.
   (etc.)You echo my sentiments exactly.  I tried using them for a couple
programs, and found one period paragraphs put several restrictions that I
had not thought of on me ... I did not like the result. It is funny, I also
found the use of the "end" constructs more of a hinderence to clear reading
than a help.  I am finding that I can read my code easily, as can all my
employees.  However, coming from modern educational facilities, they
slavishly use end-constructs and tabs to highlight blocks of code.  I find
it hard to follow.  Try this:  Grab a big chunk of code, and reformat it
just like this paragraph. See if it is readable.  I will bet my code is, and
theirs is totally illegible.
Paragraph-two.
    (etc.) Perhaps that would serve as a programming specification.

>
>But it never hurts to add a few END-IFs to old code, just to clarify the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>I haven't seen a compiler which is that dumb yet.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tldt5$7fl$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net> <ukIK3.1695$x4.45017@news2.mia> <37FB750D.870FD155@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FC680E.2F9187D3@att.net> <2e1L3.4285$eM4.345931@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <37FCB0FF.10C87F32@NOSPAMhome.com> <7tjg5t$j43$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

:    (etc.)You echo my sentiments exactly.  I tried using them for a couple
: programs, and found one period paragraphs put several restrictions that I
: had not thought of on me ... I did not like the result. It is funny, I also
: found the use of the "end" constructs more of a hinderence to clear reading
: than a help.  I am finding that I can read my code easily, as can all my
: employees.  However, coming from modern educational facilities, they
: slavishly use end-constructs and tabs to highlight blocks of code.  I find
: it hard to follow.  Try this:  Grab a big chunk of code, and reformat it
: just like this paragraph. See if it is readable.  I will bet my code is, and
: theirs is totally illegible.

Readability and illegibility are entirely what one is used to.
Make it a paragraph and _you_ will find your code readable and
their code illegible.  They may find exactly the opposite.

In exactly the same way, APL programmers read their code like
a poem while many (most) think it is hen scratchings.

They are not using END- constructs because they are slavishly
following what they were indoctrinated with, they are doing
so (IMHO) because they find it more readable, and hopefully
less bugged.

I put all reserved words in upper case and capitalise user words.
If I change a piece of code to all upper or all lower I find
it far less meaningful.  I would guess that some who are used to
colour coded editing would see just a mass of meaningless words
if they had to work on a monochrome terminal.

It is all what one is used to and how the brain extracts the meaning.
END- or '.' or upper case or colour are not inherently more
meaningful or readable, it is all just a function of the
reader.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991009161942.06135.00000136@ng-fl1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7tmase$7u0$3@news.igs.net>`

```
>I agree entirely with most of what you say. What we are used to is always
>the easiest to read.  Nevertheless, we have evolved a human language that we
>can all read THE SAME.

I think your response, in and of itself, demonstrates that we HAVEN'T evolved a
language we all read the same.  There are myriad personal styles in use.  For
example, I would not have said, "I agree entirely with most of what you say." 
It doesn't have to be, but resembles, an oxymoron.  I would have said simply,
"I agree with most of what you say" or "I partially agree with you."

Our spoken language permits great variety.  If programming languages didn't
also support great variety, they wouldn't be able to do very many things.

RoadRaat
```

PBS :-

	"There y'are son, that's 'n 'edge 'og"
	
OR this little rhyme :-

	Harry went to Hampstead

#### ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0cl0l77hu261@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net>`

```
You need to know what PC COBOL package you are using. They are different 
form one another in syntax, how to compile and run.

Here are advice on programming only.

1- The SELECT statement is one sentence. It should have only one period at 
the end. The period cannot be anywhere in the middle:
SELECT EMPLOYEE-DATA
ASSIGN TO "A:EMP.DAT"  (There is no period here)
ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL. (There should be a period here)

2-  I am not sure whether you may assign the output the printer.  Please, 
check your PC COMPILER Manual. The best way is to assign to an output file:
SELECT PAYROLL-LISTING
ASSIGN TO �A:\REPORT.TXT� (There is no period here)
ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL. (There should be a period here)

3- In the FILE SECTION you need not to declare variables here. The best 
suitable place to declare variables is in WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. The 
variables here have no value in processing.  You may get empty results. If 
you declare here, you should declare again in the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. 
Why bother? Just enough here:
FD  EMPLOYEE-DATA.
01  EMPLOYEE-RECORD.
FD  PAYROLL-LISTING.
10  LISTING-RECORD.

4- In WORKING-STORAGE SECTION, you need to declare all varibales, 
including working variable, which means an immediate variable in editable 
format, before your move it to output in un-edit-able format. 
01  EMPLOYEE-REC.
    05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN PIC X(20).
    05  HOURS-WORKED-IN  PIC 9(2).
05  HOURLY-RATE-IN   PIC 9V99.
01  PRINT-REC.
    05                   PIC X(20).
    05  NAME-OUT         PIC X(20).
    05                   PIC X(10).
    05  HOURS-OUT        PIC 9(2).
    05                   PIC X(8).
    05  RATE-OUT         PIC 9.99.
    05                   PIC X(6).
    05  WEEKLY-WAGES-OUT PIC 999.99.
Pay attention to 2 spaces between level number and variable name. In some 
compiler, one space may cause compiling errors.
77  WORKING-WAGES        PIC 999V99.
77  HOURS-WORKED         PIC     99.
77  HOURLY-RATE          PIC   9V99.
This is the intermediate varibale used in the mathematic calculation. They 
are independent, that can be in 77 level.

5- In the PROCEDURE DIVISION you may apply structured programming, that is 
useful in Objective Oriented design:
PROCEDURE DIVISION. (Main routine is in C programming. No need here.)
   PERFORM INITIALIZATION-ROUTINE.
   PERFORM PROCESSING-ROUTINE UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = �NO �.
   PERFORM CLOSING-ROUTINE.
   STOP RUN.

Try to avoid PERFORM .. UNTIL . . END-PERFORM approach, not OO design.
6- In processing routine, every statement should have a period at the 
end.  For example: MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-REC. (period here)
7- You should move all edit-able-format variables to non-edit-able-format 
variables prior and after the calculation. In Mathematic calculatioin, all 
variables in the equation should be in edit-able format:

MULTIPLY HOURS-WORKED-IN BY HOURLY-RATE-IN GIVING ...
(non-edited format variables cannot be used in mathematic calculation).
MOVE  WORKING-WAGES-IN  TO WORKING-WAGES.
MOVE  HOURLY-RATE-IN    TO HOURLY-RATE.
COMPUTE  WORKING-WAGES  =  HOURS-WORKED * HOURLY-RATE.
MOVE  WORKING-WAGES     TO WEEKLY-WAGES-OUT.

I hope this will help you in your first few steps.
Everything is easy after that. I still remember my first days with COBOL.

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38068A60.79EFC60@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net> <s0cl0l77hu261@corp.supernews.com>`

```


AnhMy Tran wrote:
> 
> Try to avoid PERFORM .. UNTIL . . END-PERFORM approach, not OO design.

If you are saying in the above 'performs contradict OO design concepts',
then I have to disagree :-

	*>-----------------------------------------------------
	Method-id. "make-collections".
	*>-----------------------------------------------------
	Local-storage section.
	01 n				pic x(4) comp-5.
	Procedure Division.
	  perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxCollections
	    evaluate n
	     when 1 invoke CustomerFile "make-collection"
		  returning os-Collection(n) *>or os-CustomerCollection
	     when 2 invoke DescripsFile "make-collection"
		  returning os-Collection(n) *>or os-DescripsCollection
	     when 3...........
	    End-evaluate
	  End-perform
	..............

And I use same concept for building a PopUp Menu from a hard-coded
table.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380b523b$2$214@nntp1.ba.best.com>`

```
Mr. Jimmy, Calgary AB is correct.
I am sorry that the PERFORM VARYING and EVALUATION WHEN is special feature
COBOL offers which are very powerful and convenient.
What I meant is that the PERFROM THROUGH a complex block of commmands is
diffecult for other programmer to read and maintain. Anyway, programmers
retain their styles of programming, regardless whether other programmers
like it or not. The point is that any program should be easy for its 
owners or creators to read and maintain even after a long time, long 
enough for it to be erased from its creator's mind.
```

<a id="t-fa3436d825"></a>
## Thread: was 60 day date check — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: was 60 day date check

- **From:** bberman@netbox.com (B Berman)
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fbd84f.220024265@news.ntplx.net>`

```
someone had asked for a routine to convert dates from Gregorian
YYYYMMDD format to Scalar Day, and back, and a method was posted
involving the use of, I believe, Function Integer-of-Date.  I had
asked if anyone had a method using 'older' cobol, and one poster had
responded with a sample, which I could not get working, and felt that
his base assumption was incorrect.  His base date date was 1901/01/01,
and he called this scalar day 0.  All other scalar functions I had
seen, including Microsoft's, use 1900/01/01 as scalar day 1.  

So I wrote one (had a couple of very slow days at work) in Fujitsu v4,
but entirely with Cobol74 constructs.  I'm sure it could be optimized.

Here is the subroutine:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. DATECVTS.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  CD-CALCULATION-FIELDS.
     05  CD-Y-CALC               PIC 9(4)      COMP.
     05  CD-Y-QUO                PIC 9(4)      COMP.
     05  CD-Y-REM                PIC 9(4)      COMP.
     05  CD-Y-CALC-2             PIC 9(4)      COMP.
     05  CD-Y-QUO-2              PIC 9(4)      COMP.
     05  CD-Y-REM-2              PIC 9(4)      COMP.

 01  CD-DAYS-STRING              PIC X(36)
                 VALUE '000031059090120151181212243273304334'.
 01  CD-DAYS-TABLE               REDEFINES  CD-DAYS-STRING.
     05  CD-NUM-DAYS             PIC 999
                                 OCCURS  12  TIMES
                                 INDEXED BY CD-DAYS-INDEX.
                                 
 01  CD-DAYS-LEAP-STRING         PIC X(36)
                 VALUE '000031060091121152182213244274305335'.
 01  CD-DAYS-LEAP-TABLE          REDEFINES CD-DAYS-LEAP-STRING.
     05  CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS        PIC 999
                                 OCCURS  12  TIMES
                                 INDEXED BY CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX.

 LINKAGE SECTION.
*  TO INVOKE TO CONVERT DATE TO SCALAR,
*      MOVE SPACES OR ZEROS TO CD-SCALAR-NUM-A
*      MOVE DATE (YYYYMMDD) TO CD-DATE-8.
*  TO INVOKE TO CONVERT SCALAR TO DATE,
*      MOVE SPACES OR ZEROS TO CD-DATE-8
*      MOVE SCALAR NUMBER (9999999) TO CD-SCALAR-NUM.
 01  CD-SCALAR-NUM               PIC 9(7).
 01  CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  REDEFINES  CD-SCALAR-NUM
                                 PIC X(7).

 01  CD-DATE-8.
     05  CD-YEAR                 PIC 9(4).
     05  CD-MONTH                PIC 99.
     05  CD-DAY                  PIC 99.
 
 PROCEDURE DIVISION  USING  CD-SCALAR-NUM  CD-DATE-8.
 
 PROGRAM-BEGIN. 
     IF (CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  =  SPACES  AND  CD-DATE-8  =  SPACES)
       OR
        (CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  =  ZEROS   AND  CD-DATE-8  =  ZEROS)
*  NO FUNCTION REQUESTED, SO RETURN        
         MOVE ZEROS TO CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  CD-DATE-8
         GOBACK.
     IF CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  =  SPACES  OR  CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  =  ZEROS
         PERFORM DATE-TO-SCALAR THRU DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT
         GOBACK.
     IF CD-DATE-8  =  SPACES  OR  CD-DATE-8  =  ZEROS
         PERFORM SCALAR-TO-DATE THRU SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT
         GOBACK.
 
*--------------------------------------------------------------

 DATE-TO-SCALAR.
*  BASIC DATE EDITS
     IF CD-DATE-8 NOT NUMERIC
         MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
         GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     IF CD-YEAR  <  1900  or  cd-year  >  2099
         MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
         GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     IF CD-MONTH  <  1  OR  CD-MONTH  >  12
         MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
         GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     IF CD-DAY  <  1
         MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
         GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     IF CD-MONTH  =  1 OR 3 OR 5 OR 7 OR 8 OR 10 OR 12
         IF CD-DAY  >  31
             MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT
         ELSE
             NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE IF CD-MONTH  =  4 OR 6 OR 9 OR 11
         IF CD-DAY  >  30
             MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT
         ELSE
             NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         IF CD-DAY  >  29
             MOVE  0  TO CD-SCALAR-NUM
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
              
     MOVE 0 TO  CD-SCALAR-NUM  CD-Y-CALC  CD-Y-QUO  CD-Y-REM.
     SUBTRACT 1900 FROM CD-YEAR  GIVING CD-Y-CALC.
     DIVIDE 4 INTO CD-Y-CALC     GIVING CD-Y-QUO
                                 REMAINDER CD-Y-REM.
*  IF NOT DIVISIBLE BY 4, NOT A LEAP YEAR - COMPUTE & GET OUT    
     IF CD-Y-REM > 0
         IF CD-MONTH = 2  AND  CD-DAY > 28
             MOVE  0  TO  CD-SCALAR-NUM
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT
         ELSE
             COMPUTE CD-SCALAR-NUM = (CD-Y-CALC * 365) +
                                 CD-Y-QUO + CD-DAY +
                                 CD-NUM-DAYS (CD-MONTH)
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     
     MOVE  0  TO CD-Y-QUO-2  CD-Y-REM-2.     
     DIVIDE 100 INTO CD-YEAR     GIVING CD-Y-QUO-2
                                 REMAINDER CD-Y-REM-2.
*  DIVISIBLE BY 4, BUT NOT BY 100 - IT IS A LEAP YEAR       
     IF CD-Y-REM-2 > 0
         COMPUTE CD-SCALAR-NUM = (CD-Y-CALC * 365) +
                                 CD-Y-QUO + CD-DAY +
                                 CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS (CD-MONTH) - 1
         GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     
     MOVE  0  TO CD-Y-QUO-2  CD-Y-REM-2.    
     DIVIDE 400 INTO CD-YEAR     GIVING CD-Y-QUO-2
                                 REMAINDER CD-Y-REM-2.
*  DIVISIBLE BY 4, AND BY 100, BUT NOT 400 - IT IS NOT A LEAP-YEAR 
     IF CD-Y-REM-2 > 0
         IF CD-MONTH = 2  AND  CD-DAY > 28
             MOVE  0  TO  CD-SCALAR-NUM
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT
         ELSE
             COMPUTE CD-SCALAR-NUM = (CD-Y-CALC * 365) +
                                 CD-Y-QUO + CD-DAY +
                                 CD-NUM-DAYS (CD-MONTH)
             GO TO DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
*  DIVISIBLE BY ALL - IT IS A LEAP YEAR        
     COMPUTE CD-SCALAR-NUM = (CD-Y-CALC * 365) +
                             CD-Y-QUO + CD-DAY +
                             CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS (CD-MONTH) - 1.

 DATE-TO-SCALAR-EXIT.
     EXIT.

*-------------------------------------------------------------- 

 SCALAR-TO-DATE.
     IF CD-SCALAR-NUM-A NOT NUMERIC
         MOVE ALL  '0'  TO  CD-DATE-8
         GO TO SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT.
*  SUBROUTINE CAN CONVERT YYYYMMDD RANGE 19000101 TO 20991231
     IF CD-SCALAR-NUM  <  1  OR  CD-SCALAR-NUM  >  73049
         MOVE ALL  '0'  TO  CD-DATE-8
         GO TO SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT.
         
     MOVE 0 TO  CD-DATE-8  CD-Y-CALC  CD-Y-QUO  CD-Y-REM.
*  THERE ARE 1461 DAYS IN EACH 4 YEAR PERIOD, THIS GIVES US THE
*    LAST LEAP YEAR AS A BASE YEAR     
     DIVIDE  1461  INTO CD-SCALAR-NUM 
                                 GIVING CD-Y-QUO
                                 REMAINDER CD-Y-REM.
     MULTIPLY  CD-Y-QUO  BY  4   GIVING CD-Y-CALC.
*  NOW FIND HOW MANY YEARS PAST THE BASE YEAR     
     MOVE  0  TO  CD-Y-QUO-2  CD-Y-REM-2.
     DIVIDE  365  INTO  CD-Y-REM GIVING CD-Y-QUO-2
                                 REMAINDER CD-Y-REM-2.
     IF CD-Y-REM-2  =  0
         IF CD-Y-QUO-2  NOT  =  0
             SUBTRACT  1  FROM  CD-Y-QUO-2
             ADD  365  TO  CD-Y-REM-2.
                                         
     ADD  CD-Y-QUO-2  TO  CD-Y-CALC.
     ADD  1900  CD-Y-CALC        GIVING CD-YEAR.
*  NOW WE HAVE THE YEAR, SO FIND THE MONTH & DAY     
     MOVE  0  TO  CD-Y-QUO  CD-Y-REM.
     DIVIDE  4  INTO  CD-YEAR    GIVING  CD-Y-QUO
                                 REMAINDER  CD-Y-REM.
*  IF YEAR NOT DIVISIBLE BY 4 - NOT A LEAP YEAR  
     IF CD-Y-REM  >  0
         PERFORM SEARCH-REG-YEAR THRU SEARCH-REG-YEAR-EXIT
         GO TO  SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT.
         
     MOVE  0  TO  CD-Y-QUO  CD-Y-REM.
     DIVIDE  100 INTO  CD-YEAR   GIVING  CD-Y-QUO
                                 REMAINDER  CD-Y-REM.
*  IF DIVISIBLE BY 4, BUT NOT BY 100 - IT'S A LEAP YEAR
     IF CD-Y-REM  >  0
         PERFORM SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR THRU SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR-EXIT
         GO TO SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT.                             

     MOVE  0  TO CD-Y-QUO  CD-Y-REM.
     DIVIDE  400 INTO  CD-YEAR   GIVING  CD-Y-QUO
                                 REMAINDER  CD-Y-REM.
*  IF DIVISIBLE BY 4 AND 100, BUT NOT 400 - NOT A LEAP YEAR
     IF CD-Y-REM  >  0
         PERFORM SEARCH-REG-YEAR THRU SEARCH-REG-YEAR-EXIT
     ELSE
         PERFORM SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR THRU SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR-EXIT.
     
 SCALAR-TO-DATE-EXIT.
     EXIT.


 SEARCH-REG-YEAR.
     SET CD-DAYS-INDEX  TO  1
     SEARCH CD-NUM-DAYS VARYING CD-DAYS-INDEX
             AT END
                 SET CD-DAYS-INDEX TO 12
         WHEN CD-NUM-DAYS (CD-DAYS-INDEX)  >  CD-Y-REM-2
                 SET CD-DAYS-INDEX  DOWN BY  1.
                 
     IF CD-NUM-DAYS (CD-DAYS-INDEX) = CD-Y-REM-2
         SET CD-DAYS-INDEX  DOWN BY  1.
                     
     SET CD-MONTH TO CD-DAYS-INDEX.

     SUBTRACT CD-NUM-DAYS (CD-DAYS-INDEX) FROM CD-Y-REM-2.
     MOVE CD-Y-REM-2 TO CD-DAY.

 SEARCH-REG-YEAR-EXIT.
     EXIT.
     

 SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR.
     SET CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX  TO  1
     SEARCH CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS VARYING CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX
             AT END
                 SET CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX TO 12
         WHEN CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS (CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX)  >  CD-Y-REM-2
                 SET CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX  DOWN BY  1.
                 
     SET CD-MONTH TO CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX.
     
     SUBTRACT CD-NUM-LEAP-DAYS (CD-DAYS-LEAP-INDEX) FROM CD-Y-REM-2.
     ADD 1 TO CD-Y-REM-2.
     MOVE CD-Y-REM-2 TO CD-DAY.

 SEARCH-LEAP-YEAR-EXIT.
     EXIT.
     

and her is a simple calling program illustrating it's use:

 identification division.
 program-id. Datecvt.
 environment division.
 configuration section.
 input-output section.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  Input-Date-A                  pic x(8).
 01  Input-Date-N  redefines  Input-Date-A
                                   PIC 9(8).

 01  CD-SCALAR-NUM               PIC 9(7).
 01  CD-SCALAR-NUM-A  REDEFINES  CD-SCALAR-NUM
                                 PIC X(7).

 01  CD-DATE-8.
     05  CD-YEAR                 PIC 9(4).
     05  CD-MONTH                PIC 99.
     05  CD-DAY                  PIC 99.

 procedure division.
 begin.
     display 'enter date as YYYYMMDD' upon console.
     accept Input-Date-A from console.
     
     if Input-Date-A = '99999999'
         go to pgm-end.
         
     move Input-Date-A  to  CD-DATE-8.
     MOVE SPACES  TO  CD-SCALAR-NUM-A.
     CALL 'DATECVTS'  USING  CD-SCALAR-NUM  CD-DATE-8.
 
     display cd-scalar-num upon console.
     if cd-scalar-num  =  0
         display 'INVALID DATE YYYYMMDD ENTERED...TRY AGAIN...'
         go to begin.
     
*    MOVE ....(input-field)  TO  CD-SCALAR-NUM-A.
     MOVE SPACES  TO  CD-DATE-8.    
     CALL 'DATECVTS'  USING  CD-SCALAR-NUM  CD-DATE-8.
     
     display cd-date-8 upon console.
     if cd-date-8  =  zeros
         display 'INVALID SCALAR DAY ENTERED...CANNOT CONVERT...'.
         
     go to begin.
 pgm-end.
     
     goback.
     


----------------------------------------------------------------------
                  Bob Berman   -    West Hartford, CT                 
 mailto:bberman@netbox.com    website: http://www.ntplx.net/~bberman  
                                                                      
                  THE TRUE TERRORISTS IN AMERICA ARE                  
          THE PEOPLE WHO DEMAND TO SEE THE STORE MANAGER !            
----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

#### ↳ Re: was 60 day date check

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fbf31d_3@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<37fbd84f.220024265@news.ntplx.net>`

```

B Berman <bberman@netbox.com> wrote in message
news:37fbd84f.220024265@news.ntplx.net...
> someone had asked for a routine to convert dates from Gregorian
> YYYYMMDD format to Scalar Day, and back, and a method was posted
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Here is the subroutine:

snippage.

An alternative way of doing it (and maybe a bit more polished) follows:

000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.    CALRPK.
000300
000400 AUTHOR.        PIETER HINTJENS.
000500 DATE-WRITTEN.  89/02/16
000600     -REVISED:  93/09/09.
000700
000800 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000900
001000 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
001100 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001200 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
001300
001400 DATA DIVISION.
001500
001600 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001700
001800 01  VARIOUS-VARIABLES.
001900     02  NBR-DAYS                PIC S9(7)  COMP.
002000     02  NBR-YEARS               PIC S9(7)  COMP.
002100     02  NBR-LEAPS               PIC S9(7)  COMP.
002200     02  DOY-DAYS                PIC S9(7)  COMP.
002300     02  YEAR-DIV                PIC S9(5)  COMP.
002400     02  WEEK-DIV                PIC S9(5)  COMP.
002500     02  NBR-MONTHS              PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002600     02  LEAP-YEAR               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002700     02  YEAR-REM                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002800     02  WEEK-REM                PIC S9(3)  COMP.
002900
003000 01  DAYS-AT-MONTHS.
003100     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE ZERO.
003200     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
003300     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 59.
003400     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 90.
003500     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 120.
003600     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 151.
003700     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 181.
003800     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 212.
003900     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 243.
004000     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 273.
004100     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 304.
004200     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 334.
004300 01  FILLER                      REDEFINES  DAYS-AT-MONTHS.
004400     02  DAYS-AT-MONTH           PIC S9(3)  COMP
004500                                            OCCURS 12 TIMES.
004600 01  DAYS-IN-MONTHS.
004700     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
004800     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 28.
004900     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005000     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 30.
005100     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005200     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 30.
005300     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005400     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005500     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 30.
005600     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005700     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 30.
005800     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 31.
005900 01  FILLER                      REDEFINES  DAYS-IN-MONTHS.
006000     02  DAYS-IN-MONTH           PIC S9(3)  COMP
006100                                            OCCURS 12 TIMES.
006200 01  WEEK-ADJUSTS.
006300     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 6.
006400     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 0.
006500     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 1.
006600     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 2.
006700     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 3.
006800     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 4.
006900     02  FILLER                  PIC S9(3)  COMP VALUE 5.
007000 01  FILLER                      REDEFINES  WEEK-ADJUSTS.
007100     02  WEEK-ADJUST             PIC S9(3)  COMP
007200                                            OCCURS 7 TIMES.
007300 LINKAGE SECTION.
007400
007500 01  CALEND-CONTROL.
007600     02  SESSION-TASK-ID         PIC X(4).
007700     02  CALEND-OPERATION.
007800         03  CALEND-OP-TYPE      PIC X.
007900             88  OP-GREGORIAN-DATE          VALUE IS "G".
008000             88  OP-JULIAN-FORMAT           VALUE IS "J".
008100             88  OP-LILLIAN-DATE            VALUE IS "L".
008200         03  CALEND-OP-CODE      PIC X.
008300     02  CALEND-FEEDBACK         PIC X.
008400         88  DATE-IS-VALID                  VALUE SPACE.
008500         88  DATE-IS-INVALID                VALUE "E".
008600     02  CALEND-GRE-DATE.
008700         03  GRE-YYYY            PIC 9(4).
008800         03  GRE-MM              PIC 9(2).
008900         03  GRE-DD              PIC 9(2).
009000     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  CALEND-GRE-DATE.
009100         03  GRE-YYYYMMDD        PIC 9(8).
009200     02  CALEND-JUL-FORM.
009300         03  JUL-YYYY            PIC 9(4).
009400         03  JUL-DDD             PIC 9(3).
009500     02  FILLER                  REDEFINES  CALEND-JUL-FORM.
009600         03  JUL-YYYYDDD         PIC 9(7).
009700     02  CALEND-LIL-DATE.
009800         03  LIL-DDDDDDD         PIC 9(7).
009900
010000     02  CALEND-DAY-OF-WEEK      PIC 9.
010100     02  CALEND-WEEK-OF-YEAR     PIC 9(2).
010200
010300 PROCEDURE DIVISION
010400     USING CALEND-CONTROL.
010500 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
010600     MOVE SPACE TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
010700
010800     IF OP-GREGORIAN-DATE
010900         PERFORM GREGORIAN-DATE-OPERATIONS
011000     ELSE
011100     IF OP-JULIAN-FORMAT
011200         PERFORM JULIAN-FORMAT-OPERATIONS
011300     ELSE
011400     IF OP-LILLIAN-DATE
011500         PERFORM LILLIAN-DATE-OPERATIONS
011600     ELSE
011700         MOVE "O" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
011800     .
011900 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
012000     EXIT PROGRAM
012100     .
012200
012300******************************************************************
012400
012500 GREGORIAN-DATE-OPERATIONS.
012600     PERFORM GRE-VALIDATE-DATE
012700     IF DATE-IS-VALID
012800         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "D"
012900             PERFORM GRE-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
013000         ELSE
013100         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "W"
013200             PERFORM GRE-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
013300         ELSE
013400         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "J"
013500             PERFORM GRE-CONVERT-TO-JUL
013600         ELSE
013700         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "L"
013800             PERFORM GRE-CONVERT-TO-LIL
013900         ELSE
014000         IF CALEND-OP-CODE NOT = "V"
014100             MOVE "O" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
014200     .
014300
014400 GRE-VALIDATE-DATE.
014500     IF GRE-YYYYMMDD < 16000101
014600     OR GRE-MM = ZERO OR > 12
014700         MOVE "ERROR" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
014800     ELSE
014900         PERFORM VALIDATE-GRE-DAYS-IN-MONTH
015000     .
015100
015200 VALIDATE-GRE-DAYS-IN-MONTH.
015300     MOVE GRE-YYYY                   TO NBR-YEARS
015400     MOVE GRE-MM                     TO NBR-MONTHS
015500     MOVE DAYS-IN-MONTH (NBR-MONTHS) TO NBR-DAYS
015600     PERFORM CHECK-IF-LEAP-YEAR
015700     IF NBR-MONTHS = 2
015800         ADD LEAP-YEAR TO NBR-DAYS
015900     .
016000     IF GRE-DD = ZERO OR > NBR-DAYS
016100         MOVE "ERROR" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
016200     .
016300
016400 CHECK-IF-LEAP-YEAR.
016500     MOVE ZERO TO LEAP-YEAR
016600     DIVIDE 4 INTO NBR-YEARS
016700         GIVING    YEAR-DIV
016800         REMAINDER YEAR-REM
016900     IF YEAR-REM = ZERO
017000         MOVE 1 TO LEAP-YEAR
017100         DIVIDE 100 INTO NBR-YEARS
017200             GIVING      YEAR-DIV
017300             REMAINDER   YEAR-REM
017400         IF YEAR-REM = ZERO
017500             DIVIDE 400 INTO NBR-YEARS
017600                 GIVING      YEAR-DIV
017700                 REMAINDER   YEAR-REM
017800             IF YEAR-REM > ZERO
017900                 MOVE ZERO TO LEAP-YEAR
018000     .
018100
018200 GRE-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK.
018300     PERFORM GRE-CONVERT-TO-LIL
018400     PERFORM RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
018500     .
018600
018700 RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK.
018800     PERFORM GET-DAY-OF-WEEK-REM
018900     MOVE WEEK-REM TO CALEND-DAY-OF-WEEK
019000     .
019100
019200 GET-DAY-OF-WEEK-REM.
019300     ADD 3 TO NBR-DAYS
019400     DIVIDE 7 INTO NBR-DAYS
019500         GIVING    WEEK-DIV
019600         REMAINDER WEEK-REM
019700
019800     ADD 1 TO WEEK-REM
019900     .
020000
020100 GRE-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR.
020200     PERFORM GRE-CONVERT-TO-LIL
020300     PERFORM RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
020400     .
020500
020600 RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR.
020700     MOVE JUL-DDD TO DOY-DAYS
020800     MOVE    1    TO JUL-DDD
020900     PERFORM CONVERT-JUL-TO-NBR-DAYS
021000     PERFORM GET-DAY-OF-WEEK-REM
021100     COMPUTE WEEK-DIV = (DOY-DAYS + WEEK-ADJUST (WEEK-REM)) / 7
021200     MOVE DOY-DAYS TO JUL-DDD
021300     MOVE WEEK-DIV TO CALEND-WEEK-OF-YEAR
021400     .
021500
021600 CONVERT-JUL-TO-NBR-DAYS.
021700     COMPUTE NBR-DAYS  = (JUL-YYYY - 1501) * 365.25
021800     COMPUTE NBR-DAYS  = NBR-DAYS - 29872 + JUL-DDD
021900     COMPUTE NBR-LEAPS = (JUL-YYYY - 1501) / 100
022000     SUBTRACT NBR-LEAPS FROM NBR-DAYS
022100     COMPUTE NBR-LEAPS = (JUL-YYYY - 1201) / 400
022200     ADD NBR-LEAPS TO NBR-DAYS
022300     .
022400
022500 GRE-CONVERT-TO-JUL.
022600     COMPUTE NBR-DAYS = DAYS-AT-MONTH (NBR-MONTHS) + GRE-DD
022700     IF NBR-MONTHS > 2
022800         ADD LEAP-YEAR TO NBR-DAYS
022900     .
023000     MOVE NBR-YEARS TO JUL-YYYY
023100     MOVE NBR-DAYS  TO JUL-DDD
023200     .
023300
023400 GRE-CONVERT-TO-LIL.
023500     PERFORM GRE-CONVERT-TO-JUL
023600     PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-LIL
023700     .
023800
023900******************************************************************
024000
024100 JULIAN-FORMAT-OPERATIONS.
024200     PERFORM JUL-VALIDATE-DATE
024300     IF DATE-IS-VALID
024400         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "D"
024500             PERFORM JUL-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
024600         ELSE
024700         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "W"
024800             PERFORM JUL-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
024900         ELSE
025000         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "G"
025100             PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-GRE
025200         ELSE
025300         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "L"
025400             PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-LIL
025500         ELSE
025600         IF CALEND-OP-CODE NOT = "V"
025700             MOVE "O" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
025800     .
025900
026000 JUL-VALIDATE-DATE.
026100     MOVE JUL-YYYY TO NBR-YEARS
026200     PERFORM CHECK-IF-LEAP-YEAR
026300     COMPUTE NBR-DAYS = 365 + LEAP-YEAR
026400     IF JUL-YYYYDDD < 1600001
026500     OR JUL-DDD = ZERO OR > NBR-DAYS
026600         MOVE "ERROR" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
026700     .
026800
026900 JUL-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK.
027000     PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-LIL
027100     PERFORM RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
027200     .
027300
027400 JUL-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR.
027500     PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-LIL
027600     PERFORM RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
027700     .
027800
027900 JUL-CONVERT-TO-GRE.
028000     MOVE JUL-DDD TO NBR-DAYS
028100     IF NBR-DAYS > 60
028200         SUBTRACT LEAP-YEAR FROM NBR-DAYS
028300         PERFORM FIND-MONTH-FOR-NBR-DAYS
028400     ELSE
028500         PERFORM FIND-MONTH-FOR-NBR-DAYS
028600         IF NBR-DAYS = 60
028700             SUBTRACT LEAP-YEAR FROM NBR-MONTHS
028800     .
028900     SUBTRACT DAYS-AT-MONTH (NBR-MONTHS) FROM NBR-DAYS
029000     MOVE JUL-YYYY   TO GRE-YYYY
029100     MOVE NBR-MONTHS TO GRE-MM
029200     MOVE NBR-DAYS   TO GRE-DD
029300     .
029400
029500 FIND-MONTH-FOR-NBR-DAYS.
029600     PERFORM FIND-MONTH-IN-YEAR
029700       VARYING NBR-MONTHS FROM 12 BY -1
029800         UNTIL DAYS-AT-MONTH (NBR-MONTHS) < NBR-DAYS
029900     .
030000
030100 FIND-MONTH-IN-YEAR.
030200     EXIT
030300     .
030400
030500 JUL-CONVERT-TO-LIL.
030600     PERFORM CONVERT-JUL-TO-NBR-DAYS
030700     MOVE NBR-DAYS TO LIL-DDDDDDD
030800     .
030900
031000******************************************************************
031100
031200 LILLIAN-DATE-OPERATIONS.
031300     PERFORM LIL-VALIDATE-DATE
031400     IF DATE-IS-VALID
031500         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "D"
031600             PERFORM LIL-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
031700         ELSE
031800         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "W"
031900             PERFORM LIL-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
032000         ELSE
032100         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "G"
032200             PERFORM LIL-CONVERT-TO-GRE
032300         ELSE
032400         IF CALEND-OP-CODE = "J"
032500             PERFORM LIL-CONVERT-TO-JUL
032600         ELSE
032700         IF CALEND-OP-CODE NOT = "V"
032800             MOVE "O" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
032900     .
033000
033100 LIL-VALIDATE-DATE.
033200     IF LIL-DDDDDDD < 6288 OR > 3074324
033300         MOVE "ERROR" TO CALEND-FEEDBACK
033400     .
033500
033600 LIL-RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK.
033700     MOVE LIL-DDDDDDD TO NBR-DAYS
033800     PERFORM RETURN-DAY-OF-WEEK
033900     .
034000
034100 LIL-RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR.
034200     PERFORM LIL-CONVERT-TO-JUL
034300     PERFORM RETURN-WEEK-OF-YEAR
034400     .
034500
034600 LIL-CONVERT-TO-GRE.
034700     PERFORM LIL-CONVERT-TO-JUL
034800     PERFORM JUL-CONVERT-TO-GRE
034900     .
035000
035100 LIL-CONVERT-TO-JUL.
035200     PERFORM CONVERT-LIL-TO-NBR-YEARS-DAYS
035300     PERFORM CHECK-IF-LEAP-YEAR
035400     MOVE NBR-YEARS TO JUL-YYYY
035500     MOVE NBR-DAYS  TO JUL-DDD
035600     .
035700
035800 CONVERT-LIL-TO-NBR-YEARS-DAYS.
035900     COMPUTE NBR-DAYS  = LIL-DDDDDDD + 139444
036000     COMPUTE NBR-LEAPS = NBR-DAYS / 36524.25
036100     ADD     NBR-LEAPS TO NBR-DAYS
036200     COMPUTE NBR-LEAPS = NBR-LEAPS / 4
036300     SUBTRACT NBR-LEAPS FROM NBR-DAYS
036400
036500     COMPUTE NBR-YEARS = NBR-DAYS / 365.25
036600     IF NBR-YEARS * 365.25 = NBR-DAYS
036700         SUBTRACT 1 FROM NBR-YEARS
036800     .
036900     COMPUTE DOY-DAYS = NBR-YEARS * 365.25
037000     SUBTRACT DOY-DAYS FROM NBR-DAYS
037100     ADD      1201     TO   NBR-YEARS
037200     .
```
