## Thread: any known problems with upgrading COBOL 4.1.10 to Server Express? — 6 msgs

### any known problems with upgrading COBOL 4.1.10 to Server Express?

- **From:** Jessica Alford <alfordjNOalSPAM@sx.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<004aa0e3.e52153ec@usw-ex0102-012.remarq.com>`

```
I'm doing a risk assessment on this upgrade: AIX 4.2.1 to 4.3.2. Has
anyone been through this version range and encountered problems? Of
what type?
To AIX 4.3.2 (currently 4.2.1)
To Cobol Server Express 1.1 (currently 4.1.10)
Apparently, For AIX 4.3 onwards there are no versions of MF Cobol
produced, but rather the product is now called Server Express.  We're
working on an assumption that Most applications only require
recompilation to take advantage of the advanced performance and
capabilities of Server Express.

My upgrade project will not occur until early 2000, but I promise I
will update the discussion group with any problems we encounter.


* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: any known problems with upgrading COBOL 4.1.10 to Server Express?

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#0knhvSE$GA.181@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<004aa0e3.e52153ec@usw-ex0102-012.remarq.com>`

```
Jessica,
      We are using the ServerExpress and haven't run into too many problems.
The biggest problem we had is that we have an app that runs on 4.1.10 COBOL
and another app that runs on ServerExpress, but both apps use the same data
files.  We had a problem with record locking between the two versions, but
MicroFocus got us a fix.  We've had some other problems (I can't think of
any off the top of my head so they must not have been all that bad), but my
recollection is that most of them stemmed from problems with OO COBOL and
not with our old software.  Best of luck!

Bill



Jessica Alford wrote in message
<004aa0e3.e52153ec@usw-ex0102-012.remarq.com>...
>I'm doing a risk assessment on this upgrade: AIX 4.2.1 to 4.3.2. Has
>anyone been through this version range and encountered problems? Of
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network
*
>The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: any known problems with upgrading COBOL 4.1.10 to Server Express?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FD5681.A60F1772@home.com>`
- **References:** `<004aa0e3.e52153ec@usw-ex0102-012.remarq.com> <#0knhvSE$GA.181@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```


Bill Wood wrote:
> 
> recollection is that most of them stemmed from problems with OO COBOL and
> not with our old software.  Best of luck!

Elucidate, elucidate ! No I'm kidding. But what problems do you
attribute to OO ? Was it truly OO, or blush, perhaps not a full
comprehension or the approach to using OO ? I'm intrigued.

Do you mean there are now three of us, (You, Simon Hart and Moi) using
OO COBOL <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: any known problems with upgrading COBOL 4.1.10 to Server Express?

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uxNEXhZE$GA.137@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<004aa0e3.e52153ec@usw-ex0102-012.remarq.com> <#0knhvSE$GA.181@cpmsnbbsa05> <37FD5681.A60F1772@home.com>`

```
No, they were OO related and more precisely a combination of OO and I-O.  We
developed I-O modules for all of our files and then we found out that the
data in these modules is contained at the class so no matter how many
instances of the object we had, it all pointed back to the same instance of
the data.  The result was we could only open a file once, if we read data
sequentially and then right in the middle of this we did a random read we
lost currency, we still can't inherit the data (we have a file with 96
record types and we have a separate I-O module for each record type.  We put
all of the common I-O routines in one program and had all of the other
programs inherit this one, but MicroFocus doesn't support that at this
time).  The problem I mentioned with the two apps running on separate COBOL
versions is they don't recognize each others record locks so two users could
lock the same record -- not usually a good feature, but we patched the
4.1.10 COBOL and added a runtime tunable and all works fine now.  I guess
our main problem is that we've been using MF COBOL for many years (we still
have machines running 1.3 COBOL and others running 3.2) and that worked
pretty well.  Now we all feel like we are running on the bleeding edge and
when a bug occurs that is difficult to reproduce or happens intermittently
we don't know whether the bug is in our code or in the COBOL.  I'll be the
first to admit that I am no expert in OO COBOL and have often gone down the
wrong path trying to solve a problem many times, but we have encountered our
fair share of problems that weren't our fault.  I think MicroFocus is doing
a good job and they have been extremely helpful (frankly, we've been a royal
pain in the ass at times) and we plan on sticking with them.  In a few years
everything will have settled down and all of this bitching will be "the good
ol' days).

Bill




James J. Gavan wrote in message <37FD5681.A60F1772@home.com>...
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
The cObOl Frog
```

<a id="t-6a15a0ad74"></a>
## Thread: Interesting Question from one of my readers. — 13 msgs

### Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Let me preface this by saying that I hear from a LOT of readers.  This
particular reader is from India, and is going through my book with a
finer tooth comb than any of my technical editors.  He has asked some
challenging and intelligent questions.  He does not frequent the news
groups, but gave me permission to post his query here.  

Also as a prelude, I explained that not all compilers do a binary
search when using Search ALL.  His real question is how does one find
out how many elements were compared to find a particular element.  For
the example he cites, I have a table holding the 50 state
abbreviations in alphabetical order, and the search is for "TX".

Here is his query:

-----------------
Hi!
        I noticed something about the Search All clause that I wanted
to
        share with you. So, here goes -

    As you have rightly explained, the Search All clause performs a
binary
    search. Out of pure curiosity, I tried using the Varying
    <Numeric-Data-Item> clause with it, in order to find out how many
    steps it took to eventually find Texas. But to my dismay, I
    encountered a compiler error. I presume that the Varying clause
simply
    counts forward from an initial value, until the search terminates.
If
    this is so, then why doesn't it work in the Search All case?

    Now, suppose I wanted to know how many steps the Search All clause
    took, then what options do I have?
 - Another When Clause ? 
       I can't code another When clause that increments a counter
       everytime the search for Texas fails.
 - Even if I could, since the counter would not be a key, I couldn't
use
 it!

        I hope you find this interesting and worth a thought!

--------------
```

#### ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane,
  I guess my question is WHY (what "programming logic") reason would you ever
WANT (or need) to know how many elements were compared?  I think that the
ANSI/ISO Standard that "SEARCH ALL" is a non-serial (and not a binary) search
method (and the fact that you canNOT use the VARYING clause) means that the
number of comparisons required to reach the "WHEN" condition is variable, not
reliable, and not something with an "external" interface.  I could (vaguely)
imagine a compiler that semi-randomly varies the technique it uses to find
the condition.  Therefore, it could take 3 comparisons to reach "TX" one
time - and 5 the next (with the same table) and that this would be
conforming - and even (possibly) a good/optimized implementation.

Bottom-line:
   You canNOT know how many comparisons resulting in reaching your WHEN
condition (or even AT END).  If you have a programming need to know how many
comparisons were required, then you need to do a sequential SEARCH (or your
own PERFORM routine).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tiim4$qoq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net> <7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com...

<<I think that the ANSI/ISO Standard that "SEARCH ALL" is a non-serial (and
not a binary) search  method (and the fact that you canNOT use the VARYING
clause) means that the number of comparisons required to reach the "WHEN"
condition is variable, not reliable, and not something with an "external"
interface. >>

Not exactly right; in particular, the word "is" in the phrase " ... is a
non-serial ... search method ..." doesn't exactly reflect the wording of the
standard.

ANSI X3.23-1985 states, on Page VI-126 in Paragraph 6.22.4(4), "If format 2
of the SEARCH statement is used, a nonserial type of search may take place
...".    ANSI X3.23-1974 has the same wording on  Page III-9 in Paragraph
3.3.4(3).

The word "may" in this context is permissive, not imperative.  It is up to
the compiler implementor to decide what to do with SEARCH ALL.  The
algorithms permitted by this wording include a binary search, a sequential
search (which means that SEARCH ALL is fundamentally no more efficient than
SEARCH without ALL), or anything in between.

Unisys A Series COBOL74 implements a *serial* search for SEARCH ALL, in
conformance with that wording, partly because our customers demanded the
same item be found by SEARCH ALL as would be found by SEARCH, even when the
table contained duplicates in violation of the specifications for the verb.
However counterintuitive this might be, a serial search algorithm for SEARCH
ALL, while perhaps arguably  *discouraged* by the intent of the standard, is
nevertheless *permitted* by the *wording* of the standard.

The Unisys A Series COBOL85 choice to use a binary search for SEARCH ALL
(thus risking different results with duplicate table entries) is probably in
better conformance with the *spirit* of the standard than the A Series
COBOL74 choice was.

But the bottom line according to the standard is that there is no obligation
on the part of the compiler implementor to use a fundamentally different
approach for SEARCH ALL than is used for SEARCH; the wording allows the
implementor to do so, but does not require it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tishj$itr@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net> <7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7tiim4$qoq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,
  Thanks for the correction.  For the rest of the NG, I just checked and the
draft of the next Standard uses the same "may" wording - which would allow a
serial or non-serial SEARCH to be performed by SEARCH ALL.
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tk0b0$s8d$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net> <7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7tiim4$qoq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <7tishj$itr@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37FD7624.4CA0B20D@att.net>`

```
William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote in message
news:37FD7624.4CA0B20D@att.net...
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
> >
> > Chuck,
> >   Thanks for the correction.  For the rest of the NG, I just checked and
the
> > draft of the next Standard uses the same "may" wording - which would
allow a
> > serial or non-serial SEARCH to be performed by SEARCH ALL.
>
> Can you give an example of a (non-serial and non-binary) SEARCH?
>
> Bill Lynch

OK (a little far-fetched - but not impossible)

Let's say that your vendor's run-time does a serial search for the first
SEARCH ALL but then actually does "data sampling" while doing this serial
search.  Then on subsequent SEARCH ALL statements (for the same table - and
assuming that nothing has changed the distribution), they could do a SEARCH
ALL starting at a "more reasonable" place then the middle and "circle in"
using a non-halving routine that would be likely to get a "hit" more quickly
than assuming a truly random (even) distribution of items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eNv9N=Ph8Hnz6xjrhqYxFUSPmQ8Q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<37fc1503.78354062@news1.attglobal.net> <7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <7tiim4$qoq$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <7tishj$itr@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37FD7624.4CA0B20D@att.net>`

```
The venerable William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Fri, 08 Oct 1999 00:42:12 -0400 these words:

>Can you give an example of a (non-serial and non-binary) SEARCH?

Though rare, they do exist.  The only one I know in any depth is the
proportional search.  I'm not sure I can describe this.  Rather than
looking at the mid-point of the table, it takes the search key's
relative position in the key space.  For example:

The key is 3 digits.  There are 20 items in the table with these keys
## Key
 1 040
 2 050
 3 052
 4 088
 5 100
 6 200
 7 220
 8 222
 9 223
10 226
11 280
12 400
13 405
14 410
15 420
16 600
17 700
18 710
19 720
20 721 

We want to find key 220.  Record 1 is key 40, record 20 is 721, so if
the keys are distributed evenly, key 220 should be record #
(220-40)*(20-1)/(721-40) + 1 = 3420/681 + 1 = 6.022
Record #6 is actually key 200, so we calculate in the space above
(220-200)*(20-6)/(721-200) + 6 = 280/521 + 6 =6.537
This rounds up to 7 (even if it rounded down to 6 it would round up to
7), which is the record we're looking for.  We found the correct
record in 2 iterations where a binary search would take 4 or 5.

If the data is relatively uniform, this search will usually have fewer
iterations.  The man who introduced me to this search boldly claimed
it was never more iterations than a binary search, which I promptly
showed him was false.  (As an exercise, try searching for 280.)
```

```

Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote in message
news:eNv9N=Ph8Hnz6xjrhqYxFUSPmQ8Q@4ax.com...
> The venerable William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> bestowed upon
> comp.lang.cobol on Fri, 08 Oct 1999 00:42:12 -0400 these words:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> relative position in the key space.  For example:

Another way of making this clear is to ask: how do we usually
look words up in a dictionary. If searching for AARDVARK, we
go straight to the beginning of the dictionary. I searching for
ZYGOTE, go straight to the end, etc. Nobody does a binary
search in dictionary.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991007090818.01320.00000012@ng-bd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```
>Subject: Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.netcom.com 
…[70 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Binary search algorithms are of order Log (2) N, where N is the number of
search elements.

While the typical implementation may vary (many use heuristics to get even
better performance) this pretty much means that an entry in a table of 32
elements can be found with no more than 5 comparisons (2**5=32); a table of
16,777,216 entries (2**24) can be searched with no more than 24 comparisons;
etc. 

These are worst case situations - your mileage will vary based upon the data
and implementation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tic6v$pes@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <19991007090818.01320.00000012@ng-bd1.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP <theodp@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19991007090818.01320.00000012@ng-bd1.aol.com...
   <snip>
> >
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 16,777,216 entries (2**24) can be searched with no more than 24
comparisons;
> etc.
>
> These are worst case situations - your mileage will vary based upon the
data
> and implementation.

What you say is true - but not really relevant.  The COBOL Standard is quite
clear that SEARCH ALL need NOT be a "binary" search (or even anything as
efficient as a binary search) - only that it be non-sequential.  *SOME*
implementations do document that they use a BINARY search for their SEARCH
ALL - and for those implementations, your information would answer the
question.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting Question from one of my readers.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Lj9NytX8Ab3WMGhm=dgrhamjPMF@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7th7gv$bmq@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <19991007090818.01320.00000012@ng-bd1.aol.com> <7tic6v$pes@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The venerable "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Thu, 7 Oct 1999 09:53:30 -0500 these words:

>What you say is true - but not really relevant.  The COBOL Standard is quite
>clear that SEARCH ALL need NOT be a "binary" search (or even anything as
>efficient as a binary search) - only that it be non-sequential.

No Bill, the COBOL Standard does not require that the search be
non-sequential, in fact it is completely compliant to just ignore the
word ALL and handle it as a sequential search.  The standard only puts
restraints on SEARCH ALL that make a binary/non-sequential search
possible.  
```

### I want to learn.

- **From:** Jacob Tomaw <jtomaw@indiana.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB9855.6D69A511@indiana.edu>`

```
I have never programmed in COBOL before and I really what to learn the
language.  I know how to program in general, but I don't know COBOL.  Is
there a good book someone can recommend and a good development kit?

Thanx,
Jacob Tomaw
```

#### ↳ Re: I want to learn.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FBA0FF.200AFC85@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37FB9855.6D69A511@indiana.edu>`

```


Jacob Tomaw wrote:
> 
> I have never programmed in COBOL before and I really what to learn the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jacob Tomaw

Welcome to the clan Jacob - you will be joining 3 MILLION others who use
COBOL. Suggested list, which should be available at a big-box book store
or you can check Barnes & Noble or amazon.com. You can go for 1 or 2
first, but having both, I'd opt for # 1 :-


1. Thane Hubbell "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 hours" comes with a
starter-kit for Fujitsu COBOL plus Flexus SP2 GUI handler which Thane
L-O-V-E-S - and you can bitch at the SOB on this NewsGroup if you have
problems <G>

2. "COBOL for Dummies" - yet another good Yellow book

3. "COBOL Unleashed" more expensive than the previous two, but very
comprehensive - I'm just off to my local big-box store to see if they
have a copy on the shelves currently, alternatively I'll order. A number
of the authors chat on this NewsGroup.

> "I know how to program in general...." chances are it was one of the 'modern' languages that has object orientation. This is included to round off-off your reading. Don't go buy it now, but having got 'structured COBOL" into your system, go check objectz.com for Wilson Price's book - excellent text. Even if you don't use OO - it will
make you aware of OO from a COBOL perspective. (Will's book also has a
compiler so you can test OO stuff).

That should get you on your way.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
><snip> Since there is more COBOL than any other language, <snip>

You understate the situation.  There is more COBOL code than *all* the
other languages, put together (60%-80% COBOL vs. 20%-40% for all other
languages). :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to learn.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tleip$7k6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37FB9855.6D69A511@indiana.edu> <37FBA0FF.200AFC85@home.com> <37FE29FD.FEEB17B0@hotmail.com>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol Lewstherin <LewsTherin__@hotmail.com> wrote:

: HAHAH!! Yeah right! If there are 3 million Cobol programmers, then there is 500x more C programmers (or any other real language out there).

And the _evidence_ for this is ?  Oh I know all the kids you went to
school with thought C was really neat.

Actually there are more Cobol programmers than any other single
language.  However, that does not mean that they only program
Cobol.  They may, for example, also be counted as C, Delphi,
Java, or many others.  Most _sensible_ programmers learn
several languages and use those that are most suitable for the
task in hand.

: Cobol isn't even a language.. it's SCRIPT, like HTML and Javascript. 
: It can't handle anything, no pointers, no references, no nothing...

That must be why so many corporations rely on Cobol for their
mission critical work.

: any real programmer uses data abtraction, even in procedural programming techniques. I could go on.. but you guys aren't worth my time,.

Any real programmer knows that all languages have their strengths and 
weaknesses and thus are more suitable for some tasks than for
others.  But do go on, I am sure that you will have plenty of time
as you ask people whether they want fries with that.
```

```
>I have never programmed in COBOL before and I really what to learn the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days is used in colleges and universities around the
workd for training COBOL programmers.  They usually use the MicroFocus Personal
COBOL Compiler with the book. The third edition of the bok will have an
AcuCOBOL Compiler. TYC21D is usaually available in bookstores. If you can't
find it or want the MicroFocus compiler, contact me.
Helen

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
King Computer Services, Inc.
COBOL Training for the Year 2000
"COBOL, Just in Time" Crash Course
"COBOL, Dates and the Year 2000" Technical Reference.
818-951-5240
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to learn.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FE55E2.3E09583F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37FB9855.6D69A511@indiana.edu> <19991007043826.16172.00001473@ngol02.aol.com> <7tl7rt$rek$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


deskware@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Jacob:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> it comes with a complete 200-page manual for $49.95.

Jacob,

The above statement with a qualifier. Probably is a good cheap tool to
learn with, and you can use it as they indicate, but from their own site
information :-

 "It is not totally ANSI compatible", that is, Standard COBOL

I would love a compiler for 50 bucks ! But they commit the cardinal sin
of using non-standard VERBS. Anything you wrote in Deskware would not
easily be transferable to other COBOL compilers containing these
Deskware inconsistencies. And here I'm assuming you want to learn COBOL
for employment - why learn non-standard, non-transferrable features ?

For a cheaper/same price, go the book-route, objectz.com for Personal
COBOL or Thane Hubbell's "Teach yourself COBOL in 24 Hours" which gives
you the Fujitsu starter-kit. And in either of these you are writing
'Standard' COBOL. 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Pi — 9 msgs

### Re: Pi

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB4E3F.AC298AB8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <37f8f95a.16762923@news2.ibm.net> <7tb010$hib@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37f95365_1@news3.prserv.net> <4KmK3.17002$SI1.144298@news1.mia> <37fa8df9_3@news3.prserv.net>`

```
I've seen people recommend using a simple fraction to replace PI while
used in arithmetic expressions.  Their reason is that the fraction is
stored in a temporary variable to more precision than we could store as
a constant.

I don't remember the recommended fraction - I suppose that would vary
depending upon the computer architecture and compiler.
```

```
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DHIK3.1734$x4.45823@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <37f8f95a.16762923@news2.ibm.net> <7tb010$hib@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37f95365_1@news3.prserv.net> <4KmK3.17002$SI1.144298@news1.mia> <37fa8df9_3@news3.prserv.net> <37FB4E3F.AC298AB8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FB5435.10A92F58@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>22 / 7  If I recall.

Pi                 = 3.141592653589793...
355/113 (6 digits) = 3.1415929... (7 significant digits)
22/7    (3 digits) = 3.142...     (3 significant digits)

Neither gives significantly more digits than you supply.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB75EE.6F4A8E2C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <37f8f95a.16762923@news2.ibm.net> <7tb010$hib@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37f95365_1@news3.prserv.net> <4KmK3.17002$SI1.144298@news1.mia> <37fa8df9_3@news3.prserv.net> <37FB4E3F.AC298AB8@NOSPAMhome.com> <37FB5435.10A92F58@zip.com.au> <DHIK3.1734$x4.45823@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> >> I don't remember the recommended fraction - I suppose that would vary
> >> depending upon the computer architecture and compiler.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Neither gives significantly more digits than you supply.

It's quite possible the solution may have been designed for something
like Apple Basic, which had a different constant size.  Still, it would
be cool to have something which would be use with mainframe COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WAIK3.1722$x4.45611@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <37f8f95a.16762923@news2.ibm.net> <7tb010$hib@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <37f95365_1@news3.prserv.net> <4KmK3.17002$SI1.144298@news1.mia> <37fa8df9_3@news3.prserv.net> <37FB4E3F.AC298AB8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>I've seen people recommend using a simple fraction to replace PI while
>used in arithmetic expressions.  Their reason is that the fraction is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>depending upon the computer architecture and compiler.

I've played around with pi a bit, and have never heard of this.  I am
familiar with 22/7 and 355/113, but these were used because they are
simple and easy to remember.  Neither gives pi to more than one extra
digit.  I know of no short ratio that will give a significant number
of digits of pi, that would be suitable for computer use.  The late
Petr Beckmann's excellent "A History of PI" doesn't mention one that
I recall.  Howard, if you can remember what it was, or if somebody
else knows of one, please post it. :-)
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCE9D7.D2091A5E@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<37F8E5E5.5FDBF622@nojunk.accumedic.com> <Hx7K3.3352$Tg3.456954@news.sgi.net> <37F9EC7C.1C8DD8A6@nojunk.accumedic.com> <8zmK3.319$iC6.18421@news.sgi.net> <37FA5274.B515D3BA@nojunk.accumedic.com> <wQHK3.709$iC6.86230@news.sgi.net>`

```
Joe,

Is your realbind.exe from Workbench 2.0 (COBOL version 5), or is it from
Realia COBOL version 4.something?

Just curious -- the 5.0 compiler that comes with WB 2.0 has a
realbind.exe that's 9/24/96 04:47p, 257,882 bytes,  and has a version
5.015.

Thanks,
Jim
```

<a id="t-715b4d5a7d"></a>
## Thread: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

- **From:** Jim Elden <elden@nojunk.accumedic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB424E.7DDF5F96@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<37F8E5E5.5FDBF622@nojunk.accumedic.com> <Hx7K3.3352$Tg3.456954@news.sgi.net> <37F9EC7C.1C8DD8A6@nojunk.accumedic.com> <8zmK3.319$iC6.18421@news.sgi.net> <37FA52D4.AB76F722@nojunk.accumedic.com> <37fa6b3a.425137117@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
>>Try realbind/d - then use FORCEDOS on it.<<

That didn't work either.

>>try some other simple application - maybe it's something like
file handles or something else, try a hello world program and see if
you can get that to run.<<

Exactly what I did. My test program had 1 line in the procedure
division: DISPLAY "Hello World", plus stop run.  After removing the
display, the program will run with FORCEDOS.  So maybe it's with the way
Realia handles the display.  I remember that there were 3 modes, BIOS,
memory-mapped, and DOS, (B, M, and D) and thet the default was
memory-mapped.   Trying this...

MOVE LOW-VALUES TO DISP-PARMS.
MOVE "B" TO DISP-OUTPUT-TYPE-FLAG.
CALL "DISPLAY-CONTROL" USING DISP-PARMS.

...the program will run (either realbind/d with forcedos or realbind/e),
but mess up the display.  The other two modes lock the DOS session with
the cursor in the upper left of the screen.

>>I maintain that MS claims to have removed support and
emulation for DOS programs.<<

Agreed.

>>Try a non-realia DOS program and see what
happens also.<<

I tried a few.  Some work, some don't.  Bummer.  We'll just have to keep
a few W9x machines around to run these programs.

Jim
```

#### ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fc2432.82241271@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<37F8E5E5.5FDBF622@nojunk.accumedic.com> <Hx7K3.3352$Tg3.456954@news.sgi.net> <37F9EC7C.1C8DD8A6@nojunk.accumedic.com> <8zmK3.319$iC6.18421@news.sgi.net> <37FA52D4.AB76F722@nojunk.accumedic.com> <37fa6b3a.425137117@news1.attglobal.net> <37FB424E.7DDF5F96@nojunk.accumedic.com>`

```
16 bit Realia WINDOWS programs work under Windows 2000 - but the file
locking has problems because of netbios.

You have to have a special Identification division and linkage section
to compile them as Windows programs, but you might try that.

Here's the key:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. 'P_Winmain'.


*****************
 LINKAGE SECTION.
*****************

 01  INSTANCE			   PIC 9(4) BINARY.
 01  PREV-INSTANCE		   PIC 9(4) BINARY.
 01  CMD-LINE			   PIC X(120).
 01  CMD-SHOW			   PIC 9(4) BINARY.

********************
 PROCEDURE DIVISION
********************

	  USING BY CONTENT INSTANCE PREV-INSTANCE
	  BY REFERENCE CMD-LINE
	  BY CONTENT CMD-SHOW.


If you need a link bat - try LINKWINM.BAT.

Still keep me posted.  This is not good.


On Wed, 06 Oct 1999 08:36:30 -0400, Jim Elden
<elden@nojunk.accumedic.com> wrote:

>>>Try realbind/d - then use FORCEDOS on it.<<
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
>>16 bit Realia WINDOWS programs [...]<<

Regards,
Jim
```

### Re: Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tgni9$t11$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37F90516.E5BE98EB@IMN.nl> <37F92719.5F13AB50@home.com> <37f95921.354958102@news1.attglobal.net> <37F97200.F4E944C6@home.com> <7tcvur$qhf$1@news.igs.net> <7tdh29$4mk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7tdh29$4mk@dfw-
<SNIP>

>The "funniest" thing is when I find myself falling into "COBOL 101 stupid
>errors" - because I don't really understand what I am doing.  My favorite
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>realized that I hadn't coded a "GOBACK" at the end of my mainline and it
was
>falling thru to the rest of the routines.  (Talk about "embarrassed" <G>).
>It seems like the normal event procedure window that it opens up uses the
>"default exit program" or something so that you don't need any explicit
>return - but once you add additional paragraphs, this fails.
>
Funny, I did the exact same thing with PowerCobol.  I could never seem to
convince my brain that those tiny little stubs were real Cobol programs.

What I actually found to work best was to code up methods in one main
program, then simply invoke the methods and exit for about 95% of all
events.  Most of the processing  was far too generic to write as a separate
module.  I found that most of what I put into the basic events was copies
... it then twigged that I could write one method in one place, and skip all
the copying.

Anyway, I started this message to ask you or Jimmy to help me with some
code.  I have encoded an oops program that should, as far as I can make out,
compile and run.  I cannot get it to even compile. I am getting the message
that the repository data cannot be found, even though the file is there, and
changes it's date every time I compile the method.  However the main program
insists that it is not.

The code is straight Fujitsu ... damned if I know how to make it invoke.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. "TEST".
      AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
      SECURITY.
            Copyright (C), 1999, Donald L. Tees.
            A main program to invoke something.
     ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
     CONFIGURATION SECTION.
      REPOSITORY.
           CLASS TEST-CLASS.
     SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
     OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
     SPECIAL-NAMES.
         CONSOLE IS CRT.
      INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
      FILE-CONTROL.
      DATA DIVISION.
      FILE SECTION.
      WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      01 MY-OBJECT             USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE TEST-CLASS.
      PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      MAIN-LINE.
           INVOKE  TESTIT "new" returning MY-OBJECT.
           INVOKE MY-OBJECT "SAY-HELLO".
           GOBACK.

*and the method*

       CLASS-ID.   TEST-CLASS INHERITS FJBASE.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       REPOSITORY.
           CLASS FJBASE.
       OBJECT.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       METHOD-ID. SAY-HELLO.
       DATA DIVISION.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN-LINE.
           DISPLAY "HELLO WORLD".
       END METHOD SAY-HELLO.
       END OBJECT.
       END CLASS TEST-CLASS.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Query

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FC34E2.64374D49@home.com>`
- **References:** `<WyAJ3.2251$mN5.102464@typhoon01.swbell.net> <37F90516.E5BE98EB@IMN.nl> <37F92719.5F13AB50@home.com> <37f95921.354958102@news1.attglobal.net> <37F97200.F4E944C6@home.com> <7tcvur$qhf$1@news.igs.net> <7tdh29$4mk@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com> <7tgni9$t11$1@news.igs.net> <7th8e9$bur@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> <snip>

Bill, I think I screwed up (yet again) - posted this to Donald but I
don't think it made it to CLC - so for your info :-
.......................................................................
donald tees wrote:
> 
> William M. Klein wrote in message <7tdh29$4mk@dfw-
> <SNIP>

Your originals :-
 
> 
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>        END CLASS TEST-CLASS.

Now the amended . You'll appreciate I've had to change some of the
syntax. It kicked out some of the 'red-tape' - but as I don't use it,
didn't bother to check why - but that wasn't the purpose of the exercise
was it ? The following works fine in N/E :-


       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. "TEST".

       *> AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
       *> SECURITY.
       *>     Copyright (C), 1999, Donald L. Tees.
       *>     A main program to invoke something.
       *> ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       *> CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       Class-Control.    *>REPOSITORY.
              TEST-CLASS      is class "dontees2".
       *> SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
       *> OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CONSOLE IS CRT.
        INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
        FILE-CONTROL.
        DATA DIVISION.
        FILE SECTION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        01 MY-OBJECT           USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE.
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        MAIN-LINE.
             INVOKE  TEST-Class "new" returning MY-OBJECT.
             INVOKE MY-OBJECT "SAY-HELLO".
             GOBACK.
......................................................................
       CLASS-ID.   TEST-CLASS
                   inherits from Base.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       Class-Control.            *>REPOSITORY.
            Test-Class is class "dontees2"
           .
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "new".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-self              object reference.
       Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
         invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
         invoke self "hello-2"
       End Method "new".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "hello-2".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.
          display "Hello World 2"
       End Method "hello-2".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       METHOD-ID. SAY-HELLO.
       DATA DIVISION.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN-LINE.
           DISPLAY "HELLO WORLD".
       END METHOD SAY-HELLO.
       END OBJECT.
       END CLASS TEST-CLASS.
..........................................

In your invoke Testit you are establishing a handle (object reference)
to the Class TestClass in the FACTORY which is returned to the invoker.
Then its your Object section which does the method work. In a Mickey
Mouse like this - you can see you could have done it in Factory 
with method "Hello-2". Now's the interesting bit - will the result work
in Fuji - let me know.

Don't want to confuse the hell out of you at this stage, but here's
another way you could get at method "hello-2" - and this is the beauty
of inheritance :-

        Program TEST doesn't change at all

        *>-----------------------------
        Class-id. OtherHellos
                  inherits from Base.

        Class-Control.     OtherHellos is class "dontees3"       
                       .
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       *> FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       *> Factory isn't required - we aren't invoking it directly
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       OBJECT.
       *>---------------------------------------------------
        Method-id. "hello-2".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.
          display "Hello World 2"
       End Method "hello-2".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       END OBJECT.
       END CLASS OtherHellos.
...............................................................

        *>-----------------------------
        Class-id. TestClass
                  inherits from OtherHellos.

        Class-Control.  TestClass   is class "dontees2" 
                        OtherHellos is class "dontees3"        
           .
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       *> FACTORY.
       *>---------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "new".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-self              object reference.
       Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
         invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
       End Method "new".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       END FACTORY.
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "say-hello".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       Procedure Division.
          display "Hello World"
          invoke super "hello-2"
       End Method "say-hello".
       *>--------------------------------------------------
       END OBJECT.
       END CLASS TestClass.
...............................................................


PS: Does Fujitsu demand all the 'red-tape' or is it your documentation
preference - if the first, what a pain.

Jimmy

............. end of copy .............................................
```

## Thread: Best COBOL Freeware Utilty Since 1990 — 2 msgs

### Best COBOL Freeware Utilty Since 1990

- **From:** "editor" <editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pIHK3.25$hf5.671@client>`

```
Free Utility Download: SIZER (No More Counting Lines of COBOL Code)
www.aboutlegacycoding.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Best COBOL Freeware Utilty Since 1990

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brRK3.1863$nm.25185@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<pIHK3.25$hf5.671@client>`

```
You might get a better response (I assume this means more downloads) if you
were as accurate as you are enthusiastic in your description of the product.

Progeni SIZER has nothing to do with counting lines of code. It is a "record
analyzer utility" which gives you the size of the largest '01' level record
currently pasted onto the Windows(r) clipboard.

It is a very nice product. I use it myself for quickie stuff. (No, not
*that* kind of quickie).

However, I can hardly call it the best freeware COBOL utility since 1990; at
least as long as my utility (COBFD) is available free at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html  (Of course, it is *remotely*
possible I am biased here).

(FWIW, I am currently migrating my product to Windows. I am shamelessly
stealing your idea and will make "clipboard import" an option).
```

```
 Your subscription is set to individual email delivery

If you are using Microsoft Outlook Express, that happens because of the
"character set" used on the message. Vadim's post includes this option in
the message header (open/properties): charset=koi8-r.

Go to "Tools/Options/Read/Fonts" to set your viewing font based on the
character set in the message header. If this charset is not in the list
(it's not in mine), I think Outlook Express just switches to this charset
and forgets to switch back unless it gets a message to read with a "charset"
option which IS in its list.

The real solution is to ask eveyone who posts to only use a "charset" option
which is supported by Outlook Express.
```

## Thread: Searching Tables — 1 msgs

### Re: Searching Tables

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB3DDC.CFAD2E6@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37ED8B5B.5DD67199@home.com> <37ed979a.103257816@news1.ibm.net> <37efb349@eeyore.callnetuk.com> <QnTH3.995$Cv4.29955@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7t8tmb$cri@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <7t8uj0$gsk@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <7tapdi$7mf@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <37f97ad6$1$cnhye$mr2ice@news.stonemedia.com> <37f98103.365169232@news1.attglobal.net> <37F9B112.FDB5B320@zip.com.au> <37FA1FC9.11ADE123@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Bugger, I thought you might ask that.

These are difficult concepts to get across and it is actually hard to
determine which is which.  It is kind of like the OO shift in
paradigm, once you get it, you have, until then you have not.

Data coupling (bad):

	move 1 to perform-type
	perform something

something.
	if perform-type is 1
		...
	else

Generally when you see the above it is a warning that you are really
trying to do two different things in the one function.  The first
routine 'knows' something of the internals of the second.  This is
data coupling.

External coupling (bad, common in cobol):

perform abc.

In this instance you don't know what abc affects without inspecting
the code contained in abc.

call abc using xyz,   qrs.

We know that only xyz and qrs are modified.  If we are not interested
in those variables we need look no further.

Stamp coupling (bad, common in old-bol):

perform calculate using entire-output-record.

This was done in one program I maintained.  It turned out that it used
two fields and updated a third based upon a look up and calculation. 
Code should have been more explicit, I had to review a lot of code in
this routine to determine what actually was affected (several hundred
code lines).

Common coupling:

This is where you store general stuff into a bin with no common
features. You should group customer info so that you can initialize
all customer stuff in one hit.   Can't give you a sample when this
caused me problems.

Content coupling:

Entry points & go to, I simply ban these outright.  There are some
good uses but they are too easily abused.


Good coupling:

call mailcentre-lookup using postcode,  mail-centre.

Version 1 of this routine used a VSAM file on look up.
        2 had a DB2 look up
        3 had a sequential file loaded into memory on the first call.

It is loosely coupled because:

1.  It can (and is) used in many programs.
2.  The underlying implementation can be changed without modifying the
    calling program.


By the way,  the main benefit of OO is that far easier for cohesion
and coupling to be done correctly in the design of OO systems. 
Classes are generally concerned with one thing, actions are related
and singular, etc.

Do you want me to try and explain cohesion or has this mangled
explanation confused you enough?

Ken
```

---

<a id="t-5799c45e67"></a>
## Thread: Some help on Cobol CGI with NetExpress (Somewhat long) — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Some help on Cobol CGI with NetExpress (Somewhat long)

- **From:** semiel@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tfirs$od3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37F9F54A.6A943B50@eii.fr> <37FA5A0E.B24E71AB@IMN.nl> <37FB160B.8B20195D@eii.fr>`

```
I will try to give you some help as i work with NetExpress for writing
CGI applications...
Well, did you notice the "Net" in the product name? The product is
really aimed at helping Cobol programmer get into Web progamming and is
constantly extended/improved in that direction (while keeping the "old"
features). Features it includes are:

'Form Designer': Its an integrated tool helping to create your HTML
forms. You can draw your input fields, combo boxes..., integrate
activeX, javascripts ... You associate ID and Names to those items and
the Cobol variables will be declared in copy files in the Cobol source
code.
The form will be declared something like that:
01 HTMLForm is external-form.
  02 HTMLControls.
.....

Those forms (you open with Form Designer) have a new format ".mff". The
designer also generates the ".HTML" files. All those files will be
integrated in your project.

There is also a great wizard for generating CGIs on SQL databases. It
generates the Inserts, Update, Delete with state management... The
skeleton it uses can be modified if needed.
(There are other wizards (GUI...) but as i dont use those....)

You have embedded HTML and embedded SQL:
EXEC HTML/SQL
...
END-EXEC

An integrated web server 'Solo' you can use while developing and which
enables animating CGIs.

There is a library called 'ACCCGI' which provides an extension to the
normal cobol ACCEPT which does the data decoding returned from the web
server. You will just have to DISPLAY/ACCEPT using the fields generated
by the Form Designer and the rest is transparent. I assume this would
replace your c-shell scripts.

There is a tool for publishing applications (including CGIs) to UNIX.

I also think that CGIs have to be in '.EXE' or '.DLL', no '.OBJ'.

There is an SQL assistant.

You can program object oriented (dont know if you have that yet...) and
there is class library browser.

It includes also 'Dialog System' (same remark..)

Hope this helps to give you an overwiew as this isnt all there is but
thats what i use.
If someone involved in MicroFocus (Merant?) reads this and isnt happy
with my overview i appologize:)

I will check back to see if you need some more,
Alain.





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> > :>What about the general concept of WS- fields for mundane constants
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> cloned).

A poor implementation of a reasonable standard.

The concept of this standard is to define a generic limit in a single
variable:

WS-CUST-TABLE-MAX    PIC 9(02) COMP VALUE 30.

Using WS-30 would be worse than useless.

Ken
```

<a id="t-df40081f16"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and CGI-BIN — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Re: COBOL and CGI-BIN

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB458F.C5550954@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37F9F54A.6A943B50@eii.fr> <37FA5A0E.B24E71AB@IMN.nl> <37FB160B.8B20195D@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote:
> 
> 1/ What does NetExpress provide more than normal COBOL ? (it is a very
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> them...).

Try http://www.boutell.com/faq/oldfaq/cgi.htm  to start for more
information.

The strings that come in are URL encoded a percent (%) sign followed
by a hex digit (from memory) so to decode the URL in cobol would not
be a major exercise.  Once that is finished you simply check the input
and away you go.

** Does anyone have Public Domain cobol code for this **

There are two types of input POST and another.  One puts the whole lot
on the command line, the other uses environment variables.

The output is simply an output to the sequential stdout (display if
you like).

To format the output you will probably need some basic HTML and the
header record.  This is not rocket science unless you need graphics
(not called static graphics but pie charts).

The simple way to design the output is to format it using a tool and
then look at the output and then put that output into your program
somehow (stripping what you can).

Good luck
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and CGI-BIN

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0fIK3.15085$48.363327@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<37F9F54A.6A943B50@eii.fr> <37FA5A0E.B24E71AB@IMN.nl> <37FB160B.8B20195D@eii.fr> <37FB458F.C5550954@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:37FB458F.C5550954@zip.com.au...
> webmaster wrote:
> >
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> ** Does anyone have Public Domain cobol code for this **

COBOL Unleashed, Chapter 29. CGIPOST.CBL provides one algorithm for doing
this. There are probably more efficient character-at-a-time algorithms but
it's as good a starting point as any.

>
> There are two types of input POST and another.  One puts the whole lot
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> somehow (stripping what you can).

I think it's better to leave as much of the HTML in a template file as
possible. The COBOL program reads in the template, substituting or adding
unique HTML as necessary, and writes the output to STDOUT. COBOL's great for
file I/O but it's a crummy place to maintain an HTML document.
>
> Good luck
> Ken
```

<a id="t-cf7aeb5e7d"></a>
## Thread: Student - packed decimal - Net Express — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student - packed decimal - Net Express

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FB478D.C681CA9F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net>`

```
zennmaster wrote:
> 
> So my question is:  is this a product of EBCDIC/ASCII conflict on
> packed data and how can I resolve this?   Or are my fears unfounded
> and I'm just not processing the data correctly?
> 

Yes...

The download tanslates characters from one character set to another. 
hex 15 for example is tab in Ebcdic and hex 08 in ASCII.

Recommend you expand the file before download or else you will have a
painful time of translating each text field individually.

Does Net express have an open file in ebcdic mode?   If so download
the file in binary.

Ken
```

```
Re my other post, the DLL I have will also do a EBCDIC-ASCII conversion. If
you tell it "input is EBCDIC" it will convert only the appropriate data
types; it will also handle the "special" sign adjustment you get on signed
USAGE IS DISPLAY.

Send e-mail for conversion info; still looking for testers.

I also have a document for you to read. It explains each step in the
conversion process. When I converted it to HTML, it looks kinda crummy and
the kind people (person?) (Bob Wolfe) at Flexus is trying to help me get it
"presentable." It has great info, but "visual presentation in HTML format"
is not one of my strengths.
```

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student - packed decimal - Net Express

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1kIK3.1773$eM4.147804@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net>`

```

zennmaster <zenn@master.net> wrote in message
news:37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net...
> Sequential  File Update assignment
> Both old-master and transaction file contain packed decimal downloaded
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and notice a shift in some records where characters are in filler.
[snip]
> So my question is:  is this a product of EBCDIC/ASCII conflict on
> packed data and how can I resolve this?   Or are my fears unfounded
> and I'm just not processing the data correctly?

Since other posters have suggested you might not have your file defined
correctly, I will only add that, until you have unpacked your packed fields,
do not translate from EBCDIC to ASCII.  If you read an EBCDIC record
containing COMP-3 fields, only translate the plain numeric or alphanumeric
fields to ASCII.
```

## Thread: Student - packed decimal - Net Express — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student - packed decimal - Net Express

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FBA7CE.537BF1E4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net> <1kIK3.1773$eM4.147804@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <37fd8c11.693046@nntp.xsite.net>`

```


zennmaster wrote:
> 
> Thanks all, for your insights and answers.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> transaction FD on the mainframe (same as oldmast sans last two fields)
<snip>

Now I'm not a bits-and-bytes man; others will cover that more
extensively - but here's a real world example of doing it with
NetExpress :-

I am transferring RM/COBOL data files to NetExpress. Firstly the
original format as used in RM/COBOL (not their current format, I should
add):-

       *---------- CSITM2.CBL ----------- 95 MAY 24 -----------------*

       FD ITEM-NAMES.

       01 ITM-RECORD.
          03 ITM-KEY-3.
             05 ITM-SYSTEM-NUMBER              PIC X(03).
             05 ITM-KEY-2.
                10 ITM-VESSEL-NUMBER.
                   20 ITM-VESSEL-KEY           PIC X(03).
                   20 ITM-VESSEL-SUFFIX        PIC X(01).
                10 ITM-KEY-1.
                   20 ITM-ITEM-NUMBER          PIC X(06).
           03 ITM-DATA.
            05 ITM-NAME                        PIC X(40).
            05 ITM-DRAWING-NUMBER              PIC X(04).
            05 ITM-NOMINAL                     PIC 9(01)V9(04) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-CRITICAL                    PIC 9(01)V9(04) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-NUMBER-OF-POINTS            PIC 9(03) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-TEMPERATURE-TABLE.
               10  ITM-TEMPERATURE OCCURS 10   PIC S9(03) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-PROBE-TABLE.
               10  ITM-PROBE-NUMBER OCCURS 10  PIC 9(02) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-SUMMARY-FLAG                PIC X.
               88 RECALC-ITEM                  VALUE "Y", "y".
            05 ITM-PIPE-SIZE                   PIC 9(02)V9(01).
            05 ITM-CODE                        PIC X(06).
            05 ITM-QUESTION-MARK               PIC X.
            05 ITM-MATERIAL                    PIC 9(02).
            05 ITM-MAX-WORKING-PRESSURE        PIC 9(05) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-MAX-WORKING-TEMPERATURE     PIC 9(04) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-NUMBER-OF-INSPECTIONS       PIC 9(02).
            05 ITM-DATE-TABLE.
               10 ITM-INSPECTION-DATE OCCURS 10 PIC 9(04) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-DIAMETER-FLAG                PIC X(01).
            05 ITM-DIAMETER                     PIC 9(03)V9(03) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-STRESS-VALUE                 PIC 9(02)V9(01) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-CRITICAL-CALC-FLAG           PIC X(01).
            05 ITM-CSA-CRITICAL                 PIC 9(01)V9(04) COMP-3.
            05 ITM-JOINT-EFFICIENCY             PIC 9(03) COMP-1.

      *------------------------ END CSITM2/CBL -----------------------*

And using an old Merant(MicroFocus) tool from DOS days, the above is
'translated' as follows for NetExpress :-

       FD RM-File.           *> ( RM Items file )
       01 RM-record.
          05  RM-SYSTEM-NUMBER            pic x(00003).
          05  RM-VESSEL-KEY               pic x(00004).
          05  RM-ITEM-NUMBER              pic x(00006).
          05  RM-NAME                     pic x(00040).
          05  RM-DRAWING-NUMBER           pic x(00004).
          05  RM-IMPERIAL                 pic x(00003).
          05  RM-CRITICAL                 pic x(00003).
          05  RM-NUMBER-OF-POINTS         pic x(00002).
          05  RM-TEMPERATURE occurs 10    pic x(00002).
          05  RM-PROBE-NUMBER occurs 10   pic x(00002).
          05  RM-SUMMARY-FLAG             pic x(00001).
          05  RM-PIPE-SIZE                pic x(00003).
          05  RM-CODE                     pic x(000006).
          05  RM-QUESTION-MARK            pic x(00001).
          05  RM-MATERIAL                 pic x(00002).
          05  RM-MAX-WORKING-PRESSURE     pic x(00003).
          05  RM-MAX-WORKING-TEMPERATURE   pic x(00003).
          05  RM-NUMBER-OF-INSPECTIONS     pic x(00002).
          05  RM-INSPECTION-DATE occurs 10 pic x(00003).
          05  RM-DIAMETER-FLAG            pic x(00001).
          05  RM-DIAMETER                 pic x(00004).
          05  RM-STRESS-VALUE             pic x(00002).
          05  RM-CRITICAL-CALC-FLAG       pic x(00001).
          05  RM-CSA-CRITICAL             pic x(00003).
          05  RM-JOINT-EFFICIENCY         pic x(00002).

The trick is getting those input fileds described as the right length
for NetExpress. And taking the new input record, I can now move
something like RM-JOINT-EFFICINCY which was originally described as 
9(03) comp-1 to a new N/E record-field as 9(03) comp-3 (it could have
been to a 9(02) comp-3 - because this application isn't concerned with a
percentage above 85%).

Hope the above helps a bit.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
R�jean Carrier wrote:
>Hello, I am running R/M Cobol 85.
```

- **References:** `<37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net> <1kIK3.1773$eM4.147804@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <37fd8c11.693046@nntp.xsite.net>`

```
zennmaster wrote:
> 
> Thanks all, for your insights and answers.
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> school wishes to teach us to be detail oriented.

Another possibility...if your input file is defined as line sequential,
check the 'N' run-time
switch.  The default is 'ON'.  When 'ON' and writting a file, any
character < x'1F' will have a
null (x'00') inserted prior to the character.  When reading a file, the
null will be stripped
off.  Your packed fields most likely have some nulls, so each first null
will go to the bit
bucket.

Mike
```

<a id="t-aacaf81492"></a>
## Thread: Student - packed decimal - Net Express — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student - packed decimal - Net Express

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1041$724c60b0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<37fbaf89.8279142@nntp.xsite.net> <1kIK3.1773$eM4.147804@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <37fd8c11.693046@nntp.xsite.net>`

```
zennmaster <zenn@master.net> wrote in article
<37fd8c11.693046@nntp.xsite.net>...
> Thanks all, for your insights and answers. 
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> remarkable - byte count remains the same.       Any ideas?

Ken Foskey's posting should point you in the right direction.  Here is a
more detailed explanation.  I am sure it has been covered numerous times in
various news groups.

The file transfer programs between MVS and the PC world can either transfer
in binary mode (each byte transfers unaltered) or in text mode (each byte
is translated to/from ASCII/EBCDIC code).  The trouble is the PIC 9(5)V99
COMP-3 fields.  These are in packed decimal representation, i.e. each byte
contains 2 decimal digits in BCD code, the last byte contains 1 digit and
the sign.  If your field has the value 12345.67 it is represented as 4
bytes with hex values 12 34 56 7F.  If transferred as text from MVS to PC,
each byte is translated (the translate table depends on the character set
used on each side).  Certain values may cause particular trouble, for
example being confused with control characters like CarriageReturn,
LineFeed, Tab, and others.  The result is generally unusable.  You have two
alternatives. 1: transfer in binary mode.  No translation takes place, you
must interpret all data as is (text fields are still in EBCDIC code).  Or
2: convert packed decimal fields to numeric display fields (unpacked) on
the MVS side before transferring, and transfer as text.  All data (also
numeric fields) will come through as ASCII coded text.  If you have defined
binary fields, they must be treated in a similar way.

Hope this helps.

Gunnar.
```

---

<a id="t-1433889e45"></a>
## Thread: WS- fields for mundane constants — 1 msgs

### Re: WS- fields for mundane constants

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FBCC6B.770C@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7tb7fq$mpb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <Eu35NzM1qHULBcPg6CDKe0wD1Rl8@4ax.com> <7tdkqj$f0p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> > :>What about the general concept of WS- fields for mundane constants
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.


Back in the 1970's and 1980's I was taught to use this method because of
the code size savings.  We were renting computer time from another
outfit, and the size of your program could be directly connected to the
cost of running your program.

Today it makes very little sense, except maybe if it makes your
procedure division code read more like english. (e.g. UNTIL
W05-PAYMENT-TABLE-SUBSCRIPT GREATER THAN W07-PAYMENT-TABLE-SIZE)

My shop follows the following working storage organization scheme so
that everything belongs somewhere, instead of just coded
helter-skelter.  Over half of our COBOL is now coded this way, and it
adds a certain comfort level when performing maintenance:
   ****------------------------------------------------------------*
   **** STANDARD WORKING STORAGE AREAS:                             
   ****       W01- ACCUMULATORS AND COUNTERS                       
   ****       W02- SWITCHES                                        
   ****       W03- CONSTANTS AND LITERALS                          
   ****       W04- DATE FIELDS                                     
   ****       W05- SUBSCRIPTS                                      
   ****       W06- KEYS                                            
   ****       W07- TABLES                                          
   ****       W08- MESSAGES                                        
   ****       W09- MISCELLANEOUS                                   
   ****       W??- RECORD AREAS                                    
   ****          - CICS MAPS                                       
   ****          - DB2 SCHEMAS                                     
   ****       L01- LINKAGE SECTION                                 
   ****       R01- REPORT WRITER SECTION                           
   ****------------------------------------------------------------*

Pete
```

<a id="t-1fa4dfc741"></a>
## Thread: Call QBASIC from COBOL...? — 15 msgs

### Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net>`

```
Is it possible to CALL a QBASIC program (compiled) and pass data to it? then
get data passed back..

Or to Call a COBOL program with a QBASIC program and pass data to it?  (More
of a QBASIC question that COBOL but related nonetheless)  I know one would
probably use PEEK and POKE however I am not real familiar with these
commands...

Then link both (using either of the above) making an EXE?

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

#### ↳ Re: Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h1bL3.2501$7p6.28079@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net>`

```
First off, QBasic is the INTERPRETER, not the compiler. QuickBASIC is the
compiler which produces EXE files.

Second, No, you can't. You can get data *to* the BASIC program via
command-line arguments, but you can't get it back into your COBOL program
(AFAIK). Same problem in reverse.

Second, you could create OBJ files with QuickBASIC, OBJ files with your
COBOL compiler and link those together with any linker to create an exe.
Note that with BOTH lanagugae products, you would need to specify the
compile/link options which allow you to create "standalone" programs, i.e.,
no run-time support required. Not that the run time would not work - I don't
have a clue - but I have to believe asking both language products to load
run-time support might make things a tad dicey vis-a-vis any spare memory in
"the lower 640."

Better idea (?): You could have the BASIC/COBOL program write a disk file
before exiting, and read that file after returning to your calling
COBOL/BASIC program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0cl8q9hu276@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net>`

```
NO.  Absolutely NO.

1- All kind of BASIC (not Visual BASIC by MS) are interpreters.
   They cannot make EXEs. User should run the source code within
   BASIC environment. Outside BASIC environment, the programs are
   merely text files.

2- In Object Oriented Design, the system memory can store variables
   back and forth from DLL or API processing. 
   There are no system memory storage for COBOL and BASIC processing.
   So COBOL EXEs and BASIC cannot call or be called from any EXEs.
   In COBOL, there is LINKAGE SECTION that declare variables as global
   to pass between calling and called programs. Called program should
   be compiled prior to calling programs being compiled.
   After the calling programs compiled, they make EXEs there.
   Differ from EXEs in OO design, they are always big, for they contain
   all called programs inside them. (In OO design, the calling EXEs are
   small, just enough to call others, not to contain them.)

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ygwN3.3635$LW6.58148@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net> <s0cl8q9hu276@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Actually, statment #2 is incorrect.

ALL COBOLs have memory storage, its just that all memory within a 'run unit'
is USUALLY
STATIC for the duration.

ALL COBOLs can be called from other Object Modules (Compiled BASIC,
assembler exes, etc)
ALL COBOLs can 'call' other Object Modules as long as each use the same
interface.


AnhMy Tran wrote in message ...
>NO.  Absolutely NO.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>                      http://www.help.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UfvN3.1405$Ba1.29407@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net> <s0cl8q9hu276@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I think you are about, oh, six to eight years behind the times.

AnhMy Tran wrote in message ...
>NO.  Absolutely NO.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   merely text files.



There are numerous compiled BASICs: Microsoft, PowerBASIC, XBasic,
TrueBASIC; even BBx Business Basic is compiled into p-code, depending only
on the availablility of the correct run-time on the target system.

>2- In Object Oriented Design, the system memory can store variables
>   back and forth from DLL or API processing.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   be compiled prior to calling programs being compiled.

Huh? DLL's are written to effect linkage within an address space under
Windows;it's a little different 16-bit vs. 32-bit, but eminently do-able.
COBOL programs can call DLL's, no problem.

Under MS-DOS, TSR programs responding to an interrupt may be used. If the
COBOL program can generate a software interrupt, sha-zam! you're in
business. Although usually in this environment, you would just have the
BASIC compiler generate an OBJ file and static-link it to the COBOL program.

Now, the original question was specifically about QBasic, an interpreted
product, in which case communications is not possible. Although either
program may shell the other, and pass data to it, it cannot get the
(modified) data back.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Call QBASIC from COBOL...?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3806b19c.49038553@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<yT5L3.4620$0l6.8798@news.flash.net> <s0cl8q9hu276@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 14 Oct 1999 22:11:38 GMT, AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com> wrote:

>NO.  Absolutely NO.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   merely text files.

Wrong.  I had my first BASIC Compiler about 10 years agi
>
>2- In Object Oriented Design, the system memory can store variables
>   back and forth from DLL or API processing. 
>   There are no system memory storage for COBOL and BASIC processing.
>   So COBOL EXEs and BASIC cannot call or be called from any EXEs.

Wrong again, on all counts.  COBOL program can call other programs, can CALL DLLs.  COBOL
Programs can be called by other programs.  COBOL Programs can be made into DLLs and be
called by other programs


>   In COBOL, there is LINKAGE SECTION that declare variables as global
>   to pass between calling and called programs. 

This one is more or less correct
>   Called program should
>   be compiled prior to calling programs being compiled.

Why should THAT be?  The CALLED program (and the CALLING program) must be COMPILED 
before the LINKER creates the EXE module, and this is only true if we are talking
about STATIC calls.  On DYNAMIC call, the sequence of COMPILE/LINK is irrelevant

     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         All generalizations are false, including this one.
```

#### ↳ Re: y2k examples

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fcdbb7.129229554@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 05:27:14 GMT, gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com (G
Moore) wrote:

>On Tue, 05 Oct 1999 20:00:52 GMT, gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com (G
>Moore) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A sliding window depends on the ability of the compiler to determine a
current 4 digit year.

We use a sliding window to determiene Date of Birth from DMV data.  We
know we won't have anyone under the age of 13 so this works.

This is not exactly the code I have in production, but it's close.

01  Base-Year Pic 9(4).
01  Current-Year Pic 9(4).
01  Work-Year Pic 9(4).

Move Function Current-date (1:4) to Current-year.
Move current-year to base-year.
Subtract 13 from base-year.
Move DOB-YY to Work-Year (3:2)
Move current-year (1:2) to work-year (1:2)

If Work-Year > Base-year
   subtract 100 from work-year
   If Work-Year > Base-Year
      subtract 100 from Base-Year
   End-if
End-IF

The two subtractions of 100 are necessary come 2000 to handle people
born in 1887-1900.

This is a sliding window - the 113 year window slides with the current
date.  It will require no future code adjustments and birth year can
continue to be stored in 2 digits.


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: y2k examples

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote in message <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>i'd like to see a
>sliding window, before and after code.

What we did was to find a point in time that matched the calendar backwards
in terms of equal day, week number and leap year. This was: 28 years ago,
ie: 1971 from today.

It is a sligthly different approach than the classic sliding window, but for
a short term solution it is good because of low source impact and
efficiency.

Considering that the problem is to be in a period where you would have dates
on both sides of a century we found that all logic, computation (to some
extent) and sorting would work painlessly if all were in the same side.

So, we changed all years fields in the data files to be current date - 28.
Like: 1999 - 28 = 1971.

The only problem with this, is if you have years that are older than 1928.
Luckily, we didn't have.

Benefit:
    - Fast implementation
    - Logic unchanged
    - Sort functions as before

Wouldn't this look stupid on screen? Like Today: 7-Oct-1971?

Yes, you are right. So what used to be:

    ACCEPT DATE-VAR

Were changed to:

    CALL "MODDATE"    USING DATE-VAR    "+"    5.
    ACCEPT DATE-VAR
    CALL "MODDATE"    USING DATE-VAR    "-"    5.

    What we do here is that we take the var DATE-VAR that holds a date:
120571, states that the year
    starts in position 5, and use the "+" to indicate that we should add.
DATE-VAR will then be:
    120599. After the ACCEPT we reverse the process.

    This applied to all "external" sources, like printer, files, system and
user input.

Yes, it is not infinite, so, one time in 2020 we will have to start figuring
out something smart :-)

Cheesle
```

```
G Moore wrote:
>Erlend Moen wrote:
>>I have to add, since I actually participated in this project, it took 5 men
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>that's 100 lines per minute.

This year I personally remediated 800,000 lines of code in about 4
months, much to the surprise of my client.  During the process, I
did quite a lot (tens of thousands of lines) of code cleanup (such
as making the coding style conform to departmental standards), and
made a few program changes at the client's request.  Believe me, I
was pedal-to-the-metal for those 4 months!  I wrote some automated
tools for discovery, but all the code remediation was done by hand.
All the code is up and running, with very few (~ 40) bugs found.  I
found and corrected well over 100 pre-existing bugs.  I estimate
that simple remediation, once the tools were written, would have
taken perhaps 55 man days.  If that estimate is accurate, then I
would have remediated about 15,000 lines/day.  As it was, over the
entire project I averaged about 7,000 lines/day, plus the product
was very clean, met standards, and with fewer bugs than when I had
received it.
```
