###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: y2k examples

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tueq3$f5h$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net> <7tk3dg$jgk$1@troll.powertech.no> <37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <4%IL3.8269$F51.94840@news3.mia> <3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com...
> On Sat, 9 Oct 1999 10:18:34 -0500, "Judson McClendon"
> <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
  <snip>
>
> i understand the theory behind sliding windows, but even with all the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The simple answer is that you can't "automatically" remove "date-windowing"
just because 1/1/00 is past.  For example - think of a state's driver license
programs - where a person MUST be 16 to get a license.  For those
applications, "00" "01" etc (as birthdates) will still remain 1900 and 1901
until (at least 2016 - or maybe 2015 - depending on the application logic).

There are really four types of date-windowing that I know of (there may be
more):

1) Those that "window" around a fixed date, for example "for today's date -
if the year in in the range 90 - 99 - it is ALWAYS  1900, otherwise it is
2000)

2) Those that have a "relative" or "sliding" window focus.  In other words,
if the two-digit year is 3 years or less before today's year, then it is
1900 - but otherwise it is 2000.

3) Those where there is some "control card" or other run-time parameter that
tells them what year to use as the switch over. (If I run the program today,
I use "99" but if I ran it last year, I would have feed it "98").

4) IBM has actually introduced "native COBOL langue" (via extensions to the
PICTURE/USAGE clauses) to identify something as a date - and they "slide"
when doing all sorts of operations (Moves, arithmetic - even SORT) depending
on a run-time (or maybe compile-time?) option.

 ***

Does this help?
```

> There are really four types of date-windowing that I know of (there may be

Then you have the method that "Cheesle" "invented" for us in our project, we
reversed the year in all date-fields by 28, so 1999 (or 99) would be
represented as 71 in all files. All computes and other logic is then
untouched, all we had to do was to add 28 to the year in front of all
displays and accepts, and subtract again right after. We had different
subprograms to do this job, as described by Cheesle earlier in this thread.
Of course, in 2027 we have to do something... :-) And this method will not
work if you have active dates before 1928.

<-emo->
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: y2k examples

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AoGM3.3$uI1.166@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net> <7tk3dg$jgk$1@troll.powertech.no> <37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <4%IL3.8269$F51.94840@news3.mia> <3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tueq3$f5h$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <7tukh6$nuk$1@troll.powertech.no>`

```
Erlend Moen wrote:
>
>Then you have the method that "Cheesle" "invented" for us in our project, we
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>work if you have active dates before 1928.

I have thought about that method for cases where people had to have a
last minute 'stop gap' solution.  But if you had the option of doing
date expansion, and used the above method to save time or money, I
expect you may have reason to regret it before 2027. :-)  There are
undoubtedly many, many places in that code where programmers will have
the opportunity to make programming errors over the next 30 years.
Every date field in every report program, and every date-based extract
program, written for those systems must have code to accommodate the
28 year offset.  Every time a programmer looks at a file dump, they
will have to remember the 28 year offset.  If your users ever need to
use a third-party reporting tool, the year offset will be in the way.
At the very least, it violates the "Rule of Least Astonishment." :-)
```

>date expansion, and used the above method to save time or money, I

I do agree with you that it is a poor man's solution, or sort of a hack.
Point is though, as stated in an earlier message, that by the year 2027
things are changed one way or another. How can I be so certain? Well, the
process are already well on the way and on the schedule. So, I doubt there
will be any regret. We chose the approach deliberately.

>Every date field in every report program, and every date-based extract
>program, written for those systems must have code to accommodate the
>28 year offset.  Every time a programmer looks at a file dump, they

You are right, they will have to remember to call the MODDATE function to
deal with it, if they do that however, as I said in the previous message, it
will all be reversible in 1-2-3.

>use a third-party reporting tool, the year offset will be in the way.

That is an issue, but it is also thought of. We do not allow direct access
to the data, but export them into an odbc compliant database, as a part of
the export the data are transfered into YYYYMMDD.

Anyway, your concerns are correct, and the rule would be that one should not
use this approach as a temporary solution unless it is a part of a wider
plan.

Cheesle

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tvicd$4mm$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net> <7tk3dg$jgk$1@troll.powertech.no> <37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <4%IL3.8269$F51.94840@news3.mia> <3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote in message <3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com>...
>i understand the theory behind sliding windows, but even with all the
>examples, it's not too clear to me when you use it. do you only use it
>when using the dates in a calculation? it's my guess that post-y2k,

I'll try to provide you an example of our implementation, although a single
variant I hope it might easen the understanding:

Pre Y2K:

    MOVE 991231 TO WS-PREV-DATE.
                                      WS-YYMMDD.
    ACCEPT WS-YYMMDD.
    IF WS-YYMMDD < WS-PREV-DATE
        GOTO OLD-DAYS...

We enter the new century, without making it Y2K compliant, the above if test
will fail for any date after 2000.01.01.

So, provided our technique, subtracting 28 years, what happens?
    MOVE 711231 TO WS-PREV-DATE    // Constants are predefined -28 years.
                                      WS-YYMMDD.
    CALL "MODDATE" USING WS-YYMMDD "+" 1. ( See 1)
    ACCEPT WS-YYMMDD.                                        (See 2)
    CALL "MODDATE" USING WS-YYMMDD "-" 1. (See 3)
    IF WS-YYMMDD < WS-PREV-DATE                    (See 4)
        GOTO OLD-DAYS...

1) Here we call a "black box" which only mission is to find a two digit date
in the passed string, the position is provided in the last parameter. Then,
if the second parameter is a plus sign, add 28 years.

2) Display the date, in contradiction to the assignment we actually do with
the MOVE command, the
user are given the year 99, not 71. Let us assume, the user types 010202
(2001-feb-03).

3) The function MODDATE are used to subtract 28 from year. Resulting in
730202, which is larger than the content of WS-PREV-DATE, hence we are not
going to OLD-DAYS. Correct interpretation.

If we had not implemented this call to MODDATE, the if test would fail,
because 010202 would be less than 991231.

I think it is necessary to mention a few things about MODDATE. Many people
would say; why not just add and subtract?

Well it isn't that easy, because the result would be unpredictable if adding
28 to a value of 99 in a 2 digit field, or subtracting 28 from 02.

So, the basic steps in MODDATE would be:

    Typecast the input to a string.
    Extract 2 digits from the string at the starting position provided as
the third parameter.
    Verify that the 2 digits received are numeric and logical according to
the requested operation.
    Alter eventual blanks to zeros.
    add 100 to the year giving a 3 digit field.
    Depending on parameter 2 being plus or minus, do the appropriate 28
computation.
    Return the new year into parameter 1.

It does some more too, but that is specialized stuff.

The obvious problem here is that we can't deal with dates prior to 1928 and
after 2027. Luckily, we don't.

Given a few political decisions which I unfortunately cannot discuss here,
the whole stuff actually is reversible as well. By replacing MODDATE with an
empty function, we may turn off the whole thing once we have all dates into
one century, for instance in 2010 and we are back where we was (well,
almost, we will have to recalculate date in the data files too).

>the windows will all have to be removed (so y2k programmers won't go

With the above approach yes. I do however not know how common the approach
is. I guess it is an approach that will not be spoken load about as it is
somewhat limiting.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: y2k examples

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<adGM3.4139$Ck.45416@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net> <7tk3dg$jgk$1@troll.powertech.no> <37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <4%IL3.8269$F51.94840@news3.mia> <3802b26b.103368@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>had more hours put in)).

I averaged about 9-10 hours a day.

>i understand the theory behind sliding windows, but even with all the
>examples, it's not too clear to me when you use it. do you only use it
>when using the dates in a calculation? it's my guess that post-y2k,
>the windows will all have to be removed (so y2k programmers won't go
>away, right?)

I think using windowing for internal processing is a mistake, if date
expansion is at all possible.  I believe sliding windows based on
today's (computer) date are acceptable for expanding input dates when
there is good reason not to expand input date fields, and when you
can reliably define a 100 year range (e.g. today's date -79 to +20)
that the input dates will always fall into.  Assuming the dates are
already stored as MMDDYY (or YYMMDD or DDMMYY), date expansion is by
far the most straightforward method in almost all cases, because it
changes no logic, except for some calculations involving year, and
there is no need for windowing.
```

G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com...
> hmm. 30 men days for 1.5 million lines of code. what's that, like
> 50,000 lines per day? an 8 hour day would make that 8000 per hour.
> that's 100 lines per minute.

Sounds impressing, doesn't it :-) ? Nevertheless it is true. But the days
where 10-hours, or even more, we booked some rooms at a hotel, far away from
the office and the wifes (!), and started to WORK. Every day ended in a fine
restaurant, with good food and drinks (not too many drinks...). This was an
intensive week, where we were treated very good at the end of every day, and
we were all so motivated to do this job. And the motivation was even higher
as we knew that our boss invited down all the ladies to the last weekend.
Everything payed by the company.

And we did a lot of analyzing before we started. We used a very good editor,
The Semware Editor (TSE), which is extremely fast and efficient, and we
could make macros for the code-lines we needed to insert or change (like the
example Cheesle mentioned above). Then we had some key-variables we searched
for, i.e. DATE, YEAR, YY and so on. So the most difficult task was to keep
concentrated for so many hours. However, we divided the day in three
sessions, just to clean our mind a bit. And to mention tha last main issue
here: We knew the code very well, as we all had developed it from the start,
using rather strict standards.

<-emo->
```

- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tiuqm$m0b$1@news.cerf.net> <7tk3dg$jgk$1@troll.powertech.no> <37fe5aaa.969975@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7ts0nl$n0g$1@troll.powertech.no>`

```
Erlend Moen wrote:
>G Moore wrote:
>> hmm. 30 men days for 1.5 million lines of code. what's that, like
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>using rather strict standards.

There is an even more important factor "How heavily and complexly were
dates used in the code?"  In my recent remediation, (none of the code
was mine, except for some COPY files) there was one day I remediated
43,800 lines in 6.5 hours, but it was because there were few, mostly
simple date references.  But at other times there were small (2-3k
lines) programs that took an hour or more, because the date handling
code was particularly complex or obscure.  I am quite confident that
there are no human beings who could correctly remediate that kind of
code at the rate of 50,000 lines/day, even 24 hour days. :-)
```

```
G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:380fc91c.5912346@nntp.ix.netcom.com...
> i think i wrote something where i forgot to finish the article. in any
> case, my question was, do you only use sliding windows where actual
> calculations are made (such as subtract mmddyyy from mmddyy)?

We converted our datafiles when we made the "sliding windows" change in the
code. We subtracted 28 from every year-field, so 1999 would be 1971 instead.
In this way we can run the code as before, without any concern on the dates.
However we had to modify the year in front of all accepts, displays and
printouts (i.e. add 28 to the year), and modify it back again afterwards.
And it was this that was the actual job, to find all places where we had to
modify these fields.

<-emo->
```

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> *mmddyy

Sorry if I may seem a bit confused here, but I'm not really sure I
understand your question...
The only reason to use the sliding windows technique is to keep the
program-logic untouched. So, if you have a program with no date-logic, just
displays and accept, there will be no reason to worry at all.
But when you have to add, subtract, compare etc. dates, then you must take
some action here.
Since our system is an accounting, order and salary system, i's obvious that
the dates are used alot in different compares and calculations, that's why
we decided to convert the data, and instead deal with the visual
input/output.

Again, I don't know if I actually managed to answer your question, feel free
to ask more if not!

<-emo->
```

Yeah, you don't have to 'read' nor 'modify' EVERY Line.  That's why we have
computers.

I have myself, done a project with 125 COBOL programs (average 25K lines
with copybooks/12 w/o)
scanned every single program / copybook, examined the listing, then went
into every program affected
(60), copybook(100+), and deleted the code referencing the file that was
being 'merged' into another
to reduce overhead (9 byte key, 2 byte code, 2 byte (3 pd) qty), key was
also in another record type
in separate file that would always exist  BEFORE this record type), put in
the code needed to refer to
the new field names, and write / rewrite / delete as needed, plus convert
all 120 (dropped 5 as no longer
needed) to COBOL II, in under 3 months at 8 - 9 hrs daily.

Without 1 (ONE) single error after moving into production.  It can be done,
just not by the 'average'
programmers!

I must have missed the beginning of this thread.  The draft of the NEXT
Standard provides functions with sliding windows.  If you happen to be
running with an IBM COBOL compiler, they have a callable service for sliding
windows.  Let me know if you need more information on either of these.

#### ↳ Re: y2k examples

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tii0m$tqg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ps$73a$1@news.cerf.net> <37fa581d.394965@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <37fd2f05.5286531@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
  gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com wrote:
 i'd like to see a
> sliding window, before and after code.

Before:
01  YYMMDD-1.
    05  YY     PIC XX.
    05  MM     PIC XX.
    05  DD     PIC XX.

01  YYMMDD-2.
    05  YY     PIC XX.
    05  MM     PIC XX.
    05  DD     PIC XX.

MOVE '991231' TO YYMMDD-1.
MOVE '000101' TO YYMMDD-2.

IF YYMMDD-2 GREATER THAN YYMMDD-1 ...
This fails because it thinks 000101 (JAN 1, 2000) is less than 991231
(DEC 31, 1999).

After:
01  CCYYMMDD-1.
    05  CC     PIC XX.
 03  YYMMDD-1.
    05  YY     PIC XX.
    05  MM     PIC XX.
    05  DD     PIC XX.

01  CCYYMMDD-2.
    05  CC    PIC XX.
  03  YYMMDD-2.
    05  YY    PIC XX.
    05  MM    PIC XX.
    05  DD    PIC XX.

(Some fields used below were not typed above to save keystrokes )

Fixed Window:
MOVE '991231' TO YYMMDD-1.
MOVE '000101' TO YYMMDD-2.

IF YY OF YYMMDD-1 GREATER THAN '79'
    MOVE '19' TO CC OF CCYYMMDD-1
ELSE
   MOVE '20' TO CC OF CCYYMMDD-1.
(Simile for YYMMDD-2 ...)
Thus DEC 31, 1999 is expressed as 19991231 and Jan 1, 2000 is expressed
as 20000101.  And the comparison below now works as we'd expect:
IF CCYYMMDD-2 GREATER THAN CCYYMMDD-1 ...

Sliding Window:
(Same fields as above)
ACCEPT MY-CURRENT-DATE FROM DATE.
COMPUTE FIVE-YEARS-AGO-TODAY = YY OF MY-CURRENT-DATE � 5.

MOVE ... etc.

IF YY OF YYMMDD-1 GREATER THAN FIVE-YEARS-AGO-TODAY
   MOVE '19' TO CC OF CCYYMMDD-1 ... Etc.

Again this sets up the proper comparison for CCYYMMDD-1 and CCYYMMDD-2.
However, instead of always making the distinction at '79', it makes it
at 5 years ago (92 in 97, 93 in 98, etc.)

I've never used a sliding window. Note that it fails in 2005 per the
way I set up the example. Note that 'GREATER THAN FIVE-YEARS-AGO-TODAY'
actually means 'more recent than 5 years ago', not 'older than 5
years'.

John





Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: old MF cobol workbench problem — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### old MF cobol workbench problem

- **From:** "gigi" <oma@mclink.it>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tidrk$eos$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
hi to all
I've an old procedure written 12 year ago with MF Vs cobol workbench  .
1.3.3 that I use only in dos systems.

Now I've tried to transfer the entire procedure under WIN95/98 and
it doesn't work.

I receive an error  -disk e:\ not found- when i execute my program.
there is someone that can help me ???

many thanks and execuse me for my english
```

#### ↳ Re: old MF cobol workbench problem

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tk3hk$5kc$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7tidrk$eos$1@news.mclink.it>`

```
    Some of the really old versions of workbench would NOT work in protected
mode.

    I am not certain about 1.3.3, but 2.1 (not as far in advnace of 1.3.3 as
you would think) would not allow access to indexed files if the pc wa in
protected mode (windows, or emm386 loaded).   Ver 1.3.3 might be even worse
off.




gigi <oma@mclink.it> wrote in message news:7tidrk$eos$1@news.mclink.it...
> hi to all
> I've an old procedure written 12 year ago with MF Vs cobol workbench  .
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: old MF cobol workbench problem

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tleue$7k6$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7tidrk$eos$1@news.mclink.it> <7tk3hk$5kc$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```

: gigi <oma@mclink.it> wrote in message news:7tidrk$eos$1@news.mclink.it...
:> hi to all
:> I've an old procedure written 12 year ago with MF Vs cobol workbench  .
:> 1.3.3 that I use only in dos systems.
:>
:> Now I've tried to transfer the entire procedure under WIN95/98 and
:> it doesn't work.
:>
:> I receive an error  -disk e:\ not found- when i execute my program.
:> there is someone that can help me ???
:>
:> many thanks and execuse me for my english

It sounds like the old DOS machine had partitioned drives to
overcome the old MS-DOS limit of 32Mbyte disks and thuis had
C:, D:, E:, .... and the drives were included in the filenames,
or possibly in a configuration file.

You now have a machine with only a C: drive and thus it can't find
E:.  Try doing some drive mapping.  If you don't have a network
use SUBST E: C:\ to map E: to C:, or similar.
```

## Thread: STDIN and CALL "SYSTEM" questions — 6 msgs

### STDIN and CALL "SYSTEM" questions

- **From:** semiel@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
-I would like to read from STDIN using ONLY ANS85 Cobol
-I need to do a CALL "SYSTEM" on WinNT without opening a DOS box at all

Thanks for your help,
Alain.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: STDIN and CALL "SYSTEM" questions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tiel8$9pb$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
semiel@my-deja.com wrote in message <7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>-I would like to read from STDIN using ONLY ANS85 Cobol

I would assume a standard ACCEPT statement uses STDIN.

>-I need to do a CALL "SYSTEM" on WinNT without opening a DOS box at all

This cannot be accomplished using standard ANS85 Cobol, but here is a
solution using Acucobol and the Windows API:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SHELLAPI.
       AUTHOR.     (c) 1999, Cheesle.
       REMARKS.
      *This program demonstrates how to perform a shell execute of a program
      *or a document that has an association set up in the windows command
      *shell.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       01 SHOW-OPTION         UNSIGNED-INT.
       01 H-ACU-WND           SIGNED-INT IS EXTERNAL.
       01 EXEC-RESULT       SIGNED-INT.

       01 ERROR-STR.
          03 FILLER           PIC X(18) VALUE "An error occured: ".
          03 EXEC-ERR         PIC S9(02).
       01 ERROR-STRING REDEFINES ERROR-STR PIC X(21).

       01 PARAMS.
          03 TASK             PIC X(30).
          03 APP              PIC X(30).
          03 APP-PARAMS       PIC X(30).
          03 DIRECTORY        PIC X(30).

       SCREEN SECTION.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
       MAIN-001.

           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.
      *We want the application minimized, and our current window to stay
active
      *we do so by setting SHOW-OPTION to 7.
    DISPLAY INITIAL          WINDOW
            TITLE-BAR        WITH SYSTEM MENU
            AUTO-MINIMIZE
            SIZE             40
            LINES     20
            TITLE            "Demo of ShellExecute".
    STRING  "TEST.TXT"       LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO             APP.
    STRING  "open"           LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE
            INTO             TASK.
    STRING  LOW-VALUES       DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO APP-PARAMS.
    STRING  LOW-VALUES       DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO DIRECTORY.
           MOVE    7                TO SHOW-OPTION.

    CALL    "SHELL32.DLL"    ON EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-900.
      *Important note: All use of NULL is to terminate strings.
    CALL    "ShellExecuteA"  USING
            BY               VALUE H-ACU-WND
      *The following command instructs the function to open the application,
      *other options are: "print", "edit", "explore", "properties"
      *You may sett this one to NULL, the default action then is"open"
            BY REFERENCE     TASK
      *The file to open
            BY REFERENCE     APP
      *If we were to open an executable, any parameters may be sent here, if
      *the file is a document, we may not send parameters, thus setting to
      *NULL.
            BY REFERENCE     APP-PARAMS
      *Here we specify the directory of the application / document
      *NULL makes use of the current directory
                   BY REFERENCE     DIRECTORY
      *Finally we decide how we would like to application to launch,
      *See below for a list of possible options here
                   BY               VALUE SHOW-OPTION
                   GIVING     EXEC-RESULT
            ON               EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-900.

           IF      EXEC-RESULT      < 33
               MOVE             EXEC-RESULT TO EXEC-ERR
                   DISPLAY          MESSAGE BOX
                                    ERROR-STRING
                                    TITLE "Error"
                                    TYPE 1
        ICON 3
    ELSE
            DISPLAY          "Done..."
            ACCEPT           OMITTED.

           CANCEL  "SHELL32.DLL".
           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 0.

       MAIN-900.
       MAIN-EXIT.
           GOBACK.

      *Possible show options:
      *SW_SHOWNORMAL       1 Launches, displays in standard mode and
activates
      *SW_SHOWMINIMIZED    2 Launches, minimizes and activates
      *SW_SHOWMAXIMIZED    3 Launches, displays maximized and activates.
      *SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE   4 Launches, displays and keep you window active.
      *SW_SHOW             5 Launches, activates and show it 'as is'.
      *SW_SHOWMINNOACTIVE  7 Launches, minimizes and keep your window
active.
      *SW_SHOWNA           8 Launches, displays, but keep your window
active.
      *SW_SHOWDEFAULT      10 Launches, activates and show window as it is
      *         programmatically set do to.

      * There are more options as well, these are however the most common
ones.

      *Error messages
      *                        0       System out of memory or resources
      *SE_ERR_FNF              2       file not found
      *SE_ERR_PNF              3       path not found
      *SE_ERR_ACCESSDENIED     5       access denied
      *SE_ERR_OOM              8       out of memory
      *SE_ERR_DLLNOTFOUND      32      Did not find the DLL (SHELL32.DLL)
      *SE_ERR_SHARE            26      Sharing violation (some other use the
      *                                file)
      *SE_ERR_ASSOCINCOMPLETE  27      Incomplete association of the
document
      *                                Control the association.
      *SE_ERR_DDETIMEOUT       28      Dynamic Data Exchange time out. Your
      *                                computer is either overloaded or your
      *                                network has a problem
      *SE_ERR_DDEFAIL          29      Could no complete DDE services
      *SE_ERR_DDEBUSY          30      Dynamic Data Exchange conquestion,
wait
      *                                and try again
      *SE_ERR_NOASSOC          31      There is no association defined for
this
      *                                file.

      * There are more errors as well, these are however the most common
ones.

Note that this example assumes that you have notepad.exe on your computer
and that TXT files are associated with notepad.exe.

Hope it helps.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: STDIN and CALL "SYSTEM" questions

- **From:** semiel@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tl5m7$q4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tiel8$9pb$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

> >-I would like to read from STDIN using ONLY ANS85 Cobol
>
> I would assume a standard ACCEPT statement uses STDIN.

As far as i know the normal ANS85 ACCEPT reads from console (Bios or
something...). It reads stuff send through the keyboard.
What im sure of, is that it doesnt read from STDIN.
No one i have asked could respond to this question.

Thanks,
Alain.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Repost: STDIN question!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tt0in$st3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tiel8$9pb$1@news.cerf.net> <7tl5m7$q4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tsof3$q1g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
semiel@my-deja.com wrote in message <7tsof3$q1g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>
>> >-I would like to read from STDIN using ONLY ANS85 Cobol
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>No one i have asked could respond to this question.

Well, that's interesting, consider the following:

MyApp <info.txt

This is typed on the console, and what happens is that MyApp will read this,
if it reads from STDIN. Hence, I may dare to say that STDIN is by default
from console.

This does however not conflict with your final statement, that the ANS85
ACCEPT does not read from STDIN, but, in that case, nor does it read from
the console.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Repost: STDIN question!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ICoM3.2354$Ck.2642@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7thktk$9rj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tiel8$9pb$1@news.cerf.net> <7tl5m7$q4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tsof3$q1g$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tt0in$st3$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
>semiel@my-deja.com wrote:
>>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>the console.

Stdin is a C concept, console is a DOS concept (CON:).  In the majority
of DOS C compilers I have seen, stdin+stdout = console.  Whether or not
a COBOL program can use stdin for simple ACCEPT statements is entirely
a function of the particular compiler, and how it does I/O.

For example, with MF COBOL 3.2, a simple:  ACCEPT data-name
does take redirected data, and echoes the data to the screen on input.
```

---

<a id="t-6aa595b6a2"></a>
## Thread: Help! Calling a C-DLL from Microfocus on NT 4.0 — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! Calling a C-DLL from Microfocus on NT 4.0

- **From:** Jan Matti Lange <jml@orimos.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCF398.90093273@orimos.com>`

```
Hi, I've got the following problem:

There is an DLL myfunction.dll written in C with VC++ 6.0 to be called
from Microfocus Cobol. The C-function to be called looks like this:

int myfunction(INPUT *in, OUTPUT *out);

where INPUT and OUTPUT are structures like that

/* input structure */
typedef struct {
	long	Number;
	int	Function;
	char	TraceFile[1024];
	int	Language;
	char  	Comment[512];
	char	TextParam1[10];
	char	TextParam2[10];
	int	Rows;
	char	Dates1[100][11];
	double	Vals1[100];
	char	Types1[100];
	char	TextParam3[4];
	double	Val1;
	double	Val2;
	char	TextParam4[11];
	double	Val3;
	char	Dates2[100][11];
	double	Vals2_1[100];
	double	Vals2_2[100];
} INPUT;


/* output (result) structure */
typedef struct {
	/* results */
	double	value;
	double	ValueArray[100];
	char	DateArray[100][11];
	/* error info */
	long		ErrorNumber;
	int		Function;
	int		Module;
	char		Message[MAX_ERROR_LEN];
}
OUTPUT;                                                                   

The function is stdcall and exported.

How to declare the structures in Cobol? Are there any alignement
requirements (in VC++6.0 I can choose structure alignement 16Byte,
8Byte, 4Byte, 2Byte, 1Byte). Do I have to order the structure
parameters  different from above. How to delare the doubles and double
arrays in Cobol?

Any comments are welcome. Thanks in advance.

Jan
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Calling a C-DLL from Microfocus on NT 4.0

- **From:** "pacman" <mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk>

I have been down this path before but using vb5 to call cobol.
It is an absolute pain, In the end I got it to work but the problem is that
if the cobol dll falls over in processing the windows environment
also crashes, therefore taking ages to debug.

If you look on the MF cobol site

www.merant.co.uk

there are example programs that you can copy that do this sort
of thing.

I little tip is to create a calling module in cobol and debug the dll
so it won't crash ( like that will never happen!)

Good luck.

phil........

#### ↳ Re: Help! Calling a C-DLL from Microfocus on NT 4.0

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QelM3.151$tZ4.3509@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37FCF398.90093273@orimos.com>`

```
Jan Matti Lange wrote in message <37FCF398.90093273@orimos.com>...
>Hi, I've got the following problem:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>where INPUT and OUTPUT are structures like that...


I am not a c programmer, so I don't have all the information you request,
but....

struct                            = Group item
double                          =  COMP-2  (assuming your COBOL uses COMP-2
for IEEE standard 32-bit float)
char  anything[number]  =   PIC X(number) or maybe PIC X(number +1)
long                              =  32 bit signed binary integer, COMP-4
(implemenation dependent)
int                                 =  EITHER 16-bit or 32-bit (depends on
the compiler and /or environment) signed binary integer, COMP-4
double[number]              =   USAGE COMP-2 OCCURS number  (or maybe OCCURS
number+1)


c stdcall for  int myfunction ( INPUT *in, OUTPUT *out)  expects two
paramerters, each a pointer to a structure; parameters pushed on the stack
right to left. The function returns an int value which, as per above, is
either 16 or 32 bits.

Note: I am still soliciting testers for a DLL a have which can convert COBOL
data types to IEEE data types. This might be a neat application for it. If
interested, send e-mail.  (The DLL is for 32-bit Windows only).

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
The views and opinions expressed herein are my own.
As I am self-employed, they also express the views of my employer.












/* input structure */
typedef struct {
long Number;
int Function;
char TraceFile[1024];
int Language;
char  Comment[512];
char TextParam1[10];
char TextParam2[10];
int Rows;
char Dates1[100][11];
double Vals1[100];
char Types1[100];
char TextParam3[4];
double Val1;
double Val2;
char TextParam4[11];
double Val3;
char Dates2[100][11];
double Vals2_1[100];
double Vals2_2[100];
} INPUT;


/* output (result) structure */
typedef struct {
/* results */
double value;
double ValueArray[100];
char DateArray[100][11];
/* error info */
long ErrorNumber;
int Function;
int Module;
char Message[MAX_ERROR_LEN];
}
OUTPUT;

The function is stdcall and exported.

How to declare the structures in Cobol? Are there any alignement
requirements (in VC++6.0 I can choose structure alignement 16Byte,
8Byte, 4Byte, 2Byte, 1Byte). Do I have to order the structure
parameters  different from above. How to delare the doubles and double
arrays in Cobol?

Any comments are welcome. Thanks in advance.

Jan
```

## Thread: Help MF COBOL on AIX problem — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help MF COBOL on AIX problem

- **From:** Dave Powers <dpowers@keller.clarke.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9910071024560.34170-100000@keller.clarke.edu>`

```

We are running Micro Focus COBOL V4.1.20 on an RS-6000 with AIX version 4.
Ever since new versions of the OS and compiler were installed about a
month ago we have been unable to use the compiler because we cannot save a
file. When we attempt to save a file from the Toolbox editor we get the
message
	File Status 9/035 opening filename.$$$
e.g.	File Status 9/035 opening PATLIST.$$$

Tech support is at a loss to find this error. We had this same problem 2
years ago but nobody remembers it or knows how to fix it.  

Any light any of you can shed on this will be greatly appreciated

TIA

Dave Powers
Assistant Professor			Office Phone (319) 588-6542
Clarke College				Home Phone   (319) 588-9316
1550 Clarke Drive			E-Mail  dpowers@keller.clarke.edu
Dubuque, IA  52001
```

#### ↳ Re: Help MF COBOL on AIX problem

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0D2E317135283C44.AE35F65C3A96AB0A.B75DAE9842620E74@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9910071024560.34170-100000@keller.clarke.edu>`

```
Dave Powers wrote:
> 
> We are running Micro Focus COBOL V4.1.20 on an RS-6000 with AIX version 4.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dubuque, IA  52001

Dave,

(from memory)
It sounds to me like you don't have write permission to the directory
containing filename.$$$.  If I recall correctly, MF will append .$$$ to
the file name if you don't have write permissions on the *file* you're
editing.  If you also don't have write permission to the *directory* it
can't write the $$$ version either.

When in doubt, always check permissions!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help MF COBOL on AIX problem

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tt8u9$n47$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<Pine.A41.4.05.9910071024560.34170-100000@keller.clarke.edu> <0D2E317135283C44.AE35F65C3A96AB0A.B75DAE9842620E74@lp.airnews.net>`

```

David W. Furin wrote in message
<0D2E317135283C44.AE35F65C3A96AB0A.B75DAE9842620E74@lp.airnews.net>...
>Dave Powers wrote:
>>
>> We are running Micro Focus COBOL V4.1.20 on an RS-6000 with AIX version
4.
>> Ever since new versions of the OS and compiler were installed about a
>> month ago we have been unable to use the compiler because we cannot save
a
>> file. When we attempt to save a file from the Toolbox editor we get the
>> message
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I had a quick look at what rts error 035 was (in the Error message manual),
it says "Attempt to access a file with incorrect permission"). So yip, I
agree looks like directory permission problem.

Regards,
Steve.

>
>--
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>LaRich Distributors, Inc.     | web:    http://www.larich.com
```

## Thread: Pi — 5 msgs

### Re: Pi

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Yi79Nx566DXqYvysC=PIdHH7bj6Q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
The venerable "Geoffrey Ketels" <geoffrey.ketels@zonien.be> bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Mon, 4 Oct 1999 18:44:42 -0700 these words:

>how do I integrate Pi in a cobol program

Pi is a constant, so the integral is zero.

If your compiler has the FUNTION PI, use it.

If your compiler doesn't have FUNCTION PI use

     77  PI  PIC 9V9(14)  VALUE 3.14159265358979.

Do not worry about using PI as a dataname.  It is not a reserved word
in the draft standard, and will be a legal dataname unless you specify
PI in the REPOSITORY paragraph.  If you do that, all you will need to
do is remove this line.

Don't use PI to mean anything other than the constant pi.
```

#### ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fd7865.187681281@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <Yi79Nx566DXqYvysC=PIdHH7bj6Q@4ax.com>`

>Pi is a constant, so the integral is zero.

<nitpick>

???  The integral of a constant is a linear function.  The differential of a constant is
zero.

INT pi dx  =  = pi * x + C

d(pi)
-------    = 0
 dx  

</nitpick>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tk02s$q0f$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7tastn$mlh$2@nickel.uunet.be> <Yi79Nx566DXqYvysC=PIdHH7bj6Q@4ax.com> <37fd7865.187681281@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37fd7865.187681281@news2.ibm.net...
> On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 16:59:17 -0700, Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
wrote:
  <snip>
> >
> >Do not worry about using PI as a dataname.  It is not a reserved word
>
> yet, because what about FUNCTION PI?  Wouldn't that make using a variable
name of PI
> illegal (cannot test it, my compiler is still asleep <g>)
>

Intrinsic Function names are not now (nor in the next Standard when PI is
included) "reserved words". HOWEVER,  the next Standard will (finally IMHO)
provide a method whereby you can "omit" the reserved word FUNCTION - when it
appears before an intrinsic (or user-defined) function name.  HOWEVER, you
can only do this for those names that are never used as user-defined words
within your program.  THEREFORE, I remain strong in my opinion that using
"PI" as a dataname is only asking for trouble in the future.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pi

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00

```

Hello all,
I was wondering about the amout of sorting fields that can be use in the JCL as
opposed to the internal sort? Is it 13 in JCL or is it 13 in the internal Cobol
sort?

thanks,
AJ
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ticss$ps8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991007080558.03971.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991007080558.03971.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>,
  ajandkris@aol.com (Ajandkris) wrote:
> Hello all,
> I was wondering about the amout of sorting fields that can be use in
the JCL as
> opposed to the internal sort? Is it 13 in JCL or is it 13 in the
internal Cobol
> sort?

The SYNCSORT (version 3.6) documentation at this shop says up to 64
SORT/MERGE FIELDS can be used. This would be for an external sort. I
tried 15 and it worked fine.
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tngst$ckb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991007080558.03971.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991007080558.03971.00000004@ng-ca1.aol.com>,
  ajandkris@aol.com (Ajandkris) wrote:
> Hello all,
> I was wondering about the amout of sorting fields that can be use in
the JCL as
> opposed to the internal sort? Is it 13 in JCL or is it 13 in the
internal Cobol
> sort?

If you use DFSORT control statements ("external sort"), the limit
depends on the type of sort fields you use.  For example, you could sort
 more than a hundred 5-byte character fields.

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
DFSORT is on the Web at URL:
http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

On Tue, 05 Oct 1999 16:39:55 GMT, Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com> wrote:

>FWIW, my MF experience is HPUnix based but the only similar
>intermittent problem I've had was related to over running a table.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>delayed and not at all indicative of a storage violation problem.

Thanks Bill. Your problem may be related (it sounds similar) The systems ARE
different. My system runs on a PC under DOS, but even with those differences it
may be a course to persue. I need some information from you:

1. What did you need to do to solve it and why did that solve it?

2. I am not sure whether the changes were to tables (which inthis case they
were not) or whther to data near he tables.

3. I have copied below the full linkage section used. Those lines with YEAR2K
were those modified. If you notices the second 01 array is close to a change,
even though the total amount of lines remained unchanged. Is there any
similarity to the changes and ones that you made?


----------------------------------------------------
       LINKAGE SECTION.

       01 SYS-PASS-VALUS.

           03 PASS-FIL-ERR-VALUS.
               05 NXT-PGM-NAM                  PIC X(78).
               05 LST-PGM-NAM                  PIC X(78).
               05 NXT-PGM-ID                   PIC X(8).
               05 LST-PGM-ID                   PIC X(8).
               05 PASS-FLG                     PIC X(2).
               05 PASS-COMP-RPT-NAM            PIC X(50).

           03 PASS-COMP-DSPLY-NAM              PIC X(30).

YEAR2K* THIS LINE BELOW CHANGED AND PLACED BELOW TO AVOID LINK ERROR
YEAR2K     03 PASS-SYS-DAT-PLACE-HOLDER        PIC X(6).

           03 PASS-MULTI-USER-FLG              PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-COMP-PASSWORD-USAGE-FLG     PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-USER-PASSWORD               PIC X(10).
           03 PASS-COMP-CHNG-LOG-USAGE-FLG     PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-USER-ID                     PIC X(3).
           03 PASS-COMP-ALLOW-PROT-CHNG-FLG    PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-COMP-MAIN-ACCT-SIZ          PIC 9(1).
           03 PASS-COMP-SUB-ACCT-SIZ           PIC 9(1).
           03 PASS-MULTI-COMP-FLG              PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-COMP-ID                     PIC X(2).
           03 PASS-COMP-IS-CONSOL-FLG          PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-USER-ID-AREA                PIC X(10).

           03 PASS-COMP-MENU.
               05 PASS-COMP-MENU-SYS           PIC X(1)
                   OCCURS 20 TIMES
                   INDEXED BY PASS-COMP-MENU-IDX.

           03 PASS-USE-LIN-25                  PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-DSPLY-MOD                   PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-SYS-ID                      PIC X(2).
           03 PASS-DRIVER-ID                   PIC X(2).
           03 PASS-MENU-TYP                    PIC 9(1).
           03 PASS-MENU-ACTIV                  PIC 9(1).
           03 PASS-TEMP-ACCESS                 PIC X(1).
           03 PASS-TEMP-ACCESS-PGM-NAM         PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-ACTIV                PIC X(5).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-TOP-PGM-NAM          PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-CURR-PGM-NAM         PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-CALLED-THRU-NAM      PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-LOAD-PGM-NAM         PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-ACCESS-PGM-TO-RUN           PIC X(8).
           03 PASS-MENU-SEL                    PIC X(1).
           03 LST-PASS-MENU-SEL                PIC X(1).

#### ↳ Re: Program Problem in MF COBOL

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tl2b7$nna$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37f8d79f.12810399@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ko$62a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37fcbbe1.9205773@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
I wish I could be more help, I discovered the reason during my reducing
the program to its simplest form while preparing to send it to MF for a
bug fix.  The abend was fairly consistant and while making it smaller
and simpler, the problem went away.  Finally I found one change that
turned on/off the abend/problem and discovered the (stupid) error.
Needles to say, I reported back to MF that it was a user error (Pride
goeth before the Fall).

After words, I experimented with the subscript ranging it from one over
table to huge over table only to discover the three general types of
handling, MF error, nothing and sporatic random strangeness, I think I
recall the break at 22 and 150 but your results may vary (grin).

If your problem is with the table handling, you might try defining the
array VERY long and address down to the 101st row with a VERY large
elementry definition.  Preload the VERY large area (the size of the
array minus the first 100 rows) with nulls or spaces or something
unique and then occasionally compare that addressed VERY large area
with its preloaded value.  A not equal will be proof, while an equal
will not be proof but it might be information.

Good luck,
Bill

Don't forget, GOD has a sense of humor, even if we don't always
understand it.....

In article <37fcbbe1.9205773@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
  Moshe@NOSPAM.chassidus.net (Moshe Shulman) wrote:

snip

> Thanks Bill. Your problem may be related (it sounds similar) The
systems ARE
> different. My system runs on a PC under DOS, but even with those
differences it
> may be a course to persue. I need some information from you:
>
> 1. What did you need to do to solve it and why did that solve it?
>
> 2. I am not sure whether the changes were to tables (which inthis
case they
> were not) or whther to data near he tables.
>
> 3. I have copied below the full linkage section used. Those lines
with YEAR2K
> were those modified. If you notices the second 01 array is close to a
change,
> even though the total amount of lines remained unchanged. Is there any
> similarity to the changes and ones that you made?
>

SNIP

```
William Lynch wrote:
>
>Another good example of Lynch's Law: "Every bug, once found, is trivial
>(stupid)"

I would agree with "Most bugs, once found, are trivial (stupid)". :-)
I have seen some very, very subtle bugs.  One bug I found was so subtle
it was extremely difficult to explain to my associates without getting
a blank stare.  Particularly when you get into multi-user systems, they
can be tough.  But those are very much the exception; most bugs are
simple blunders.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FFF427.841CF3C8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37f8d79f.12810399@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7td9ko$62a$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37fcbbe1.9205773@nntp.ix.netcom.com> <7tl2b7$nna$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37FEAE30.22D74661@att.net> <dpIL3.8203$F51.93555@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> William Lynch wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> most bugs are simple blunders.

For subtle bugs look at why that gizmo in Mars kept loosing data.  It
was to do with multi processing different priority tasks.  This caused
a periodic reboot.

The other thing about bugs is that after the event you get a question
from management.

'You could have tested for this couldn't you'

If I had worked out to test for it then I would also have removed the
bug.   This sort of comment is non productive and shallow.

Ken
```

## Thread: JCL Question -SL Label Dump - IEHLIST? — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### JCL Question -SL Label Dump - IEHLIST?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Good day!  I've just been asked by another coder if IEHLIST has the
ability to dump the label of an SL tape; my memory is, admittedly,
porous and as I'm new on the contract here I've not yet brought in my copy
of Trombetta & Finklestein.

Does anyone know of a web-site I can visit (this job allows me Internet
Access!) to confirm/deny this?  If so, might that person be so kind as to
post it?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question -SL Label Dump - IEHLIST?

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tioaj$314$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In article <Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net>,
  docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
>
> Good day!  I've just been asked by another coder if IEHLIST has the
> ability to dump the label of an SL tape; my memory is, admittedly,
> porous and as I'm new on the contract here I've not yet brought in my
copy
> of Trombetta & Finklestein.
>
> Does anyone know of a web-site I can visit (this job allows me
Internet
> Access!) to confirm/deny this?  If so, might that person be so kind
as to
> post it?

Don't know of a web site - we have a online dox feature on ISPF.
So here's an alternative: (untested)
HTH John
Sample IEBPTPCH JCL:

//JS10    EXEC PGM=IEBPTPCH,REGION=1024K
//SYSPRINT  DD SYSOUT=*                              Messages
//SYSUT1    DD DSN=...,DISP=SHR                      Input File
//SYSUT2    DD SYSOUT=*                              Output File
//SYSIN     DD *                                     Control Statements
 control statements...
/*
LABELS:
      The labels statement is used when you want to read the data set
      labels as data or data set descriptors.  Only one LABELS
      statement will used, the last, if more than one is included.
      The format of the LABELS statement is:

       {label} LABELS {CONV=(PZ | XE)}
                      {,DATA=(YES | NO | ALL ONLY)}

      CONV= defines the type conversion to be done, 'PZ' converts
      packed decimal to decimal, and XE converts alphanumeric to
      hexadecimal. If CONV is not specified, no change is made to the
      data.

      DATA= indicates how user labels will be handled and can be:

       'YES' which is the  default and indicates user labels are to
       handles as data, if they have not been rejected by a specified
       EXITS routine.


         'NO' to prevent the user labels from being processed as
data
         and must be specified for nonstandard (NSL) label data sets
to
         turn off standard user label exit
processing.


         'ALL' to indicate that all user labels will be processed
as
         data and that a return code of 16 will cause the step
to
         terminate after any remaining user labels have been
processed.


         'ONLY' to indicate that only the user header labels
are
         processed as data and job processing will terminate after
OPEN
         has completed.

>
> DD
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL Question -SL Label Dump - IEHLIST?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DD4B587EE447FB10.0BFBCAA904A1DEA3.18040A295D9A71DE@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`

```
On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 15:46:49 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () enlightened
us:

>
>Good day!  I've just been asked by another coder if IEHLIST has the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

Doc,

I don't know a web site but my OS Utilities book by Doug Lowe
indicates that you cannot use IEHLIST to dump the label of an SL tape.
It doesn't specifically say this, but it says IEHLIST is used to list
the contents of a PDS or the contents of either a VTOC or system
catalog.

If you have DITTO at your site, it has the function you need.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Get a New Car for Your Spouse. It'll Be a Great Trade.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: JCL Question -SL Label Dump - IEHLIST?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fd8161.171646381@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net> <DD4B587EE447FB10.0BFBCAA904A1DEA3.18040A295D9A71DE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Good call.  My boss sent DD the JCL we use to do this - using IEBGENR.
Dunno if I should post it or not, but here goes:

Courtesy of my boss Dwight Miller, PHD - who because of our companies
narrow minded view of the value of news groups - can READ but not
post... One doctor to another said:

Use IEBGENER!  IEHLIST does PDS/PDSE and (shudder) CVOL, not tape.

//BLPLOOK JOB (OS390),VERIFY,MSGCLASS=H,TIME=1,CLASS=9 
//NANCY PROC  TAPE=TAPE,SPLV=     
//ID EXEC PGM=IEBGENER                   
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*                     
//SYSIN DD DUMMY                                 
//SYSUT2 DD SYSOUT=*                          
//SYSUT1 DD UNIT=&TAPE,                   
// VOL=SER=&SPLV,LABEL=(,BLP),DSN=WHATS.THE.VOLSER,     
//  DCB=(BLKSIZE=8000,RECFM=U)      
//   PEND
// EXEC NANCY,SPLV=MV0570                                     
//* TO READ ROUND TAPES, ADD ",TAPE=ROUND" AFTER THE VOLSER (SPLV)
//*
FOR EXAMPLE:     EXEC NANCY,SPLV=DS7036,TAPE=ROUND  

Change JOB card as appropriate, ditto for tape esoteric names,
symbolics,
etc as dictated by local practice.  The key to it is the BLP in the
LABEL
parameter.  Only 'gotcha' is job execution class must allow
specification
of BLP (JES2 startup parameter).

--------------


On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 16:02:31 -0400, SkippyPB
<swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote:

>On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 15:46:49 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () enlightened
>us:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

>--------------

(a good solution bears repeating)

Once again, my thanks to all.

DD

- **References:** `<Jf3L3.1141$N64.33403@dfw-read.news.verio.net> <DD4B587EE447FB10.0BFBCAA904A1DEA3.18040A295D9A71DE@lp.airnews.net>`

```
On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 16:02:31 -0400 SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
wrote:

:>On Thu, 07 Oct 1999 15:46:49 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () enlightened
:>us:

:>>Good day!  I've just been asked by another coder if IEHLIST has the
:>>ability to dump the label of an SL tape; my memory is, admittedly,
:>>porous and as I'm new on the contract here I've not yet brought in my copy
:>>of Trombetta & Finklestein.

   [ snipped ]

:>I don't know a web site but my OS Utilities book by Doug Lowe
:>indicates that you cannot use IEHLIST to dump the label of an SL tape.
:>It doesn't specifically say this, but it says IEHLIST is used to list
:>the contents of a PDS or the contents of either a VTOC or system
:>catalog.

:>If you have DITTO at your site, it has the function you need.

If you can't get to DITTO you can always use IEBGENER:

        //  EXEC  PGM=IEBGENER
        //SYSPRINT DD DUMMY
        //SYSUT2   DD SYSOUT=whatever
        //SYSUT1   DD DSN=tape.dsn,UNIT=tapeunit,VOL=SER=tapevol,
        //           DISP=OLD,LABEL=(1,BLP)
        //SYSIN    DD DUMMY,DCB=(BLKSIZE=80)
```

John

#### ↳ Re: Cut/Paste in ISPF

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991007151144.22841.00000056@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7tikll$3n$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
john_mindock writes...



>At my last two code-bustin' stops, the ISPF option 2 permitted a poor
>typist to CC/CC some lines from PDS member 1, then type 'CUT' on top,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Help appreciated in advance.

First, I always taught my students the proper way was to do a block copy
(cc...cc) followed by a "create temp" command in the source file, then get into
the target file, place an 'a' or 'b' (after or before) in the line number then
enter "move temp". This copied in the created lines and then deleted the 'temp'
member.

I did this because not every shop installed the CUT and PASTE execs.

Many emulators support cut and paste using the mouse.

Now, in the new version of ISPF (available this month) CUT and PASTE are now
standard primary commands. For shops that used the CUT and PASTE execs, you
might watch out for command conflicts when you install the new ISPF.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rvsrckthh1s71@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<7tikll$3n$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <19991007151144.22841.00000056@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
FWIW, with PC/3270, I modified the keyboard layout to specify ctrl-C &
ctrl-V as COPY & PASTE.
```

### wait needed for mvs cobol II batch program

- **From:** Stefan Raabe <stefan.raabe@dresdner-bank.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FC5399.9B258862@dresdner-bank.com>`

```
hi,

has anyone a wait routine (either an assembler subroutine) or something
similiar for an mvs cobol ii batch program? just want to wait a specific

ymount of time...

regards

stefan
```

#### ↳ Re: wait needed for mvs cobol II batch program

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ti3ua$eiq@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<37FC5399.9B258862@dresdner-bank.com>`

```
Stefan Raabe wrote in message +ADw-37FC5399.9B258862+AEA-dresdner-bank.com+AD4-...
+AD4-hi,
+AD4-
+AD4-has anyone a wait routine (either an assembler subroutine) or something
+AD4-similiar for an mvs cobol ii batch program? just want to wait a specific
+AD4-
+AD4-ymount of time...
+AD4-
+AD4-regards
+AD4-
+AD4-stefan
+AD4-
+AD4-
Here's one I wrote several years ago.

G1F024   CSECT
+ACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAq-
+ACo-
+ACo-              AUTHOR: AL RUSSELL 8/14/90
+ACo-              PROGRAM TO WAIT FOR A CERTAIN NUMBER OF MINUTES
+ACo-
+ACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAq-
         SETUP SVAREA+AD0-SAVEREGS
         ST    R13,SAVE13
         USING SAVEREGS,R13
+ACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAq-
+ACo-
+ACo-              R1  POINTER TO PARM
+ACo-              R2  LENGTH OF PARM
+ACo-              R3  PARM
+ACo-              R4  WAIT TIME
+ACo-              R5  WORK FIELD
+ACo-
+ACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAqACoAKgAq-
         L     R1,0(,R1)               POINT TO PARM
         LH    R2,0(,R1)               GET PARM LENGTH
         LA    R3,2(,R1)               GET PARM DATA
         SR    R4,R4                   CLEAR WAIT TIME
         AH    R4,+AD0-H'1'                ASSUME 1 MINUTE
GETPARM  EQU   +ACo-
         CH    R2,+AD0-H'1'                SEE IF PARM NOT THERE
         BL    WAITLOOP                IF NOT ASSUME 1 MINUTE
         IC    R5,0(,R3)               GET PARM LENGTH
         N     R5,+AD0-X'0000000F'         CONVERT TO HEX
         LR    R4,R5                   MOVE TO LENGTH REGISTER
         CH    R2,+AD0-H'2'                SEE IF TWO BYTES LONG
         BL    WAITLOOP                DONE IF NOT
         IC    R5,1(,R3)               GET PARM BYTE 2
         N     R5,+AD0-X'0000000F'         CONVERT TO HEX
         MH    R4,+AD0-H'10'               SHIFT FIRST BYTE
         AR    R4,R5                   ADD TWO BYTES TOGETHER
WAITLOOP EQU   +ACo-
         STIMER WAIT,BINTVL+AD0-MINUTE  WAIT FOR ONE MINUTE
         BCT   R4,WAITLOOP
+ACo-
RETURN   GOBACK RC+AD0-0                   RETURN
+ACo-
SAVE13   DS    F
SAVEREGS DS    18F
MINUTE   DC    F'6000'     ONE MINUTE IN .01 SECONDS
         END

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah+AEA-apci.com
Home - AHRussell+AEA-computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

#### ↳ Re: wait needed for mvs cobol II batch program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ticfk$pn9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37FC5399.9B258862@dresdner-bank.com>`

```
IBM supplies one.  It is called ILBOWAT (but I think you need to call it by
the alias ILBOWAT0).  It requires that the parameter passed to it be "below
the line" and a full-word binary (I think - we had a discussion about this
quite a while ago in C.L.C.)  This routine should be in your "standard" IBM
COBOL run-time library.  (Try it with a small test program first, I think the
parameter tells you the "absolute" number of clock seconds to "wait" - but I
won't swear to that either)
```

#### ↳ Re: wait needed for mvs cobol II batch program

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tk7ms$59j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37FC5399.9B258862@dresdner-bank.com>`

```
In article <37FC5399.9B258862@dresdner-bank.com>,
  stefan.raabe@dresdner-bank.com wrote:
> hi,
>
> has anyone a wait routine (either an assembler subroutine) or
> something similiar for an mvs cobol ii batch program?
>

Hi Stefan,

I wrote a routine in COBOL, based on Bill Klein's knowledge. Works with
all COBOL releases from OS/VS COBOL until the current COBOL for OS/390.
Here's the code (consider DATA(24) because ILBOWAT0 is AMODE(24)!):

       CBL DATA(24)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. WAIT.
      ***
      *** DELAY IN COBOL VIA CALL TO ILBOWAT0
      ***
      *** USAGE:
      *** //S1 EXEC PGM=WAIT,PARM=NNNN
      *** NNNN IS A NUMBER IN THE RANGE OF 0001 TO 9999 SECONDS
      ***
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  DELAY-AMT               PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  ILBOWAT0                PIC X(8) VALUE 'ILBOWAT0'.
       01  CURRENT-TIME            PIC 9(8).
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  PARAMETER-TO-MAIN.
         05 PARM-LENGTH PIC S9(4) COMP.
         05 PARM-TEXT   PIC X(100).
         05 PARM-REDEF REDEFINES PARM-TEXT.
          10 PARM-NUMERIC PIC 9999.
          10 FILLER       PIC X(94).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARAMETER-TO-MAIN.
       MAIN SECTION.
           MOVE PARM-NUMERIC TO DELAY-AMT
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY 'TIME OF DAY BEFORE ILBOWAT0: ', CURRENT-TIME
      *
           CALL ILBOWAT0 USING DELAY-AMT
      *
           ACCEPT CURRENT-TIME FROM TIME
           DISPLAY 'TIME OF DAY AFTER ILBOWAT0 : ', CURRENT-TIME
           GOBACK.

If you have installed a current version of OS/390 and your USS (unix) is
active, you can use the following piece of JCL (consider the
lowercase-character!):

//SLEEP EXEC PGM=BPXBATCH,PARM='SH sleep 30s'
//STDOUT DD PATH='/dev/null'
//STDIN DD PATH='/dev/null'
//STDERR DD PATH='/dev/null'

I saw this JCL in the newsgroup (mailing list) 'bit.listserv.ibm-main'.
Works in my shop. Seems to be an orinary unix-command, called from
batch.

Cheers

Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at:
http://www.winterthur.com


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-625cc595d7"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and CGI-BIN — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Re: COBOL and CGI-BIN

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FCF505.C876B744@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<37F9F54A.6A943B50@eii.fr> <37FA5A0E.B24E71AB@IMN.nl> <37FB160B.8B20195D@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote:
> 
> > MicroFocus / CGI:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> product and I can not see what it is useful for ?)
What is "normal", one could ask. I'll interpret "normal" as "a compiler". NE
(NetExpress) provides:
OO-COBOL, like what's coming in the next standard
Direct programming of CGI programs in COBOL
Full access to all Windows API's
Interlanguage calling/called, f.e. making DLL's callable from others like
C(++)/Pascal
OO-GUI-tree re-usable classes/objects
IDE: editor, windows resource editor (bitmap, cursor, icon a.s.o)
HTML-editor, which generates COBOL datastructures for use in CGI
> 
> 2/ We use object COBOL programs (running with rts32) instead of exe or dll
> programs. Can a COBOL cgi program be an object program ?

No of course not. A CGI-program is activated by a plain call from the
web-server-software, so the CGI has to be procedural. But you could program:
WORKING-STORAGE
01  anObj  usage object-reference.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
Main.
    Invoke "SomeClass" "new"
          [using ...]
           returning anObj

and from there on go full-blown OO, like

    Invoke anObj "startMethod"

SomeClass and all others could be instantiated and sent messages, including
objects from the (rich) provided class tree of predefined classes.

> 
> -> at the moment we have written c-shells scripts that decode the query strings
> into single parameters then run the object COBOL program with a "cobrun progname
> parameters" string.
Not needed with NE. It gets the fields of the defined HTML-forms directly using
an COBOL accept statement. It write HTML with an "Exec HTML ... end-exec
construct, just like embedded SQL.
> 
> -> that works correctly but do you think it is possible not to use the c-shell
> scripts (for maintenance raisons we would prefer not to use them...).
Definitly. I did write a complete web-app, running under IIS without using any
scripts. Just wrote some HTML, including some JavaScript for client-side things
like hyperlink-animation, and all COBOL.
> 
> thanks
Your welcome
> 
> Christophe
COBOL Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and CGI-BIN

- **From:** deskware@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tl6t3$qup$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37F9F54A.6A943B50@eii.fr> <37FA5A0E.B24E71AB@IMN.nl> <37FB160B.8B20195D@eii.fr> <37FCF505.C876B744@IMN.nl>`

```
With CobolScript, the POST-method string is auto-decrypted for you with
a special command called ACCEPT DATA FROM WEBPAGE.  This command places
the values of all incoming CGI variables into like-named variables in
your CobolScript program.

CobolScript is also pretty cheap, at $49.95 per license.  Check it out
at http://www.cobolscript.com .


In article <37FCF505.C876B744@IMN.nl>,
  The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote:
> webmaster wrote:

NetExpress for
> > > that. Or you have already written the CGI's in some other
language? Then invoke
> > > a COBOL DLL, also made with NetExpress.
> >

very expensive

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 2/ We use object COBOL programs (running with rts32) instead of exe
or dll

>
> No of course not. A CGI-program is activated by a plain call from the

program:
> WORKING-STORAGE
> 01  anObj  usage object-reference.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

including

query strings

"cobrun progname
> > parameters" string.
> Not needed with NE. It gets the fields of the defined HTML-forms
directly using
> an COBOL accept statement. It write HTML with an "Exec HTML ...
end-exec
> construct, just like embedded SQL.
> >
> > -> that works correctly but do you think it is possible not to use
the c-shell
> > scripts (for maintenance raisons we would prefer not to use
them...).
> Definitly. I did write a complete web-app, running under IIS without
using any
> scripts. Just wrote some HTML, including some JavaScript for
client-side things
> like hyperlink-animation, and all COBOL.
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-c74b19f12d"></a>
## Thread: looking for y2k cobol joke — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Re: looking for y2k cobol joke

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19DEC050547765C9.33BDC8DDC388A71A.48C46E8868AEE17C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<eXNkpk4D$GA.355@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
On Tue, 5 Oct 1999 17:31:41 -0700, "phillips"
<phillips001@email.msn.com> enlightened us:

>Remember that story about the Cobol programmer that put himself in cyrogenic
>sleep this year, but because of a y2k bug, he didn't wake up until 9999? I'm
>looking for the text. Thanks.
>

Someone already posted that for you, but here is a couple of others:

 Warning!!!!!

  Please take time out of your busy lives to check
  your toilet paper stockpile. Make sure it's Y2K
  compliant!!!  Word has it, if it isn't, come Jan 1, 2000, it
  will roll back to 1900, then turn into a Sears Catalog!!!!!

  YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED!! 

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Twas The Night Before Y2K
  
    Twas the night before Y2K and all through the nation,
    We awaited The Bug....the Millennium sensation.
    The chips were replaced in computers with care,
    in hopes that ol' Bugsy wouldn't stop there.
    While some folks could sleep, they were snug in their beds.
    Others had visions of dread in their heads.
    Ma with her PC and I with my Mac,
    had logged on the Net and kicked back with a snack.
    When over the server there arose such a clatter,
    I called Mister Gates to see what was the matter.
    He was not there, so I flew like a flash,
    off to my bank to withdraw all my cash.
    Then what with my wandering eyes did I see?
    My good old Mac looked real sick to me.
    The hack of all hackers was looking so smug,
    I knew that it must be the Y2K Bug!
    His image downloaded in no time at all.
    He whistled and shouted, "Let all systems fall!
    All the controls, that planes need for their flights,
    all microwaves, trains and all traffic lights.
    Go Intel!  Go Gateway!  Now HP!  Big Blue!
    Everything Compaq and Pentium too!
    All processors big, all processors small...
    Crash away!  Crash away!  Crash away all!"
    As I drew in my breath and was turning around,
    out through the modem, he came with a bound.
    He was covered with fur, and slung on his back
    was a sackful of virus, set for attack.
    His eyes - how they twinkled! His dimples - how merry!
    As midnight approached, things soon became scary.
    He had a broad face and a little round belly.
    His sack filled with virus quivered like jelly.
    He was chubby and plump, perpetually grinning.
    I laughed when I saw him, though my hard drive stopped spinning.
    A wink of his eye, and a twist of his head,
    soon gave me to know a new feeling of dread.
    He spoke not a word, but went straight to his work.
    He changed all the clocks, then turned with a jerk.
    With a twitch of his nose and a quick little wink,
    all things electronic soon went on the blink.
    He zoomed from my system, to the next folks on line.
    He caused such a disruption, could this be a sign?
    I heard him exclaim with a loud, hearty scream,
    "Happy Y2K to all!  This is only a bad dream!"

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Get a New Car for Your Spouse. It'll Be a Great Trade.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```
