<a id="t-28e5363a2f"></a>
## Thread: Wanted: Cobol program to write calendar — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: Wanted: Cobol program to write calendar

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fcfd46.156165564@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7tioqr$ju$2@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
On Mon, 4 Oct 1999 22:42:22 +0200, "Evy De Block" <Evy.De.Block@village.uunet.be> wrote:

>I have to write a cobol calendar for an assignment.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
       CBL  NOTERMINAL
       CBL  NOSEQUENCE
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  CALENDAR.
      *this program prints a calendar for a given year
      *or the current year (range 1951 to 2050)
      *
      *to run type CALENDAR and on prompt press return for this year,
      *or a 2-digit (year) value
      *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT FS-CALENDAR
                  ASSIGN "CALENDAR.RPT"
                  ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL
      *this MODE makes the output easier to read on-screen
                  MODE IS NOCC.
      *
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  FS-CALENDAR
           REPORT IS RS-CALENDAR.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  WS-GENERAL.
           05  WS-TODAY.
             07  WS-YEAR-XX.
               09  WS-YEAR         PIC 99.
             07  FILLER            PIC X(4).
           05  WS-START-DAY        PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  WS-MONTH            PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  WS-DAY-COUNT        PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  WS-LEAP             PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  WS-TEMP             PIC S9(9) COMP SYNC.
           05  WS-MONTH-NAME-TAB.
             07  FILLER            PIC X(54) VALUE
           ' January  February   March    April      May     June '.
             07  FILLER            PIC X(54) VALUE
           '   July    August September October  November December'.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-MONTH-NAME-TAB.
             07  WS-MONTH-NAME     PIC X(9) OCCURS 12.
           05  WS-MONTH-DAYS-TAB   VALUE '312831303130313130313031'.
             07  WS-DAYS-IN-MONTH  PIC 99 OCCURS 12.
           05  WS-DAY-TABLE.
             07  WS-DAY-NUM        PIC 99 COMP  OCCURS 42.
       REPORT SECTION.
       RD  RS-CALENDAR   PAGE LIMIT 42   FIRST DE 7.
      *this is the page heading:
       01  TYPE PH  LINE 2.
           05  COL 50   'Calendar for'.
           05  COL + 2  '19'    WHEN WS-YEAR > 50
                        '20'    WHEN OTHER.
           05  COL 65   PIC 99  WS-YEAR.
      *this prints any single month;
      *the REPEATED clause puts them side-by-side "4-up"
       01  RS-MONTH-BLOCK  TYPE DE     REPEATED 4 TIMES EVERY 30 COLS.
         03  LINE + 4   COL 6   PIC X(10)  WS-MONTH-NAME (WS-MONTH).
         03  LINE + 2           '  Su M  T  W  Th F  S '.
      *the OCCURS clause defines an array of lines or columns:
         03  LINE   OCCURS 6    VARYING RS-SUNDAY FROM 1 BY 7.
           05  COL 2    PIC Z9  OCCURS 7 STEP 3
      *SOURCE and VARYING place different contents in each item
                    VARYING RS-DAYNO FROM RS-SUNDAY BY 1
                    SOURCE WS-DAY-NUM (RS-DAYNO)  BLANK WHEN ZERO.
      *this is the page footing
       01  TYPE PF  LINE.
           05  COL 35  'Produced using COBOL Report Writer on'.
      *there are a number of functions - note the variable PICTURE here:
           05  COL + 2  PIC <X(9)>              FUNC DAY.
           05  COL + 2  PIC <X(9)>B<99>B9(4)    FUNC MDATE.
      *
      *%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
       CALENDAR-BEGIN.
           DISPLAY 'Input required year or return for this year: '
               WITH NO ADVANCING
           MOVE SPACES TO WS-TODAY
           ACCEPT WS-YEAR-XX
      *if blank or bad, assume today's year
           IF   WS-YEAR NOT NUMERIC
                ACCEPT WS-TODAY FROM DATE.
      *calculation uses a well-known congruence:
           DIVIDE 4 INTO WS-YEAR GIVING WS-TEMP REMAINDER WS-LEAP
           COMPUTE WS-TEMP = (1461 * WS-YEAR) / 4
           IF   WS-LEAP = 0
                MOVE 29 TO WS-DAYS-IN-MONTH (2)
           ELSE ADD 1 TO WS-TEMP.
      *necessary since we arbitrarily assume 00-50 is century 2000:
           IF   WS-YEAR < 51
                ADD 6 TO WS-TEMP.
           DIVIDE 7 INTO WS-TEMP GIVING WS-TEMP REMAINDER WS-START-DAY
      *now print the calendar!
           OPEN EXTEND FS-CALENDAR
           INITIATE RS-CALENDAR
           PERFORM VARYING WS-MONTH FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL WS-MONTH > 12
                INITIALIZE WS-DAY-TABLE
                PERFORM VARYING WS-DAY-COUNT FROM 1 BY 1
                  UNTIL WS-DAY-COUNT > WS-DAYS-IN-MONTH (WS-MONTH)
                     ADD 1 TO WS-START-DAY
                     MOVE WS-DAY-COUNT TO WS-DAY-NUM (WS-START-DAY)
                END-PERFORM
      *the GENERATE statement produces one entire month's data:
                GENERATE RS-MONTH-BLOCK
                DIVIDE 7 INTO WS-START-DAY
                  GIVING WS-TEMP REMAINDER WS-START-DAY
           END-PERFORM
           TERMINATE RS-CALENDAR
           CLOSE FS-CALENDAR
           DISPLAY "Calendar printed! - please see CALENDAR.PRT"
           STOP RUN.
      *37 data entries;  27 procedural "verbs"
      *END%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Mathematicians take it to the limit.
```

## Thread: COBOL on os/390 OE websphere — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL on os/390 OE websphere

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tj7ja$sq1@journal.concentric.net>`

```
I am trying without much luck to use COBOL as a CGI program on the
OS/390 web server. The CGI functions actually work but I cannot
process any files. Someone told me to use BPXWDYN to allocate the
files. This works okay. But COBOL will not open the file when it's run
from the web server. If I execute the program from ISHELL it allocates
and opens, reads, closes fine. When the exact same load mod is run
from in the web server the open fails with the wonderful COBOL file
status 90 which means "No further information". Not much help.  The
file is just a standard sequential QSAM file. We want to use VSAM as
well if it will work.  Does anyone have any idea what the deal is with
this?

Thanks.
```

## Thread: Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000): Call for Papers — 1 msgs

### Testing Computer Software Conference (TCS2000): Call for Papers

- **From:** uspdi@earthlink.net (USPDI)
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.c++,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.forth
- **Message-ID:** `<uspdi-0610992006190001@ip89.washington13.dc.pub-ip.psi.net>`

```
         
                  CALL FOR PAPERS and PRESENTATIONS
                               for the

           17th International Conference and Exposition on

                TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000)

         Theme: Testing Technology vs. Testers' Requirements
 
              June 12-16, 2000 * Washington, D.C. * USA
       (Conference: June 14-16; Workshops/Tutorials: June 12-13)

In cooperation with:

   ASSOCIATION FOR COMPUTING MACHINERY (ACM) SIGSoft
   AMERICAN SOCIETY FOR QUALITY (ASQ) Software Division
   IEEE RELIABILITY SOCIETY
   SOFTWARE TECHNOLOGY SUPPORT CENTER (STSC)

            PLAN NOW TO ATTEND ON JUNE 12-16, 2000!

  We are soliciting 45 to 90 minute presentations on software testing!

Participate in the leading international conference on software testing.
Learn how testing professionals and experts are using advanced methods and
testing applications to improve the effectiveness of their testing, reduce
testing costs and meet schedule deadlines. Share your own experiences in
testing with others. Learn: 
     
* The impact of increasingly complex software on testing
* Ways to improve the testing process     
* Practical experience with the various techniques     
* Which efforts have been most successful     
* Which efforts have been least successful
     
While we encourage  presentations that directly relate to the theme, other
topics relevant to improving the testing process are encouraged and will
be accepted.

CONFERENCE FEATURES:
* International speakers
* Presentation of current and emerging best testing practices
* Presentations based on the experiences of actual testing practitioners     
* Opportunities to share experiences and lessons with your peers in
  other organizations     
* Vendor show of products and services

* PLANNED TRACKS:
  * Management
  * Technical
  * Automation
  * Advanced Topics (Refereed papers)
  * Case Studies

* PRE-CONFERENCE WORKSHOPS/TUTORIALS:
Two-day and one-day technical sessions for software testing practitioners.

* VENDOR SHOW:
Providers of test support services and software will display and explain
their products.

* SUBMISSION INFORMATION:
     
Proposals for presentations should be 1 to 2 pages long, with enough
detail to give an understanding of the presentation, including an outline
of its contents. Indicate if the most likely audience is technical or
managerial, and please make clear the topic's relevance to software
testing.
           
The attached contribution cover sheet is to be completed and returned with
your submission.
           
Submissions can also be made through the Conference website at:
http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/
             
                PRESENTATION PROPOSALS ARE DUE:

                      October 15, 1999

We are interested in hearing from you. If you want to submit a proposal
for a presentation, or explore an idea for a presentation, please contact
the Program Chair:
     
                      G. Houston-Ludlam
                  Frontier Technologies, Inc.
             175 Admiral Cochran Drive, Suite 103
                     Annapolis, MD 21401
                     Call: 410-266-8244
                 E-mail: ginger@fron-tech.com
      (preferred submission method, ascii text only please)

GUIDELINES FOR REFEREED PAPERS:
If you believe that your proposal belongs to the Advanced Topics track,
please check out the website:
         http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/tcs2000callref.html

IMPORTANT DATES:

Presentation Proposals: October 15, 1999
   Notice of Selection: December 15, 2000
    Materials Deadline: March 15, 2000
            Conference: June 12-16, 2000

If you are interested in attending the TCS2000 Conference, contact USPDI.

                  Conference Management by:

          U.S. PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT INSTITUTE
             612 Ethan Allen Avenue, Suite 100
                  Takoma Park, MD 20912
                   Phone: 301-270-1033
                    Fax: 301-270-1040
                 E-mail: admin@uspdi.org
          Web: http://www.uspdi.org/conference

   + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + 

            TESTING TECHNOLOGY VS. TESTERS' REQUIREMENTS

    Technology for testing and automated solutions have grown remarkably
in the past decade. But the question is still before us -- do we have the
support from technology that we need?  Has technology solved the right
problems? Is the technology accessible enough to today's tester? What are
the pitfalls of using such technology? This year, we will be looking at
the experiences with various aspects of testing technology � some
successful, and some not.
        
    We look forward to having you share your experiences and learn from
others. I look forward to seeing you there.

                            Genevieve Houston-Ludlam, Program
Chair                                                    

   + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + + +

========================================================================

          17th International Conference and Exposition on
 
                TESTING COMPUTER SOFTWARE (TCS2000)

            June 12-16, 2000 * Washington, D.C. * USA
                   Hyatt Regency (Bethesda)

                   CONTRIBUTION COVER SHEET
      
          * Note: This form is available on the Web: *
               http://www.uspdi.org/tcs2000call/

Bullets for Conference Program:

<a id="t-c85d5f5b87"></a>
## Thread: Deskware, Inc. releases CobolScript — 1 msgs

### Deskware, Inc. releases CobolScript

- **From:** cobolscript@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7th96v$1aa$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Deskware, Inc. is pleased to announce the release of CobolScript 1.10.
CobolScript is a COBOL-like interpreted programming language with
specialized syntax for file processing, CGI programming, and
internetworking.  CobolScript also has a wide range of advanced math and
business functions available to facilitate quick and easy calculating.
CobolScript is available for Microsoft Windows, Linux, FreeBSD, and
SunOS.

CobolScript is available for immediate purchase from the Deskware web
site ( http://www.deskware.com ) for $49.95.  This price includes a 200
page manual in Adobe Acrobat format, example programs, and a year of
on-line support and product updates.  Sample manual pages and sample
applications written in CobolScript are accessible from the web site, as
well as a detailed FAQ page.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-80bed75ba7"></a>
## Thread: Indexed By and Search in 2 Occurs levels? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Indexed By and Search in 2 Occurs levels?

- **From:** "M. Lenz" <Mogens.Lentz@sas.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tht28$9lq$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
I want to change  the field UCZIPRIO-AREA to Occurs 2.
If I do that, the compiler says something about indexed by must be in level
1 too.
The program that does the search on the table, will in the future get a
field containing either the value 1 or 2. The search should only search the
level which this field tells it to.
How do I accomplish this?

The table definition today:

03  UCZIPRIO-AREA.
  05  PRIO-MAX              PIC 999 VALUE 300.
  05  PRIO-TABLE.
    07  PRIO-TAB  OCCURS 300 TIMES
                  DESCENDING KEY IS PRIO-LINENO
                  INDEXED BY PRIO-IX.
      10  PRIO-LINENO       PIC S9(7)  COMP-3.
      10  PRIO-PRIORITY     PIC 99.

The search statement used today:

SEARCH ALL PRIO-TAB
AT END
  MOVE PRIO-NOT-FOUND TO SUB-STATUS
WHEN PRIO-LINENO (PRIO-IX) = IRS-LINENO
  MOVE PRIO-FOUND TO  SUB-STATUS

  MOVE PRIO-PRIORITY (PRIO-IX) TO IRS-PRIORITY
END-SEARCH
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed By and Search in 2 Occurs levels?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<751L3.51$7p6.3304@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7tht28$9lq$1@news.dknet.dk>`

```
M. Lenz wrote in message <7tht28$9lq$1@news.dknet.dk>...
>I want to change  the field UCZIPRIO-AREA to Occurs 2.
>If I do that, the compiler says something about indexed by must be in level
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>


If UZCI-PRIOAREA OCCURS 2,  the new search should work fine if you change
all the subordinate references (e.g., PRIO-LINE-NO and PRIO-PRIORITY) to add
the second level of indexing; ie. each of these requires two indices.

SEARCH ALL PRIO-TAB( First-index)
 ...
   WHEN PRIO-LINENO(First-index prio-ix)....
...
END-SEARCH.

(Not tested, but I've done this before without problems)

MCM
```

<a id="t-5648d54bf4"></a>
## Thread: y2k examples — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Re: y2k examples

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tihsg$tp6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <37f97815.87440@nntp.ix.netcom.com>,
  gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com wrote:
> anyone know where to find y2k examples of cobol code using the data
> expansion method, as opposed to the file expansion method?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. Y2KCONV.
      *AUTHOR. SHINE.

      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * CONVERT A SIX DIGIT DATE WITH A FORMAT OF 'YYMMDD' TO AN
      * EIGHT DIGIT DATE FORMATED 'CCYYMMDD' USING A SLIDING WINDOW
      * TECHNIQUE AND A COMPUTED PIVOT.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * THE FIELD PIVOT-NUM-YEARS CAN BE POSITIVE (+) OR NEGATIVE (-).
      * IF THE PIVOT IS NEGATIVE, THE BEGINNING CENTURY OF THE WINDOW
      * WILL BE THE PREVIOUS CENTURY AND THE END CENTURY WILL BE THE
      * CURRENT CENTURY. IF THE PIVOT IS POSITIVE, THE BEGINNING
      * CENTURY OF THE WINDOW WILL BE THE CURRENT CENTURY AND THE END
      * CENTURY WILL BE THE NEXT CENTURY.
      *
      * FOR EXAMPLE USE -18 TO DERIVE THE CENTURY FOR A CURRENT OR
      * PREVIOUS EMPLOYEE WHERE AGE REQUIRE FOR EMPLOYMENT IS 18.
      *
      * MOST USES WILL SPLIT THE CENTURY AND USE +50. IF THE CURRENT
      * DATE IS 1998 THEN YEARS 48 THRU 99 WILL BE 19TH CENTURY, YEARS
      * 00 THRU 47 WILL BE 20TH CENTURY.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------

       01  PIVOT-NUM-YEARS    PIC S9(02) VALUE +50.

       01  PIVOT-DATE VALUE ZEROES.
           05 PIVOT-CCYY-GRP.
              10  PIVOT-CC    PIC 9(02).
              10  PIVOT-YY    PIC 9(02).
           05  PIVOT-CCYY REDEFINES PIVOT-CCYY-GRP PIC 9(4).
           05  PIVOT-MM       PIC 9(02).
           05  PIVOT-DD       PIC 9(02).

       01  WRK-DATE           PIC 9(08).
       01  WRK-DATEX REDEFINES WRK-DATE.
           05  WRK-CC         PIC 9(02).
           05  WRK-YY         PIC 9(02).
           05  WRK-MM         PIC 9(02).
           05  WRK-DD         PIC 9(02).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES WRK-DATE.
           05  WRK-CCYY       PIC 9(04).
           05  FILLER         PIC 9(04).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES WRK-DATE.
           05  FILLER         PIC 9(02).
           05  WRK-YYMMDD     PIC 9(06).

       01  CUR-DATE           PIC 9(08).
       01  CUR-DATEX REDEFINES CUR-DATE.
           05  CUR-CC         PIC 9(02).
           05  CUR-YY         PIC 9(02).
           05  CUR-MM         PIC 9(02).
           05  CUR-DD         PIC 9(02).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES CUR-DATE.
           05  CUR-CCYY       PIC 9(04).
           05  FILLER         PIC 9(04).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES CUR-DATE.
           05  FILLER         PIC 9(02).
           05  CUR-YYMMDD     PIC 9(06).

       01  DOB-DATE VALUE ZEROES.
           05  DOB-YY         PIC 9(02).
           05  DOB-MM         PIC 9(02).
           05  DOB-DD         PIC 9(02).

       01  AGE                PIC S9(03) VALUE 0.
       01  E-AGE              PIC ZZ9-.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * SET CURRENT DATE USING A FIXED WINDOW
      * This is demo code only. I use MVS using COBOL II and quite
      * frankly don't know how to get a date from the OS that has a
      * century. We use a date from a date card in all our aps anyway.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
           ACCEPT CUR-YYMMDD FROM DATE.
           IF CUR-YY > 50
               MOVE 19 TO CUR-CC
           ELSE
               MOVE 20 TO CUR-CC
           END-IF.

           DISPLAY 'CUR DATE= ' CUR-DATE

      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * DERIVE CENTURY USING A VARIABLE WINDOW
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------

           MOVE -18 TO PIVOT-NUM-YEARS.
           PERFORM SET-PIVOT-DATE.

           MOVE '990717' TO DOB-DATE.
           MOVE DOB-DATE TO WRK-YYMMDD.
           PERFORM DERIVE-CENTURY.
                                     PERFORM COMPUTE-AGE.
           DISPLAY
               '     DOB = ' DOB-MM '/' DOB-DD '/' WRK-CC DOB-YY
               '  AGE=' E-AGE.
           GOBACK.

       SET-PIVOT-DATE.

           MOVE CUR-DATE TO PIVOT-DATE.
           ADD PIVOT-NUM-YEARS TO PIVOT-CCYY.

       DERIVE-CENTURY.

           IF WRK-YY > PIVOT-YY
               COMPUTE WRK-CC = PIVOT-CC - 1
           ELSE
               IF WRK-YY = PIVOT-YY
                   IF (WRK-MM > PIVOT-MM)
                   OR (WRK-MM = PIVOT-MM AND WRK-DD > PIVOT-DD)
                       COMPUTE WRK-CC = PIVOT-CC - 1
                   ELSE
                       MOVE PIVOT-CC TO WRK-CC
                   END-IF
               ELSE
                   MOVE PIVOT-CC TO WRK-CC
               END-IF
           END-IF.

       COMPUTE-AGE.

           COMPUTE AGE = CUR-CCYY - WRK-CCYY.
           IF (DOB-MM > CUR-MM)
           OR (DOB-MM = CUR-MM AND DOB-DD > CUR-DD)
               COMPUTE AGE = AGE - 1
           END-IF.
           MOVE AGE TO E-AGE.

Hope this helps.

~shine!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-3d03f898c4"></a>
## Thread: Record Locking while doing data validation — 11 msgs

### Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
I don't know what environment this was taking place in but in CICS at least we do a full screen edit and don't lock the record until the update is to take place, to do otherwise would cause the file to lock (sorta).

Anyway I read the file, edit the fields on the map which in many cases have field dependent edits, then read the record again for update which locks it, rewrite the record with appropriate updates (which unlocks it) and go on my way.

While I don't have a single user environment it is not likely that two users would want to update the same record as each has their own little slice of the file that they are responsible for.  There are maybe 20 users doing updates (not all at the same time) and maybe 100 other users/programs doing inquiry.  I can't afford to lock a record for the time it takes to edit and update even if it is nano seconds as it has a tendency to cause weird things to happen as these requests pile up on each other as others jostle in line waiting their turn.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tlr7o$a17$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
In article <s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
  "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
> I don't know what environment this was taking place in but in CICS at
least we do a full screen edit and don't lock the record until the
update is to take place, to do otherwise would cause the file to lock
(sorta).
>
If you've got a RDB you can do something like this:
Make the record have a field called VERSION_NUMBER.

EXEC SQL UPDATE... WHERE...  AND VERSION_NUMBER
= :the_version_number_I_retrieved_when_I_read_the_record.
So if someone else got at the record since your retrieval, you get a
not-found.
Of course you set VERSION_NUMBER up by one. There might be performance
concerns because VERSION_NUMBER can't be a part of the key.
If it's part of the key then you need to do a DELETE/INSERT -, I'd
guess there'd be performance concerns on that too.  (no way am I a SQL
expert)

Just a thought at the end of a Friday.
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<37FE72DB.FF506C65@home.com>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
> I don't know what environment this was taking place in but in CICS at least we do a full screen edit and don't lock the record until the update is to take place, to do otherwise would cause the file to lock (sorta).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> While I don't have a single user environment it is not likely that two users would want to update the same record as each has their own little slice of the file that they are responsible for.  There are maybe 20 users doing updates (not all at the same time) and maybe 100 other users/programs doing inquiry.  I can't afford to lock a record for the time it takes to edit and update even if it is nano seconds as it has a tendency to cause weird things to happen as these requests pile up on each other as others jostle in line waiting their turn.

Eileen,

Get one your tech-whizzies to look at the Cinemascope issue. I can read
it fine in the window I'm answering you in - but your message in the NG
------>----->------>--------->-------->

What you've said above is perfectly OK - but with one slight flaw -
which I'll cover later. Back to Bill's original enquiry - his background
in programming was mainframe and multi-user wasn't an issue, (I believe)
- now he thinks to himself on a PC what if....? Mine in mainframe as a
Systems Manger - we had 30 programmers salivating like Pavlov's dogs to
use the one and only VDU connected to the mainframe - so multi-user
wasn't an issue, it was multi-people <G>.

Thought about it, and as a systems man dreamed up the Betsy situation -
BOOM! back comes Ken from Down Under with a real-world occurrence that
really screwed up the application. 

What I'm currently working on - even if my user goes multi-user there
wont be a problem :-

(a) He's out in the boonies doing corrosion testing at oil/gas plants -
one machine in the back of the van.
(b) Even when he gets back to base, and assuming he's gone
LAN/multi-user, still no problem. I do a set of calculations based on
his data which recommends when vessels should be replaced as corrosion
levels are getting dangerously close. If I give him, "This one should
have been junked three years ago", he's into quality control, checks his
input data and realizes he entered 0.001 inches instead of 0.100 - so he
just adjusts. And nobody else cares or needs access to this file.

I've got Donald Tees' answer tucked away to read. But both you and he
have indicated safety measures. And Ken indicated don't make the order
record locked, but subtract from inventory - and if the order-screen is
<Cancelled> go back and re-adjust inventory.

My point - to cover the various issues, some sort of white paper on
record locking and source code. We've been into record-locking for what,
roughly 20 years; newcomers should be able to look at a resource for
advice.

Now back to your flaw :-

> ... it is not likely that two users would want to update the same
> record as each has their own little slice of the file that they are
> responsible for. .....

I think Richard's comment contained something similar about user
discipline - but that aint the real world - it works fine, but some day
somebody is going to screw your system - by breaking the house rules.

Sort of in the same vein, from a systems design cooncept, "Should we put
a security check on Chart of Account numbers ?". "Nah, that's not
necessary. The Controller's department issues the chart of accounts.
Each account has a check-digit. And It's only the Controller and his
immediate underlings, who need the Bank Account numbers, so that page
isn't issued to the rest of the serfs". So quite by accident, my little
friend Betsy in A/P accidentally, by transposing digits hits an account
number combination which passes the check-digit validity -  it is one of
the Bank accounts. And poor little Betsy currently has problems meeting
her mortgage payments, with the bank threatning to foreclose....... 

In emphasis of the above, I don't watch much TV but my Eileen had it on
this morning - a photographer, (we'll call him Hengel) was promoting his
new book with magnificent aerial shots of Canadian beauty-spots. He was
asked about the problems with photographing from a helicopter :-

  "I believe in Murphy's Law, if it can happen it will. And Hengel's
   Law - I think Murphy was an optimist" .

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote in article
<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>...
> I don't know what environment this was taking place in but in CICS at
least we do a full screen edit and don't lock the record until the update
is to take place, to do otherwise would cause the file to lock (sorta).
 
> Anyway I read the file, edit the fields on the map which in many cases
have field dependent edits, then read the record again for update which
locks it, rewrite the record with appropriate updates (which unlocks it)
and go on my way.

With that kind of attitude you shouldn't be programming multi-user systems,
IMHO.

1. How can you be sure that someone hadn't changed the record after you
read it the first time and before you read it again for updating?  (Yes,
you could compare the contents and retry if there was a difference, but the
user wouldn't be too happy having to re-do his job.)

2. If you really could garantee that two users would never try to update
the same record at the same time, how could a lock on the record level
cause the file to lock?

When updating files in systems like CICS, you should take advantage of the
builtin locking mechanisms.  If you need to lock multiple resources (very
common), just be sure (by program design) that each user lock them in the
same sequence, and dead-locks will be avoided.

Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380365f7.12661466@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
On 12 Oct 1999 06:59:37 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
>
>1. How can you be sure that someone hadn't changed the record after you
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>builtin locking mechanisms. 

While you are correct in principle, you are not correct in a real life situation.

Usually, the business transaction will be done using a pseudo-conversational design, which
means

Task 1 will display the current state of the record, and end, thus releasing all locks it
might have

Task 2 will read the user's input, validate it, and then try to do the updates to the
file, after reading the records with the UPDATE option, thus locking it.

If you haven't designed your own, private locking mechanism, there is no way to make sure
that the record is still the same as before, and CICS does not provide any solution for
this


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         I'm entirely too busy to waste time choosing a tagline to use.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380378F2.E27A39FD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <380365f7.12661466@news2.ibm.net>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On 12 Oct 1999 06:59:37 GMT, "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> this

Volker has raised a good point, and as he knows I'm dealing with a
German perfectionist, like himself <G>

My application is very specialized and represents a very tiny, tiny part
of oil industry applications so I'm not knowledgeable about the oil
industry per se. However, I could foresee circumstances, say in a
control office, where engineers might be controlling oil or gas flow,
and both needing access to the same 'screen' to make an adjustment -
they could be playing tug-of-war with the same record - so it requires
very careful thought when designing.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf19a7$0631c0e0$36e5d9c1@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen> <380365f7.12661466@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net> wrote in article
<380365f7.12661466@news2.ibm.net>...
> If you haven't designed your own, private locking mechanism, there is no
way to make sure
> that the record is still the same as before, and CICS does not provide
any solution for
> this

(Sorry for late reply, having trouble with the news rdr, refuses to load
from comp.lang.cobol)

I am quite aware of that.  All changes made in one CICS transaction are
committed and cannot be rolled back later.  So taking care of the integrity
spanning several CICS transactions traditionally has been an application
responsibility.  However, the new BTS facility included with Transaction
Server 1.3 should account for some progress.  The Business Transaction
Services is described in
http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DFHJAP90/CCONTENTS?SHE
LF=DFHJSH02

Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IgwN3.3639$LW6.58148@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Every multi-user system I've worked on (IBM min thru Mainframe, WANG,
Honeywell, Unix)
allows you to 'detect' that a record is locked and by whom.

On most systems, the chances that two or more users will almost
simultaneously update the
same 'record/row' is VERY SLIM, BUT the time it takes to enter the data to
update, verify
the changes, etc is very long compared to the READ FOR UPDATE, SO THIS IS
THE
PREFERRED METHOD EVERYWHERE.



Gunnar Opheim wrote in message <01bf1446$8401a5f0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>...
>Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote in article
><s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1481$b63f9460$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote in article
<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>...
> I don't know what environment this was taking place in but in CICS at
least we do a full screen edit and don't lock the record until the update
is to take place, to do otherwise would cause the file to lock (sorta).
> 
> Anyway I read the file, edit the fields on the map which in many cases
have field dependent edits, then read the record again for update which
locks it, rewrite the record with appropriate updates (which unlocks it)
and go on my way.
> 
> While I don't have a single user environment it is not likely that two
users would want to update the same record as each has their own little
slice of the file that they are responsible for.  There are maybe 20 users
doing updates (not all at the same time) and maybe 100 other users/programs
doing inquiry.  I can't afford to lock a record for the time it takes to
edit and update even if it is nano seconds as it has a tendency to cause
weird things to happen as these requests pile up on each other as others
jostle in line waiting their turn.

With that kind of attitude you shouldn't be programming online systems,
IMHO.

1. How can you be sure that someone hadn't changed the record after you
read it the first time and before you read it again for updating?  (Yes,
you could compare the contents and retry if there was a difference, but the
user wouldn't be too happy having to re-do his job.)

2. If you really could garantee that two users would never try to update
the same record at the same time, how could a lock on the record level
cause the file to lock?

When updating files in systems like CICS, you should take advantage of the
builtin locking mechanisms.  If you need to lock multiple resources (very
common), just be sure (by program design) that each user lock them in the
same sequence, and dead-locks will be avoided.

If the requests seem to pile up even though you think they are not updating
the same record, then maybe you have another kind of problem, say lack of
VSAM strings.

Gunnar.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking while doing data validation

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38105F9B.41775310@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<s7fdecf8.025@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <01bf1481$b63f9460$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
> If the requests seem to pile up even though you think they are not updating
> the same record, then maybe you have another kind of problem, say lack of
> VSAM strings.
>
> Gunnar.

I missed the beginning of this thread so I don't know what the original problem
was, but It may be helpful to point out that that when updating a VSAM file
using CICS, VSAM will lock not only the record but also the Control Interval
(CI) containing that record, until the update is committed.

The theory is that CICS is using VSAM Locally Shared Resource (LSR) buffer
pools (assuming that's the way it's been set up) to hold a copy of the CI in
storage. It means read requests can be satisfied much quicker than the time
taken for a 'real' I/O  but there must be some way of ensuring the in-storage
records agree with the ones on disk if an update takes place. A not-uncommon
problem in my experience occurs when two tasks both deadlock on different
records in the same CI.

This applies to CICS v4 and below. I believe the situation is different in
Transaction Server.

HTH
```

---

<a id="t-6cb0c217a4"></a>
## Thread: Student: Help with Arrays — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Student: Help with Arrays

- **From:** wcmm6@aol.com (Wcmm6)
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991008143008.13197.00000502@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

```
I've written a program that reads in an old master file with obvious errors,
validates the record fields, produces a validation report, error listing, and
sorted master file. The input consists of: school-code-in     pic x(3)
                                                 athlete-no-in        pic 9(3)
                                                 athlete-name-in    pic x(20)
                                                 coach-no-in        pic 9(3)
                                                 score-in             pic 9(4)
Each athlete has 10 scores to be validated, so I read them into an array and
distributed them to the appropriate reports. My problem is that I am getting 23
lines of only error messages on my error report. After these erroneous lines,
the output is accurate and as I expect it should look. I assume it is a problem
with my coding relating to the score array, but after reading through it a
number of times and making numerous changes, I feel stuck!
Another problem in the error report is that it is reading a score with blanks
as being "out of range" and as "non-numeric". It should be just "non-numeric".
It seems it is reading the blanks as zeros. Should I make the pic clause
alphanumeric instead of numeric? I'm posting the procedure division and error
report below for your consideration. I would appreciate comments - suggestions
regarding the correction of the problem.I hope this isn't viewed as a "Do my
homework for me".... I've put a lot of time in this and feel I just need a push
in the right direction! 
Thanks in advance for your time and knowledge.   Bill W.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
100-MAIN.
         SORT SORT-FILE
         ON ASCENDING KEY COACH-NO-IN
         ON ASCENDING KEY ATHLETE-NAME-IN
                INPUT PROCEDURE 200-INITIALIZE-RTN
                   GIVING SORTED-MASTER-OUT
          STOP RUN.
200-INITIALIZE-RTN.
         OPEN INPUT MASTER-FILE-IN
              OUTPUT ERROR-LIST-OUT
              OUTPUT VALID-LIST-OUT
          PERFORM 600-HDR-RTN
          PERFORM 700-HDR-RTN 
          PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS = 'NO'
               READ MASTER-FILE-IN
                           AT END
 	                MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS
                            NOT AT END
                               PERFORM 300-VALIDATE-RTN
                END-READ
              END-PERFORM
              CLOSE MASTER-FILE-IN
                    ERROR-LIST-OUT
                    VALID-LIST-OUT. 
300-VALIDATE-RTN.
         MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-REC  
         MOVE 'N' TO ERR-SWITCH
       IF NOT STA AND NOT USC AND NOT UCL AND NOT UCB AND NOT ORS AND NOT ORU
       AND NOT WAS AND NOT WAU  
                  MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
                  MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER 
                  MOVE SCHOOL-CODE-IN TO EL-ERROR-FIELD
                  MOVE 'INVALID SCHOOL CODE' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
                  PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
          END-IF
         IF ATHLETE-NO-IN NOT NUMERIC
                    MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
                    MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER
                                          EL-ERROR-FIELD
	      MOVE ' CONTAINS NON-NUMERIC DATA' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
             PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
         END-IF
         IF ATHLETE-NAME-IN = SPACES
               MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
               MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER 
               MOVE ATHLETE-NAME-IN TO EL-ERROR-FIELD
               MOVE 'NO NAME PRESENT' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
             PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
         END-IF
         IF COACH-NO-IN NOT NUMERIC 
                 MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
    	  MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER 
	  MOVE COACH-NO-IN TO EL-ERROR-FIELD
	  MOVE 'INVALID COACH NUMBER' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
          PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
          END-IF
           PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
          IF SCORE-IN (SUB) NOT NUMERIC
          MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
          MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER
          MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB) TO EL-ERROR-FIELD
          MOVE 'NON-NUMERIC DATA' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
          PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
           END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
          PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
          IF SCORE-IN (SUB) NOT > 399 OR NOT < 2001
          MOVE 'Y' TO ERR-SWITCH
          MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO EL-NUMBER
          MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB) TO EL-ERROR-FIELD
          MOVE 'SCORE OUT OF RANGE' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
          PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN
          END-IF
           END-PERFORM.
         IF ERR-SWITCH = 'N'
         PERFORM 400-VALID-RTN
         END-IF
       RELEASE SORT-REC FROM MASTER-REC.
400-VALID-RTN.
        IF VALID-LINE-CT > 19
         PERFORM 700-HDR-RTN
        END-IF
        MOVE SCHOOL-CODE-IN TO DL-SCHOOL-CODE
        MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN TO DL-ATHLETE-NUMBER
        MOVE ATHLETE-NAME-IN TO DL-NAME
        MOVE COACH-NO-IN TO DL-COACH-NUMBER
         PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
          MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB) TO DL-SCORES (SUB)
         END-PERFORM
       WRITE VALID-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
       ADD 1 TO VALID-LINE-CT.    
500-ERROR-RTN.
         IF ERROR-LINE-CT > 25
          PERFORM 600-HDR-RTN
         END-IF
         WRITE ERROR-REC FROM ERROR-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
         ADD 1 TO ERROR-LINE-CT.
600-HDR-RTN.
         WRITE ERROR-REC FROM HDR-4 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
         WRITE ERROR-REC FROM HDR-5 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
         WRITE ERROR-REC FROM HDR-6 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
         MOVE ZEROS TO ERROR-LINE-CT.
700-HDR-RTN.
         WRITE VALID-REC FROM HDR-1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
         WRITE VALID-REC FROM HDR-2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
         WRITE VALID-REC FROM HDR-3 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
         MOVE ZEROS TO VALID-LINE-CT.
                    
     	   		             
 	 
                                DECATHLON ERROR LISTING                        
  

ATHLETE        FIELD                       TYPE                                


 NUMBER      IN ERROR                    OF ERROR                              


                        INVALID SCHOOL CODE                               

                         CONTAINS NON-NUMERIC DATA                        

                        INVALID COACH NUMBER                              

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       NON-NUMERIC DATA                                  

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

                       SCORE OUT OF RANGE                                

  11A     11A                  CONTAINS NON-NUMERIC DATA                       


  239                         NO NAME PRESENT                                  


  355                         INVALID COACH NUMBER                             


                               DECATHLON ERROR LISTING                         
 

ATHLETE        FIELD                       TYPE                                


 NUMBER      IN ERROR                    OF ERROR                              


  441                         NO NAME PRESENT                                  


  441     04F                 INVALID COACH NUMBER                             


  450     050J                NON-NUMERIC DATA                                 


  582     WSA                 INVALID SCHOOL CODE

In article <19991008143008.13197.00000502@ng-bh1.aol.com>,
Wcmm6 <wcmm6@aol.com> wrote:
>I've written a program that reads in an old master file with obvious errors,
>validates the record fields, produces a validation report, error listing, and
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks in advance for your time and knowledge.   Bill W.

No problem... you look to have done a good part of it on your own.

[snippage]

>300-VALIDATE-RTN.
>         MOVE SPACES TO ERROR-REC  
>         MOVE 'N' TO ERR-SWITCH
>       IF NOT STA AND NOT USC AND NOT UCL AND NOT UCB AND NOT ORS AND NOT ORU
>       AND NOT WAS AND NOT WAU  


Ow OW ow ow ow... what are you doing, writing Production code for a Major
Insurance Company?  No, couldn't be... you're using 88s here and all this
stuff would be hard-coded if you were.

Suggestion:

05  SCHOOL-CODE PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
    88 VALID-SCHOOL-CODE VALUES 'STA', 'USC', 'UCL', 'UCB', 'ORS',
                                'ORU'...etc etc.

... and then this rather... inelegant condition becomes 'IF NOT
VALID-SCHOOL-CODE'.

... and that's my hint for the program.  Now, look at the errors:

[snippage]

>
>  450     050J                NON-NUMERIC DATA                                 

[snip]

>
>  623     090C                NON-NUMERIC DATA                                 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It looks to me as though your data file doesn't match your FD (or maybe
even your SELECT, if this is a PC-based system... Sequential versus Line
Sequential and all that).  Get a dump of a few of your data records and
sit with them in one hand and the record you're reading them into in the
other; match them up column-by-column.  You might find that you missed a
byte here or there.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Student: Help with Arrays

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tlnol$ub6$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19991008143008.13197.00000502@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

```
Others are replying to parts of this post, but I thought that I would give
some "general guidance" related to data-validation of numeric input fields.
(This assumes that the input is usage DISPLAY and the field is defined EITHER
as "numeric" - with V's and S's or numeric-edited - with periods, commas, and
+/-. If the input is coming in from a terminal or similar input device, I
hope - for your sake - that it is numeric-edited, but you never know!)

There are (at least) two approaches to working with such fields during "data
validation" phases (where you can't really BELIEVE that they will only have
numeric data in them).

If the input comes in from same place (like a terminal with a number lock)
that only allows numbers - but MIGHT have a field with all spaces, then look
at using the BLANK WHEN ZERO phrase on your field definition.  This means
that you won't get any "invalid" data messages - and that your program can
handle such fields as "zero" when moving FROM that field.

On the other hand, (and I am afraid that this is more common in "real-world"
cases of data validation phases),  if your input field MIGHT have a space in
the middle of a bunch of numbers - or worse "alphanumeric" and/or unprintable
characters in it, then it is best to have a redefinition of the field as
alphanumeric (PIC (X)) and do an "if numeric" test first, then an "if spaces"
test, and only after these pass should you reference the numeric definition
of the field (and do any range or sign tests).

It is also important (IMHO) that you NOT combine these tests into a single
conditional expression (lots of ANDs and ORs) - but instead do the tests one
at a time - either with nested IFs (or evaluates) or with a "OK-til-now"
switch and sequential IFs.

Clearly, "in the real world," you will find different "reliable" and
"unreliable" types of input data.  What type of data validation you must do
and/or CAN do - will depend on your actual environment.  The only "rule of
thumb" that I think is almost universal is do NOT believe the user when they
say "Well, I never SHOULD enter data like that".

P.S.  For the more "advanced" users, you might want to check out the VALIDATE
verb/facility that is being added in the draft of the next Standard.  This
does lots and LOTS of this "uninteresting" stuff in a very nice (IMHO) way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Student: Help with Arrays

'It seems it is reading the blanks as zeros'

If you are using MF cobol there is a default compilier directive on some
releases (SP0) which treats spaces or low-values as a 0 (like VME cobol)

just an idea...............
```

#### ↳ Re: Student: Help with Arrays

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0cl7kt7hu293@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<19991008143008.13197.00000502@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

```
Dear:

The followings are not exactly the source code of the program.
They are merely the sample that may give you some suggestions.
You are a very good programmer, for your good attitude.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     SORT SORT-FILE
          ON ASCENDING KEY COACH-NO-IN
          ON ASCENDING KEY ATHLETE-NAME-IN
          INPUT PROCEDURE  000-PROCESS-RTN
          GIVING           SORTED-MASTER-OUT.
     STOP RUN.

000-PROCESS-RTN.
     OPEN INPUT  MASTER-FILE-IN
          OUTPUT ERROR-LIST-OUT
          OUTPUT VALID-LIST-OUT.
     PERFORM 600-ERR-HDR-RTN.
     PERFORM 700-HDR-RTN.
     PERFROM 100-READ-RTN.
     PERFORM 300-VALIDATE-RTN
             UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS = 'NO'. 
     CLOSE MASTER-FILE-IN ERROR-LIST-OUT VALID-LIST-OUT.

100-READ-RTN.
     READ MASTER-FILE-IN
              AT END MOVE 'NO' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECS.

300-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM SCHOOL-CODE-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM ATHLETE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM ATHLETE-NAME-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM COACH-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM SCORE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
     RELEASE SORT-REC            FROM  MASTER-REC.

SCHOOL-CODE-VALIDATE-RTN.
    MOVE 'N' TO ERR-SWITCH
    IF NOT (STA AND USC AND UCL AND UCB
            AND ORS AND ORU AND WAS AND WAU)
       MOVE 'Y'                    TO  ERR-SWITCH
       MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN          TO  EL-NUMBER
       MOVE SCHOOL-CODE-IN         TO  EL-ERROR-FIELD
       MOVE 'INVALID SCHOOL CODE'  TO  EL-ERROR-MESSAGE.

ATHLETE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
    IF ATHLETE-NO-IN NOT NUMERIC
       MOVE 'Y'                    TO  ERR-SWITCH
       MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN          TO  EL-NUMBER
                                       EL-ERROR-FIELD
       MOVE ' CONTAINS NON-NUMERIC DATA' TO EL-ERROR-MESSAGE.

ATHLETE-NAME-VALIDATE-RTN.
    IF ATHLETE-NAME-IN = SPACES
       MOVE 'Y'                    TO  ERR-SWITCH
       MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN          TO  EL-NUMBER
       MOVE ATHLETE-NAME-IN        TO  EL-ERROR-FIELD
       MOVE 'NO NAME PRESENT'      TO  EL-ERROR-MESSAGE.

COACH-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
    IF COACH-NO-IN NOT NUMERIC
       MOVE 'Y'                    TO  ERR-SWITCH
       MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN          TO  EL-NUMBER
       MOVE COACH-NO-IN            TO  EL-ERROR-FIELD
       MOVE 'INVALID COACH NUMBER' TO  EL-ERROR-MESSAGE.

SCORE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
   PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
     IF  SCORE-IN (SUB) NOT NUMERIC
         MOVE 'Y'                   TO  ERR-SWITCH
         MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN         TO  EL-NUMBER
         MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB)        TO  EL-ERROR-FIELD
         MOVE 'NON-NUMERIC DATA'    TO  EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
     ELSE
     IF  SCORE-IN (SUB) NOT > 399 OR NOT < 2001
         MOVE 'Y'                   TO  ERR-SWITCH
         MOVE ATHLETE-NO-IN         TO  EL-NUMBER
         MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB)        TO  EL-ERROR-FIELD
         MOVE 'SCORE OUT OF RANGE'  TO  EL-ERROR-MESSAGE
     ELSE
         MOVE 'N'                   TO  ERR-SWICTCH.
* All above modules result in the value of ERR-SWITCH.
* At that point, we do once and for all which RTN to do.
     IF  ERR-SWITCH = 'N'
         PERFORM 400-VALID-RTN
     ELSE
         PERFORM 500-ERROR-RTN.

400-VALID-RTN.
*  Some compiler do not move spaces prior to writing.
*  So the programmer should clear the garbage.
   MOVE  SPACES                     TO  VALID-REC.
   IF VALID-LINE-CT > 19       PERFORM  700-HDR-RTN.
   MOVE  SCHOOL-CODE-IN             TO  DL-SCHOOL-CODE.
   MOVE  ATHLETE-NO-IN              TO  DL-ATHLETE-NUMBER.
   MOVE  ATHLETE-NAME-IN            TO  DL-NAME.
   MOVE  COACH-NO-IN                TO  DL-COACH-NUMBER.
   PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL SUB > 10
       MOVE SCORE-IN (SUB)          TO  DL-SCORES (SUB).
   WRITE VALID-REC                FROM  DETAIL-LINE AFTER 2.
   ADD 1 TO VALID-LINE-CT.

500-ERROR-RTN.
   MOVE  SPACES       TO ERROR-REC.
   IF ERROR-LINE-CT > 25 PERFORM    600-ERR-HDR-RTN.
   WRITE ERROR-REC  FROM ERROR-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
   ADD  1             TO ERROR-LINE-CT.

600-ERR-HDR-RTN.
   MOVE  SPACES       TO ERROR-REC.
   WRITE ERROR-REC  FROM HDR-4 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE. 
   MOVE  SPACES       TO ERROR-REC.
   WRITE ERROR-REC  FROM HDR-5 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE. 
   MOVE  SPACES       TO ERROR-REC.
   WRITE ERROR-REC  FROM HDR-6 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
   MOVE  ZEROS TO ERROR-LINE-CT.

700-HDR-RTN.
   MOVE  SPACES       TO VALID-REC.
   WRITE VALID-REC  FROM HDR-1 AFTER ADVANCING PAGE. 
   MOVE  SPACES       TO VALID-REC.
   WRITE VALID-REC  FROM HDR-2 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE. 
   MOVE  SPACES       TO VALID-REC.
   WRITE VALID-REC  FROM HDR-3 AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
   MOVE ZEROS TO VALID-LINE-CT.

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: Student: Help with Arrays

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0cl3qeshu277@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<19991008143008.13197.00000502@ng-bh1.aol.com>`

```
1- You do not have a period sign for each of your statement.
It is confusing for programmers who read your codes.
In most of compiler, they are compiling warnings,
That contain potential errors (bugs that kill you any time)
But conceptually they are really errors.

2- Please, try to use PERFORM without END-PERFROM approach,
which is modular approach, easy to maintain.

3- When you need to validate the data, the best way is
To declare the variable as PIC Xs and
REDEFINE it as PIC 9s.
Some COBOL compiler read PIC 9s with spaces as ZERO.
In the validation process if the value is NUMERIC,
PERFROM the NORMAL-RTN, ELSE PERFORM the ERR-RTN.
In the NORMAL-RTN, use the variable name with PIC 9s.
In the ERR-RTN, use the variable name with PIC Xs.

4- The structured approach is that:
PERFORM  VALIDATE-RTN UNTIL ARE-THRE-MORE-RECORDS = �NO �.

VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM ATHLETE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM ATHLETE-NAME-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM COACH-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.
     PERFORM SCORE-NUM-VALIDATE-RTN.

Then, in each routine, you expand it into detail codes.
I have really a hard time reading your code.
It is a funny saying that your programming coding style
is that of CONSULTANTS. If you write your code that way,
every time your customers encounter your programs, they
have no ways rather than calling you to work, and you
have opportunity to charge them again.

In that case, you have problem yourself debugging your
own program.

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL SUCKS

- **From:** "KansasCityJobs" <kansascityjobs@kc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00

- **References:** `<ZAeL3.1513$Jp4.4287@news20.bellglobal.com> <7tm9ls$hif$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I would agree... Whoever wrote this post does not understand the power of
COBOL.  Ten years ago I took COBOL in college to get the credits (but of
course the language was dying... soon to be replaced by C and other fun
languages).  I then graduated and got my first job for 18k using COBOL in
Kansas City... but once again the language was dying..   Four years later
our starting salary had jumped to 36k for programmers who knew that dying
language.  This year our salary for college graduates is 40k without any
experience for a dying sucky language.  I think COBOL is one of the best
languages around, and our company will continue to use it for years.  When
it's combined with DB2 it's one of the best mainframe processing giants
around.

Anyway that's my two cents... and here's a plug... If you have COBOL
experience and would like to work in the KC area.  Please email me your
resume.. I am not a headhunter or contractor.  I'm an employee at that same
company that hired me to code a language my college instructors told me
would die... (I hope their making 24k).

-- Kathy

trawl n. 1. a large, baglike net dragged by a boat along the bottom of a
fishing bank. 2. a long line supported by buoys, from which many short fishing
lines are hung  --vt., vi. TO FISH OR CATCH WITH A TRAWL (emphasis added)

## Thread: "low-values" — 8 msgs

### Re: "low-values"

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7fe1a8e.005@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


>>> The COBOL Frog 10/04 3:58 pm >>> wrote:
 
William Lynch wrote:
> RoadRaat wrote:
> > That won't work, will it?  The default USAGE in this case will be DISPLAY.
> > Therefore, assigning '0000' will leave in storage the hex values of 'F0F0F0F0'.
> He didn't assign a value of '0000'; but X'0000', which is LOW-VALUES in
> the IBM mainframe world.
Except, Bill, when an alternative alphabet has been defined in the
special-names. In that case x'00' could even be the highest-value!

:(
```

#### ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FEADBB.BA0A5A25@att.net>`
- **References:** `<s7fe1a8e.005@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
> >>> The COBOL Frog 10/04 3:58 pm >>> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> special-names. In that case x'00' could even be the highest-value!

Could be, but that's not the way the smart money bets. 

What are the options on alternate alphabets: Cyrillic, Greek, Hebrew?
Are you suggesting someone's going to define another alphabet and make
this universal throughout their files/databases/code? Maybe the NCI or
CIA.

Bill Lynch :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38023678.4023632@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<s7fe1a8e.005@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <37FEADBB.BA0A5A25@att.net>`

```
On Fri, 08 Oct 1999 22:51:39 -0400, William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
wrote:

>Eileen Preston wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have to tell that because the guys that invented the ASCII and other
characters sets had a language that did not use special alphabet
charaters like ï¿½ or ï¿½ or ï¿½ or ï¿½ and a lot others did not have to worry

about getting them on the proper sort order.
In Portugal and in other countries if we wanï¿½t to have our "Customers"
or our "Articles" sorted by name then we have two options,
1 - Do not allow portuguese characters in the names, and do not allow
    lower case letters.

2 - Define a alphabet in every program and define every indexed file
    to use this collating sequence. (I did use this a lot, although
    it caused some problems when converting file between UNIX/DOS, 
    which made me have two copybooks one with the UNIX collating, and
    the other with the Codepage 860, and two compiled versions of the
    programs).

Although I did the second I would not change the order of the
unneeded characters, so it would not change the "low-value" or
"high-value" character.


FF
```

Frederico,

I see you used that dreaded word 'Portuguese' again in your text <G>. 

Off-track from what you are talking about, but associated. You are able
to enter 'inflections' over 'a's and 'e's in your text, which we see.
Is this an automatic feature because Windows is picking up your locale
as Iberia, and when you talk about not using them - do you have to
switch this facility off ?

Volker - I assume the same applies with your umlauts and the French with
their 'accents', or our Scandinavian friends, from your recent visit ?

What prompted this - I recall a message a short while back where
somebody was working in Cyprus I believe - and ran into similar alphabet
problems. 

Nudging away in the back of my mind - a system is written in N. America,
apart from files for message/dialog texts - what other problems are
their for an 'Anglo' designed system for switching to other languages?
No, I don't want to translate my system to Malay - but we did have a
query from there some three years ago - long before we were prepared to
even consider the enquiry.

If we were talking 'Mother Russia' - I'm sure their national pride would
demand a product be in Cyrillic (?) characters.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "low-values"

- **References:** `<s7fe1a8e.005@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <37FEADBB.BA0A5A25@att.net> <38023678.4023632@news.indigo.ie> <37FFFDCC.E9D185F6@home.com>`

```
>
>Off-track from what you are talking about, but associated. You are able
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>switch this facility off ?
Well, if i want to create the letter "ï¿½" I just have to hit "~"
followed by "a". If I donï¿½t want to have portuguese characters I just
donï¿½t use the 2 character combination.



>
>Volker - I assume the same applies with your umlauts and the French with
>their 'accents', or our Scandinavian friends, from your recent visit ?
It does.

>Nudging away in the back of my mind - a system is written in N. America,
>apart from files for message/dialog texts - what other problems are
>their for an 'Anglo' designed system for switching to other languages?
First problem is that the translated message may be to long for the
resulting field, and if changing screens that can be hard to adapt,
normally by changing the source progtrams (or if you just use a screen
manager(like SP2 or RM/PANELS) by changing the screen fields).
If you are translating to languages that use a double byte character
it may be even more dificult, although I had never done this one.

Second you have the sort sequence as I mentioned in a previous post,
although this is normally ignored (to much work to do if you really 
need it).

Third and more important of all, all the legal stuff, and way of 
doing thing in terms of accounting, personnel and so on. This normally
means customization of certain pieces of the software.

It is easier to convert a general software like a cheques/bank control
system than to convert an fully integrated accounting system.

Hope this helps

FF
```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> Frederico,
> Off-track from what you are talking about, but associated. You are able
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> switch this facility off ?
In windows:
- Start
- Settings
- Keyboard
From here on it is sligthly different for NT versus ' 95/' 98:
- Add language english (US/UK whichever you like) and keyboardlayout US.
- Add also english with keyboard leyout US-international.
Now you can switch between these via the start/settings/etc sequence above and
then switching which one is set as default. Faster is to enable the checkbox
"indicator on taskbar" and switch from there (just click on the small indicator
)

Having selected the US/US combi, the characters ' " ~ ` ^ work as normal. Nice
for typing COBOL like MOVE "STRING" TO CHARX.
Having US/International selected, these characters ('"~`^) kind of wait for the
next key. If that is a character which can be combined, it wil. Typing "
followed by e yields ï¿½. Lots of combinations possible of course.
ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½.

And how to get a separate " without switching? Just type the " followed by a
space.

No need to remeber alt-sequences. Works in each and every windowsapp. (Some
people remeber all those obschure Word-alt-control-key-combi's, but these work
in notepad, write, .... all windows-apps.)

I don't know how to get ï¿½'s ï¿½'s etc. with the above method, but I have a great
other little tool for that (as you see :) ), which can freely be downloaded fron
http"//www.whiteants.com, writen by one of those famous dutch programmers!

!

The Frog
```

I have heard about (but never actually seen) that some programs have a
"display" zero (X'0F' in IBM mainframe world) setup as low-values.  They then
set all the display numerics followed by the display alphabetics, and then
have all the "other stuff" next the programming collating sequence.

### Converting MF to FUJITSU - NEED HELP CALLING DOS FUNCTION

- **From:** "Bob Smith" <bobsmith@netnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tlj9p$28pj$1@news.inc.net>`

```
Can anyone help me with a Micro Focus to Fujitsu conversion problem?

Under Micro Focus, I could call a DOS command with the following
   DISPLAY (DOS COMMAND) UPON COMMAND LINE...
   CALL X"91"

Fujitsu doesn't want to accept this.   What is the Fujitsu equivalent please
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting MF to FUJITSU - NEED HELP CALLING DOS FUNCTION

- **From:** "Joe Hunter" <@usaor.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CdsL3.1411$iC6.240314@news.sgi.net>`
- **References:** `<7tlj9p$28pj$1@news.inc.net>`

```

Bob Smith wrote in message <7tlj9p$28pj$1@news.inc.net>...
>Can anyone help me with a Micro Focus to Fujitsu conversion problem?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fujitsu doesn't want to accept this.   What is the Fujitsu equivalent
please
>
download cbl_routines from Fujitsu Web site.
**********   example of copy to a print-file***************
          MOVE 1 TO CBLEXEC-EXECUTION-MODE.
           MOVE 0 TO CBLEXEC-SHOW-MODE.
           MOVE 35 TO COMMAND-STRING-LENGTH.
           MOVE "COMMAND.COM /C COPY CEM200.PRT LPT1" TO COMMAND-STRING.
           CALL "CBLEXEC" USING CBLEXEC-CREATION-FLAGS,
               COMMAND-STRING-LENGTH, COMMAND-STRING,
               CBLEXEC-STATUS-CODE.


Joe Hunter
pcs@usaor.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting MF to FUJITSU - NEED HELP CALLING DOS FUNCTION

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
Joe Hunter wrote:
> 
> Bob Smith wrote in message <7tlj9p$28pj$1@news.inc.net>...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> pcs@usaor.net

Tip for new players.

When you do use non standard features please isolate them in a
subroutine that you call.

Plus
----

When you move compiler's it is easy to change the non-standard part
for all parts of the program

minus
-----

Another layer of complexity.  If it is just one line efficiency nuts
will complain.

Enforcing using the standard routine.  This applies to any corporate
standard.

Ken
```

- **References:** `<7tlj9p$28pj$1@news.inc.net> <CdsL3.1411$iC6.240314@news.sgi.net> <37FFEF1F.F551DBAE@zip.com.au> <3800044A.B0797A9A@home.com> <38009cc0.375291721@news1.attglobal.net> <7tt5c5$1oo$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>     You did not mention compiler/environment, but with Microfocus in the
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Seeing that Russell continues the topic, I'll add the following. I sent
it to Thane but accidentally didn't copy to NewsGroup. El Dumbo (wonder
how Babelfish would translate that ?).

My reply to Thane :-
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ironic you picked on DELETE FILE. It didn't do it for me in Microfocus -
didn't research why, possibly my approach, so instead went for the
MicroFocus call "CBL_DELETE_FILE". But that didn't do it completely for
me either, wanted to delete with a wildcard. I missed it, when initially
reviewing classes, but found a class Filename, and that lets me do this
:-

       *>------------------ filenam2.cbl --------------------------

       Class-id.       FileNames2
                       inherits from Base.

       Class-control.  FileNames2           is class "filenam2"

                       CharacterArray       is class "chararry"
                       FileName             is class "filename"
       .

       *> Coded as a separate class so that more methods can be added
       *> later

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
       FACTORY.
       *>--------------------------------------------------------------
        etc...
       OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
       *> Nothing currently
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "delete-using-wildcard".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-collection             object reference.
       01 ls-filename               pic x(100).
       01 ls-length                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-size                   pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-StatusCode             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-string                 object reference.

       Linkage section.
       01 lnk-WildCardName          pic x(50). 
       Procedure Division using lnk-WildCardName.

        *> I'm talking OilCorp/Plant so the root for the
wildcardname           *>could be :-

        *> "c:\ucs\4-Temporary-Data\CHEV01-34"

        move length of lnk-WildCardName to ls-length
        invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
               using     ls-length
                         lnk-WildCardName
               returning ls-string
        invoke FileName "expandwildcards" using     ls-string
                                          returning ls-Collection
        invoke ls-Collection "size" returning ls-size

        perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size
           move spaces to ls-filename
           invoke ls-Collection "at" using n returning ls-string
           invoke ls-string "getValueZ" returning ls-filename
           call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     ls-filename
                                  returning ls-StatusCode
        End-perform
	*> PS : I could care less about the status-code - it either
	*> gets deleted or it doesn't
        invoke ls-Collection "deepfinalize" returning ls-Collection

       End Method "delete-using-wildcard".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------

       End OBJECT.
       End CLASS FileNames2.

       *>--------------------------------------------------------------

As you can see it is compiler specific, still uses CBL_DELETE and at
this time must consider the Class Filename to be specific to MicroFocus.

Does anybody have a routine to handle wildcards ?

Back to Pete's contention, and the transportability issue, we do need
open source of generalised routines, (1) several for mainframes (2) Unix
(3) Windows (4) Linux etc.......  COBOL-xxx when it happens - whether
people take the approach of coding User Functions or go the whole hog
with OO, we do need generalised souce, to save us all having to
re-invent the wheel. 

Jimmy
--------------------------- end of copy -------------------------------
```
