<a id="t-356b803d5a"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aFuL3.664$_4.1374@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
(Continued)

It is interesting to look at Tucker's comparison of a data processing
application in COBOL and PL/I ["Programming Languages", McGraw-Hill, 1977].

As a language, PL/I rated better than COBOL in:
	* programming features
	* overall writing
	  * compactness
	  * uniformity
	  * locality (rated equal with COBOL)
	  * segmentation
	* Overall reading
	  * Structural clarity
	* execution diagnostics
	* other debugging facilities
COBOL rated better than PL/I in:
	  * Local clarity.
	* data processing functional support.

In other words, PL/I rated superior in nine out of twelve points, and was rated
equal on one point, while COBOL rated superior on only two points out of twelve.
Note: run times for the PL/I compilers, particularly the Optimizing Compiler are
not valid because the REORDER keyword for the main procedure has not been
specified -- the compiler cannot perform full optimization.  The REORDER keyword
must be specified in the PROCEDURE statement in order to obtain full
optimization.

Note: The PL/I program for master file maintenance required two pages and
9 lines of code.

The equivalent COBOL program required six pages of code.
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tm1af$ihu$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<aFuL3.664$_4.1374@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Also note that the copyright date on that book is 1977.  This is 8 years
before the '85 ANSI COBOL Standard and more than 10 years before the '89
Amendment.  Also it was few years before the time that ANSI/ISO "gave up" on
PL/I - and said that it was "functionally" frozen as a programming language
that they wouldn't be providing any future ANSI/ISO PL/I Standards (while
COBOL continues to be supported in this "portable" ANSI and ISO environments.
Similarly, COBOL is included in the UNIX X/Open Programming Specification -
and PL/I is not.)

Therefore, the comparison is significantly out of date - and ignores
portability - but other than that it is a "nice" historical document!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Denis" <grossnou@antispam.skynet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tru6n$dke$1@hardcopy.ny.jpmorgan.com>`
- **References:** `<aFuL3.664$_4.1374@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Robin,

I do not agree with many points you defend:
- About debugging:
In the context of OS/390 and Language environment, it is much easier to read
LE dumps for COBOL programs than for PL/I:
If you compile your COBOL  module with TEST(NONE,SYM) your load-module is
slightly bigger  but the performances are the same (We've measured it), you
directly have in the dump the statement number where the problem occur and
you can directly read in the le dump (card CEEMSG) the content of all the
variables of all the modules stacked in your enclave at the time of the
abend (variable name + content) if you dump with the correct option.
This is impossible for PL/I (read the manual section CEE3DMP that states
that the 'variable' option is valid for all languages except PL/I). PL/I LE
dumps display only the statement number but not directly the content of the
variables (compilation option GOSTMT)

About the debugger:
The debugger is common for all the language, so not advantages here

- about error checking
You can activate option that check the same things as PL/I (either clause ON
ERROR in the code or compilation option to check arrays out-of-bound
conditions or overflows)

- about the size of the source
It is much easier to create smaller modules in COBOL and dynamically call
them (even under CICS). Even if PL/I allowsto dynamically fetch modules,
there are really annoying limits due to the language itself that block you
in doing this (the famous fetch limitation, that should be alleviated in the
coming release)

- about optimization
In COBOL also you can request local optimization by specifying a number
after the section name. however nobody uses this in COBOL because there is
no such limit in COBOL than in PL/I about the size of stuff that the
compiler can optimize (for example the PL/I compiler optimizes only the
first 255 variables...)

- about the easiness of PUT DATA:
 With DISPLAY in COBOL you can do exactly the same thing by simply writing
DISPLAY <var>, even under CICS, the output goes in card CEEMSG by default.

Denis +4447




robin wrote in message ...
>(Continued)
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>In other words, PL/I rated superior in nine out of twelve points, and was
rated
>equal on one point, while COBOL rated superior on only two points out of
twelve.
>Note: run times for the PL/I compilers, particularly the Optimizing
Compiler are
>not valid because the REORDER keyword for the main procedure has not been
>specified -- the compiler cannot perform full optimization.  The REORDER
keyword
>must be specified in the PROCEDURE statement in order to obtain full
>optimization.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The equivalent COBOL program required six pages of code.
```

```
Eileen Preston writes ...

>Thanks for the list Bill (I think).  I've kinda taught myself COBOL II by the
>seat of my pants (hate skirts/dresses) and now there's the 390 one, but never
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's the idea behind our course "Beyond COBOL II". It covers all the goodies
introduced with COBOL II that most programmers never got a chance to get
familiar with, then discusses all the new features of the new compiler(s), in
the order of the releases:

COBOL/370: intrinsic functions & need for LE

COBOL for MVS & VM: many major enhancements (see course outline on our website
[http://www.trainersfriend.com] for details)

COBOL for OS/390 & VM: primarily DLL support.

This two day course is a hands-on opportunity to get familiarity with and
experience on the new compilers: get current in your COBOL knowledge.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-f65b9bdc9f"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KOlL3.418$_4.1086@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"Denis" <grosnou@antispam.skynet.be> writes: > Hello,
> I'm trying to compare the COBOL & PL/I languages in order to write
> recommendations for our developers. Both language are available. Thousands
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Many thanks in advance

My observations of COBOL and PL/I are:
.
It is quicker to write and to debug PL/I programs compared to the COBOL
equivalent.
.
A PL/I source program is about one-third shorter than its COBOL counterpart.
Thus the human resource cost of entering the source is less for PL/I.
.
COBOL programs take longer to reach a succesful compilation because:
.
	(1) the COBOL program has 33% more lines: more typos to correct;
	(2) the COBOL source is column sensitive: errors waste time for nothing;
	(3) COBOL has some 300 reserved words: inadvertant use of a keyword as
	    a variable name wastes time. (PL/I has no reserved words.)
.
COBOL programs take longer to debug, following successful compilation.
This is on account of the superior debug facilities of PL/I:
.
	* simple statements to write out variables (e.g., PUT DATA);
	* error trapping and display of variables, at the point where the
	  error occurs.
	* precise location of the statement in error;
	* enabling fixed-point and floating-point checks for overflow/underflow
	  for any part of or the whole program.
	* enabling checks for subscript violations for any part of or the whole
	  program;
	* enabling checks for substring violations for any part of or the whole
	  program;
	* trapping of computational errors, subscript errors, and substring
	  errors.
PL/I is largely machine-independent.
Optimization can be confined to specific regions of program, maintaining
statement order.
Specific sections of the program or the whole program can be highly optimized.
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** deskware@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-08T00:00:00

<a id="t-fbef3621e6"></a>
## Thread: RM/COBOL file copy problems — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### RM/COBOL file copy problems

- **From:** "Rob" <no.spam.pls@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.sco.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com>`

```
We're using RM/COBOL in our transaction processing systems and have taken to
periodically refreshing our test environment with production data. While
this refresh process generally works fine (we have a script that just copies
the files from our production environment to our test environment), on two
occasions we've had problems.

In one case, one of our big data files (over a gig) seems to have been
corrupted. The copy process did not report any errors and we have plenty of
disk space and i-nodes. The file was fine in production environment, but
appeared to have been truncated in our test environment and any applications
that tried to open that file would abend, effectively complaining that the
file was corrupted.

In another case several weeks later, a different, but equally large, data
file appears to have been truncated, too. But this time applications would
not complain about any problems, but the last 100,000+ records (about a
month worth of data, we've got several years of data in the file) were
missing. Again, no errors were reported during the refresh of our test
environment. Unfortunately, this time applications did not abend, so the
problem went undetected through all of our testing.

This is a vexing problem, because we can't reproduce it and we don't know
what the cause is. The only thing that I can dream of is that users might
still have files open, leaving the files in inconsistent states. In fact,
we've recently introduced nightly reporting of who still has what files open
(using the fuser command) and we've discovered that users haven't been
exiting applications properly. (With changes in our SOPs, the users are
logging off more consistently, now.) The problem with this theory is that I
suspected prior to executing this second conversion, so I actually rebooted
the system prior to doing the refresh, to effectively kick everyone off and
make sure that everything was clean. So I cannot imagine why the copying of
the data files didn't work.

Has anyone else seen strange problems copying RM/COBOL data files in SCO
Unix? Our environment is  RM/COBOL-85 - Version 6.09.01 for SCO Unix System
V R 3.2.2. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated: Our confidence in
our test environment (to say nothing of our production backups) has been
severely shaken, and we'd love to figure out what the problem is.

Thanks.

Rob
```

#### ↳ Re: RM/COBOL file copy problems

- **From:** "Rob" <no.spam.pls@execpc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.unix.sco.programmer
- **Message-ID:** `<37ffbddf$0$79673@news.execpc.com>`
- **References:** `<37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com>`

```
Needless to say, despite my comments about users being logged on, no one was
actually using the system during either incident. Both conversions were done
outside of business hours, though users had left their workstations without
logging off properly. Also, we do not have any background jobs that were
running, either.

Rob
```

```
Rob,

Not my bag because I'm not into multi-user. But I recall similar
problems with multi-user systems being corrupted,(quoted on the
NetExpress Answerlab). More often than not, the problem was related to
the server/multi-user software - not the compiler. You're looking at
RM/COBOL - just a suggestion, does the problem lie elsewhere ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00

```
machine for our testing environment, so that's good to know). I guess in the
future I will make the file verifications part of the refresh script, but it

problems, but it doesn't sound like it (at least so far).
```

```
In article <37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com>, "Rob" <no.spam.pls@execpc.com>
writes:

>
>Has anyone else seen strange problems copying RM/COBOL data files in SCO
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I can't say for any particular environment, but the re-boot of the server to 
knock everyone off before the copy is likely to have left the files in an
'open'
state.  I know that when cancelling a program that has an ISAM file open, 
the ISAM file is left with a flag in the header indicating an active status.
You may need to do a rebuild or some form of recovery process to reset 
the file before doing any backup or copy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: RM/COBOL file copy problems

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AWEAOD0euF3mLv6Oab9VET8oLS75@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com> <19991009213118.22857.00000110@ngol07.aol.com>`

```
On 10 Oct 1999 01:31:18 GMT, sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:

>In article <37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com>, "Rob" <no.spam.pls@execpc.com>
>writes:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>the file before doing any backup or copy.
If you reboot the machine then the file IS closed for the operating
system, and all buffers ARE written to the harddisk before shutting
down the machine.
The fact the the file has an internal flag with the count of logical
active "open" does not afect the copy of a file.

If he is missing some records is because the copy (operating system
dependent) did not work properly.
I have seen this happen in big files while copying across two machines
The only way to make sure that the copy is correct is to make
sure that nobody is using the file at the time of the copy, get the
sum of the file (in SCO is "sum -r") before the copy, and after the
copy do it again on the destination file and compare them.

And I also have to say that the previous times I saw this fail was not
with UNIX, but with Novell and WINNT, although I think it is not
directly related with the operating system...


FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RM/COBOL file copy problems

- **From:** ray@noisp.com (Raymond Leech)
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.126c4be7ca5e514098969f@news>`
- **References:** `<37ffa524$0$79676@news.execpc.com> <19991009213118.22857.00000110@ngol07.aol.com> <AWEAOD0euF3mLv6Oab9VET8oLS75@4ax.com>`

```
Frederico,
Painfully I have learned that the statement should be "If you reboot... 
files WILL BE closed". Under NT, we have found occasions when files are 
left open after the application is shut down and also after the 
workstation crashes the files can be left open for a while. Generally 
they are freed by the time a user logs back in. This is due to a 
registry entry on the server that allows closed files to be cached.

I know the original discussion was about SCO but thought maybe someone 
would find this tip useful.

ray

In article <AWEAOD0euF3mLv6Oab9VET8oLS75@4ax.com>, 
frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com says...
> If you reboot the machine then the file IS closed for the operating
> system, and all buffers ARE written to the harddisk before shutting
> down the machine.
```

```
We have about a hundred clients running various flavors of both RM/COBOL
and SCO Unix. The number of reported problems is very small compared to
what I hear from other platforms and languages. So while I can't give
you a direct answer to your question, I don't think you should worry
about language and operating system. Your problem must be related to the
copy process. With such big files, one user can modify the file while it
is beeing copied, so the copy could be inconsistent. You don't mention
any problems with the original files.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

```
It might help to run "vutil -i *" on the files from time to time to see if
they need to be rebuilt.  Note the user count, it should reflect ONLY the
number of users who currently have the file open, but can remain > 0 if the
systems crashes or the application/user doesn't close the file after use.
After a lot of i-o and record deletes, you can rebuild them with
vutil -rebuild -k0 -a *  .  Check your user's guide  for options to use in
your shop, YMMV.
```

```
It is the first time I ever heard that someone can work with the files
opened by others. I do not know how to solve that problems. In companies
I have worked with, the users have no access to permanent data.

Besides, the followings are some suggestions:
1- It may be hardware problems, rather than programming problems.
   Solution:  purchase new equipments.
2- Re-design your system:
   a- Interactive data files should always be temporary data files.
   b- Temporary data files are always created as new.
   c- Temporary data files should be finished and validated periodically.
   d- Temporary-permanent update process should be done
      by batch programming at a fixed schedule.
   e- All data files should have back up coppies and destroyed carefully.

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

<a id="t-867023f385"></a>
## Thread: Perform statement question — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Perform statement question

- **From:** "Peter Newman" <newmanpj@NOSPAMibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37ffbb2d_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
I've heard said that one should use PERFORM THRU for one or more paragraphs
instead one or more PERFORM section-name(s). Why? Isn't it merely matter of
convention or standards? I would have thought that PERFORM THRU was more
error prone. The compiler we're using is COBOL for MVS & VM. Sincerely
appreciate any direction on this.

Thanks, Peter Newman
please remove the NOSPAM is replying by email
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<37ffbb2d_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Peter Newman <newmanpj@nospamibm.net> wrote:
: I've heard said that one should use PERFORM THRU for one or more paragraphs
: instead one or more PERFORM section-name(s). Why? Isn't it merely matter of
: convention or standards? I would have thought that PERFORM THRU was more
: error prone. The compiler we're using is COBOL for MVS & VM. Sincerely
: appreciate any direction on this.

Both of these can be prone to a certain type of error which is
complete loss of control.  This is because both PERFORM section
and PERFORM THRU are done primarily to allow a paragraph name or
names within the performed scope to allow a GO TO to jump over
code or to get to an EXIT paragraph.

That is, if there is no such GO TO then there is no need to
PERFORM THRU or to PERFORM a section, PERFORMing a paragraph
works well.

Where there is a GO TO in the performed scope it is potentially
that the GO TO may be incorrectly coded, or the Exit-label may
be wrong.  This, of course, can be checked by processing the
source with a special program as Judson will tell you, but it
is not detected by the compiler and can give exceptionally
subtle bugs because the program may do strange things including
_almost_ working correctly.

PERFORM section also has a potential problem in that it is
terminated by the presence of the next section header.  If
this header is missed (ie the label is just a paragraph)
then the perform does not stop where expected.

It is best to program as per the site standards as this is
what the next programmer expects.  However, I have found
that just performing paragraphs (no thru, no section) and
thus no GO TO, is a much more reliable coding structure.
Others may find different results, especially if they have
invested in special checking programs that eliminate the
potential problems.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
>I have found
>that just performing paragraphs (no thru, no section) and
>thus no GO TO, is a much more reliable coding structure.

Now, hey, I was wondering about precisely this question.  My COBOL class
emphasizes structured programming.  They want us to have only one entrance and
exit for each sub-routine.  A forward GO TO is the only kind permitted, and
preferably it should be to an EXIT paragraph.

Great.  I get it.  I like it.  But I kept asking them, "Why do we then need to
use PERFORM THRU?"

The standard at my school is to code something that looks like this:

MAINLINE-PROCESS.
    PERFORM 100-INIT THRU 100-INIT-EXIT.
    PERFORM 200-READ THRU 200-READ-EXIT.
    PERFORM 300-PROCESS UNTIL EOF.

etc.

Now my question to them was, "if the return happens when the paragraph finishes
anyway, why on earth do I need the THRU?"  Indeed, as mentioned earlier in the
thread, it's very possible to mis-type those EXIT paragraph names.  I did it,
but my teachers still want me to abide by their standard.  I suppose there is
merit in learning to abide by a shop standard, but I thought that my instinct
must have just been wrong.  I'm gratified to hear some corroboration in the
group.

RoadRaat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
You might want to check Deja.Com for some of the threads on "exit immediate"
functionality. (In other words, how do I "get out quickly" from the middle of
a performed Paragraph (or Section).)  In the next Standard there is a feature
EXIT PARAGRAPH or EXIT SECTION which does exactly what you want (without a GO
TO).  Check your compiler, it may already support this feature - and then you
won't need any Whatever-Exit PARAGRAPHS (to go to). (However, you will be
using a technique that isn't available yet on all compilers.)

Now, to explain why you should NOT do what I think you are asking about.  If
I understand you, your teachers would have you code something like:

  Procedure Division.
    AAA.
        Perform BBB thru BBB-Exit
        Stop Run
          .
    BBB.
        Display "In BBB"
        If Some-Condition
             Go To BBB-Exit
        End-IF
        Display "Only get here when Some-Condition is false"
          .
    BBB-Exit
        Exit.
    CCC.
        Display "This program never gets here"
           .

   *****

What I think that you would like to code (and think would work the same way
is):

  Procedure Division.
    AAA.
        Perform BBB
        Stop Run
          .
    BBB.
        Display "In BBB"
        If Some-Condition
             Go To BBB-Exit
        End-IF
        Display "Only get here when Some-Condition is false"
          .
    BBB-Exit
        Exit.
    CCC.
        Display "This program MIGHT get here"
           .

      ***

But UNFORTUNATELY (and the reason that your teachers tell you to use THRU -
if you are going to use "forward GO TOs") is that the logic changes
DRAMATICALLY with this modification.  Once you "GO TO BBB-EXIT",  there is no
"return" from the original PERFORM - and you will "fall thru" to CCC.

Does this explain why they tell you to use the THRU (or did I misunderstand
what you wanted to do)?
```

```
GO

you

> using a technique that isn't available yet on all compilers.)
>
> Now, to explain why you should NOT do what I think you are asking about.
If
> I understand you, your teachers would have you code something like:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

   <snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tq3ca$f54$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com> <7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7tpd1c$df5$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
The new EXIT PARAGRAPH will go to an "implicit CONTINUE" immediately before
the end of the current paragraph.  This means that it will exit the current
paragraph (not a range of paragraphs) and then return to where the paragraph
was PERFORMed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tqmvd$ion$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com> <7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <7tpd1c$df5$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@compuserve.com> wrote:
:     I prefer to use thru paragraphs as below.  The biggest single cause of
: errors in this case is when I clone a paragraph range, and forget to change
: ALL of the go to exit's.  The EXIT PARAGRAPH statement will be a god send in
: this case.  Question - if I have a perform thru, will the EXIT PERFORM go to
: the end of the CURRENT paragraph, or the end of the LAST paragraph (in this
: thru group)?

If you have EXIT PARAGRAPH, and thus don't need a GO TO xxx-EXIT
then there is no need fot the exit paragraph and thus the THRU
is no longer necessary.  In this case the end of the current
paragraph and the last paragraph is always the same.

Of course if your code is spagetti in having several paragraph
labels within the THRU range then you may need to worry about
this question.  Given that the only purpose of labels within
a performed range is to act as targets of go to (unless you
do strange things such a perform an internal label and then
jump over it) if you don't have go tos other than to exit
yopu can just comment out those labels.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<140060C286B8EA16.C2E1CD75E14D1CB7.127FE6CD1F8D2A3F@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com> <7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sat, 9 Oct 1999 21:24:46 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>You might want to check Deja.Com for some of the threads on "exit immediate"
>functionality. (In other words, how do I "get out quickly" from the middle of
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
>what you wanted to do)?

I hate to correct someone so esteemed as Mr. Klein, but what you said
above is not correct.  The rule for the PERFORM verb (at least in the
IBM mainframe world) is:

If procedure-name-1 (in your example BBB) is a paragraph-name and
procedure-name-2 (another paragraph name used with the THRU option) is
NOT specified, control is returned after the execution of the last
statement of procedure-name-1 paragraph, which in you example is
Display "Only get here when Some-Condition is false" or Exit.  

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Get a New Car for Your Spouse. It'll Be a Great Trade.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tqpr3$h9n$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com> <7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <140060C286B8EA16.C2E1CD75E14D1CB7.127FE6CD1F8D2A3F@lp.airnews.net>`

```
You are correct that it would return - but ONLY in the case that
"Some-Condition" is false (in other words, the IF fails - so no GO TO is
performed).  If, HOWEVER, "Some Condition" is true - and the GO TO is
performed, then you will definitely NOT return from the PERFORM.  Try it and
you will see that it does work this way.

Now, there is a separate issue of whether the NEXT time you come thru the end
of the PERFORMed paragraph you do or do not "return" - no matter how you get
there.  This is where "stack-based" versus "perform-bucket" implementation is
used.  Therefore, if you had a "Go To BBB" at the end of the CCC paragraph -
some but NOT ALL - implementations would return back to the MAINLINE at the
end of the BBB paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tr1e8$gd5$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7toheo$27s$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <19991009210040.09854.00000305@ng-fb1.aol.com> <7totio$ge2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <140060C286B8EA16.C2E1CD75E14D1CB7.127FE6CD1F8D2A3F@lp.airnews.net> <7tqpr3$h9n$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7tr0jg$k7h$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
What does your compiler do with the following then,

  AAA.
    Perform BBB
    Display "After Perform"
    GoBack.
  BBB.
      Display "HERE"
      Go To CCC.
   BBB-Exit
      Exit.
    CCC.
        Display "In CCC" .
    DDD.
        Display "Fall thru Logic".

    ***

If you compiler does NOT "fall thru" - then it is a mind-reader and is
certainly NOT ANSI/ISO conforming.  If you compiler can figure out the
difference between GO TO BBB-EXIT versus GO TO CCC, then it has a "bunch" of
problems.
```

```
RoadRaat wrote:

…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

Want to throw a real zinger at those instructors ? Smile at them sweetly
and tell them the shop you tentatively have a job with is into OO COBOL
<G> Self-taught in COBOL, I have NEVER used PERFORM SECTIONS or THRU.

Switching to OO it was a dream - I was able to take chunks of
self-contained performs and just change them into methods. As a
generalization OO is in-line performs only, e.g. :-

	perform varying........

	  if A <> B
	     set LousyConditionFound to true
             Exit-Perform ( or can be Exit-Method )
	  End-if

	  Otherwise carry on processing........
	End-perform

with the very occasional perform paragraph in the same method, saving
the necessity of passing a bevy of parameters to a separate method, for
what is a very simple process (not conditional). It is valid syntax
within OO - but attempting PERFORM SECTION or THRU in OO defeats the
whole concept of methods.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tohq6$976$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<37ffbb2d_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
This is an area of "religious wars" that pop up every few months in
comp.lang.cobol.  People who are used to (like? advocate?) one approach often
can't even see the possibility of using other techniques.  In general,
however, I think that most "posters" do NOT like the use of PERFORM anything
THRU something-else - unless the "something-else" is an EXIT paragraph for
"anything" and there are no paragraph (or section) names in-between.  If you
have multiple paragraphs that you want performed in sequence - you do NOT
need to use the THRU option.  Instead, you can

A) perform a single paragraph - that in turn PERFORMs the individual
paragraphs (in sequence)

B) just put the PERFORMs of the individual paragraphs in sequence - where you
"thought about" using the PERFORM THRU.

  ***

Either of these techniques are significantly less "error-prone" than using
PERFORM/THRU - especially if you (or some future maintenance person) happens
to have a "GO TO" in the middle of the PERFORMed paragraphs.

For those who do advocate the use of SECTIONs instead of paragraphs (to be
performed), it is common (although not universal) to recommend AGAINST
putting multiple paragraphs (other than an EXIT paragraph) in each SECTION.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0cud44thu26@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<37ffbb2d_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
 PERFORM THRU is a kind of GO TO approach.
 Difficult to maintain. The flow of commands looks like a maze.
 Experienced programmers or the ones who rites it may be lost in it.
 There may be many EXITs and you never know which EXITs to get out.
 If you do not like GO TO, you should dislike THROUGH and EXIT.

 PERFORM THRU was more error prone? No. It runs well.
 It only give programmer a hard time, even to its creators.

 PERFORM A-MODULE (or A-ROUTINE) is structured approach.
 Each Module or Routine is a smallest unit of code,
 which is so small that no one can GO TO inside it.
 Therefore, there will not be a maze and no one will be lost in it.

  Structure approach is similiar to Function oriented in C programming
  of Object Oriented Design.

------------------  Posted via CNET Help.com  ------------------
                      http://www.help.com/
```

---

<a id="t-4ea43bf8cb"></a>
## Thread: Passing Parameters — 10 msgs

### Passing Parameters

- **From:** hank_heng@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,

  I am very new in Cobol language, hope that what I ask will not make
you laugh.. :)

  Anyway, can a variable being passing back to the caller?

  e.g :

  I have p1.cbl and p2.cbl.

  p1.cbl CALL p2.cbl and pass a variable to p2.cbl.

  So, can p2.cbl pass back a variable or change the contain of a
variable that being decalre in p1.cbl ?

  It is like return a value back to the caller program, how is it
posible ?

  Thanks for reading this, please help me out.

Hank


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There are two ways to pass a value "back" from a called subprogram to the
calling program.

Method 1 (and this is the MOST portable and will work with ALL COBOL
compilers)

1) Define a field in the Working-Storage of the CALLING program and then pass
it (BY REFERENCE - which is the default) to the CALLED subprogram.

2) Subprogram defines the field in its LINKAGE SECTION - and updates it - and
then returns  control to the CALLING program (via EXIT PROGRAM - or GoBack -
if your compiler has this - not all do)

3) The value that was updated in the called subprogram now is in the
Working-Storage field of the CALLING program.

    ***

Method 2 (not currently portable - but many compilers do support it)

Check to see if your compiler supports the RETURNING phrase on
  - the CALL statement, or
 - the Exit Program (or GoBack) statement, or
 - the Procedure Division header.

Depending upon which (if any) of these are supported by your compiler, you
can use this phrase to "pass bacK" a value (often restricted to a numeric
value) to the CALLING program from the CALLED subprogram.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38002abf.1022152@news.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Third method, portable depending on the COBOL version
used.

Define a variable in working (even file section)
of both programs with the same name and PIC as EXTERNAL.

ex: 01  W01-VALUE     PIC 9(8) EXTERNAL.

This way the variable contents is available for all 
programs that define this variable the same way.

FF


On Sat, 9 Oct 1999 00:48:18 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>There are two ways to pass a value "back" from a called subprogram to the
>calling program.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>value) to the CALLING program from the CALLED subprogram.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37FFF0DE.6A210B40@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38002abf.1022152@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Frederico Fonseca wrote:
> 
> Third method, portable depending on the COBOL version
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> FF

Please do not recommend this practice.  It creates a maintenance
nightmare.  I have coded like this in C and it has always caused more
problems than it solves.

rule 1:
This should only be used where efficiency comes into play.

rule 2:
Efficiency comes into play extremely rarely.  Passing a single pointer
around does not cost much and it makes the interface more explicit.


The easiest way to reuse code is to program to an interface.
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tp4hg$fph$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38002abf.1022152@news.indigo.ie> <37FFF0DE.6A210B40@zip.com.au>`

```
We have had previous discussions in comp.lang.cobol about whether using the
EXTERNAL attribute for "passing" parameters is good, bad, easy-to-maintain,
hard-to-maintain, error-prone, or whatever.  There is no UNIVERSAL consensus
on this - but what Ken says is certainly a very common view.

The one exception that I would make is that it is often QUITE useful to
define a FILE as "EXTERNAL" (in the FD definition).  I really, REALLY, like
being able to OPEN and CLOSE a file in one program - and then "process it"
(READ, WRITE, REWRITE, whatever) in another.  This often fits into my
"modular" application design.

The ONLY way to do this (in a Standard way) is by using the EXTERNAL
attribute (Well, GLOBAL would work - if the other programs were nested - but
that isn't the design that I am thinking about.)

Now, I know that it is possible to use
  A) ENTRY statements (not portable) for OPEN, READ, CLOSE, etc - all in the
same program
  B) A "switch" telling a single program what I/O operation to do.

However, I find that the EXTERNAL approach is SOMETIMES a much better way to
go.

Obviously, this is just a "personal opinion" and your shop (or individual)
standards may prohibit it - but it is at least a good target for EXTERNAL -
where there is no other "good" way to distribute your I/O among separate
programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "pacman" <mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tpl8c$u2$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38002abf.1022152@news.indigo.ie> <37FFF0DE.6A210B40@zip.com.au> <7tp4hg$fph$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Another use for external is when creating your own file drivers, (MF)
as the file driver is called during the open,read etc the variables cannot
be passed so the only way of passing variables is by external.
(tell me if i'm wrong)

But apart from these external is like a go to (should never be used apart
from if this is the only option), it can create many problems
which are nearly impossible to solve.





William M. Klein wrote in message <7tp4hg$fph$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>...
>We have had previous discussions in comp.lang.cobol about whether using the
>EXTERNAL attribute for "passing" parameters is good, bad, easy-to-maintain,
>hard-to-maintain, error-prone, or whatever.  There is no UNIVERSAL
consensus
>on this - but what Ken says is certainly a very common view.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>attribute (Well, GLOBAL would work - if the other programs were nested -
but
>that isn't the design that I am thinking about.)
>
>Now, I know that it is possible to use
>  A) ENTRY statements (not portable) for OPEN, READ, CLOSE, etc - all in
the
>same program
>  B) A "switch" telling a single program what I/O operation to do.
>
>However, I find that the EXTERNAL approach is SOMETIMES a much better way
to
>go.
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing Parameters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380008A1.2F42B435@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7tmgm5$oah$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7tml0c$j0g$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38002abf.1022152@news.indigo.ie> <37FFF0DE.6A210B40@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Frederico Fonseca wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> problems than it solves.<snip>

May I offer a suggestion allied to parameter passing for the
'youngsters' to the language; not the old hands who have established
their style. Lay down some ground rules how you pass parameters; in big
shops they may already have documented standards.

I do a lot of parameter passing in OO, reference to other classes and
file DB interfaces. I have a simple rule, (for myself) :-

	first - object references - alphabetized
	text (pic 9s or pic x)    - alphabetized, so :-

Linkage Section.
01 os-ClientsDBI		object reference.
01 os-DateandTime		object reference.
01 os-DescripsDBI		object reference.
01 os-MetricConversion		object reference.
01 os-ParentWindow		object reference.
01 os-ResourceFile		object reference.
01 mynum1			pic 9(04) comp-5
01 mynum2			pic 9(05) comp-5.
01 mytext1			pic x(10).
01 mytext2      		pic x(02).

Procedure Division using (above.....)

(Typically, the above 'handles' could be passed from a selection from
the Main Menu).

It sure saves you a lot of headaches. And in the case of OO, if you try
and access the wrong object, because your sequence is out of kilter -
Woweeee !

OK, if you are doing Windows API calls or other third-party calls, you
have no choice - you have to discipline yourself into their specific
parameter passing sequence.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
True, I am 'spelling them out' - but it is unambiguous and perhaps the

use things like anObject - but I hate it, OK when it's localized,
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> There are two ways to pass a value "back" from a called subprogram to the
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Interesting quote you give Bill - I've been away from structured now for
so long...

Merant OO - Do NOT use p1 as sending and receiving, otherwise you are
going to get indeterminate results, so :-

  Working(Local)-storage seciton.
  01 ws-Primekey.
     05 p-rectype		pic 9(02).
     05 p-key			pic x(04).
  01 ws-values.
     05 ws-result		pic x(4) comp-5.
     05 ws-CustomerName 	pic x(30).    

  invoke MyOtherClass "get-name" using  ws-PrimeKey
		      returning ws-values (or ls-values)

   if ws-result <> zeroes
      .............

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Hello,

Training software (CBT).
```

#### ↳ Re: CBT software for cobol

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991012225318.01540.00000331@ng-da1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<kBbM3.3382$j35.104816@newscontent-01.sprint.ca>`

```
>Can anyone lend me or tell me where i can get the software Computer Based
>Training software (CBT).

I'm not aware of a specific program called CBT.  My understanding is that CBT
is a generic term that stands for any computer based training.  I believe that
some training programs for COBOL are available for purchase, but there's some
very useful stuff available for free.  Try this...

http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/

The lecture notes on that page are Powerpoint slide presentation.  I think they
are a splendid tool for drilling and review.  Another great tool is the Fujitsu
COBOL compiler.  It's free and it comes with extensive documentation.

http://www.adtools.com

Alternatively, you can buy Thane Hubbell's book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
Hours"  published by Sams.  It includes the Fujitsu compiler and documentation
with it.

There are a lot of other good ideas in the FAQ maintained by the organizers of
this newsgroup.

RoadRaat
```

## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes: 
> Also note that the copyright date on that book is 1977.  This is 8 years
> before the '85 ANSI COBOL Standard and more than 10 years before the '89
> Amendment.

And 21 years before the latest PL/I compiler incorporating the Y2K
Millennium Language Extensions.

And -- please note -- the PL/I compiler received the Millennium
Language Extensions BEFRE the COBOL compiler did.

>  Also it was few years before the time that ANSI/ISO "gave up" on
> PL/I - and said that it was "functionally" frozen as a programming language

Well, if that is what they said,  they couldn't have been more wrong,
because PL/I received a host of enhancements in
the 1990s, including strong typing, etc,  new built-in functions.

> that they wouldn't be providing any future ANSI/ISO PL/I Standards (while
> COBOL continues to be supported in this "portable" ANSI and ISO environments.
> Similarly, COBOL is included in the UNIX X/Open Programming Specification -
> and PL/I is not.)

Nevertheless, PL/I is available on a range of Unix systems.

> Therefore, the comparison is significantly out of date -

Changes occurred in both languages, so one language is not
significantly advantaged or disadvantaged by time in the
comparison.

> and ignores portability

PL/I is relatively machine indpendent, and is highly portable.

> - but other than that it is a "nice" historical document!!!
> 
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tnr44$jiu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:
> > Also note that the copyright date on that book is 1977.  This is 8 years
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > PL/I - and said that it was "functionally" frozen as a programming
language
>
> Well, if that is what they said,  they couldn't have been more wrong,
> because PL/I received a host of enhancements in
> the 1990s, including strong typing, etc,  new built-in functions.
>

I see (from the last two updates) that we aren't really talking about
"PL/I" - but IBM's PL/I compilers.  That is true - that IBM has kept on
updating their product.  I particularly like the announcement that they made
this past Sept - for a compiler that had all sorts of "goodies" in it - with
the minor <G> problem that it is "object code incompatible" with previous IBM
Mainframe PL/I compilers.  Also, as far as I know, this NEW (incompatible)
version of IBM's mainframe PL/I compiler finally has support for ANSI/ISO
conforming PL/I (conforming to the now functionally stabilized compiler) even
though most IBM PL/I programmers "hate" having PL/I behave (or limited) to
Standard syntax and functionality.

Even so, I still would say that there have been MORE significant changes to
the IBM mainframe COBOLs than to the IBM mainframe PL/I compilers - since
1977.  However, that is a matter of opinion - but whatever you think about
the amount or type of change, it is certainly true that any comparison made
in 1977 has little to do with the language advantages, structure, or uses
today.

NOTE WELL: In general I do not participate in "language wars" - because I
really do think that it is both fruitless and like comparing apples to
christmas trees.  I only replied to this previous post because it was so
clearly out-of-date.

 --
 Bill Klein
     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com
```

<a id="t-94011afbf8"></a>
## Thread: small utility illustrating line sequential processing — 1 msgs

### small utility illustrating line sequential processing

- **From:** bberman@netbox.com (B Berman)
- **Date:** 1999-10-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37fe92e6.2756281@news.ntplx.net>`

```
Sometimes when I see a thread of interest developing in a newsgroup,
(this one or others) I will wait until it builds up a volume of
messages, and then save the entire thread to disk as a text file, for
reading later.  Unfortunately, many, many header lines from the
messages are saved as well, and interfere with reading, so I wrote a
small routine to strip them out.  I had originally written it in VB4,
and now in Fujitsu4.  I was amazed at how much faster it runs in
Cobol.


 identification division.
 program-id.  Filestrp.
 author.  b.berman.
 installation. p&w.
 date-written. August 1999.
 remarks.
* this program reads a line sequential txt file, strips out
*     unwanted lines, and outputs a new line sequential txt file.
*     It was specifically written to reformat a file consisting
*     of a saved newsgroup thread, and the unwanted lines are
*     all the various headers that are not necessary, and only
*     interfere with the readability of the file.
 environment division.
 configuration section.
 source-computer. ibm-pc with debugging mode.
 object-computer. ibm-pc.
 input-output section.
 file-control.
     SELECT TextFile-In ASSIGN TO Trimmed-Path-In-W
             ORGANIZATION IS line SEQUENTIAL
             FILE STATUS  IS FileStatus-In-W.
         
     SELECT TextFile-Out ASSIGN TO Trimmed-Path-Out-W
             ORGANIZATION IS line SEQUENTIAL
             FILE STATUS  IS FileStatus-Out-W.

 
 data division.
 file section.
*  the manual leads one to believe that text character strings
*      (lines) are limited to 80 characters, but this is not
*      true, and I have encountered lines of 570 characters
*      before the CRLF.

 FD  TextFile-In
     record is varying in size from 1 to 1000 characters
             depending on Record-Size-In-W
     data record is Record-In.
		
 01  Record-In.
     05  filler                  pic x
                                 occurs 1 to 1000 times
                                 depending on Record-Size-In-W.		
 FD  TextFile-Out
     record is varying in size from 1 to 1000 characters
     depending on Record-Size-Out-W
     data record is Record-Out.
		
 01  Record-Out.
     05  filler                  pic x	
                                 occurs 1 to 1000 times
                                 depending on Record-Size-Out-W.


 working-storage section.
 01  File-Work-Areas-W.
     05  PathName-In-W           pic x(255).
     05  FileStatus-In-W         pic 99.
     05  DriveName-In-W          pic x.
     05  Folder-In-W             pic x(255).
     05  FileName-In-W           pic x(255).
     05  PathName-Out-W          pic x(255).
     05  FileStatus-Out-W        pic 99.
     05  DriveName-Out-W         pic x.
     05  Folder-Out-W            pic x(255).
     05  FileName-Out-W          pic x(255).
     05  Input-EOF-Flag-W        pic x.
         88  Input-EOF                   value 'Y'.
     05  Input-Validation-Flag-W pic x.
         88  Input-Line-Valid            value 'Y'.
         88  Input-Line-Substitute       value 'S'.
         88  Input-Line-Skip             value 'N'.
     05  Record-Size-In-W        pic 9(4)    comp    value 0.
     05  Record-Size-Out-W       pic 9(4)    comp    value 0.
  		
 01  File-Name-Area-W.
     05  Trimmed-Path-In-W.
         09  Filler              pic x
                                 occurs 1 to 255 times
                                 depending on Path-Cntr-W.
     05  Trimmed-Path-Out-W.
         09  filler              pic x
                                 occurs 1 to 255 times
                                 depending on Path-Cntr-W.
  
 01  Program-Counters-W.
     05  Input-Line-Cntr-W       pic s9(7)   comp-3  value +0.
     05  Output-Line-Cntr-W      pic s9(7)   comp-3  value +0.
     05  Path-Cntr-W             pic s9999   comp    value +0. 

 linkage section.
 screen section.

 procedure division.

 Begin-it.
     initialize  File-Work-Areas-W.
     initialize  Program-Counters-W.
     move  'N'  to  Input-EOF-Flag-W.
     perform  Get-File-Names.
     open 	input 	TextFile-In.
     open	 output  TextFile-Out.
		
     perform  Read-Textfile-In.
     perform  Process-Input-File  until  Input-EOF.
		
     perform Terminate-Routine.
     goback.	

		
 Process-Input-File.
*  this is the main routine which examines the input line for
*  any of the keywords which identify a header line.  If a
*  header line is detected, the line is skipped.  If the
*  beginning of a new posting is encountered, a separator block
*  of 3 lines is output.  Because some lines consist only
*  of a CRLF, where Fujitsu reads a record with 0 length, it
*  cannot handle writing a 0 length record, so 1 blank charater
*  is added to each record to avoid abending on the write.

     perform  Check-Input-Line.
     evaluate  True
         when  Input-Line-Valid
             move  Record-Size-In-W  to  Record-Size-Out-W
                 add  1  to  Record-Size-Out-W
             move  Record-In  to  Record-Out
             perform  Write-TextFile-Out
         when  Input-Line-Substitute
             move  5  to  Record-Size-Out-W
             move  spaces  to  Record-Out
             perform  Write-TextFile-Out
             move  40  to  Record-Size-Out-W
             move  '---------------------------------------'
                                 to  Record-Out
             perform  Write-TextFile-Out
             move  5  to  Record-Size-Out-W
             move  spaces  to  Record-Out
             perform  Write-TextFile-Out
         when  other
             continue
     end-evaluate.
     perform  Read-Textfile-In.

		
 Check-Input-Line.
*  these character strings identify header records.  The x'1a'
*  is a character that I occasionally encounter.  It is an
*  unprintable character, with nothing else on the line, so the
*  line is skipped.

     if  Record-In (1:1)  =  x'1A'
             or
         Record-In (2:1)  =  x'1A'
             or
         Record-In (1:3)  =  'To:'
             or
         (Record-In (1:5)  =  'Xref:'  or
                              'Path:')
             or
         Record-In (1:6)  =  'Lines:'
             or
         (Record-In (1:7)  =  'Sender:'  or
                              'X-Auth:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:8)  =  'X-Trace:'  or
                              'X-Cache:'  or
                              'X-Admin:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:9)  =  'Reply-To:'  or
                              'X-Mailer:'  or
                              'X-Notice:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:10)  =  'X-Mimeole:'  or
                               'X-MimeOLE:'  or
                               'X-MIMEOLE:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:11)  =  'Newsgroups:'  or
                               'Message-Id:'  or
                               'Message-ID:'  or
                               'References:'  or
                               'X-ELN-Date:'  or
                               'X-Original-'  or
                               'X-Priority:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:12)  =  'Followup-To:'  or
                               'X-Auth-User:'  or
                               'X-Post-Time:'  or
                               'In-Reply-To:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:13)  =  'Mime-Version:'  or
                               'X-Newsreader:'  or
                               'Organization:'  or
                               'MIME-Version:'  or
                               'Content-Type:'  or
                               'X-No-Archive:'  or
                               'X-Abuse-Info:'  or
                               'Distribution:'  or
                               'X-Http-Proxy:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:14)  =  'X-Problems-To:'  or
                               'X-MyDeja-Info:'  or
                               'X-NETCOM-Date:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:16)  =  'X-Complaints-To:'  or
                               'Cache-Post-path:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:18)  =  'NNTP-Posting-Host:'  or
                               'Nntp-Posting-Host:'  or
                               'X-Posted-Path-Was:'  or
                               'NNTP-Posting-Date:'  or
                               'Nntp-Posting-Date:'  or
                               'X-MSMail-Priority:'  or
                               'X-Accept-Language:'  or
                               'X-Http-User-Agent:')
             or
         (Record-In (1:20)  =  'X-NNTP-Posting-Host:'  or
                               'X-Nntp-Posting-Host:')
             or
         Record-In (1:23)  =  'http://www.dejanews.com' 
             or
         Record-In (1:24)  =  'X-Article-Creation-Date:'
             or
         Record-In (1:26)  =  'Content-Transfer-Encoding:'

             or
         Record-In (1:28)  =  '-----== Posted via Deja News'

             move 'N' to Input-Validation-Flag-W
     else  if  Record-In (1:6)  =  'From -'
             move  'S'  to  Input-Validation-Flag-W
     else
             move 'Y' to Input-Validation-Flag-W
     end-if.                     
          
		
 Read-Textfile-In.
     read  TextFile-In
         at end
             display  'Input EOF File Status = '  FileStatus-In-W 
                 move  'Y'  to  Input-EOF-Flag-W
         not  at end
             add  +1  to  Input-Line-Cntr-W
     end-read.

		
 Write-Textfile-Out.
     write  Record-Out.
     if  FileStatus-Out-W  =  '00'
         add  +1  to  Output-Line-Cntr-W
     else
         display  'Output Write File Status = '  FileStatus-Out-W

         display  '*****   PROGRAM ERROR   *****'
         goback
     end-if.

		
 Terminate-Routine.
     move  5  to  Record-Size-Out-W.
     move  spaces  to  Record-Out.
     perform  Write-TextFile-Out.
     move  40  to  Record-Size-Out-W.
     move  '---------------------------------------'
                                 to  Record-Out.
     perform  Write-TextFile-Out.

     close  TextFile-In	TextFile-Out.
     display  ' '.
     display  ' '.
     display  'Input Lines Read  = '  Input-Line-Cntr-W. 
     display  'Output Lines Written = '  Output-Line-Cntr-W. 
     display  '*****   Successful Program End   *****'.

		
	Get-File-Names.
*  this routine accepts the input path and file name from the
*  console.  It assumes drive c:, and a file extension of .txt.
*  The output file name is the same as the input, with the
*  addition of the letter 's' appended to the filename.  Note
*  that Fujitsu seems to require 8.3 path and file names, and
*  abends with long path/filenames.  I'm not sure if it was
*  necessary to right trim the resulting path/file name,
*  but I did it anyways.

     display  'key in the input folder path (no embedded spaces),'
                                 ' and press enter'.
     accept  Folder-In-W  from  console.
     display  'key in the input file name (no embedded spaces),'
                                 ' and press enter'.
     accept  FileName-In-W  from  console.
		
     string  'c:\'  delimited by size	
             Folder-In-W  delimited by space
             '\'  delimited by size 
             FileName-In-W  delimited by space
             '.txt'  delimited by size
             into  PathName-In-W.
     move  +0  to  Path-Cntr-W.
     inspect  PathName-In-W  tallying Path-Cntr-W
             for  characters  before  initial space.
     move  PathName-In-W (1:Path-Cntr-W)  to  Trimmed-Path-In-W.
		
     string  'c:\'  delimited by size 	
             Folder-In-W  delimited by space 	
             '\'  delimited by size 
             FileName-In-W  delimited by space
             's.txt'  delimited by size
             into  PathName-Out-W.
     move  +0  to  Path-Cntr-W.
     inspect  PathName-Out-W  tallying  Path-Cntr-W
             for  characters  before  initial space.
     move  PathName-Out-W (1:Path-Cntr-W)  to  Trimmed-Path-Out-W.



----------------------------------------------------------------------
                  Bob Berman   -    West Hartford, CT                 
 mailto:bberman@netbox.com    website: http://www.ntplx.net/~bberman  
                                                                      
                  THE TRUE TERRORISTS IN AMERICA ARE                  
          THE PEOPLE WHO DEMAND TO SEE THE STORE MANAGER !            
----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+
```

```
OK Guys. My comments and then the messages. English version first and
then tail-end Portugese version.

The easy part :-

think an example from Thane's book will answer it.

The slightly harder part :-

Nilo, knowing that Jose has PC_PRINT routines with Visoc I am curious
why he makes reference to COBOL '74 Standards. Explain that the OO
program that I sent to him and you, can be a structured program and
still invoke(call) an OO Program OR he could take my PC_PRINT OO program
and turn it into a 'structuered' and even use entry-points. 

I sense from his message that he thinks he has to define the printer in
a File Section  - No - if his HP is his default printer, PC_PRINT when
he sends the "Open printer" will automatically pick-up the device driver
for his HP from Windows - alternatively he could use the Print Common
Dialog to select another HP printer. My example(Desprint.cbl) does not
need a reference to the printer, just a handle (object reference) to the
program/class PrintFile so that he can invoke it.

The only thing he needs to do in my example(DesPrint.cbl) is specify his
print-lines 

e.g 01 myLine1		pic x(80).
    01 myline2	        pic x(120).

    move "Jose Capucho is a good hombre - I think ?" to MyLine1
    move length of Myline1 to PrintBufferLen
    move MyLine1           to PrintBuffer
    invoke os-Printfile "print-and-newline" using PrintBufferLen
						  PrintBuffer
					    returning ws-ErrorCode

    move "Portugese is a wonderful language <G>" to MyLine2
    etc....

will be OK.
```

## Thread: meaning of error? — 10 msgs

### meaning of error?

- **From:** Babak Behnia <b-behnia@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu>`

```
Hi,

I'm getting an error message at run-time and I don't know what it means.

    File error 35 on filename
    COBOL error at 000060 in cbl.out

I'm using Cobol 85.  Also, can anyone direct me to sample code dealing
with creating
relative files from sequential files and updating the relative files?
What's a good site to
learn about sequential, indexed, and relative files?

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: meaning of error?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZHvM3.314$o94.27578@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu>`

```
3 + 5
    3 = permanent error condition
    5 = "The OPEN statement in INPUT, I-O, or EXTEND mode was
        executed for a file without the OPTIONAL clause specification
        The file has yet to be created, however"

In other words, you attempted to open a file in anticipation of
reading it and there was no file there.

One source for these IO status codes (assuming they don't
exist in your textbook) is www.adtools.com. Other vendors
of compilers no doubt have the messages on their web sites.


Babak Behnia <b-behnia@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu> wrote in message
news:380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu...
> Hi,
>
> I'm getting an error message at run-time and I don't know what it
means.
>
>     File error 35 on filename
>     COBOL error at 000060 in cbl.out
>
> I'm using Cobol 85.  Also, can anyone direct me to sample code
dealing
> with creating
> relative files from sequential files and updating the relative
files?
> What's a good site to
> learn about sequential, indexed, and relative files?

I don't know of any 'sample' code sites for this. Even if you found
one,
you would go nuts trying to figure it out. Here's how.

Create Relative File:
    OPEN INPUT SEQUENTIAL-FILE
    OPEN OUTPUT RANDOM-FILE.
    PERFORM VARYING RANDOM-RECNO FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
        INPUT-FILE-STATUS NOT = '00'
        READ INPUT-FILE NOT AT END
            WRITE RANDOM-REC FROM INPUT-REC
            END-READ
    END-PERFORM.
    CLOSE INPUT-FILE.
    CLOSE RANDOM-FILE.

To update a relative file:

OPEN I-O RANDOM-FILE.


MOVE record-number TO RANDOM-RECNO.
READ RANDOM-FILE.
do some processing
REWRITE RANDOM-REC.

Hope this helps.
```

```
>     3 = permanent error condition
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

FYI, (and I know that Jerry P already knows this)
  You can use almost any (ANSI'85) compiler's documentation for getting file
status codes and what they mean AS LONG AS the first digit isn't "9".  In the
case of 9x file status codes, you MUST check the documentation for the
specific compiler that you are using (as these are "implementor defined" and
really do vary from implementor to implementor).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Implementor defined file status codes (Locking methods) - was Re: meaning of error?

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3802b964.513716944@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu> <ZHvM3.314$o94.27578@typhoon01.swbell.net> <7tu3mr$qhf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 11 Oct 1999 20:42:14 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
>news:ZHvM3.314$o94.27578@typhoon01.swbell.net...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just another general FYI,

I've been writing code using Realia for years.  I've written new
programs using Fujitsu COBOL - but until recently I had not converted
any code.

My Realia programs use Lock Mode Exclusive.

Talk about meaning two different things with the two compilers!  (I'm
not complaining, just a heads up).

With Realia, a file status 9A was a successful open on a file with
exclusive access specified.  Multiple programs can open a file input,
but only one for IO or Output.  

A status of 9L meant the file was locked.

I would look when encountering 9L's until the file was unlocked, or I
gave up.

With Fujitsu, if you leave the Lock Mode Exclusive specified, it's
REALLY EXCLUSIVE - only one program can open the file at a time.  A
lock is indicated by file status 93.  There is no 9A equivalent (nor
need there be).

To get the same behavior as the Realia programs with Fujitsu one must
NOT specify ANY lock mode.  It was easy to find out how to handle it
from reading the user manual, but my first attempt was to test the
programs running side by side reading the same file.  Boy was I
suprised to see the 93 when both programs opened the file INPUT.

No on to more happy conversion......


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: meaning of error?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3802AC81.76984063@home.com>`
- **References:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu>`

```


Babak Behnia wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks in advance.

We keep getting confused by this one "I'm using COBOL 85" it's the last
Standard issued not the 'name' of the compiler you are using.

First suggestion, as you are university-based, any of the good COBOL
text books in your library will cover the following in much greater
detail.

Your Error 35 - (unless it's something compiler specific), if it is a
file-error -  I get Open/Input/External non-optional - check the
text-books on file-status errors. 

You need, again from the text books, to get a handle on the difference
between sequential and relative.

Sequential - unordered :-

If I enter invoices into an A/P system, and assuming they are entered as 
follows :-

	Green Giant
	Badcombe & Widdock
	Heinz
	Cadburys etc....

That's the sequence they are in your 'unordered' sequential file.
As you'll appreciate the file can be sorted to make them ascending on
descending on certain fields. Primarily you are using sequentials for 
temporary/intermim transactions.

Now to relative. I first queried merits of 'relative' back on the old
Compuserve MicroFocus Forum. Michael Mattias here and Pete Dashwood
responded. Pete pointed out that relative had its day in early 60's
before indexed(ISAM) came into play. Michael favours using Relatives as
temporary working files.

With Relatives you don't have a key, but use an external key, e.g
ws-Relative-Key Pic 9(03) or whatever, in Working Storage. Your records 
are stored using that ws-Key pointing to a 'physical' position in the
file. The disadvantage of relatives can be that if you write your first
record giving it key = 100 then you finish up with a chunk of space
where records 1 through 99 would fit.

I don't know under what circumstances you would want to do what you are
asking about, but if it is a test, here's the pseudo code.

  ws-RelKey	pic (03).

  first make sure your Sequential and Relative BOTH have file-names
  initialize ws-RelKey
  Open sequential as INPUT
  Open relative as OUTPUT
  perform read sequential until end
  move sequential-info to relative-record
  add 1 to ws-RelKey
  write relative-record
  AND close both files before STOP RUN

Now here's the problem - there is no index or key system for the
Relative-File - if you want to get at Record-22 you've got to know it is
Record-22 -

move 22 to ws-RelKey 
Read relative record,
change it then rewrite it.

Now take it from a topic with which you will be familiar. Your Registrar
has a file of students. Assuming there are only ever 100 then he
allocates the next number. Somebody leaves, he deletes their info and
re-uses the number. But that's no good, because he may need to check up
on Student 22 = Was Joe Blow and is now 22 = Mickey Mantle.

Doesn't work. And in your case let's assume 4,000 on campus at one time,
Registrar allocates next number and keeps on going but he does it for an
Indexed File. If he did it with Relative the file gets HUGEeeee... and
even if he deletes records from a Relative the space is still there.
Don't know how Registrars do it but suggest they probably have a file
covering current students and probably hive off the dead-ones (those no
longer at the university, not just the ones in the cemetery), and if he
gets a query, refers to the old 'condensed' history file.

As I said - check the text books - but hope above is a reasonable intro.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Thanks again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: meaning of error?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JYaP3.396$7X2.33006@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu> <3802AC81.76984063@home.com> <3802B2CF.FB4422F9@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu>`

```

Babak Behnia <b-behnia@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu

> wrote in message news:3802B2CF.FB4422F9@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu...
> Thank you Jerry P, WM Klein, and JJ Gavan.
>
> You've helped me clarify some questions I've had about the files.
The class
> textbook is really not that good. The textbook doesn't list the
runtime error
> meanings.
>
WARNING - Runtime errors are different from file status errors. The
file
status error you originally asked about could be normal (If the file
is not
present, create it - if it is present, extend it). Runtime errors are
abnormal, usually catastrophic, and usually terminate the program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: meaning of error?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1b5e$83df52b0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<380165CC.20651A85@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu> <3802AC81.76984063@home.com> <3802B2CF.FB4422F9@mrel.beckman.uiuc.edu> <JYaP3.396$7X2.33006@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in article
<JYaP3.396$7X2.33006@typhoon01.swbell.net>...
> WARNING - Runtime errors are different from file status errors. The
> file
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> abnormal, usually catastrophic, and usually terminate the program.

Runtime errors are all errors that occur at runtime.  File status errors
belong to this class.

Gunnar.
```

<a id="t-b02e6c82b0"></a>
## Thread: Multi-dimensional table SEARCH correction — 3 msgs

### Multi-dimensional table SEARCH correction

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yx6M3.1979$Z7.66228@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
10/10/99
>Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net>  [me]  wrote in message
><751L3.51$7p6.3304@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>...

 [ in response to a question about seraching a table which itself is
subordinate to an OCCURS clause]

>> If UZCI-PRIOAREA OCCURS 2,  the new search should work fine if you change
>> all the subordinate references (e.g., PRIO-LINE-NO and PRIO-PRIORITY) to
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Well, I should have tested, because I made a big boo-boo here.

While you *do* need to add the second index in the WHEN clause, you
do NOT search a subscripted or  indexed area using the subscript; you set
the subscripts only in the WHEN clause of the search.

Corrected code follows, and sorry for any extra time spent by anyone
following this road.

Michael Mattias
Racine WI USA



       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. IDXTEST.
       AUTHOR. MCM.
       SECURITY. Demo search of multi-indexed table
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 FLAGS-AND-SWITCHES.
           05 PROGRAM-STATUS-SW           PIC X(01) VALUE 'Y'.
              88 PROGRAM-STATUS-OK VALUE 'Y'.
       01 ASSORTED-CONSTANTS.
           05 YES-LITERAL                 PIC X(01) VALUE 'Y'.
           05 NO-LITERAL                  PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
           05 ONE                         PIC 9(02) VALUE 1.

    * HERE IS THE NEWSGROUP TABLE SEARCH W_S

       01  FILLER.
           03  UCZIPRIO-AREA OCCURS 2 INDEXED BY ICZI-IDX.
             05 PRIO-MAX                  PIC 999 VALUE 300.
             05 PRIO-TABLE.
                07 PRIO-TAB   OCCURS 300
                     DESCENDING KEY PRIO-LINENO
                     INDEXED BY PRIO-IX.
                   10 PRIO-LINENO       PIC S9(07) COMP-3.
                   10 PRIO-PRIORITY PIC 99.


      * LINKAGE SECTION.
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        000-MAIN.
              DISPLAY 'Hello World'.
              SET PRIO-IX TO 1.
              SEARCH PRIO-TAB
                AT END
                   DISPLAY 'AT END OF SEARCH'
                WHEN PRIO-PRIORITY ( 1 PRIO-IX) = 'XX'
                   DISPLAY 'FOUND PRIO-PRIORITY = XX'
              END-SEARCH.
              STOP RUN.
```

```
> First off, the question was HOW TO SORT A 'NESTED TABLE',    N O T  a
table

 With my problems reading the NG, I haven't been able to see all of
this thread.  If the real question is "how do I sort a table?"  -
nested or otherwise - then it is time for me to "plug" the draft of the
next COBOL Standard which includes an option of the SORT statement to
sort any table (nested or otherwise).

I know that Micro Focus already supports this - and other vendors may
too.  Check your compiler's documentation for current availability.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: Q: "Invoke-the-EXE" in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL... — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Q: "Invoke-the-EXE" in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL...

- **From:** jgwolfe@netcom.ca (Gerry Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3800bee3.4607143@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```
Yet another question from "the lurker who's now getting into
PowerCOBOL"...

BACKGROUND:

I'm building a PowerCOBOL application, and have the need to
asynchronously fire off a batch task.  I'm using r4.0

Checked the hardcopy that came with the product, the PDF softcopy and
the online HELP and have found no reference to anything which looks
like it could do what I need.

Checked into the ADTOOLS website, and came across the "Invoke-The-EXE"
item under the FAQs.  Altho referencing r4.2, I think this should also
work under 4.0 since it is simply CALLing stuff (syntax has not
changed).

However, I am getting link errors since "Invoke-The-EXE" does CALLs to
"CreateProcessA", "WaitForSingleObject" and "CloseHandle"; these are
not found.

QUESTIONS:

Q1: Anyone know where I can find source or OBJ for these CALLed
modules?
Q2: Anyone out there done what I am trying to do -- examples available
under r4.0?

REWARD:

For reading this post... a cutesy joke:

AIBOHPHOBIA: Fear of palindromes

As always,
advTHANKSance, Gerry
```

```
> Yet another question from "the lurker who's now getting into

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> modules?
## It's in the DDE control.

> Q2: Anyone out there done what I am trying to do -- examples
available
> under r4.0?

 Move 'Text' of TXT-PGM-PATH-NAME to CMD-LINE.
  INVOKE CMDDE1 'EXECUTE'  USING CMD-LINE POW-SWSHOWNORMAL
        RETURNING RETURNVALUE.
```

```
path.  (Each compiler has calls for this including Fujitsu, Realia and
MicroFocus).  I save these and then SET them to current (with
corresponding calls) upon return from the call to execute another
program or the open file dialog box.  It was very frustrating for a
```
