On Mon, 11 Oct 1999 04:40:10 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

Here's what I did... (relevant code only):

01 CommandLine pic x(29) value 	'E:\UserData\CMIS\CMIS5100.BAT'.

	Invoke CmDDEBat "OpenDDE"
		Returning ReturnValue
	Invoke CmDDEBat "Execute"
		Using	CommandLine
			POW-SWHIDE
		Returning ReturnValue
	Invoke CmDDEBat "CloseDDE"

Compile was successful.  The error I received at execution time was "A
data link with the server cannot be established ErrorCode=4009".  I
checked the properties from the DDE sample program I used as a source,
and the only different property is that my CmDDEBat has DDEFormatName
of CF_Text (option mentioned CSV Excel, which I thought
inappropriate).

Looks like the error is in the OpenDDE area.  

Any help, again, is appreciated.

BTW, thanks to all who have been providing some assistance in
flattening my learning curve.  As you can see, the level of my
questions is getting higher, so maybe the end is in sight.

#### ↳ Re: "Invoke-the-EXE" in Fujitsu PowerCOBOL...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00

- **References:** `<3800bee3.4607143@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```
Those called modules are part of the Windows-32 API. They should not be
linked to your program; they should be loaded at run-time when the called
program is summoned into action. Can you link with a "call dynamically"
option of some sort?

Gerry Wolfe wrote in message <3800bee3.4607143@nntp.netcom.ca>...
>However, I am getting link errors since "Invoke-The-EXE" does CALLs to
>"CreateProcessA", "WaitForSingleObject" and "CloseHandle"; these are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>modules?
```

- **Message-ID:** `<7tv7p1$8j1$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3800bee3.4607143@nntp.netcom.ca>`

```
000010 @OPTIONS NOALPHAL
Put the above as the first statement in your code. The ampersand should be
in column 7.  Your problem is that the routines you need to link to are case
specific. whereas the default in Cobol is not case-specific.  The above
option changes that, and is required for most links to C.

<a id="t-6f3e65c96f"></a>
## Thread: Sort question — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Sort question

- **From:** Darlene Jessup <wrinkles@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380071C5.A4993C8E@bellsouth.net>`

```
Hello I am having trouble with this program, I am trying to sort three
differnt files, then merge them together and compare the merged sorted
file with a transaction file, if the cliend id's match then the
requested info from the transaction file is to be crossreferenced with
the master file and the appropriate info is to be sent to a transaction
report. Errors go to an error report. Could any one please look at this
and give me some pointers? Or at least help me look for the correct
things to fix it. I would appreciate it and if anyone out there in
beginning programming is having trouble I did pretty good in that.

Thank you for the help
darlene
wrinkles@bellsouth,net
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort question

- **From:** Susan Allen <Susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3803B6CA.7E8E5B37@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<380071C5.A4993C8E@bellsouth.net>`

```
Darlene Jessup wrote:
> 
> Hello I am having trouble with this program, I am trying to sort three
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> wrinkles@bellsouth,net

being a simple person I sort my files before I process them
then in the set up

perform do-set-up-stuff thru set-up-stuff-end
perform read-file-a     thru read-file-a-end
perform read-file-b     thru read-file-b-end
perform compare-files   thru compare-files-end
                       until eof-a and eof-b
perform end-job-stuff   thru end-job-stuff-end
goback.


compare-files.
*** note this assumes file-a has only one record to match to file-b ***

if match-key-a = match-key-b
    perform keys-equal   thru keys-equal-end
    perform read-file-a  thru read-file-a-end
    perform read-file-b  thru read-file-b-end
else
     if match-key-a > match-key-b
	perform unmatched-b  thru unmatatched-b-end
        perform read-file-b  thru read-file-b-end
     else
	perform unmatched-a  thru unmatatched-a-end
        perform read-file-a  thru read-file-a-end	
     end-if
end-if.

truth be told, I usually code the read in the equal, high or low logic
paragraphs (which would have the appropriate processing, matching,
formating or writing steps, any required counters and so forth).

for three file matching (matching 3 files) is more complex and if you
have multiple correct matches then I would add add a flag test and if
the low-record was a match in the previous test, let it go, but if it
was never matched, then error code and report.

good luck -- Susan
```

```
Logically removes a record from a disk file. It can be specified only for
files with relative or indexed organization.

Opcode Operation File Types
F7 DELETE vS  R  I
Input Fields

Offset 43 Non-sequential access to relative record number (R)

Offset 56 Non-sequential access to record pointer (I)

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status
```

- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Releases all record locks in all files held by this run unit.

Opcode Operation File Types
DC COMMIT (UNLOCK all files) L  S  R  I
Input Fields

Always requires FCD used by the file.

Output Fields

None

Physically removes the specified file from the physical devices on which it
resides.

Opcode Operation File Types
F8 DELETE FILE L  S  R  I
Input Fields

Filename area

Offset 5 File organization

Offset 11 Filename length
Offset 34 File format
Offset 60 Pointer to the filename area

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status
```

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trh0k$2uu$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Initiates the processing of files. It also performs checking and/or writing
of labels and other I/O operations.

Opcode Operation File Types
00 OPEN INPUT L  S  R  I
01 OPEN OUTPUT L  S  R  I
02 OPEN I-O L  S  R  I
03 OPEN EXTEND L  S  R  I
04 OPEN INPUT WITH NO REWIND L  S
05 OPEN OUTPUT WITH NO REWIND L  S
08 OPEN INPUT REVERSED L  S
Input Fields

Filename area
Key definition block (I)

Offset 5 File organization

Offset 6 Access mode
Offset 7 Open mode
Offset 11 Length of filename
Offset 24 Lock mode flags
Offset 25 Other flags
Offset 34 File format
Offset 38 Maximum record length
Offset 47 Recording mode
Offset 50 Minimum record length
Offset 60 Pointer to the filename area
Offset 64 Pointer to the key definition block (I)
Offset 78 Data compression
Offset 91 Interlanguage locking

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status

Offset 7 Open mode
FCD Use for all subsequent accesses to this file

Opening a File More Than Once

You can assign several FCDs to the same physical file and have them all open
at the same time. The operating system counts it as just one open file. The
physical file is not closed until every logical file assigned to it has been
closed.
```

of labels and other I/O operations.

Offset 34 File format

Offset 64 Pointer to the key definition block (I)

Offset 91 Interlanguage locking

Output Fields

Offset 7 Open mode
FCD Use for all subsequent accesses to this file

Opening a File More Than Once

You can assign several FCDs to the same physical file and have them all open
at the same time. The operating system counts it as just one open file. The
physical file is not closed until every logical file assigned to it has been

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trh1e$vot$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For sequential access, READ makes available the next or previous logical
record from a file. For random access, READ makes available a specified
record from a disk file.

Opcode Operation File Types
8D READ(sequential) WITH NO LOCK L  S  R  I
D8 READ (sequential) WITH LOCK L  S  R  I
D9 READ (sequential) WITH KEPT LOCK L  S  R  I
F5 READ (sequential) L  S  R  I
8C READ PREVIOUS WITH NO LOCK R  I
DE READ PREVIOUS WITH LOCK R  I
DF READ PREVIOUS WITH KEPT LOCK R  I
F9 READ PREVIOUS R  I
8E READ (random) WITH NO LOCK R  I
DA READ (random) WITH LOCK R  I
DB READ (random) WITH KEPT LOCK R  I
F6 READ (random) R  I
8F READ (direct) WITH NO LOCK L  S  R  I
D6 READ (direct) WITH LOCK L  S  R  I
D7 READ (direct) WITH KEPT LOCK L  S  R  I
C9 READ (direct) L  S  R  I
F1 READ (position) L  S  R  I
Input Fields

Offset 43 Relative record number (R) if READ random or direct

Offset 52 Key identifier (I)
Offset 56 Pointer to the record area

Output Fields

Record area

Offset 0 User file status

Offset 48 Current record length
Offset 13 or 72 Relative byte address

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>

```
Provides a basis for logical positioning in a relative or indexed file, for
subsequent retrieval of records.

Opcode Operation File Types
E8 START equal to full length prime key R  I
E9 START equal to (any key/record number) R  I
EA START greater than (>) R  I
EB START not less than (>=) R  I
FE START less than (<) R  I
FF START less than or equal to (<=) R  I
Input Fields

Offset 43 Relative record number (relative files)

Offset 52 Key identifier (indexed files)
Offset 54 Effective key length (indexed files)
Offset 56 Pointer to the record area

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status
```

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trh3b$1l6$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Steps to the next physical record. This operation enables very fast access
to a record.

Opcode Operation File Types
90 STEP NEXT WITH NO LOCK L  S  R  I
D4 STEP NEXT WITH LOCK L  S  R  I
D5 STEP NEXT WITH KEPT LOCK L  S  R  I
CA STEP NEXT L  S  R  I
92 STEP FIRST WITH NO LOCK L  S  R  I
D0 STEP FIRST WITH LOCK L  S  R  I
D1 STEP FIRST WITH KEPT LOCK L  S  R  I
CC STEP FIRST L  S  R  I
Input Fields

Offset 56 Pointer to the record area

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status

STEP operations are a method of sequentially retrieving records in a
relative or indexed file without having to read via a key or relative record
number. Generally, this is a faster method of accessing data in the file.

A STEP NEXT operation returns the record that is physically stored
immediately after the one that was last retrieved in the file (either by a
STEP or READ operation).

If you are using relative byte addressing and you have specified that the
current record pointer should be updated to the record you have just
accessed, STEP operations are relative to this new position.

The relative byte address is returned on every STEP operation, so if you
need to update the current record pointer to the record just retrieved, you
can use the READ (direct) operation using the address returned.

Note:

The current record pointer is not affected by STEP operations.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trh4a$gs9$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Writes a new record to a file.
For sequential files, it can also be used for vertical positioning of lines
in a logical page.
The REWRITE operation replaces an existing record.

Opcode Operation File Types
E1 WRITE BEFORE L  S
E2 WRITE AFTER L  S
E3 WRITE BEFORE TAB L  S
E4 WRITE AFTER TAB L  S
E5 WRITE BEFORE PAGE L  S
E6 WRITE AFTER PAGE L  S
EC WRITE BEFORE mnemonic-name S
ED WRITE AFTER mnemonic-name S
F3 WRITE L  S  R  I
F4 REWRITE L  S  R  I
Input Fields

Offset 43 Relative record number (R)

Offset 48 Current record length
Offset 52 Line count (WRITE only) (S)
Offset 56 Pointer to the record area

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status

Offset 13 or 72 Relative byte address
```

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trh3q$9ks$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

Releases all record locks held by the run unit on a named file.

Opcode Operation File Types
0E UNLOCK L  S  R  I
Input Fields

None

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status
```

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>

```
Terminates the processing of files and reels/units with optional rewind and
locking where appropriate. For record sequential files, the terms "reel" and
"unit" are interchangeable; the treatment of sequential disk files is
logically equivalent to the treatment of a file on tape or similar
sequential media.

Opcode Operation File Type
80 CLOSE L  S  R  I
81 CLOSE WITH LOCK L  S  R  I
82 CLOSE WITH NO REWIND L  S
84 CLOSE REEL/UNIT L  S
85 CLOSE REEL/UNIT FOR REMOVAL L  S
86 CLOSE REEL/UNIT WITH NO REWIND L  S
Input Fields

None

Output Fields

Offset 0 User file status

> 2.4 (8 years old) for DOS & have managed to work out the START, READ, &
> WRITE codes. I have no doc's on this

to

#### ↳ Re: MF EXTFH

- **From:** Andreas Strzoda <as@as-data.de>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38018F1E.86430D65@as-data.de>`
- **References:** `<7trdrr$qva$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


Alister Munro schrieb:

> Anyone got the op-codes for the External File Handler. I'm using ver MF WB
> 2.4 (8 years old) for DOS & have managed to work out the START, READ, &
> WRITE codes. I have no doc's on this
> except a small demo program (may be an early release feature but it seems to
> work).

This code is from my EXTFH, a BTRIEVE-File -handler for the
old MF-Cobol. The first Code is the op-code from MFC, the next word the
relevant BTRIEVE-Fkt.:

;--MicroFocus -> BTrieve Fkt-----
MFC2BTR         DW      00  ;open input
                DW      00  ;open
                DW      01  ;open output
                DW      14  ;create
                DW      02  ;open i-o
                DW      00  ;open
                DW      14  ;unlock
                DW      27  ;unlock
                DW     128  ;close
                DW      01  ;close
                DW     140  ;Read Prev no lock
                DW      07  ;Get Prev
                DW     141  ;Read next no lock
                DW      06  ;Get Next
                DW     142  ;Read no lock
                DW      05  ;Get Equal
                DW     215  ; ** "START TRANSAKTION"**
                DW      19  ; Begin Transaction
                DW     216  ;Read next with lock
                DW      06 + NoWaitSLock ;Get Next + No Wait Single Lock
                DW     217  ;Read next with kept lock
                DW      06 + NoWaitMLock ;Get Next + No Wait Multiple Lock
                DW     218  ;Read with lock
                DW      05 + NoWaitSLock ;Get Equal + No Wait Single Lock
                DW     219  ;Read with kept lock
                DW      05 + NoWaitMLock ;Get Equal + No Wait Multiple Lock
                DW     220  ;Commit
                DW      20  ;End Transaktion
                DW     221  ;Rollback
                DW      21  ;Abort Transaktion
                DW     222  ;Read prev with lock
                DW      07 + NoWaitSLock ;Get Prev + No Wait Single Lock
                DW     223  ;Read prev with kept lock
                DW      07 + NoWaitMLock ;Get Prev + No Wait Multiple Lock
                DW     232  ;Start with no key value
                DW      05 + GetKey; Get Equal + Only key
                DW     233  ;Start with key value
                DW      05 + GetKey; Get Equal + Only key
                DW     234  ;Start greater key value
                DW      08 + GetKey; Get Greater + Only key
                DW     235  ;Start not less key value
                DW      09 + GetKey; Get Greater or Equal + Only key
                DW     243  ;Write
                DW      02  ;Insert
                DW     244  ;ReWrite
                DW      03  ;Update
                DW     245  ;Read next
                DW      06 + NoWaitSLock  ;Get Next + LOCK
                DW     246  ;Read
                DW      05 + NoWaitSLock  ;Get Equal + LOCK
                DW     247  ;Delete
                DW      04  ;Delete
                DW     249  ;Read Prev
                DW      07 + NoWaitSLock  ;Get Prev + LOCK
                DW     254  ;Start key less
                DW      10 + GetKey ;Get less + Only Key
                DW     255  ;Start key less or Equal
                DW      11 + GetKey ;Get less or Equal + Only Key
;---Btrieve -> MFC-Fehler
;
The Call-Structure of the BFC-File is:

;  Micro Focus Datenstrukturen
;
;----------------------------------------------------------------------------
MFC Struc
      Stat1     db ?     ; File-Status-Byte1
      Stat2     db ?     ; File-Status-Byte2
                dw ?     ; Reserve
                db ?     ; Reserve
      FOrg      db ?     ; 0=Line Seq, 1=Seq, 2=Indexed, 3=Rel
      FMode     db ?     ; 0=No Status-field, 128=Status-Field,
                         ; 0=Seq, +4=Random, +8=Dynamic
      OpMode    db ?     ; 0=input, 1=output, 2=I-O, 3=Extend
                db ?,?,? ; Reserve
                db ?     ; erster Teil Lï¿½nge Name
      FNameL    db ?     ; Length file-Name (beim Open)
                db 11 dup (?) ;Reserve
      LockMode  db ?     ; Lock-Bit-Flags
      Flags     db ?     ; Weitere Flags
                db ?,?,?
      Handle    dw ?     ; Handle-Nummer
      Handle2   dw ?     ; auch noch Handle
      ANSIFLAG  db ?     ; Bit 8 0 = Ans74, 1 =Ans85
      FFormat   db ?     ; 0=Current, 1=C-ISAM, 2=level IIV2
                db ?,?,?
      MaxRecLen dw ?     ; Maximum Rec-Length
                db ?,?,?
      RelRecnr  dd ?     ; Rel. Rec-Nummer
      RecMode   db ?     ; 0 = fixed, 1 = variable
      RecLen    dw ?     ; Aktuelle Satzlength
      MinRecLen dw ?     ; minimale Satzlï¿½nge
      KeyId     dw ?     ; Key-Identifier (Index) oder Line-Count (Line Seq)
      EffKeyLen dw ?     ; effektive key length (only START)
      RecPoint  dw ?     ; Pointer to Rec-Area
      RecPointS dw ?     ; Segment to Rec-Area
      NamePoint dw ?     ; Offset to File-Name
      NamePointS dw ?    ; Segment to File-Name ??
      KeyPoint  dw ?     ; Offset to Key-Description
      KeyPointS dw ?     ; Segment to Key-Description ????
                db 32 dup (?) ;Reserve
MFC   EndS

I hope this helps..

Andreas
```

<a id="t-c66ef4b78b"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<or9M3.2034$_4.3415@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
From: "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>, 
MindSpring EnterprisesDate: Sat, 9 Oct 1999 11:38:48 -0500
robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:
>> > Also note that the copyright date on that book is 1977.  This is 8 years
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > PL/I

"few" years?  Surely you're not referring to X3.74 of 1987,
some ten years later?

> - and said that it was "functionally" frozen as a programming
>> >language
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mainframe PL/I compilers.

There's nothing new in this.  IBM's OS PL/I Version 2 object code was
incompatible with Version 1 object code, owing to changes with certain
library functions.

Also, different interfaces with various operating systems required
different object code for each different version.

>  Also, as far as I know, this NEW (incompatible)
> version of IBM's mainframe PL/I compiler

AFAIK, it's a new compiler, not a new version of the existing IBM Mainframe
compiler.

> finally has support for ANSI/ISO
> conforming PL/I (conforming to the now functionally stabilized compiler) even
> though most IBM PL/I programmers "hate" having PL/I behave (or limited) to
> Standard syntax and functionality.

That's their preference, of course.

> Even so, I still would say that there have been MORE significant changes to
> the IBM mainframe COBOLs than to the IBM mainframe PL/I compilers - since
> 1977.

What changes to IBM COBOL since 1977 do you think would affect Tucker's
ratings of COBOL vs PL/I ?  Bearing in mind that PL/I was then streets
ahead of COBOL in the areas cited.

>  However, that is a matter of opinion - but whatever you think about
> the amount or type of change, it is certainly true that any comparison made
> in 1977 has little to do with the language advantages, structure, or uses
>today.

See above.

---
Bill Klein
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trb0s$sg2$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<or9M3.2034$_4.3415@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:or9M3.2034$_4.3415@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
> From: "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>,
> MindSpring EnterprisesDate: Sat, 9 Oct 1999 11:38:48 -0500
> robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
> news:CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
  <much snippage>
>
> What changes to IBM COBOL since 1977 do you think would affect Tucker's
> ratings of COBOL vs PL/I ?  Bearing in mind that PL/I was then streets
> ahead of COBOL in the areas cited.
>

Let's see what are just SOME of the changes to IBM's mainframe COBOL since
1977.

 - Scope-terminators and other "structured" constructs added
 - Intrinsic Functions
 - Native OO programming
 - Recursive Coding
 - CALL by VALUE, RETURNING, and other ILC enhancements for complete
availability of access to "Standard" API's
 - Pointers
 - Easy interface with LE callable services

If we were to look at the "What's new" section of each LRM for VS COBOL II (4
releases), COBOL/370, IBM COBOL for MVS & VM, and IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM -
I am certain we would find plenty of other additions.

P.S. It is obviously "old news" and not really relevant any more, but would
like to comment on WHY IBM themselves, were so "hesitant" in adding PL/I to
SAA? Could it be that - unlike COBOL which was one of the first languages
added - they didn't really see the future for the customers in that language
across platforms.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ts015$j2f$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<or9M3.2034$_4.3415@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7trb0s$sg2$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think it is unfair to talk about IBM lack of commitment to PL/I, given the
evidence for increased support.

Yes, at one stage IBM was not keen on adding PL/I to SAA. After this
changed, compilers were released for OS/2, NT and AIX, and this compiler has
now been ported to OS/390.

## Thread: Accessing Dynamically Allocated files ? — 2 msgs

### Accessing Dynamically Allocated files ?

- **From:** "Carl Holme" <carl.holme@ukgateway.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tqto6$8pg$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
I am trying to write a IBM MF Batch COBOL program that will dynamically
allocate files, and then be able to either read from or write to them.

I know that you can allocate and assign a DD Name to it using the TSO
Services. But I don't know how to then reference the file within the code.
The problem is the parameters for the file, will be read in as a parameter,
therefore are variable.

The difficult part is - having allocated the file, and assigned the DD Name,
then trying to reference the DD Name without it being hard coded and defined
correctly in the File Section.

I'm wondering if this kind of request is something that would be done via an
Assembler routine?

Has anybody any suggestions?

## Thread: Perform statement question — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tr1qh$652$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Also try the following:

   AAA.
      Perform BBB
      Display "After Perform"
      GoBack.
   BBB.
       Display "In BBB"
       Go To BBB-Exit
       Display "After Go To" .
   CCC.
       Display "In CCC".
    BBB-Exit.
        Exit.
    DDD.
        Display "Fall thru to DDD".

P.S.  The following is what the ANS'85 Standard says about the EXIT statement
(in part),

 "Such an exit statement has no other effect on the compilation or execution
of the program."
```

again, another example of poor code...I do not see a perform thru in this

the performed paragraph, not poor coding...

had the perform bbb been written as perform bbb thru bbb-exit, all would be
fine...the "display 'after go to'" statement the ccc routine and the ddd
routine would never be executed...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7trb7m$n4i$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7tr1qh$652$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7tr2mj$8ma$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Private email already sent - but the entire POINT of the original question
was WHY did they need to keep the "PERFORM THRU" and why couldn't they change
this to a simple PERFORM.  There is no doubt whatsoever that if you do a
PERFORM THRU and then GO TO the "thru-paragraph" that you will always return
to the PERFORM statement's location.

If you read my original note, you will see that I show two examples, one with
Perform paragraph-name THRU paragraph-exit - and another without the "THRU
paragraph-exit".  It was the fact that this WAS a "poor" coding technique
(and more importantly would NOT function the way the person asking the
question thought it would) that had me show both coding techniques.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```
On Mon, 11 Oct 1999 07:05:19 +0200, Peter van Zeeland
<peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl> wrote:

>> FWIW programs compiled under the Workbench 3.0 run fine on Windows 2000,
>> so I think we'll go that route rather than convert the 4.0 stuff to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Wait a minute.  You mean CA Realia WB 3 uses a different file system
than regular CA-Realia?  What is it?

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

- **From:** Jim Elden <elden@nojunk.accumedic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38032AC9.C4123055@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Hi Thane,

WB3 uses the c-tree file system, developed by Faircom Corporation.  This
is (finally!) a true record-locking file system and does not suffer from
the file-locking that the Realia file systerm v2.1 had, nor the dreaded
Index File Error.  Most implementations of c-tree provide for ODBC
access; unfortunately, Realia's implementation omits two key elements
necessary for ODBC access to the file system from outside apps.  Based
on conversations I had with Faircom (about 2 years ago), those two
elements were called IFIL and DODA.

Besides that disappointment, our experience with C-Tree has so far been
positive.

I have not tried it, but the COBOL 6.0 that is part of WB3 supposedly
allows for a ( [Y]? ) file descriptor that will allow reading of the old
file system.  I'm not sure about writing.   The REALCOPY that comes with
WB3 will read the old format and write a c-tree file, which means that
if you are porting an app from COBOL 4.x or 5.x, and not changing FDs,
you don't have to write any special conversion programs to get your data
into c-tree.

Hope this is informative.

Regards,
Jim Elden
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

      03  The-Body,
            05  Part-Desc X(40).

Record Key The-Key

* MicroFocus Syntax
Alternate Record Key is Some-Split-Key 
                                      Part-Desc
                                      The-Key

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jim Elden <elden@nojunk.accumedic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3805CA7E.960A7FC4@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net> <38032AC9.C4123055@nojunk.accumedic.com> <3803d784.586975986@news1.attglobal.net> <38047C2C.265F4EB7@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38052163.42068282@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Hi Thane,

Here is a simple test program I tried with our usual dialect,
REALIA4-74, and with ANSI-85 High-Level COBOL.  Both compilations failed
with "Unrecognizable word or literal 'SKF-PK'".

I have never used a key such as this.  Alternate keys are always
contiguous here -- if we need to prevent duplicates in the data we
include a copy of the primary key, e.g.

               05  SKF-OTHER-KEY.
                   05  SKF-ALTERNATE-PART PIC X(4).
                   05  SKF-COPY-OF-PK           PIC X(4) .

Hopefully I understood.

Regards,
Jim

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SPLITKEY.
       AUTHOR. JE.
      *
      *
      * program to test split keys
      *
      *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
       OBJECT-COMPUTER. IBM-PC.
      *
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT SPLIT-KEY-FILE ASSIGN TO "SPLIT.DAT[X]"
               ORGANIZATION INDEXED
               ACCESS DYNAMIC
               RECORD KEY SKF-PK
               ALTERNATE RECORD KEY SKF-OTHER-KEY SKF-PK.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  SPLIT-KEY-FILE.
       01  SKF-REC.
           03  SKF-PK PIC X(4).
           03  SKF-DATA.
               05  SKF-OTHER-KEY PIC X(4).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       M1.
       MX.
           STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Jim Elden <elden@nojunk.accumedic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38061450.20A778BA@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net> <38032AC9.C4123055@nojunk.accumedic.com> <3803d784.586975986@news1.attglobal.net> <38047C2C.265F4EB7@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38052163.42068282@news1.attglobal.net> <3805CA7E.960A7FC4@nojunk.accumedic.com> <7u52rh$meq@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Hi Bill,

IMHO what Thane is referring to by "split key" is akin to a segmented
key in Btrieve, where all the parts of the key are not necessarily
contiguous within the record, and the primary key can be contained as
_part_ of an alternate key, often to make the key unique and physically
prevent duplicates.   From the Microfocus syntax Thane gives, it looks
(from here) as if that is just what he is doing.

Regards,
Jim
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

to duplicate prime key information in the record.  Maybe the syntax is

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Jim Elden <elden@nojunk.accumedic.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380B79B5.B47E81E7@nojunk.accumedic.com>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net> <38032AC9.C4123055@nojunk.accumedic.com> <3803d784.586975986@news1.attglobal.net> <38047C2C.265F4EB7@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38052163.42068282@news1.attglobal.net> <3805CA7E.960A7FC4@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38067793.768085@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Hi Thane,

>>Maybe the syntax is different?<<

I suspect so.  Since the MF and Fujitsu syntax differs, I would gues
that the implementation would be vendor-specific.   The ANSI X3.23-1985
standard that I have shows that part of the syntax as

ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS data-name-1 [WITH DUPLICATES]

(ALTERNATE, RECORD, and DUPLICATES are underlined)

I don't see how the standard allows for a split key.  If it did then
"data-name-1" would changed to something like

{data-name-1} ...

Regards,
Jim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Realia 4.x programs won't run on Win 2000 RC2

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ug0pq$5o8$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF69853FB82@NL-AMB-MAIL01> <380277d3.496928917@news1.attglobal.net> <38032AC9.C4123055@nojunk.accumedic.com> <3803d784.586975986@news1.attglobal.net> <38047C2C.265F4EB7@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38052163.42068282@news1.attglobal.net> <3805CA7E.960A7FC4@nojunk.accumedic.com> <38067793.768085@news1.attglobal.net> <380B79B5.B47E81E7@nojunk.accumedic.com>`

```
The '85 Standard (even with the 2 amendments since then) does *NOT* support
split keys.  The draft of the NEXT Standard does. It uses syntax with "SOURCE
is " that isn't the same as either Fujitsu's or Micro Focus' current
implementations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: TSO commands embedded in COBOL.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38052445.CF0A7D41@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<Ox8w$BDF$GA.338@cpmsnbbsa05> <7ttrg5$hq5$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <38032972$1$tfs$mr2ice@news> <X0oEOGjU6ff8oZbJaBbqIqe3frIx@4ax.com> <3804a1e9$2$tfs$mr2ice@news>`

```
1.  IKJEFTSR needs to run in a TSO environment.  You can set up a
TSO environment, or you can run in an existing one.  

The easiest way to run in an existing one is:
//BATCHTMP  EXEC  PGM=IKJEFT01,DYNAMNBR=99,REGION=0M,TIME=99
//STEPLIB   DD  DSN=SYS1,SYS2.COBLIB,DISP=SHR  and other run time
stuff
//STEPLIB   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=SYS1.CEELIB   LE/370, names vary
//SYSPROC   DD  DISP=SHR,DSN=SYS1.CLIST     CLISTS/REXX EXECs here
//SYSTSPRT  DD  SYSOUT=*
//SYSTSIN   DD  *
  CALL  'YOUR.LOAD.LIB(MYCOBPGM)' 'ANY PARAMETERS YOU NEED'
//ISPPROF  DD   ISPF profile, panels, etc.

Another advantage is you can run ISPF in batch with this setup.

2.  If you aren't invoked from a TSO terminal or via the batch
TMP, then you call call IKJTSOEV to establish a TSO environment. 
Doing this is 90% of the overhead that the TSO TMP will incur.  It
is often used for non-batch, non-TSO type programs.

3.  Long time ago, I am 99% sure that I ran a COBOL program in
batch which used IKJEFTSR to invoke an ALLOCATE command and I
didn't have to set up a TSO environment.  I could be wrong, and
IKJEFTSR has changed a lot since then.  But it might be worth a
quick shot...


Gilbert Saint-flour wrote:
> 
> In <X0oEOGjU6ff8oZbJaBbqIqe3frIx@4ax.com>, on 13 Oct 1999 at 13:47,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

#### ↳ Re: TSO commands embedded in COBOL.

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3802AF81.F0C55296@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<Ox8w$BDF$GA.338@cpmsnbbsa05>`

```
You can call IKJEFTSR, documented in the TSO/E Programming
Services Book.  SC28-1875-08

## Thread: READ PREVIOUS - immediately after an OPEN — 3 msgs

### READ PREVIOUS - immediately after an OPEN

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tudeh$l2g$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As has been discussed in this newsgroup on several occasions, the next
ANSI/ISO Standard will support (in most cases) a READ PREVIOUS statement
(similar to the current READ NEXT statement).  I know that several existing
implementations already support this (as an extension).  There is a current
J4 paper that is proposing a change (correction?) to the draft.  Can those
who have access to compilers that support this already (and who actually use
this feature), look at the following paper and tell me if you agree with what
it is doing (or not)?  I *think* it is reasonable, but I don't know if it is
or is not the way existing implementations really work.

Please check out:
  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0498.doc

and send me and/or J4 via
  doncobol <at> mediaone.net

if you have a comment indicating that there is a problem with this proposal.
```

#### ↳ Re: READ PREVIOUS - immediately after an OPEN

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3803d597.586482716@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7tudeh$l2g$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Mon, 11 Oct 1999 23:28:33 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>As has been discussed in this newsgroup on several occasions, the next
>ANSI/ISO Standard will support (in most cases) a READ PREVIOUS statement
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm about to find out.  With Realia need to do a start before a read
"prior" (4.2) I found that to get the real prior record, took two read
priors (the way the draft is written <= current file pointer) where
Don want's to change it to <.  This will break some current programs
that do a read prior.

However, MicroFocus (Hence Don's bias because of experience) does NOT
suffer this "problem" when using read previous.  It behaves as he has
proposed.

I don't care either way, just so long as it's clear that if you want
to do a START and get the = record you must do a read next.  If you
then want to go backward, you do a read previous (prior) - and it
works the same in both compilers.

Now to say it clearly:

Start - then Read Prior in Realia returns a different record than
Start and Read Previous with MicroFocus.

However, BOTH behave the same if one does a 
Start, Read Next, Read (prior) Previous.

I do not know how Fujitsu behaves - but I'm converting some Realia
code to Fujitsu.  However, I started using the convention of Start,
Read Next, Read Prior (previous) some time ago and I expect the
programs to behave the same even of they don't treat the backward
reads immediately after a start or open the same.

PS - I find the implications of action after a START more important
than the implications of the action after an open.  


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: READ PREVIOUS - immediately after an OPEN

- **From:** trblshtr <trblshtr@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u4kvj$i3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7tudeh$l2g$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3803d597.586482716@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
In article <3803d597.586482716@news1.attglobal.net>,
  thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> On Mon, 11 Oct 1999 23:28:33 -0500, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>
> >As has been discussed in this newsgroup on several occasions, the
next
> >ANSI/ISO Standard will support (in most cases) a READ PREVIOUS
statement
> >(similar to the current READ NEXT statement).  I know that several
existing
> >implementations already support this (as an extension).  There is a
current
> >J4 paper that is proposing a change (correction?) to the draft.  Can
those
> >who have access to compilers that support this already (and who
actually use
> >this feature), look at the following paper and tell me if you agree
with what
> >it is doing (or not)?  I *think* it is reasonable, but I don't know
if it is
> >or is not the way existing implementations really work.
> >
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Because it was requested:
Having used read previous in RM and ACU I agree with the proposal.  In
essence: a read previous should return an eof if there is no previous
record given the current file position whether that position has been
set via open *or* start. As proposed: previous = less than, next =
greater than or equal to.
-trblshtr-
```

## Thread: NetExpress / TCP-IP / Lockup — 7 msgs

### NetExpress / TCP-IP / Lockup

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991011194036.11995.00000368@ng-cl1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I have two Win98 machines connected to a hub using TCP-IP.  The problem I am
having is if the server Win98 machine goes down the program locks up.  For
instance I step thru the program until just before I use a "start" statement on
one of the files that I have opened on the server.  I then pull the plug on the
cable connecting the computer to the hub and then I then try to execute the
line of code that contains the "start" statement.  The computer will hang and
if I [ctrl]-[alt]-[delete], I get a message that states that "runw is not
responding".  If I try this with a compiled EXE then I get the message that the
"program is not responding".  Any suggestions ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

```

there is at least a 2 minute timeout before control is returned to your

does it time out after a couple of minutes? you should get a 124 error code
I think indicating a communications error...

## Thread: OLE or API — 2 msgs

### OLE or API

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HVpM3.11$kV2.419037@news1.i1.net>`

```
I'm trying to write several programs that will automatically print a word
2000 document.  Can someone point me to how to use ole fuctions..like a
sample or something...or list the api's I need to call.  Like StartDoc..or
PrintDlg to get the printer first.

Any help would be appreciated.  I am using MF cobol workbench 4.32

Tom Wright
twright@larimore./net
```

#### ↳ Re: OLE or API

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u03m2$q9g$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<HVpM3.11$kV2.419037@news1.i1.net>`

```
Tom,

You can do many things with OLE I use it quite often.
If you just simply want to print a file this is easy.

You need to define word as an OLE class in the class section
like:

 wordServ "$OLE$word.application"


Then create an instance of it:

 invoke word "new" returning wordServer

Open the document:
 invoke wordServ "FileOpen" using Path

Then print..
 invoke wordServ "FilePrint" using by value 0

This example the word program will be running in the background but will not
be shown on the screen. If you want it to be shown on the screen then you
can code:

invoke wordServ "AppShow"

This is very primitive Automation if you would like more code then mail me.

Simon R Hart
Eaton, Ottery St. Mary UK.


Tom Wright wrote in message ...
>I'm trying to write several programs that will automatically print a word
>2000 document.  Can someone point me to how to use ole fuctions..like a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-6fd82fce66"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nGuM3.2730$_4.4950@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
From: "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>, 
MindSpring EnterprisesDate: Sat, 9 Oct 1999 11:38:48 -0500
robin <robin_v@bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:CpIL3.1233$_4.2153@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...
>> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:
>> > Also note that the copyright date on that book is 1977.  This is 8 years
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> > PL/I

"few" years?  Surely you're not referring to X3.74 of 1987,
some ten years later?

> - and said that it was "functionally" frozen as a programming
>> >language
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mainframe PL/I compilers.

There's nothing new in this.  IBM's OS PL/I Version 2 object code was
incompatible with Version 1 object code, owing to changes with certain
library functions.

Also, different interfaces with various operating systems required
different object code for each different version.

>  Also, as far as I know, this NEW (incompatible)
> version of IBM's mainframe PL/I compiler

AFAIK, it's a new compiler, not a new version of the existing IBM Mainframe
compiler.

> finally has support for ANSI/ISO
> conforming PL/I (conforming to the now functionally stabilized compiler) even
> though most IBM PL/I programmers "hate" having PL/I behave (or limited) to
> Standard syntax and functionality.

That's their preference, of course.

> Even so, I still would say that there have been MORE significant changes to
> the IBM mainframe COBOLs than to the IBM mainframe PL/I compilers - since
> 1977.

What changes to IBM COBOL since 1977 do you think would affect Tucker's
ratings of COBOL vs PL/I ?  Bearing in mind that PL/I was then streets
ahead of COBOL in the areas cited.

>  However, that is a matter of opinion - but whatever you think about
> the amount or type of change, it is certainly true that any comparison made
> in 1977 has little to do with the language advantages, structure, or uses
>today.

See above.

---
Bill Klein
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "John W. Kennedy" <rri0189@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38037DE8.2C7E6468@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<nGuM3.2730$_4.4950@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
robin wrote:
> There's nothing new in this.  IBM's OS PL/I Version 2 object code was
> incompatible with Version 1 object code, owing to changes with certain
> library functions.

That was not generally true, although some PLRSHR configurations,
including ours, had problems.  I wrote a high-speed delinker program
that stripped the runtime from all our load modules and relinked them
with the V2 library; we ran it one Sunday afternoon and were up again
with no problems on Monday morning, except for one group of programs
that someone had, for no particular reason, linked with the NOEDIT
option (which discards all the ESD information and consolidates
everything into one large control section); those, naturally, had to be
recompiled.
 
> AFAIK, it's a new compiler, not a new version of the existing IBM Mainframe
> compiler.

Yes.  It uses the Toronto compiler engine that was already being used by
C and C++ for MVS, OS/2 and Windows and by PL/I for OS/2 and Windows. 
Some of us have been expecting the MVS PL/I version for five years or
longer.
 
> What changes to IBM COBOL since 1977 do you think would affect Tucker's
> ratings of COBOL vs PL/I ?  Bearing in mind that PL/I was then streets
> ahead of COBOL in the areas cited.

COBOL 85 at least makes a decent attempt at structured code.  However,
just as with COBOL 59->COBOL 60, COBOL 60->COBOL 68, and COBOL 68->COBOL
74 before, COBOL 74->COBOL 85 entails radical incompatibilities with the
prior dialect (including many cases where the same valid source has a
different meaning), and, without the aid of a translation program, can
be used only with code written de-novo.  My old employer was still using
99% COBOL 68 in 1995 or so, since no-one wanted to make a new COPY
library.

The COMPUTE statement also remains, in effect, a random-number
generator, since intermediate precision is undefined.
```

#### ↳ Re: The best Cobol compiler for MS SQL Server 7.0.....?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ttene$f3b$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

Is it just one of your clients that has SQL Server 7.0 - or just one who has
COBOL?  If you are using COBOL (but not SQL Server), then almost all the
major players in the WinTel COBOL compiler have a way to reach SQL Server.
(This includes - but is not limited to Fujitsu, Micro Focus, AcuCOBOL, RM,
and IBM).  However, what you need for each of the compilers may vary.
Therefore, if you tell us which COBOL compiler you are using for other
things, we can tell you out to get SQL Server working.  If, on the other
hand, it is "COBOL" that only one of your clients uses, that significantly
impact what we might recommend.

<a id="t-50778f5ee2"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IKuM3.2731$_4.4746@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"Denis" <grossnou@antispam.skynet.be> writes: > Robin,

Subject: Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison
From: "Denis" <grossnou@antispam.skynet.be>, J.P. Morgan & CoDate: Mon, 11 Oct 1999 07:55:03 +0200
> Robin,
> I do not agree with many points you defend:
> - About debugging:
> In the context of OS/390 and Language environment, it is much easier to read
> LE dumps for COBOL programs than for PL/I:

That may be so, but I haven't looked at a memory dump for a PL/I program for
some 30 years.  Haven't needed to, because the facilities provided by
PL/I for locating errors are excellent.  These facilities include
printing the source statement number when an error occurs, and the subsequent
printing of the values of variables under the control of the programmer.

>If you compile your COBOL  module with TEST(NONE,SYM) your load-module is
>slightly bigger  but the performances are the same (We've measured it), you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>abend (variable name + content) if you dump with the correct option.

I can do that in PL/I -- WITHOUT A MEMORY DUMP -- simply by specifying
the GOSTMT option (or in one of IBM's compilers that doesn't support it,
by specifying OFFSET to provide an offset table).

The values of variables can be printed after an error occurs, in decimal
or character -- that is, in a readable form (see later).

> This is impossible for PL/I (read the manual section CEE3DMP that states
> that the 'variable' option is valid for all languages except PL/I). PL/I LE
> dumps display only the statement number but not directly the content of the
> variables (compilation option GOSTMT)

As I said, the GOSTMT option option will give all that to
you in PL/I without the need for a memory dump at all.
Furthermore, after an error has occurred, a PL/I program can print the contents
of variables, decimal variables in decimal, character variables in character
form, and so on.
A memory dump is not a good place to begin looking for the values of variables.

> About the debugger:
> The debugger is common for all the language, so not advantages here

One can use the debugger, but PL/I has its own built-in debugging facilities.

> - about error checking
> You can activate option that check the same things as PL/I (either clause ON
> ERROR in the code or compilation option to check arrays out-of-bound
> conditions or overflows)

If it's a compilation option, it applies to the whole compilation unit.  But
that's a facility that's not part of the language.
When it's an ON ERROR clause, it applies to a statement.

PL/I has the means to enable the subscript check on a particular statement,
or to suppress it on a particular statement.

The check can be enabled for an entire procedure or BEGIN block, or disabled
for a whole block.  Individual statements within that block may 
enable or disable it.

These facilites are part of the language.

>In PL/I
> - about the size of the source
> It is much easier to create smaller modules in COBOL and dynamically call
> them (even under CICS).

One can write simple procedues in PL/I.  They can be as small as one
statement (apart from the PROCEDURE and END statements)
They are simpler to write than COBOL.

> Even if PL/I allowsto dynamically fetch modules,

IBM mainframe PL/I does.

> there are really annoying limits due to the language itself that block you
> in doing this (the famous fetch limitation, that should be alleviated in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> after the section name.

A standard facility?

> however nobody uses this in COBOL because there is
> no such limit in COBOL than in PL/I about the size of stuff that the
> compiler can optimize (for example the PL/I compiler optimizes only the
> first 255 variables...)

There is no such limit in any of the PL/I compilers I have used.
Such a limit is NOT a language feature, it's an implementation restriction
completely unrelated to the PL/I language.

> - about the easiness of PUT DATA:
> With DISPLAY in COBOL you can do exactly the same thing by simply writing
> DISPLAY <var>,

PUT DATA; is a complete statement that writes out the NAMES of the variables
AS WELL as their VALUES.
PUT DATA (K,X); writes out the names K and X as well as their values, in
the form K = 12345, X = 9.8765;
Does DISPLAY do this?
DISPLAY would, however, be equivalent to PUT LIST ('V=', V); which tends to
become clumsy for structures and long names.

> even under CICS, the output goes in card CEEMSG by default.

Denis +4447

==========================================================================
robin wrote in message ...
>(Continued)
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>In other words, PL/I rated superior in nine out of twelve points, and was
rated
>equal on one point, while COBOL rated superior on only two points out of
twelve.
>Note: run times for the PL/I compilers, particularly the Optimizing
Compiler are
>not valid because the REORDER keyword for the main procedure has not been
>specified -- the compiler cannot perform full optimization.  The REORDER
keyword
>must be specified in the PROCEDURE statement in order to obtain full
>optimization.
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tu3h6$br5$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<IKuM3.2731$_4.4746@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
For the information of comp.lang.cobol (without CC'ing comp.lang.pli)

I am "dropping out" of this thread.  It is (as always) pretty useless to have
"language wars". I (personally) think that the place of COBOL in
multi-platform/multi-vendor (portable) environments is quite safe - while the
future for the IBM-(semi-)only PL/I programmers is limited to those
sites/applications where it is already "in situ".  However, I don't think
that I'll convince many PL/I programmers of this - and I don't think they'll
convince me. <G>
```

<a id="t-02138e5fbb"></a>
## Thread: IBM-Cobol in OS390 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM-Cobol in OS390

- **From:** schupeta@sdm.de (Achim Schupeta)
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tsk61$qs1$1@solti3.sdm.de>`

```

```

#### ↳ Re: IBM-Cobol in OS390

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7tst1i$fd$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7tsk61$qs1$1@solti3.sdm.de>`

```
Several parts to answer:

1) IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM is truly the release (actually version as I
recall) that follows IBM COBOL for MVS & VM (both of which follow IBM SAA
AD/Cycle COBOL/370 - which in turn followed VS COBOL II).  Therefore you have
the latest documentation - but not the latest software. (You might want to
check your site to see if using a different Steplib/Joblib would actually
point to the newer compiler.)

2) There are a couple  of other ways to get there, but one way to see the
online version of the "latest and greatest" IBM COBOL manuals is to go to:

   http://www.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/library.htm

This includes pointers to online dox for BOTH of the compilers that you are
looking for. (Unlike some other links, this allows you to download the
manuals in PDF format - or use them online via Bookmanager)

3) If you check the "Summary of Changes" in any of the manuals, it will give
you a summary of what has changed between products, versions, releases, or
whatever. I usually look at the Programming Guide for this to get the most
"user-friendly" summary - but really any of the manuals will do.  For
example, check out:


http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/IGYPG204/FRONT_2%2e1%2e
1

   ***

I hope this helps.
```

---

### variable record size

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr>`

```
Hi

I am a new COBOL programmer, so sorry if
my question are a bit naive...

I have to read a ascii non indexed file
but the record size is variable...

How to read this kind of file ?

THanks in advance

Christophe
```

#### ↳ Re: variable record size

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gCIM3.9695$UG5.592698@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr>`

```
We don't have enough information to know.  Is this a text file with a
delimiter between lines (CR and/or LF between each record)?  Is the file
blocked?  If so, is it variable block with variable length records or fixed
block WVLR (padding on unfinished block).  Do the records/blocks have a
standard header to describe their length?

webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr> wrote in message
news:3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr...
> I have to read a ascii non indexed file
> but the record size is variable...
>
> How to read this kind of file ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: variable record size

- **From:** jcotton <jcottonNOjcSPAM@excite.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0a0133f8.5f6afcbc@usw-ex0101-001.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr> <gCIM3.9695$UG5.592698@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
variable length records are defined like this:

In the FILE SECTION, say
RECORD CONTAINS integer-1 TO integer-2 CHARACTERS

In the record description, use a variable length record, with a OCCURS
DEPENDING ON clause.   like this...

01  RECORD-DESC.
    05  FIXED-PART.
       10 REC-HEADER  PIC (...)
       10 TABLE-OCCS  PIC S9(4) COMP.
    05  VARIABLE-PART.
       10  TABLE-ENTRY  PIC X(nn) OCCURS 0 TO 5 TIMES DEPENDING ON
                                  TABLE-OCCS.




* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: variable record size

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uih5e$kio$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr> <gCIM3.9695$UG5.592698@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <0a0133f8.5f6afcbc@usw-ex0101-001.remarq.com>`

```
Please, PLEASE don't use this technique (unless you are STUCK with a '74
Standard compiler).  With any compiler from the last decade and a half, a
MUCH better way to code this same thing would be:

In the FILE SECTION, say
RECORD Varying in Size from integer-1 TO integer-2 Depending On
Working-Storage item.

The rest of the record description is probably OK, but with the new
(improved) syntax you do not NEED to have a "counter" actually in your record
telling how large the variable part is.
```

#### ↳ Re: variable record size

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38047D5C.A6625479@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3803587E.3D561DD2@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

in your fd put record is varying depending on rec-size

01  record.
	05 record-x    pic x(01)  occurs depending on rec-size.

in your working storage:

01   rec-size   pic 9(04) comp.

(the definition is a little compiler dependant though).

If this does not help make the query more explicit.

Ken
```

           move p2ce-MouseMoved to Event
           invoke otoolbarButton "setEvent" using Event aCallback

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using toolbar.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-10-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u2j09$b1h$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<EtFM3.213$kh.55@news1.online.no> <7u05c3$16o$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <XzXM3.435$7G2.3787@news1.online.no> <tRXM3.444$7G2.3509@news1.online.no> <3804AF94.E68CE36D@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>Come on Stephen Biggs - if your looking in. Bet you can give Tommy a
>definitive answer <G>

Sorry, I've not been following this thread at all. :-)

My immediate comment is to ask why Tommy would want to change the 
appearance of the button as the mouse moves over it - 'flat' toolbars 
already indicate their "clickable" status (cf the TBARDS demo) by raising 
the border.

My limited knowledge of toolbars can only provide this:
If you trap the WM_NOTIFY/TBN_GETDISPINFO message (perhaps via 
subclassing the window class and implementing "wmGadgetNotify"?), you can 
set the image of the button requested using the image list associated with 
the toolbar. I'm not sure how easy this is to get to via the class library 
- it may require several extra methods in a new subclass. You'll probably 
have to send the toolbar TB_SETBUTTONINFO messages to change the default 
image for each button to I_IMAGECALLBACK, too.

This all avoids having to deal with individual control's mouse messages by 
leaving that work to the OS.

And there might even be an easier way - sorry, I've no definitive answers 
today :)

Steve.
```

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 13 Oct 1999 17:09:56 GMT, Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Thane,

I was going to comment elsewhere, but in view of above...

Enough already ! As they say in New York. Thane - you are being non-
productive taking a swipe at Merant to which Stephen can't respond.
He contributes, to quote his own words "Because he cares" - AND - he is
a developer NOT tech support NOR a policy-maker. And this is not jumping
to the defence of Merant - Stephen is more than aware of my various
comments about what I personally consider is missing - BUT overall their
N/E product is SUPERB. Pete Dashwood commented the Animator was a superb
debugging tool - but he compromised and can get by with what's available
in Fujitsu. You might care to ask Donald Tees what he thinks about Fuji
IDE and Project control.

How come you haven't asked Fujitsu for the same feature - ahhh "24
Hours" covers the use of Screen Section - nice learning feature - but do
we really want 20-year olds designing systems in DOS mode for the coming
century; then I have to wait until Chapter 24 to see the reference to
SP2/Flexus. How come no mention of Fujitsu GUI handling. A footnote
would have sufficed, like "I think Merant's Dialog System is the pits,
and the same goes for Fujitsu GUI". 

And my friend Ray Leech left me with egg all over my face. From what he
had written to me privately some three/four months back, I thought he
was telllng me Dialog System was the best thing since sliced bread. He
has since expressed his thoughts clearly, and I understand.

Now here's the irony, and Stephen already knows - I don't like Dialog
System :-

a) It uses text tables - no good for me with OO where I want to use
collection/dictionary objects

b) It doesn't cover every single control and manipulation

c) You are very much tied-in to a compiler specific product, with the
ability to do a lot of validity within the GUI Interface as opposed to
the Business interface - and that is not a very good principle, as I see
it.. And your previous swipe " Tie you into their product...." So what's
wrong with that - perfectly good marketing ploy if you can pull it off -
but lousy from the consumers' perspective. Imagine the joy on Fujitsu
faces if you deleted Chapter 24 from the next re-print of "24 Hours" -
Fujitsu :) and Bob :(

d) And one of the points made by Ray - having gone so far, you can't
afford to change. Similarly, I'd already committed to OO/GUI before D/S
became available in N/E

And as regards your comment 'Why..." did he ask. Then suggest you start
reading some books up on systems analysis. (Frankly I think you've
either got it or you aint got it - no book is going to teach you to be
analytical). That's what got me into this game in the first place,
always asking why, both back in the RAF and subsequently. Don't just
jump in - start with a "Why....? I commented to Pete Dashwood that Thane
said DB was slow. His reply, "They probably don't know the first thing
about systems design". And I think their might be a smidgen of truth in
that. There is a VAST difference between system design and producing
clever program code - as many oil companies have found at a very high
cost. There are plenty of hot-shots in downtown Calgary. I briefly had
contact with one - he gets about $60K CDN a year - and the silly bugger
didn't know/or want to know about sending a zipfile by modem, "Send it
to me by courier". But they sound good - I call it verbal diarrhoea.

Any compiler/IDE is a compromise; they are conceived as the compiler
developer views the consumers' needs. As we all know too often they
don't quite do it for us. How I'd love to walk out of an auto showroom
with a custom-designed Jaguar/Porsche ! The result would make 007
salivate.

Nothing to do with Fujitsu or Merant - as I've already indicated to Bob,
third-party product(s) that address the needs of COBOL users to
interface with other modules is the way to go - PROVIDING they also
acknowledge the COBOL-xxx intent of OO and collections/classes. Saw the
term '3-tier system' recently, but in fact, in the context of
third-party products it is far more tiered :-

	Business Logic	- 	COBOL compiler & IDE

	User Interface	- 	a) GUIs
				b) Touch/Pen
				c) Verbal/Music
				d) Photographic
				e) Front-End devices - OCR/OMR/POS  	
				f) ??? etc...

	Storage Interface -	a) Flat-files COBOL
				b) DBs - endless list

	Printer Interface -     a) Another endless list or perhaps 
					Crystal ??


And we bloody-well don't need to jump into cutesey C++ or Java to get at
these devices from COBOL if third-party COBOL-oriented tools become
available.

And unless some genius dreams up a better way - the only tool I see
currently to merge this all together is OO with OPEN SOURCE available to
ALL developers to pick and choose the bits they want.

Now I've done ranting - but felt somebody had to respond.

PS: I'll still recommend your book <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Caught you at a bad time?  Sorry I stepped on toes - didn't mean to!
But what a cat swipe I got back!

On Thu, 14 Oct 1999 03:21:06 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:



>Enough already ! As they say in New York. Thane - you are being non-
>productive taking a swipe at Merant to which Stephen can't respond.

No I answered the WHY question.  I also let out my frustration dealing
with OTHER vendors who keep asking me WHY instead of talking about HOW
to add/change something in their product.  And I'm not naming names.
I was also expressing my contentment with my relationship with one
vendor who does NOT ask WHY all the time.

>He contributes, to quote his own words "Because he cares" - AND - he is
>a developer NOT tech support NOR a policy-maker. And this is not jumping
>to the defence of Merant - Stephen is more than aware of my various
>comments about what I personally consider is missing - BUT overall their
>N/E product is SUPERB. 

Nothing in my post said it wasn't.  I've USED it.  I appreciate his
contributions to the group.

> Pete Dashwood commented the Animator was a superb
>debugging tool - but he compromised and can get by with what's available
>in Fujitsu. 

Again, I agree - NOTHING beats the MicroFocus Animator - at least that
I have used.

>You might care to ask Donald Tees what he thinks about Fuji
>IDE and Project control.

I like it better than what I was using.  

>
>How come you haven't asked Fujitsu for the same feature - ahhh "24
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Theres one cat swipe.  Let me defend myself - I am teaching the
beginner programmer.  You gotta walk before you can run.  The reason I
have not aske Fujitsu for the button feature is that I don't use
POWERCOBOL - and my Book does NOT ADDRESS POWERCOBOL.   It is by the
simplicity of Sp2 that I was able to introduce GUI at all in a
beginner book.  The GUI is important, but not as important as the
underlying COBOL foundation.

I had several goals when *I* designed and wrote the book.  The TOC was
not given to me, I started with a blank slate and *I* made the rules.

1.  A simple text that was easy to follow.
2.  Teach structured coding and design.
3.  Teach in a logical progression, each chapter reinforcing and
building on the previous chapter.
4.  NOT A Y2K Hype book.
5.  Give the reader a complete toolset - they can buy my book and
write COBOL programs that work.
6.  Teach STANDARD CURRENT COBOL - not the new standard that is under
Flux, and not the "old school" that needs to be updated.  This gives
me the added byproduct of TWO markets - NEW COBOL programmers and
COBOL programmers that want to update their skillset to the current
standard.  I did NOT use standard COBOL in the one respect of the
Screen Section.  But why the screen section - because it is simple.
We can get results right away.  And it works.

>And my friend Ray Leech left me with egg all over my face. From what he
>had written to me privately some three/four months back, I thought he
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fujitsu :) and Bob :(

Uh, what can I say, you know I agree.

>
>d) And one of the points made by Ray - having gone so far, you can't
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>to me by courier". But they sound good - I call it verbal diarrhoea.

Now that was personal and uncalled for.  Shame on you.  I didn't
design "my" slow database systems - I just have the pleasure of
working with them - others where I work did that.  I *DO* know system
design however, and I do know the value of asking WHY - but you are
overreacting a bit here.  We are talking about buttons on a toolbar -
and YES I have requested this exact same feature from Flexus.

>
>Any compiler/IDE is a compromise; they are conceived as the compiler
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks I'm still your friend.  Let me lick my wounds and we'll get on
with life.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using toolbar.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u5tg1$qnn$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<EtFM3.213$kh.55@news1.online.no> <7u05c3$16o$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <XzXM3.435$7G2.3787@news1.online.no> <tRXM3.444$7G2.3509@news1.online.no> <3804AF94.E68CE36D@home.com> <7u2j09$b1h$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <38052373.42595732@news1.attglobal.net> <38054E1F.A2B89614@home.com> <380553fc.9970562@news1.attglobal.net>`

```

Thane Hubbell wrote in message
>
>>You might care to ask Donald Tees what he thinks about Fuji
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I do not.  Two years back, I had a well organized library, where I could
find anything that I wanted.  I could simply type "compile systemname" and
be absolutely assured that any code I had written in 15 years in 3 languages
(mixed) and several lifetimes of version numbers would completely and
accurately generate themselves.  (I would have to preface that with a
statement like "MSCOBOL4.5" to indicate the generation, but that would be
it.

Today, with modern tools, I have stuff all over my disks, I have completely
revised my entire libraries three times trying to attain a manageable state,
and I cannot trust anything less than complete rebuilds of anything but
simple test programs.

Three quarters of what is on my disk is generated, and I seems to be
absolutely impossible to maintain a clean library of source code. There is
garbage everywhere; every piece of Cobol software on the market seems to
think it their right to place garbage anywhere and everywhere on my disk
with nary a thought about cleanup.

The IDE's all decide for me what my file naming conventions will be, and
their software enforces it.  Unfortunately, their naming conventions are so
weak that any project of more that sophomore standards becomes unmanageable
if you use it. Every copy file, for example, must have an identical
extension chosen by the Vendor, regardless of the type of copy.

Not to mention editors.  I have yet to see an IDE with a professional
quality editor.  50% of the IDE functionality disappears if you choose not
to use the editors provided.

I like my current compiler.  I am staring to like the oops extensions. The
Pstaff is useful, although it would be a thousand times better if iterative.

They all work well enough to get the job done, which is the bottom line.
However, I do not think them better than what I used to use.  I think they
have some real advantages, but also some real weaknesses. Maybe next
generation they'll be better.  Now they are worse.
```
