## Thread: "Vendor" comments in the COBOL Newsgroups — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### "Vendor" comments in the COBOL Newsgroups

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7u53t1$n0k@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com>`

```
(Obvious follow-up on at least one other current thread),

As a "happily unemployed" (on Long-Term Disability) *NON-*moderator of these
Newsgroups - who also happens to maintain the (a?) COBOL FAQ,  Let me stress
for the new and old readers of these newsgroups:

1) All posts to these Newsgroups from current (or past) employees of any
VENDOR do *NOT* reflect the "official" support/marketing/etc policy of their
companies - UNLESS said post says something to the contrary.

2) As far as I know, every one of the COBOL (and possibly all of the tool)
vendors who have employees OR "enthusiasts" who post in these newsgroups -
also have SOME "official" channel for handling both support and marketing
issues (including enhancement requests and "complaints" against this, that,
and the other thing). These channels MAY require "payment" of either initial
or ongoing fees - but you do get what you pay for!

3) All of us who are not "vendors" really DO appreciate any and all
*UNOFFICIAL* input that we get in the newsgroups.  Please, PLEASE, vendors -
keep participating (and we promise not to "beat you up" - or at least not
unless we are tired, drunk, or in a particularly dark mood - on a particular
day).

  ***

End-of-SoapBox <G>
```

```
whomever) is causing me a slight NIGHTMARE in seeing the COBOL newsgroups.
```

### intelligent search in indexed file

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-10-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3806E741.FC27F07F@eii.fr>`

```
Hi

I have a massive indexed file. One of
the index fields is something like pic
x(50) and contains names (but these
names contains several words each).

When my user type a name, I have to find
it in my file, but the user often does
not type the name as it is in my file so
I do not find the answer although I
should.

Exemple :
real name in the file = the london
symphony orchestra
user enters = london... or symphony
orchestra...

Is there an intelligente solution to
find the correct record even if my user
does not enter the beginning of the name
without looking the whole file that is
hundred thousand of records ?

thanks

Christophe
```

#### ↳ Re: Borland C & Fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ufg1i$lm8$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7u1us0$hqj$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7u294m$ade$1@news.cerf.net> <7ue0la$vg$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ue7l9$qte$1@news.cerf.net> <7uf5iv$pff$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`

```
Nilo wrote in message <7uf5iv$pff$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>...

Have you tried to use the function from any other application, like; making
a Borland C dll that uses the function?

If you have done so, are you loading the DLL dynamically or are you linking
statically? In case of the latter, you should be aware that there are
different techniques and dependencies.

I am not certain of how Fujitsu deals with DLL's. But I know that it has no
way of finding a function inside a DLL unless it is loaded prior to the
call.

I don't know if Fujitsu supports static linking.

If I were doing a load to the dll in Delphi, this is what I would do:

DllHandle := LoadLibrary('mydll.dll');
if (DllHandle > 32) Then
 @my_insert_string_func := GetProcAdress(DllHandle, '_insert_string');
if (my_insert_string_func <> NIL) then
 my_insert_string_func(bla, bla);

In Acucobol this would be accomplished by:

CALL "mydll.dll" ON EXCEPTION GOTO LOAD-ERR.
CALL "_insert_string" USING bla bla.

Are you using a somewhat similar approach?

Cheesle
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Nilo wrote in message <7uj4ih$43p$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>...
>If I try to load the DLL dinamically, using CALL "literal" in PROCEDURE
>DIVISION and compiler option DLOAD, I get run-time error telling me
>
>JMP0015I-U CANNOT CALL PROGRAM "LIBRARY.DLL". CODE=0x485 PGM=CALLDLL
>ADDR=004012F6

Am I correct if I understand that your Cobol code is eventually ending in
the LIBRARY.DLL?

>ERROR LNK2001: unresolved external symbol "_insert_string"

At this level, are you positive that the DLL has been linked in without
error?

If not, one thing that stroke me is; Do you have the dll present in the same
directory?

>JMP0015I-U CANNOT CALL PROGRAM "LIBRARY.DLL". CODE=0x485 PGM=CALLDLL
>ADDR=004012F6

Too bad I don't know Fujitsu good enough, it surprises me that there is no
other Fujitsu users out there having coped with this and being able to help
you.

>fatal error LNK1123: failure during conversion to COFF: file invalid or
>corrupt. return code '0x19'

I believe there is a compiler directive in Borland C that makes it MS
compatible, or am I wrong?

>I'm almost giving up... But I found one very interesting point in your
>answer: what you think about call API's LoadLibrary and GetProcAddress from
>Cobol? Could it work?

Generally speaking; yes it should work. It only demands that Fujitsu
supports the handle data type and function prototypes. In case so, it is
worth giving it a shot. At least you will be able to narrow down the problem
area. If it does not work here either, there is definitely something wrong
in the Borland C DLL. You don't have any other language you may try to load
the DLL from, just to make sure it is proper?

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Borland C & Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991021184832.05216.00000118@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7um03a$s6m$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`

```
In article <7um03a$s6m$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>, "Nilo" <npaim@zaz.com.br>
writes:
  >>>> major snippage <<<<<<
>>area. If it does not work here either, there is definitely something wrong
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

If you are able to call the DLL from another Borland C program, then it is
reasonably safe to figure that this is in LIBRARY.DLL with an entry-point
of _insert_string.

I have some concerns for how you might be able to compile a COBOL program
with the '_' as part of the DLL call. Skipping last this.

If you call the DLL like 
       CALL "_insert_string" USING ......
This is NOT a DYNAMIC call and the LIBRARY.LIB file must be included
in the LINK process.   I am not sure about how to get a C .LIB file into the
LINK unless it is entered on the C-Library line.   When working with COBOL
.DLL files, I include the .LIB of  the DLLs with the OBJ of the Calling
program.

If you call the DLL like 
      CALL MYDLLNAME USING .....
where MYDLLNAME is in Working-Storage and contains a value of '_insert_string',
This would be DYNAMIC and requires the DLOAD compile option to be set to YES.
In this case the .LIB file from the .DLL is not required during the LINK
process.

In either case , since you are using multiple entry-points, You must setup an
[ENTRY] cross reference area to identify the 'entry point'='dllname'.

There may be additional syntax to tell the COBOL compiler use C-calling syntax
versus pascal or system.
   CALL..... WITH [C|PASCAL|STDCALL] LINKAGE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Borland C & Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Borland C & Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v1voi$ih7$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7u1us0$hqj$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7u294m$ade$1@news.cerf.net> <7ue0la$vg$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ue7l9$qte$1@news.cerf.net> <7uf5iv$pff$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ufg1i$lm8$1@news.cerf.net> <7uj4ih$43p$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7uknad$nk3$1@news.cerf.net> <7um03a$s6m$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7un8dl$8j7$1@news.cerf.net> <7v0192$q2i$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`

```
Nilo Paim wrote in message <7v0192$q2i$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>...
>I've send you the files. No problem, except that doesn't work  8>)...

Well it did for me. Unfortunately, my email has some other problems :-) So I
have to post the modified source here. As you will see of the comments there
are some small but significant modifications.
I particularly think of the dll call convention and the zero termination. I
do not know if this applies to Fujitsu, but I successfully called your dll
from Acucobol.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. CALLDLL.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           DECIMAL-POINT   IS          COMMA.

       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       77 WS77-WORK           PIC X(070) VALUE SPACES.
       77 WS77-WORK2          PIC X(070) VALUE SPACES.
       77 WS77-POSITION       USAGE SIGNED-INT.
       77 WSERROR             USAGE SIGNED-INT.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       P1.

           MOVE "Nilo" TO WS77-WORK.
           STRING  "My name is " LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO
            WS77-WORK2.
           MOVE 0 TO WS77-POSITION.
      *Acucobol does not auto NULL terminate strings, I have to do that
      * manually.
    MOVE X"00" TO WS77-WORK(70:01).
           INITIALIZE WSERROR.
      *Specify pascal (aka standard) call convention, this may be your
problem
           SET ENVIRONMENT "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.

           CALL    "LIBRARY.DLL"
                    ON EXCEPTION GO TO LOAD-ERR.

           CALL    "_insert_string"
                   USING   BY REFERENCE WS77-WORK
                           BY REFERENCE WS77-WORK2
      *The host function is expecting a value not a pointer
                           BY VALUE WS77-POSITION
                           GIVING WSERROR
                           ON EXCEPTION GO TO EXEC-ERR.

           DISPLAY WS77-WORK AT 0201.
    ACCEPT OMITTED.
           STOP    RUN.

       EXEC-ERR.
           DISPLAY "Error during call of _insert_string" AT 0101.
           STOP    RUN.
       LOAD-ERR.

           DISPLAY "Error during call of library.dll" AT 0101.
           STOP    RUN.

Hope this helps, at least there is nothing wrong with the DLL.

Cheesle
```

```
you need to be - you will link with Borlands Linker rather than the
default MicroSoft linker, and it may very well work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Borland C & Fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Nilo Paim" <npaim@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v4u43$u62$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`
- **References:** `<7u1us0$hqj$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7u294m$ade$1@news.cerf.net> <7ue0la$vg$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ue7l9$qte$1@news.cerf.net> <7uf5iv$pff$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ufg1i$lm8$1@news.cerf.net> <7uj4ih$43p$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7ukau0$bu6$1@news.igs.net> <7v4a3f$gdh$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br> <7v4d00$skn$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Cheesle <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> escreveu nas not�cias de
mensagem:7v4d00$skn$1@news.cerf.net...
> Nilo Paim wrote in message <7v4a3f$gdh$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>...
> >A lot of guys have posted answers about this saga... Well, finally it
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Dear Cheesle,

A lot of things you told me was part of the solution.

1. You said that Acucobol does not auto NULL terminate strings. Well,
neither Fujitsu does.

2. Pascal call convention is valid only in Fujitsu 16 bits compiler, not in
32 bits, that is the situation. When used CALL xxxx WITH STDCALL LINKAGE
(tip from Sff5ky) it works.

3. The command SET ENVIRONMENT ...  is not valid under Fujitsu Cobol V3.
Item 2 replaces it (so and so).

4. You are completely right about the use of BY VALUE and BY REFERENCE. If
the C function expects an *int* and not a pointer, i must pass it BY VALUE.

5. USAGE SIGNED-INT does not work in Fujitsu. The correct in this case is
PIC S9(4) COMP-5.

6. There's no need of CALL "LIBRARY.DLL" before calling functions inside th
dll. The trick in Fujitsu is made in a file called COBOL85.CBR, that makes
the association from entry points to the file that contains the dll. Here's
the content of the file:

[CALLDLL.ENTRY]
_insert_string=library.dll
_edit_line=library.dll
_str_index=library.dll
_str_len=library.dll

....and so on....

Finally, i think the congratulations are for all the guys that made this
work, including you, my friend.

Thanks a lot.

Nilo npaim@zaz.com.br
```

```
On Mon, 18 Oct 1999, Bob Wolfe wrote:
[a bit of redaction here...]
>
> Sure!  The meaning of the word, "minimum" is "at least" so if your
> maximum is 50, you're all set......just don't let it drop a point or
> two!

Hmmmmmm, "...a point or two" suggests that I could get away with a one
point drop ... which would be 49 ... which is less than a minimum of 50.
This suggests something ... but with an IQ of 50 (max) I cannot discern
it clearly!


> 
> Do they teach COBOL at Foothill - De Anza Community College District?
> Enquiring minds want to know!!
> 
> 

They used to teach COBOL at both Foothill and De Anza; now there is only
a two quarter course at Foothill. Yet the District itself is a 95% DEC
OpenVMS COBOL shop; indeed, one of their system programs which allows
users to submit job requests for the student records system is written in
COBOL (written in-house by one system programmer who prefers COBOL and DCL
to C and AWK). De Anza eliminated its COBOL courses when they moved the
students off of the DEC VAX and onto PCs. Despite the fact that COBOL
programs handle the human resource data, the financial resource data, and all 
student data, apparently the college CIS department decided COBOL was a
dead language. Yet every paycheck these folk cash is printed via a COBOL
program with embedded PostScript code. 

ibis
=======================================================
COBOL and Latin are dead languages only to dead minds.
=======================================================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I want to learn.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38129EB9.D1BC1097@att.net>`
- **References:** `<37FB9855.6D69A511@indiana.edu> <37FBA0FF.200AFC85@home.com> <37FE29FD.FEEB17B0@hotmail.com> <7tla50$cak$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <37FE5D54.4193E3D7@home.com> <37ffa248.9029552@news.enter.net> <Pine.LNX.4.10.9910160718040.5571-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <380b0eef.4045927@news.enter.net> <Pine.LNX.4.10.9910201150080.10143-100000@octane.dasb.fhda.edu> <7usl6a$2kg$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <38121591.16DC822B@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Ib Tanding wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> my Pitman's).

Not shorthand, e.g., Pitman (Pittman?), it's mostly a bunch of
abbreviations based on Latin words/phrases. For example, BID = twice a
day, TID = three times a day, QID = four times a day, Q4H = every 4
hours, Q6H = every 6 hours. HS = bedtime, PRN = at the nurse's
discretion, PO = orally, etc. BTW, Q6H is not usually the same as QID.
QID might be be 10am, 2pm, 6pm & 10pm. Q6H would literally be every 6
hours. You never worked on a formulary.

Bill Lynch
```

```
Are you a company looking for a talented programmer who not only knows
what he is doing, but loves what he is doing?

I have just recently graduated with a degree in computer programming
(gpa=3.9), and I am looking for work.  I have experience with both
desktop and mainframe programming.  Everything you need to know about me
can be found on my website at:

http://www.netins.net/showcase/joeycool/resume.htm

Thank you for your time, and remember, the company that gives me a
chance is the company that wins!

- Joey
```

```
bit harsh which would you rather have? An honest opinion or just a "It's 
fine" comment?? If I noticed it I bet someone else did and it might have 
turned them off as well. 

 Here is one thought.. when companies go through staff reductions there 
are numbers of very good Cobol programmers are let go(Staff Adjusted). 
Most programmers are viewed as costs and if the cost is reduced then it 
will improve the bottom line and that's what matters. 

 It should be a win win situation .. you get money and befits and the 
company profits from your skills. And given your GPA I am sure any 
company would befit by making you part of there staff.

Jeff..
```

#### ↳ Re: Give a graduate a chance!!!

- **From:** Joey <joeyprog.takethisout@takethisout.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380FDF85.1CAD231@takethisout.yahoo.com>`
- **References:** `<380BEB36.85E39E4B@yahoo.com>`

```
OK, in reading a lot of what has come about this original posting, I think
some people need to read it again.  I did NOT "sign" my name as joeycool.
It is just in my website address, and to get  free web space would just put
distracting advertisements on the web page.  And yes it showed up in my
reply-to address for some reason, but I did get that fixed.  If you were to
see my website, you would see the email address potential contacts are
supposed to use, which is jrockhold@"see.it.yourself", and that the website
is designed very professionally.

Next, I read someone mentioning a smiling face typed in this message I
posted.  Where exactly is that? (see below and reply with an arrow in it)

And my final remark, because I feel like this could go in circles forever.
Yes, the word 'joeycool' may seem childish, but it was fun to me when I got
it OVER 3 YEARS AGO.  And yes, I still like it, it's a play off of Snoopy's
character "Joe Cool".  My friends like, I like it, my website has it in it's
address.  So???  It's for my own personal use anyways.

And if a company passes me over because of this insignificant thing that has
created all this discussion, well then it is their loss.  A company like
that is a company that would probably complain if I put a humorous
programming cartoon on my cubicle wall.  A happy programmer is a productive
programmer.

And besides, I know how to act like a professional, I've worked in office
environments for years now.  Thank goodness the places I worked before were
never this petty about things.

Joey Rockhold wrote:

> Are you a company looking for a talented programmer who not only knows
> what he is doing, but loves what he is doing?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Joey
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Give a graduate a chance!!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1PZP3.11087$y61.103533@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<380BEB36.85E39E4B@yahoo.com> <380FDF85.1CAD231@takethisout.yahoo.com>`

```
Joey, believe me, no one has been trying to dump on you, but give
you good advice from many years of experience.  Personally, I have
been gainfully employed writing software for over 31 years, the last
20 years owning my own company.  My comment about the crayon face
was just an attempt to illustrate by reductio ad absurdum, that
frivolity has no place in a business *introduction*.  Like you, I
would hate (and probably refuse) to work at a place that would not
tolerate reasonable humor.  But that is not the issue here.  The
issue is 'First Impression'.  A typical manager has 10,000 things
begging his attention.  If you have ever managed people, you will
know that it is an environment of constant change of focus as your
attention is diverted over and over again by interruptions upon
interruptions.  A manager is constantly filtering out tons and tons
of noise, trying to use his mental energy on what is important.  If
his first impression of your post is not very good *immediately*,
then it's just noise, as far as he is concerned, because he has
probably wasted his time on a thousand zeros who weren't worth the
effort, and is not eager to do so again.  If this happens, you will
not *get* that chance to impress him with your ability.  You would
have blown it for that manager.  You can complain and boost
yourself until you are blue in the face, talking about how the
company is the loser, but when you remain unemployed, you are the
loser too, no? :-)

My suggestion is simply this, keep your humor and cutesy names to
yourself, until you have had an opportunity to present your case
fairly to a prospective employer.  From the dawn of time, when
people sent out resumes, they work very hard making them as perfect
as possible, using expensive paper, professional printing, etc.,
just to make that First Impression a good one.  And after all, if
you are unable to restrain yourself even that little bit, then what
does that say about your ability to be serious when needed. ;-)

And Joey, I really hope you get that job. :-)
```

---

<a id="t-a4062df3b2"></a>
## Thread: Assignment Problems — 15 msgs

### Assignment Problems

- **From:** "Judy Shepherd" <sheps@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pxDO3.2639$E4.4337@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
I'm having a few problems with my latest assignment and am hoping there are
a few of you here that may have the time to give me some pointers....I have
done my homework....but I'm dong this course externally and there isn't a
study group or anyone I can ask in this district.

The basic problem is that I need to write an interactive display, starting
with a Main screen, this screen should then ask the user a question...if
"yes" then it should go to another screen (this screen will eventually be
the starting point for calling up another program)....and the user should be
able to remain here until they decide to quit this screen at which point
they should return to the first screen....if "no" then the main screen
should ask another question....again if "yes" to a third screen which will
also call another program again etc etc....if "no" the main screen should
ask if the user wants to quit and if so display an end of session message
and close the screen...if the user does not want to quit...the whole process
should repeat.

I have written about 5 versions of this program so far with no luck and I
feel like I'm painting myself into a corner.....I don't know whether to use
a screen section for all screens or not or whether to just display the
questions and the looping is just not happening....

I am working on a PC...using RM/Cobol 85 and have attached a copy of the
last version that I have written.

Hopefully someone will have some suggestions for me.

Jude.


begin 666 Prog07.cbl
`
end
```

```
The following is the last version of my program in its entirety....typed...I
couldn't attach the file.


identification division.
program-id.   prog07.
author.   j.  shepherd.

environment division.

data division.
working-storage section.

01    ws-cust-enq-resp                pic x.
01    ws-update-resp                   pic x.
01    ws-end-cust-enq                 pic x.
01    ws-end-update                    pic x.
01    ws-end-session                   pic x.

screen section.

01    main-screen            background blue foreground white
    03    blank screen.
    03    'PAINT SHOP MAIN SCREEN'
    03            line 3 col 30.
    03    'Do you wish to do a customer enquiry?'
                    line 6 col 5.
    03    'Do you wish to update the master file?'
                    line 10 col 5.

01    main-clear-screen.
    03    line 6 col 50                value '_'.
    03    line 10 col 50              value '_'.

01    main-input-screen.
    03    line 6 col 50                pic x to ws-cust-enq-resp.
    03    line 10 col 50              pic x to ws-update-resp.

01    main-output-screen.
    03    line 6 col 50                pic x from ws-cust-enq-resp.
    03    line 10 col 50              pic x from ws-updat-resp.
    03    line 24 col 1                value
                'Press enter to continue or "Quit" to end'
                blank line.

01    cust-enq-screen.
    03    blank screen.
    03    'CUSTOMER ENQUIRY SCREEN'
                line 3 col 30.

01     cust-clear-screen.
    03    line 6 col 50            value '_'.
    03    line 10 col 50          value '_'.

01    cust-input-screen.
    03    line 24 col 60        pic x to ws-end-cust-enq.

01    cust-output-screen.
    03    line 24 col 1            value
            'Press enter to continue or "Quit" to end'
            blank line.

01    update-screen.
    03    blank screen.
    03    'MASTER UPDATE SCREEN'
            line 3 col 30.

01    update-clear-screen.
    03    line 6 col 50            value '_'.
    03    line 10 col 50           value '_'.

01    udate-input-screen.
    03    line 24 col 60            pic x to ws-end-update.

01    update-output-screen.
    03    line 24 col 1            value
            'Press enter to continue or "Quit" to end'
            blank line.

01    end-session-screen.
    03    blank screen.

procedure division.

control-module.
    perform a-open-main-screen
    perform b-select-option
            until ws-end-session = 'Q' or 'q'
    perform c-close-daily-session
    stop run.

a-open-main-screen.
    display main-screen.

b-select-option.
    display main-clear-screen
    display main-input-screen
    display main-output-screen
    accept main-intput-screen
            if ws-cust-enq-resp = 'y'
                    perform ba-process-cust-enq
                            until ws-end-cust-enq = 'Q' or 'q'
            if ws-update-resp = 'y'
                    perform bb-process-update
                            until ws-end-update = 'Q' or 'q'
    accept ws-end-session
            line 24 col 60
            prompt '_'.

ba-process-cust-enq.
    display cust-enq-screen
    display cust-output-screen
    accept ws-end-cust-enq
            line 24 col 60
            prompt '_'.

bb-process-update.
    display update-screen
    display update-output-screen
    display ws-end-update
            line 24 col 60
            prompt '_'.

c-close-daily-session.
    display end-session-screen
    display 'Session ended as Requested'
            line 20 col 14.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Assignment Problems - with typed prog

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380BD7FD.5DB5337F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<pxDO3.2639$E4.4337@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FtFO3.2775$E4.4237@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```


Judy Shepherd wrote:
> 
> Judy Shepherd <sheps@bigpond.com> wrote in message
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > study group or anyone I can ask in this district.

Judy,

Think you've done a pretty good job on your homework, but you've
confused yourself putting too much on the screen at the same time. I
tried hacking your program around, but I think you will find the
following pseudo code more useful to get what you want. This is NOT the
only way to do it, and others could give you a variation on a theme -
but it should guide you in the right direction :-

- (1) Display a Customer Screen -A/c #, Name, address etc.
- Accept Customer # (Pic x or 9)
- if Customer # = spaces/zeroes - then quit
- if not spaces/zeroes
  set CustomerFound to true
  Read CustomerFile using Customer #
    invalid key set RecordNotFound to true

NOTE : You DO NOT allow user to change an Account # once entered.
       (i.e. the file PrimeKey )

NEW RECORD : CustomerNotFound -

- Prompt user to enter fields systematically
- then display additional line "change" "OK Update" or "next"
- (2) Change - let user selectively change fields EXCEPT the Account #
- OK to update -  do validity checks :-

	if errors - highlight errors - takes you back to (2)
	if not errors WRITE record go back to (1)

- Next - ignore and go back to (1)

EXISTING RECORD : CustomerFound

-  set RecordNotChanged to true
-  display additional line "change", "delete", "OK" or "next"

- (3) if change - let user select fields to change - and as field  
selected set RecordChanged to true - Again don't let user changed
Account #
  
- if OK AND RecordChanged - do validity checks

	if errors - highlight errors - takes you back to (3)
	if not errors REWRITE record go back to (1) 

- if OK AND RecordNotChanged - go back to (1)

- if delete prompt - "OK to delete this record ?"
     - if delete 'Y' DELETE record go back to (1)
     - if delete 'N' go back to (1)

- Next - ignore and go back to (1)

As and when you get into Windows the same pseudo logic applies.

PS: In case you are not clear on the "set" business :-

 01 RecordFlag		pic 9.		These "flags" can be
    88 RecordFound      value 1.	numeric or alphabetic
    88 RecordNotFound   value 0.

 01 ChangeFlag		pic 9.
    88 RecordChanged	value 1.
    88 RecordNotChanged value 0.

......................

Hope you aren't confused <G>.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Assignment Problems

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380c9544.11906100@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<pxDO3.2639$E4.4337@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7uflor$o9l$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <zHSO3.2977$E4.4799@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"Judy Shepherd" <sheps@bigpond.com> wrote:

>Unfortunately my COBOL lecturer would frown on me using another platform for
>what is essentially a COBOL assignment...lol.

Judy:

COBOL sp2 uses ANSI standard COBOL CALL USING statements and COBOL
WORKING STORAGE variables to control the user interface.

I don't really consider it to be another platform and it does support
RM COBOL very well, but I do see a few possible problems.  I suspect
that your instructor *wants* you to use DISPLAY/ACCEPTS or screen
section for your user interface screens.

This would certainly prevent you from using sp2.

You might consider getting one or more of the introductory COBOL books
to help you with the user interface.  There are several, including:

Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours by Thane Hubbell...and
Teach Yourself COBOL in 21 Days by Mo Budlong.

I believe if you access Amazon.com, you'll be able to find both of
these books.

Good luck!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-37eea428fe"></a>
## Thread: Perform statement question — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Here the standard is mostly PERFORM THRU.  I use it to GO TO EXIT and yes I've been bit (wrong exit - result - infinate loop at 2am).  Many moons ago when I learned structured programming the GO TO statement was verbotin.  Internal sorts were not allowed because they required a GO TO to skip over the input procedure (well - that's another discussion).  I would prefer just a straight PERFORM as I don't get a lot of calls at 2am as a result but when doing maintenance on some very old code ya go with the flow.

In any event I have found that in CICS programs it is rather hard to avoid the PERFORM THRU and the use of the GO TO EXIT if one uses HANDLE CONDITION instead of response codes.

For those of you not familiar with CICS coding a HANDLE CONDITION tells the program what to do in the case of an 'error' such as a not found in the read of a file.  These end up translating into GO TO statements - so if you don't find the record you're looking for the program will GO TO the paragraph you designate to handle that condition. 

So...
In a CICS program you would code a PERFORM THRU where you perform the read paragraph with a GO TO EXIT after the read (which means you've found the record) followed by the paragraph that does what you want done if you don't find the record, followed by the exit.  Control returns to where you did the initial perform and execution continues.

PERFORM READ-FILE THRU READ-EXIT

READ-FILE.
   EXEC CICS READ 

   GO TO READ-EXIT

RECORD-NOT-FOUND.
   MOVE 'N' TO FOUND-SW

READ-EXIT.
   EXIT.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380BB7AA.7130644A@att.net>`
- **References:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> In any event I have found that in CICS programs it is rather hard to avoid the PERFORM THRU and the use of the GO TO EXIT if one uses HANDLE CONDITION instead of response codes.

So why not omit the "HANDLE CONDITION" commands, code your imperative
commands with RESP(response_field), then EVALUATE your response_field?
Works for me.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "Gunnar Opheim" <gunnar.opheim@eunet.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1a6a$ff87f920$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`
- **References:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote in article
<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>...
> In any event I have found that in CICS programs it is rather hard to
avoid the PERFORM THRU and the use of the GO TO EXIT if one uses HANDLE
CONDITION instead of response codes.

Then simply don't use it.  HANDLE CONDITION was a bad idea from the CICS
lab.  It is far more safe to code explicit tests on response codes after
each command. I find no trouble at all using single period paragraphs and
avoiding PERFORM THRU and GO TO in CICS programs.

perform Read-file

Read-file.
    exec cics read ... nohandle end-exec
    evaluate EIBRESP
        when DFHRESP(NORMAL) continue
        when DFHRESP(NOTFND) move 'N' to FOUND-SW
        when other perform Error-routine
    end-evaluate
    .

The Error-routine may set return info and do exec cics return.

Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380DD055.9C698078@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
>Many moons ago when I learned structured programming the GO TO statement was verbotin. > Internal sorts were not allowed because they required a GO TO to skip over the input 
> procedure (well - that's another discussion).

OK, starting the other discussion.  I have never written an internal
sort with a GO TO.  Why do you need to "skip over the input procedure"?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ukng2$9bp$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <380DD055.9C698078@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
NONE of the following applies to the '85 Standard, but if you had a
"strictly" conforming '74 Standard COBOL program with an INPUT or OUTPUT
procedure, you DID need to use the GO TO.  This was because:

A) The Input/Output procedure had to be a Section name

B) It would be "performed" once

C) You could NOT PERFORM anything outside of that Section.

Therefore, you would end-up with logic either like,

 ... Sort ... Input Procedure XYZ

XYZ Section.
    Get your data
   If Not out of input data
     Release your data
     Go to XYZ
    .

       *** or   ***

XYZ Section.
   AAA-Para.
      Perform BBB-Para until whatever
      If Whatever Go To XYZ-EXIT
   BBB-Para.
      do your gets and releases.
XYZ-Exit.
     Exit.

Either you had to use the GO TO to do the loop or you had to use it to avoid
"falling thru" a paragraph after your loop was done.

NOTE: Lots of compilers allowed you to PERFORM something outside the range of
the INPUT/OUTPUT procedure - but that wasn't standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perform statement question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380DEB38.C1ACF719@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s80b66e8.004@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <380DD055.9C698078@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ukng2$9bp$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> NONE of the following applies to the '85 Standard, but if you had a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> C) You could NOT PERFORM anything outside of that Section.

I guess the compilers I used did not comply with "C)" above.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<KW5P3.1480$pJ3.101745@news.flash.net>`

```
I know this is a Cobol NG, but there must be someone reading this that works
on an AS/400 and is at least familiar with the name RPG (as in Report
Program Generator and NOT Role Playing Game)

I am trying to find a (x86) compiler for RPG 2 (which I know exists
somewhere) and/or RPG AS/400 (may exist for x86 but not sure)  Any kind of
resources are heplful as well.

Can anyone help me out?

Thanks,

Steve K.
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 Related

- **From:** paulr@delphi.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380bb8aa$1$cnhye$mr2ice@news.stonemedia.com>`
- **References:** `<KW5P3.1480$pJ3.101745@news.flash.net>`

```
Sure Steve - though I admit you would be a lot better off with COBOL. :) 
For an AS/400 compatible RPGII style compiler, you will probably need to
use 'Baby/400 From California Software Products.' This is an AS/400
emulator that comes with CL and RPG compilers, but... I would not
recommend buying from the company.  Cost is something like $4K I think.

For an AS/400 RGG IV compatible compiler, you can pick up VisualAge RPG,
which lets you do a lot of things, including client/server front ends on a
PC running Windows95 or Windows NT. It comes from IBM and much more
information  is available on their web site about it. Cost is around $2700
I think. 

Hope that helps. 

-Paul
```

<a id="t-5041219940"></a>
## Thread: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ueh3s$d9c$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <3809c85e$1$200@nntp1.ba.best.com> <7udr9q$9to$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Switch P2 is Logical Cancel. By default (16-bit shared runtime),
CANCEL releases program from memory. Specfying +P2
prevents releasing the program from memory when CANCELed.

Switch M2 is Active Program Discard. By default (16-bit shared
runtime), the RTE will discard procedure code of active programs
and reload when needed. This may cause "out of memory" if there
is no room to reload. Specifying +M2 does not discard procedure
code and may cause "memory full" if the CALLed program cannot
be loaded.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uh243$6fl$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <3809c85e$1$200@nntp1.ba.best.com> <7udr9q$9to$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <7ueh3s$d9c$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
    Bottom line answer to original question - If you do not cancel, than the
memory is NEVER freed, working storage, etc anyway.  The procedure division
might be discarded (a la windows 1.0), but the rest of the meory is NEVER
released.

    The +M0  (+M) switch is supoosed to control overwriting command.com.  I
have no idea if it still preserves the memory image of command.com with the
newest versions of dos.  That switch can be used at all times unless you use
XM, in which case who cares about 30K or so of memory.

    Of course the original problem was in a 32 bit environment.  I believe
that when switch p2 is unset (-P2), that it marks the program as inactive,
but does not free memory.  If you call it again soon enough, it will run in
an initial state, but will not access the file system.  If you run out of
memory, it would do a garbage collection of sorts and discard all canceled
programs from memory.
```

## Thread: Student needs help with printing blank line.... — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student needs help with printing blank line....

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ugos0$ei1$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<SCbO3.2058$Gj1.15395@newsfeed.slurp.net> <7ugibk$sf$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Disagreeing (but only a little).

"Line Sequential" is "by definition" not ANSI/ISO conforming (at least in any
implementation that requires you to "say" Line Sequential).  Therefore, it
may (or may not) work as described below.  (I think it probably does - in
most implementations).

HOWEVER, in any ANSI/ISO conforming implementation WRITE (with no
AFTER/BEFORE clause) of a file that is ANSI/ISO conforming (i.e. nothing to
"say" it is line or record sequential) *MUST* work like a WRITE AFTER
ADVANCING 1.

Now what that means in "practice" will vary TREMENDOUSLY on whether you are
talking about files that are really intended for "printers" (or the like) or
just as "data".  This also varies a LOT on the operating system, compiler,
and "file system" (which may or may not be designed with COBOL in mind).
```

## Thread: OLE Automation — 4 msgs

### OLE Automation

- **From:** "Alluyn Peter" <pa@schaubroeck.be>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1953$7bdb1ac0$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be>`

```
Hello,

I have a question about OLE Automation,

After defining word as an OLE class in the class section you can create an
instance of the word ole-server by  invoke word "new" returning wordServer.
 How can you get an instace of the word ole-server when it's already
running ?

Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: OLE Automation

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380B75EF.4BCE83A0@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<01bf1953$7bdb1ac0$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be>`

```
Alluyn Peter wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Peter
Peter,

In NetExpress, to make two or more instances from a class, I would code:

Data Division
...
01  usage object-reference.
    02  firstInstance.
    02  secondInstance.
    02  thirdInstance.
...
Procedure division.
...
    Invoke "Word" "new" returning firstInstance
    Invoke "Word" "new" returning secondInstance
    Invoke "Word" "new" returning thirdInstance
a.s.o.

The Frog
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OLE Automation

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ug2j7$1a6$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<01bf1953$7bdb1ac0$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be> <380B75EF.4BCE83A0@IMN.nl>`

```

>Alluyn Peter wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> After defining word as an OLE class in the class section you can create
an
>> instance of the word ole-server by  invoke word "new" returning
wordServer.
>>  How can you get an instace of the word ole-server when it's already

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


That solution is not the solution to the original problem.

Peter, to my knowledge there is no way to do this using OLE calls.
However you may find a API call which grabs the external resource returning
a handle.
If this is possible(which IM sure it is) I would be interested in the
solution.
However I've never had a need for this kind of problem, tell my why
would you want to do this?.



Simon R Hart
Eaton, Ottery St. Mary, UK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OLE Automation

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uhfb1$2f4$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bf1953$7bdb1ac0$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be> <380B75EF.4BCE83A0@IMN.nl> <7ug2j7$1a6$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
"Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com> wrote:
>Peter, to my knowledge there is no way to do this using OLE calls.
>However you may find a API call which grabs the external resource returning
>a handle.
>If this is possible(which IM sure it is) I would be interested in the
>solution.

The capability requested by Peter is largely controlled by the server 
application, not the client.

Word registers itself as "Multiple use" server when it starts running. 
This means that each client, when asking for the server via the standard 
OLE API(s) to create server objects (CoCreateInstance(Ex)), gets its own 
version of the server started for it (at least for local servers).

(Had Word registered itself as a "Single use" server, each client would 
connect to the same running version.)

Another API (CoGetInstanceFromFile) does allow you to connect to 
some existing servers when specifying a file (although in Word's case, it 
doesn't actually load the file!). The current Micro Focus Net Express 3.0 
product doesn't (yet) support this use directly via its run-time OLE 
support.

Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: WebMaster's Censor

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ug17s$rjm$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s0msku8r0163@corp.supernews.com>`

```
comp.lang.cobol is *NOT* a moderated newsgroup.  There is NO way that anyone
can be (much less would be) censoring your posts.  HOWEVER, a number of us
(myself included) have been finding increasing problems with ISP's not
posting all of the notes from this (or other) newsgroups.  If you use the
Deja.Com facility to "view" all the posts to comp.lang.cobol, I think you
will see that all your posts (some with replies) are indeed there.

On a purely personal point of view, I have found some of your "individual"
posts to be a little "absolute" in seeming (to me as a reader) to indicate
that what your views are of "good" programming to be the ONLY way to program.
However, I would strongly support your right to post such views to the NG - I
just may disagree with them - either publicly or privately.

NOTE WELL: if you do post to other newsgroups, do check and see if they are
or are not "moderated".  "Moderated" newsgroups explicitly DO "censor"
posts - to meet pre-specified rules and topics.
```

```
I believe that when everyone has his/her voice, we can see the truth
from different points of views.

Thanks to your web, I learn a lot from posters against me. Without
them, it takes me long or never learn the technologies beyond my reach.
Programming skills helps open mind to accept ideas that may offend me
at first.

Please, note that I do not post ideas that have been posted already.
```

## Thread: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uel0f$eiu$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <3809c85e$1$200@nntp1.ba.best.com> <8xvO3.6647$iu4.80234@news2.mia> <7ue1as$3li$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hopefully I can clarify some of the problems. After this you'll still be
confused - but in a higher level.

I took the liberty to copy/paste the RTS-Switches. I took them from
Workbench 4.x, because some of them changed when the 32-bit compiler was
introduced. This list contains the meaning of switches both in 16-bit and in
32-bit.

The CANCEL statement has no effect on OS/2.

In 16-bit systems only MF-cobol programs will be removed from memory. Mixed
language applications will depend on other compilers ability to clean up.

In 32-bit systems te cancel-statement will remove the module from memory,
and you will depend upon the 'foreign' compilers ability to handle open
files, pointers etc.etc.

As far as I can see, the CANCEL will free up memory without any switch
settings. Actually there was a bug in the earlier versions of WB 3.2 with
Dialog System 2.5.<39. The memory was not freed properly. One of my
customers had to reboot at 1 p.m. until the bug was fixed.

The switch, you remember could be the 'M'-switch - It frees memory (28K)
below 640K in old DOS-systems, but if you invoke the application with .BAT
files, you might have a problem.

Please, Bill. Look at the list, and see if one of the switches ring a bell,
or if a switch is missing (I shouldn't be surprised).

Regards
Ib
PS.: Here is the list:

0, 1, 2, ... 8 Programmable switches
A Choose ANSI file handling and status codes (16-bit only)
A Invoke Animator for intermediate code (32-bit only)
A1 Display trailing spaces (DOS, Windows and OS/2 only)
A1 Invoke Base Animator for intermediate code (UNIX only)
B, B1 Skip locked record
B2 Choose the sorting algorithm
C Enable screens longer than 25 lines
C1 Nonstandard size screens

C2 Disable COBOL Text Window
C4 Sets screen into 43-line mode
C5 Sets screen into 50-line mode
D Invoke the ANSI COBOL Debug module
d Set memory size for dynamic paging buffer
D1 Control search method for .dll files
D2 Disable DLL-cancel error reporting
E Execute code containing S-level errors
e Search for C as well as COBOL programs
/E Signal-handling stack size
F Validate numeric data in intermediate code

/F Change file open limit
G Reduce the size of I/O buffers
i Enable keyboard interrupts
I1 Change program search order
I2 Redirect RTS errors to STDERR
K Enable sequence checking on indexed write
K1 Use old IBM PC keyboard handling
K2 Use extended IBM PC keyboard handling
K3 Enable Shift+Ctrl+Break behavior
K4 Pause on RTS error
L Make I/O errors return to program

l Specify memory available for application
L1 Enable the exception popup on OS/2
L2 Determine record terminator for line sequential files
L5 Handle LEVEL II COBOL files
L6 Specify subprogram search order
/L Specify number of record locks
M Free up memory on DOS
M2 Disable paging of active programs
/M Change default number of heaps on OS/2
N Insert nulls in line sequential files

O Disable divide-by-zero error
P Use high-level printer I/O
p Fix up all linkage item addresses on entry
P1 Sound system bell using the BIOS
P2 Enable logical cancels
P3 Increase number of programs loadable
/P Specify address of port for security key
Q Map extended file status codes
R Retry locked record until available
S Order duplicates in sort/merge operations
s Specify memory available for sort

S2 Disable video "snow" protection
S3 Enable video "snow" protection
S5 Use ansi.sys-compatible console I/O
S6 Change DISPLAY UPON CRT attribute behavior
/S Change default stack size
T Insert tabs in line sequential files
Z Control conversion of DBCS spaces
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ugfip$i6r$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com> <3809c85e$1$200@nntp1.ba.best.com> <8xvO3.6647$iu4.80234@news2.mia> <7ue1as$3li$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <7uel0f$eiu$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <s0msmb3qr0187@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The switch is giving you command.com's transient area for your application.
That means, that command.com cannot 'remember' last command entered. You
need that when you return to the batch file to stop or execute next program.

Remember that this switch is only for good old DOS. If you need to maintain
programs on DOS, I can recommend that you use XM. Another thing about old
DOS systems if they run on old computers - the problem will be solved in a
couple of months - they will not pass Y2K.

If this didn't help you out, let me hear about it. I have this funny feeling
that your problem is something else - Very large WS- or Linkage-areas,
Directives or Local Storage.
Regards
Ib
```

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: COBOL compiler options problem!

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380B1CCE.37395A30@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7ue0oc$o7r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ue73v$1an$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Mr Klein,

I don't have access to Listserv:...

> >   Secondly,do subroutine need to be linkedited?

Link them NCAL  (never call) it is a parameter option, you can link
them like this and then link edit your load module into you main
program.  Never call makes them a load module but does not resolve the
calls in the module.  Be careful that you make the subroutine DYNAM as
well or S0C4 there will be.

> > I think main program must be linkedited ,but subroutine need  not be
> > linkedited,because they will not run themselves? Is it right?

The system calls have stubs linked in (they must be linked) and the
runtime resolves most of the calls (not all of them, some are
statically linked).

> > Recently,I have tried to compile subroutine without linkedit,but
> > when I tried to compile & linkedit main program,there is an error,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

name(xyz)
name(sub1)
name(sub2)
..... whatever you do now ....

Ken
```

```
Pat,

Firstly, try 'COPY file  LPT1' to print the file.  NO need to actually
use Word.

Second.  When formatting data either use the advancing clause or don't
things tend to get confusing otherwise.  If you can hex dump the file
you might find that the characters are linefeeds (control J (Hex 0A)
and not the carriage return (Hex 0D) linefeed pairs.

Ken
```

<a id="t-16b058d4be"></a>
## Thread: Student needs help with printing blank line.... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student needs help with printing blank line....

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0mslsl0r0161@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<SCbO3.2058$Gj1.15395@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Dear:

In all programming output, there could be output-to-sytem-storage such
as databases or data files. There could be output as reports to users.

In your case, you are talking about output as reports to users. In this
case, programmers should be the one who design the reports, that are
already formated in the programming codes. Reports should not be expected
to be formated manually by the users. That is the reason no programming
books discussing on how to use word processing to format reports generated
by a certain programming application. If you want to do that, please,
consult word processing teachers or clerks. Your instructors are wrong
telling you to manually edit a report generated by programming application.
In business, this practice may cause the security issue, for no one is
allowed to edit a report. Reports should be in their original physical
state: files or in databases in the system. A boss who attempts to edit
a report is going to commit a crime.

If you want to print your report, you may open it in any text editors
such as DOS, MS LearnC, Borland C, Visual Basic, Visual C++,
WordPerfect5.1, WordStar or MultimateAdvantageII (they are distinct few
years ago) and print the report from there. They do not have problems as
you have using Windows word processing applications.

The other way to print a report in Text format is sending it to the printer
by the following command:  C:\>copy report.txt LPT1
(LPT1 or LPT2 is the COM port the printer connect to)

Good luck.
```

---

<a id="t-34d9f466b2"></a>
## Thread: Student needs help with printing blank line.... — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student needs help with printing blank line....

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s0msmfccr0173@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<SCbO3.2058$Gj1.15395@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Dear:

Please, use the following codes, that works with all COBOLs in the world.
Some codes work fine with some specific COBOL compiler, not all.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  BLANK-LINE       PIC X(80) VALUE ALL SPACES.
01  GRID-LINE        PIC X(80) VALUE ALL '- '.
 
870-PRINT-HEADINGS. 
        MOVE REPORT-TITLE-LINE    TO  OUT-LINE-RECORD.
        WRITE OUT-LINE-RECORD  AFTER  ADVANCING 1 LINES.
        MOVE COLUMN-HEADING-LINE1 TO  OUT-LINE-RECORD.
        WRITE OUT-LINE-RECORD  AFTER  ADVANCING 3 LINES.

* Move spaces to clear the variables from previous data.
* In some COBOL compilers there is no need to do that.
* This practice works all the time with all COBOL compilers
* although it look tedious to most of programmers, and to me.

        MOVE SPACES               TO  OUT-LINE-RECORD.
        WRITE OUT-LINE-RECORD   FROM  COLUMN-HEADING-LINE2 
                               AFTER  ADVANCING 1 LINE.
        MOVE SPACES               TO  OUT-LINE-RECORD.
        WRITE OUT-LINE-RECORD   FROM  GRID-LINE 
                               AFTER  ADVANCING 1 LINE.
        MOVE SPACES               TO  OUT-LINE-RECORD.
        WRITE OUT-LINE-RECORD   FROM  BLANK-LINE 
                               AFTER  ADVANCING 1 LINE.
The 2 approaches can be used interchangeable.

When you have report, please, use MfCobol Personal(v2.0.02) editor
to view and print it as you do with the source codes.

I have never seen MfCobol Personal(v2.0.02) but I believe it does.
Why don't you try it?
```

#### ↳ Thanks

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WZLO3.316$M5.1964@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<SCbO3.2058$Gj1.15395@newsfeed.slurp.net> <s0msmfccr0173@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Dear:

I read the description of your problem, but I have not read your code yet.

Your problem is: open and read the report in MS Words.

Please, open and read it in DOS. You will see the rectangulars in Words,
or black dots in Notepad are carriage return and line feed in DOS editor.
Most of editors can read your report correctly. You may try with Visual
BASIC or Visual C++ editor by copying the Text in Word and paste in these
editors. MS Words or Notepad is not meant to be programming or text editor.

Good luck.
```

---

<a id="t-199246be1b"></a>
## Thread: FLOAT(HEX) format Help needed — 3 msgs

### FLOAT(HEX) format Help needed

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AyMO3.424$7n3.23970@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
10.18.99

As Mr Klein so often reminds us, COBOL COMP-1 and COMP-2 data definitions
are 'implementor-defined" and I am stuck on one implementor's version of
COMP-1 and COMP-2 data. (It's a BIG implementor, though!)

I have been searching the my manuals, IBM's online bookshelf, the whole
internet, and anyhthing else I can think of and cannot find the
"description" of the data format for COMP-1 and COMP-2 when using FLOAT(HEX)
in IBM COBOL for S/390.

Here are comparable descriptions for FLOAT(IEEE) if anyone needs them (also
to show an example of what I'm looking for?)

COMP-2,  IEEE single precision: 32 bits total. Approx  Range +/- 8.43 E37
to +/-. 3.37E-38
  Bit   31:         Sign Bit (0=positive, 1=negative)
  Bits 30-23      8 bit exponent, positive bias 'x7F'
  Bits 0-22      23 bit normalized mantissa

COMP-1,  IEEE double precision: 64 bits total.  Approx Range +/- 1.67 E 308
to +/- 4.19E-307
  Bit   63:         Sign Bit (0=positive, 1=negative)
  Bits 62-52      11 bit exponent, positive bias 'x3FF'
  Bits 0-51        52  bit normalized mantissa

I have some data I am working with, and for integers I can almost 'deduce'
the FLOAT(HEX) format, but I just cannot seem to figure out how much of the
mantissa is valid when there are decimals in the value. It seems as though
the binary values terminate partway though the length of the four or eight
bytes (depending on exponent?).

Can anyone point me to a reference work on this? On-line, paper (I have a
couple of clients with paper manuals, but finding the correct manual in a
stack 25 feet high.......), type it out..whatever.

NOTE: this format "appears" to be the same as used in VS/COBOL II (MVS) for
COMP-1 and COMP-2. (Can't find that, either).


Regards,
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: FLOAT(HEX) format Help needed

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ug6am$osn$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<AyMO3.424$7n3.23970@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Assuming that you are correct that what FLOAT(HEX) means is the same as what
VS COBOL II (and OS/VS COBOL and IBM COBOL for this-and-that) all mean by
COMP-1 and COMP-2 on the mainframe, then what you need is in the "principles
of operations".

Try (online):

 http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/DZ9AR004/9%2e3
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FLOAT(HEX) format Help needed

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fhPO3.596$7n3.32806@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<AyMO3.424$7n3.23970@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7ug6am$osn$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Perfect! Thank you!
```

```
Just out of curiosity.

How many programmers are compensated by their employers for "on call"
responsibilities.  I talking about being woken up to fix something 3-4 times
per week.

-Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** terrywin.nospam@pronetisp.net (Terry Winchester)
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380cddf3.654094@news.pronetisp.net>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Tue, 19 Oct 1999 05:00:31 -0400, "Chris Skinner"
<chris.skinner@mindspring.com> wrote:

>Just out of curiosity.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>-Chris

Compensated?    BWAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHA.

Seriously, I think it depends on your immediate manager and the type
of individual he/she is.  We go through a manager about every 18
months - some have been great, others were worthless.  The "great"
ones always insisted on comp-time because company policy doesn't 
allow paid overtime for "exempt" types (unfortunately we didn't have
many of these).  The "worthless" PHM's didn't offer anything except an
occasional "thank you".  

A couple of years ago we ditched an old homegrown system and went with
a purchased package.  The late-night calls have been far less, but, as
we continue to "bastardize" the system its probably going to get worse
as time goes on.  

At the end of 1998, we gave up a 4-day weekend (over New Year's) to
install an upgrade.  Due to improper user community testing, we were
fighting fires for over a month.  After the "calm" of the storm, I
informed management that I was no longer available for overtime,
unless it was *directly* related to my work/programs -- if they didn't
like this, they could _fire_ me.  

Funny, I'm still working. . . . . . . 

YMMV
Terry
(who doesn't care anymore)
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

```
times
>per week.

In my experience, if an employer requires 24/7 cover then he pays for a
proper shift system.  Or he get used to high staff turn-over.    It's been
interesting reading the responses to this post.  I think they reflect a
divide between practice in the US and Europe.  In the UK few developers
would tolerate being 'owned' by their employer to such an extent !

Regards,

Stephen Thomas.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: on call

```
I agree, what people tolerate is baffling. You need to realize that you are
selling
your labor to your employer. If he wants more he should pay more, you should
be
compensated for your labor, that's what you are selling. Now this doesn't
mean that
there can't be some give and take, reasonable people do the right thing. But
when things
get to the point of abuse then you should reassess your position and decide
if you
can afford to continue to give away you labor. I did a few months ago, my
employer
was unwilling to be reasonable, unwilling to compensate me so I found
another job.
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Yv5ydFAFRJD4Ewww@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I joined a company back in 1987 which expected all staff to be on call
with payments of 12.50 pounds sterling (about 18.50 US dollars) per
night. There was no extra compensation for being called on Saturdays
although callout did not officially exist for the weekends. After
complaining about the low levels and the frequent call outs (per night)
we got an extra 2.50 pounds. That stayed at that level for 10 years
before being incorporated into the salary. Multiple call outs were the
result of the developers not being the ones who had to maintain their
bodges. 

I agree with the correspondent who said that if you don't like it then
you should move jobs. I did.

In article <7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>, Chris Skinner
<chris.skinner@mindspring.com> writes
>Just out of curiosity.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>-Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s5aP3.352$7X2.28263@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Here's how you stop this silliness.

Note the first call of the week. Whichever programmer is
responsible for the program generating the trouble report
gets the on-call duty for the following week.

Alternative #1: The responsible programmer with the
highest number of trouble calls on his work for the week
gets the duty next week.

Alternative #2: (Suggestions?)

I remember reading that Microsoft had a policy of assigning
the weekly software build to the lowest turnip in the patch.
"Good experience," they said. When the assignment was
changed to the programmer responsible for the most bugs
the week before, quality improved dramatically.
```

```
want to be paid for when asking for your salary.
```

```
We have a rotating schedule.
Each on call stint is for two weeks and being on call is a 7am - 11pm duty.
We are paid an extra day for each week we are on call.

The pager and cell phone will go off as often as you produce lousy code.
We must be doing things right, since our most frequest problems are
UNIX spooler issues...
```

```
To the 'young' man (?) who first raised this topic - go find a new job
at an installation where they know what they are doing.

Given that the installation you are at has decent programming standards
and competent programmers - then the problem lies squarely in the system
design. If some imbecile functioned in the role of 'systems analyst' and
designed a hodge-podge system, then you can expect to remain being on
call until Y3K. No matter how you 'tweek' the programs - until the
system is right(re-designed) the programming will never be right.

If in fact there are no decent programming standards, or the team you
are working with are amateurs - again go find a new job for peace of
mind.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7uiutu$no2$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Join the union or go on strike.

It is interesting to follow this 'one-level' thread. Many of you are working
for nothing - just because you like your job - or afraid to loose it.

Did you promise the company always to be near the phone? If not, pull out
the plug.

Don't you have friends, family, dogs or cats? Slavery is illegal isn't it?

Please young fellows: search your heart. Who would you like to divorce
first - your company or your wife?

If your company expects you to deliver good quality at daytime, they
shouldn't keep you awake at nights.

Well, it's your life as long as it last. If you are not killed in the
traffic or by your wife, you will probably die from a heart failure at the
age of 37. And all that for nothing.

I am being payed to be on call one or two times a month. I go to bed early
to catch some sleep. 2 out of 3 times I am sleeping all night and I earn a
full days payment. The third time i am called to solve something. I log on
to the company from home and the clock starts tikking. If I have 3 hours of
work during the night i'll get paid for 6 on top of the compensation i get
to be on call.

Good luck to everyone - remember to be paid when you work.

Regards
Ib

times
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ukm4k$9eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>,
  "Chris Skinner" <chris.skinner@mindspring.com> wrote:
> Just out of curiosity.
>
> How many programmers are compensated by their employers for "on call"
> responsibilities.

Here's a related question:
I'm looking for opinions: How should a sub-contractor charge for being
on-call (with pager, cell phone, dial-in)?
One client gave us 1 hour's extra pay per day, in addition to 'straight
time' when we did actual work due to a call. Another paid time-and-a-
half for all actual work but nothing for simply carrying the pager.
Has anyone seen/used any other methods of compensation?

At one place we told them we wouldn't go on call, but they said that
was part of the job. No extra compensation - just comp time which we
rarely had time to use. Luckily that project was short-lived.

The real problem is not simply the inconvenient calls, but the implicit
assumption that we'd live a lifestyle conducive to being available
immediately 24/7. I.e., no keg parties, no hiking in remote areas, no
swimming (because we couldn't hear the page), etc.

TIA
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> The venerable "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> bestowed upon

No seriously - I didn't know it. It was a kindly gentleman who wore USAF
blue who wised-up this RAF blue-boy. And as Volker was really saying -
those bloody Yanks are playing word-games with the language again.
Working from home, I'm almost in a cryogenic state - like Michael
Jackson. The only difference is that I am/look like I'm still alive.

I think Donald has already acquainted you with the merits of living in
the Grande Branco Norte. It makes us so much more productive having a
shorter day. Don't knock that ice - the Newfies aren't stupid, even if
their watches are out of sync by half an hour. They imported the damned
stuff, to set the scene, for all those tourists from down south who pour
up, perchance they might find that jewel the lady threw off the back of
the Titanic.

Meanwhile, here in the West we are experiencing above average seasonal
weather. And sitting at 3,500 feet, the oxygen gives me a much clearer
head than you folks down on the Plains.

Seeing as you started it - let's get back to unfinished business.

JEOPARDY! Got to give you that one, because your legalistic mind will
claim it is a trade name as opposed to jeopardy. Talking of
legalistic/jesuitical - perchance you are a drop-out from the same
Jesuit seminary as our friend Alex ? (Still he keeps you lot on your
toes by ensuring JEOPARDY! contains questions relating to above the
49th).

Shame on you - make some effort to answer the movie quiz - First actor
said "Get thee to a nunnery" and the last actor, his eldest son is
contemplating marriage to a gorgeous English(Welsh) beauty. And an even
more direct clue, Warren Simmons should know her name. 

Really didn't have time to check the bears - my childhood books did
raise query, pandas were close but not same family as bears. The latin
names are different aren't they ?

Stop pandering, (pun intended) to us - make an effort with the movie
quiz !

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS: Liked you suggestion to Bill about solving his Level 78 problem.
```

```
The venerable "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Thu, 21 Oct 1999 19:47:35 GMT these words:

>JEOPARDY! Got to give you that one, because your legalistic mind will
>claim it is a trade name as opposed to jeopardy. Talking of
>legalistic/jesuitical - perchance you are a drop-out from the same
>Jesuit seminary as our friend Alex ?

No, I didn't know Alex was a Jesuit.  He doesn't seem the type.  I'm
not a Jesuit either -- I'm not Catholic -- but I understand your
comparison.  

>Shame on you - make some effort to answer the movie quiz - First actor
>said "Get thee to a nunnery" and the last actor, his eldest son is
>contemplating marriage to a gorgeous English(Welsh) beauty. And an even
>more direct clue, Warren Simmons should know her name. 

I don't have a clue.  It's not the kind of question I'm good at.
There are columns on the JEOPARDY! board where I don't ring in.

>Really didn't have time to check the bears - my childhood books did
>raise query, pandas were close but not same family as bears. The latin
>names are different aren't they ?

It was actually a taxonomic dilemma until DNA analysis.  The problem
is that the giant pandas seems so like a bear, the red pandas seems so
like a raccoon, and yet they seem so like each other.  There are
several attributes where the two pandas appear to be closely related,
most notably penis shape.  Reproductive differences are closely
related to speciation.

But in the late 1980s, DNA analysis told the story:  Giant pandas are
related to bears, red pandas are related to raccoons, and the
similarities derive from their common proto-carnivore ancestors.

Last I read, the giant panda is classified in the super-family
Ursoidea, and the family Ursidae, but not in the sub-family Ursinae.

>Stop pandering, (pun intended) to us - make an effort with the movie
>quiz !

No idea.  You'll have to give me a lot more clues, and even then I
don't know if I'll be able to figure it out.
```

```
I smiled, "He doesn't seem the type.", but I understand what you mean.
Don't know too much about them other than their lousy reputation from

worldly-wise of the orders, and I believe, behind the scenes, contribute
to the sciences in USA. Tom Clancy in his fiction uses one as a mentor
for Jack Ryan. And one or two of them have PO'd the Vatican with their
brand of theology - Rome has told them to shut up - but that's
man-organised religion for you - so very far from its original roots in
Judaism/Christianity/Islam.

One last effort on the movies - then we'll call it quits and go to press
:-

(a) Actor 1 - "Get thee to a nunnery" - Jeeves surely has to help you
here  with quotes.
(b) Actor 2 - we already know he was Stewart Granger(nee James Stewart)
- would have thought the Englishman who picked up on that could have
followed through.
(c) Actor 3 - you tie Actor 1 and Actor 4 to the 4th movie, and by a
process of elimination you've got Actor 3 - no Actor 3 doesn't come from
Brooklyn. He's a Limey like 1 and 2.
(d) Actor 4 - I said - his eldest son is contemplating marriage to a
gorgeous English(Welsh) beauty. In the newspapers this week. And the 
4th movie directed by Actor 4 - the name of the movie rhymes with your
e-mail address. Gawd - how much more help do you want. 

(e) Our heroine/actress - I've already told you part of her name. 
 
Just think how many $$$$s this would have given you on JEOPARDY!

BTW : You are getting me nervous, like Pete Dashwood, quote you two
verbatim or there's hell to pay. Now with Michael Mattias it's easier to
remember, "It's the house-decorator not the paint-roller".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: O Canada (Was: COBOL Contractors' Fees : was Re: on call)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00

- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ukm4k$9eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380DF650.9E104628@home.com> <i5oOOBTEzPvfWqn8qXstcdZ7HHaG@4ax.com> <380F6FCD.A3C24F12@home.com> <cAEQOB+Dci83xoRwKGGkhYbf149F@4ax.com> <38112FC3.887ADC08@home.com> <IloQ3.82$OK4.14298@typhoon01.swbell.net> <38121F1E.577AFAB7@home.com> <6ktQ3.239$OK4.29721@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
>
>Since you put it that way, I suppose not. To their misfortune, however.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>lived.  The shock on their faces is truly awesome.

I don't doubt that, because the statement is completely false. ;-)
As the sources below show, there are over 36.6 million Baptists in
the U.S. alone.  Yet the entire population of the Mexico City
metropolitan area is only 15.5 million, and projected to be only
18.8 million even by 2015.  There as more Southern Baptists in the
U.S. (15.6 million) than there are people in Mexico City. :-)

It may also interest you to know that the number of Protestants in
Latin America, particularly Pentecostals, has literally skyrocketed
over the last 20 years or so.  In some Latin areas the number of
Pentecostals has increased from effectively 0% to over 20 million,
since 1980.  I believe this is one of the reasons why Protestants
are being persecuted by the Roman Catholic Church in Latin America.

===================================================================

         Baptist churches in the United States

Membership  Baptist Denomination                         Churches
----------  -------------------------------------------- --------
```
