- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ukm4k$9eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380DF650.9E104628@home.com> <i5oOOBTEzPvfWqn8qXstcdZ7HHaG@4ax.com> <380F6FCD.A3C24F12@home.com> <cAEQOB+Dci83xoRwKGGkhYbf149F@4ax.com> <38112FC3.887ADC08@home.com> <IloQ3.82$OK4.14298@typhoon01.swbell.net> <38121F1E.577AFAB7@home.com> <6ktQ3.239$OK4.29721@typhoon01.swbell.net> <%PEQ3.16392$y61.162062@news3.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> I believe this is one of the reasons why Protestants
> are being persecuted by the Roman Catholic Church in Latin America.

Oh dear, I had hoped we could get off this one. Although you do say
'Latin America' - by implication your message, and previous - does tend
to tar all with the same brush. Again man-organized religion - but local
culture plays a large part in thinking too. I can assure you there are
many distinct flavours of theology within Catholicism depending upon
your locale. Taught by rigid-thnking nuns in S.Africa - I know of an
Afrikaner who thinks the only way you can make it to Heaven is by having
a Catholic priest chuck water on your head, (suggests to me their
theology is slightly tainted by the Boer strait-laced approach). I can
assure you that approach is diametrically opposed to what I was taught -
and I wasn't being taught the new Catholic 'liberalism'.

Am I to assume all Baptists are made from the same mould ? I'm a married
man with two kids - so it doesn't require any deep thought as to what my
views would be on homosexuality - although laced with a large degree of
compassion. Then I pick up today's paper, didn't read the article;
picture of Jerry Falwell meeting with a Rev. Mel White in Lynchburg, Va.
- and outside a picture of the Baptist Rev. Fred Phelps protesting with
a placard which says, "GOD HATES GAYS" - does he know something that we
don't know, or has Sprint laid on a telephone line where he talks
directly to the Almighty ? As a Baptist I'm sure you wouldn't want me to
think Phelps 'My way or the highway' is totally representative of all
Baptists.

Similarly, should I assume all Sikhs are tarred with the same brush ?
Just very recently we had incidents in Vancouver, where the police had
to intervene in a Temple. A respected Sikh newspaper editor was murdered
for his views. And the problem in the temple - the fundamentalists
wanted people sitting down on the floor in traditional style, while the
easier-going were for having chairs to sit on. Incredible isn't it -
man-made religion. The Sikhs I know in Calgary are very family-oriented,
loving people.

I don't know Donald Tees political or religious convictions, other than
that he is a volunteer for a charity - good for you Donald - but I do
get the feel that he and I are on the same wavelength. Many Canadians
bemoan that our country is a 'non-event' - nothing exciting happens here
- that's the way I like it - and is the prime reason Canada was my #1
choice when emigrating.

To add a note of sanity to a world-obsession with bigotry, the following
quote from Jane Goodall (the Chimp Lady) from a program to be shown on
PBS this week :-

... as warlike as both man and chimp seem to be, she is still
optimistic; "I have one absolute belief; that there is an all-powerful
spirit that humans are moving towards, a destiny which is moving away
from cruelty and destruction and towards love and compassion. Thus is
our moral evolution and we are just at the beginning. I just pray we
don't destroy the planet before we reach it."

As we say re Windows - let's set focus - get back to COBOL and discuss
religion or politics in their appropriate newsgroups. (And I promise -
unless somebody has an urgent need to know my answer's to Randall's
'movie-quiz' question - I wont even include movies in this NG <G>).

As I recall, at this point to terminate the topic, I'm supposed to add
"Hitler".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

bothering me for quite some time.  With all the "year 2000" shit that has
been going on, nobody yet has talked about CELEBRATING, the millennium.  All
the talk has been about problems, and rather trivial ones at that.  The
collating sequence of computer programs, in my not so humble opinion, is
pretty trivial compared to, for example, world hunger.

start tackling the big ones ...

- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ukm4k$9eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380DF650.9E104628@home.com>`

```
I've never been asked to do that, but I can tell you what I would
charge for it.  For on-call during the day when I am going to be
in-office anyway, 25% of my hourly rate.  For off-hours on-call,
50% of my normal rate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Contractors' Fees : was Re: on call

- **From:** "Nilo" <npaim@zaz.com.br>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ulut9$rfl$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <7ukm4k$9eb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380DF650.9E104628@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote <380DF650.9E104628@home.com>...
>
>
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

Hi Folks,

I don't know about you, but me, here in Brasil, as a self-employed, I
consider something before hiring myself for a job:

1. If i must be awaiting for calls made out of 9-17, i must be payed
proportionally 50% plus each hour inside 9-17. If the contractor doesn't
agree with that, i have two choices: don't accept the job (which i hate :) )
or negotiate another point of the contract... Of course, the 50% plus hour
value is due all the time the contract exisits, no matter the customer call
me or not! All i have to do is establish a 'x' number of hours a week i must
be (theoretically) waiting for calls (and I do) and charge for it. Someone
could call it 'insurance'... Obviously, 'x' depends on the type and level of
the contractor... Worst the contractor, greater is 'x', and so on...

2. Some systems, after developed, demands a very little maintenance work...
The main question is: how much the contractor needs me 24 hours a day? If
the answer is a big number, 'x' must grow proportionally...

3. I myself consider that NO ONE contractor can have ALL my time. If I buy
all my hours to just one, why would me self-employed? Which time I would
"waste" in myself development, learning, training, etc...?

Regards

Nilo npaim@zaz.com.br
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** john@watkins.cix.[nospam]co.uk (John Watkins)
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.19991021004949.233H@jwatkins.compulink.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>, 
chris.skinner@mindspring.com (Chris Skinner) wrote:

> How many programmers are compensated by their employers for "on call"
> responsibilities.  I talking about being woken up to fix something 3-4 
> times per week.

Working for a global bank means 24x7 support is required, though we don't 
take the calls from users (there's a shift to do that).

We get paid 25GBP per night (2 payments per day at w/e) to be on-call 
(pager), and then 15GBP if we're actually called and then an extra 10 GBP 
if we have to go in to the office. Response time to actually starting to 
fix the problem is usually 1 hour, but the payment systems have a 
half-hour response time. All on-call staff have a company portable and 
mobile and can dial-in from home on a free number.

On-call rota can vary between 2 weeks in 4 to as little as 1 week in 6. It 
is almost impossible to design a system which is totally reliable, as you 
can't design out user error and 'transparent' system changes. 

On-call is a pain, but at least we're paid well for it. Thankfully we can 
train the operations people to deal with most of the user errors. Being 
woken up at 2.30am with a "There's a user in Brazil, who's forgotten their 
password" is somewhat mollified by being paid 15GBP for 30 seconds to the 
operations to follow the procedures!


+--------------+------------------------------------+
| John Watkins | mailto:john@watkins_dot_cix.co.uk  |
|              | http://www_dot_cix.co.uk/~watkins/ |
+--------------+------------------------------------+
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XpwP3.2502$Ua7.60250@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A long while back, my company asked me to provide 'on call' support. I
suggested tripling my salary since my time away from the office is worth at
least as much to me as my time at the office is worth to them. That was 12
years ago and I have yet to be 'on call'.

At the moment my company is debating the merits of 24/7 support over
Christmas and New Years given that pesky Y2K thing. Our product is a
development tool, not a production system. It has been thoroughly tested for
date-dependencies and the Y2K rollover and found to have no problems. The
'powers that be' are proposing keeping the call centre open 24/7 but they
have no plans to staff R&D, shipping, or consulting so there's no way to fix
any potential problems or get said fix into the hands of a client.

I don't want to imply I do not care about our clients, I do. I proposed that
the call centre maintain regular business hours over the New Years weekend
rather than closing for the holiday as we normally would. I believe it is
better to give a client a polite intelligent answer rather than a
sleep-deprived, surly response while under the influence of alcohol.

Since I have been unsuccessful in conveying this wisdom to the
decision-makers, I am trying another means of swaying their decision. I have
proposed they give me a bonus equal to 6 months salary in recompense for
missing the biggest party of the millennium, not to mention giving up sleep
for a week.  They're thinking about it...


Chris Skinner <chris.skinner@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net...
> Just out of curiosity.
>
> How many programmers are compensated by their employers for "on call"
> responsibilities.  I talking about being woken up to fix something 3-4
times
> per week.
>
> -Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: on call

- **From:** Greg Ferro <gferro@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3811E392.877D2777@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<7uhei6$etu$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I ran into an interesting arrangement at Federal Express in the '94-'95 time
frame.

At FedEx, only employees carried a beeper.  An employee had the duty for one
week and earned sixteen hours of overtime pay for it.

If the coverage employee was beeped during the week and spent time working on
problems, the time was charged against the sixteen hours of overtime pay that
the employee received for carrying the beeper.  If more than sixteen hours of
coverage calls were experienced during the week, the employee was paid for the
hours in excess of sixteen.

If no beeper calls were received, the employee received the sixteen hours of
overtime pay for the INCONVENIENCE of carrying the beeper.  Obviously this was
also an incentive to clean up buggy systems so that you'd receive no coverage
calls.


Chris Skinner wrote:

> Just out of curiosity.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Chris
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COPY in Procedure Division

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380DFECB.5B0D014E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7sbvj6$au6@dfw-ixnews10.ix.netcom.com> <37e9a353.171144403@news1.ibm.net> <37EF7851.B0338CB6@NOSPAMhome.com> <380bcdc3.1152065@news.gw.total-web.net> <380DCBDE.60FF6B65@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> That makes sense.  Haven't seen it, but it is a logical use.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > you're concerned with.

Here's another practical example, just from last week. I had a
five-minute panic attack. My user 'phoned me. 'Could I please change the
old RM/COBOL system, written 20 years ago, to read data from the CD ?'
He had an engineer who wanted to view the data on his laptop. Now who in
his right mind would want to run a system from a 5.25 on drives a or b
back in those days? (Oh, yes, and while I was at it - make it read from
D or E). How many days to Y2K ?.

Some thirty programs to change, back to uppercase, areas A and B, no
End-ifs etc... Help !!! Pleasantly surprised myself - the system was
designed with a fixed set of parameters passed from the Main Menu to all
programs - regardless of whether or not all parameters were required -
not necessarily terribly efficient, but it worked. Fortunately I had a
copy file :-

    .................. copy ..........
    Linkage Section.
    01 param-1 etc...
    Procedure Division using param-1 etc....

    ......................................

Some ten minutes of thought, five lines of code in the Main Menu,
re-compile and done in half an hour. Separate start-up batch-files for
drives a, c, d, e with run-time switches set to denote drive and the
system runs from all four. I used drive a: to test it on my machine.
Pheww! - thank the Lord.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Well thanks!

unweildy that they split it into a driver and three sub-programs.  But
some of them needed to run the same code, so it was copied in.

First of all - send your teacher back to Jurassic Park - he will feel fine
in that environment.
Next - What compiler do you have?
Finally, would it be acceptable to use another compiler if necessary?

It seems to be very common that teachers are not aware of the great
possibilities offered in up-to-date tools and compilers. To me it is illogic
that students have to start from scratch when we hire you directly from
universities and schools.

You can quote me anywhere, you like for this. They steel your youth and
educate you to unemployment - I think the business should demand better
conditions for students.

After all - we have noone else to pay our pension.

Back to reality - find out the limits for your project, and check your
posibilities to choose appropriate tools.
Regards
Ib

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

While not geared toward Fujitsu 4.2, my book has been used by Fujitsu
4.2 users successfully.  It's title is Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
Hours, and I stress actual examples and not syntax diagrams.  (There's
nothing wrong with Syntax diagrams either - I use the composite
language skeleton published in the back of COBOL Unleashed quite
regularly!).

However, if it's examples you want, my book has them.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uko09$nqe$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7uka42$bkk$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote in message <7uka42$bkk$1@news.igs.net>...
>Does anybody have a Win95 Cobol subroutine (I am using Fujitsu) to find
>files in a directory using wildcards?

You may use the Windows API, here is an example of how to solve it in
Acucobol:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. FILELIST.
       AUTHOR.     (c) 1999, Cheesle.
       REMARKS.
      *This program must be compiled with -Dw32
      *For use with 32 bit Windows only
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       77 WS-COUNT            PIC 9(03).
       77 WS-PATH             PIC X(60).

      *File attrbutes may be combined logically
       01 ATTRIBUTES.
          03 RO-FLAG          PIC 9(02) VALUE 01.
          03 HID-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 02.
          03 SYS-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 04.
          03 VID-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 08.
          03 DIR-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 16.
          03 ARC-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 32.
          03 ANY-FLAG         PIC 9(02) VALUE 63.

       01  FILE-FIND-DATA.
           03 FILE-ATTR       SIGNED-LONG.
           03 CREATION-TIME.
              05 CREAT-LOW    SIGNED-LONG.
              05 CREAT-HIGH   SIGNED-LONG.
           03 LAST-ACCESS.
              05 ACCES-LOW    SIGNED-LONG.
              05 ACCES-HIGH   SIGNED-LONG.
           03 LAST-WRITE.
              05 WRITE-LOW    SIGNED-LONG.
              05 WRITE-HIGH   SIGNED-LONG.
           03 FILESIZE.
              05 SIZE-HIGH    SIGNED-LONG.
              05 SIZE-LOW     SIGNED-LONG.
           03 WIN-RES-0       SIGNED-LONG.
           03 WIN-RES-1       SIGNED-LONG.
           03 WIN-FILE-NAME   PIC X(260).
           03 DOS-FILE-NAME   PIC X(13).

       01  FILE-HANDLE        HANDLE.
       01  FIND-STATUS        SIGNED-LONG.
           88 IS-FALSE        VALUE 0.

       01  FILE-SELECTION     PIC X(260).

       SCREEN SECTION.
       01 FILE-INFO.
          03 LINE WS-COUNT.
             05 PIC X(40) FROM WIN-FILE-NAME.
             05 " (".
             05 PIC X(13) FROM DOS-FILE-NAME.
             05 ") ".
      05 PIC Z(03)BZ(03)BZZ9 FROM SIZE-LOW.
      05 " bytes".
       01 GET-PATH.
          03 LINE 01.
             05 "Provide path including filename, *? ok:".
          03 LINE 02.
             05 PIC X(50) USING WS-PATH.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       MAIN SECTION.
       MAIN-001.

    PERFORM LOAD-DLL.
    PERFORM INITIALIZE-FILE-VARS.
    PERFORM INITIALIZE-REC.
    MOVE    "C:\*.*"         TO WS-PATH.
    MOVE    2                TO WS-COUNT.
    DISPLAY GET-PATH.
    ACCEPT  GET-PATH.
           MOVE    ANY-FLAG         TO  FILE-ATTR.
           INITIALIZE               WS-COUNT.
           STRING  WS-PATH          LOW-VALUES DELIMITED BY SIZE
                   INTO             FILE-SELECTION.
           CALL    "FindFirstFileA" USING
                   FILE-SELECTION
                   FILE-FIND-DATA
                   RETURNING        FILE-HANDLE
                   ON               EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-800.

           IF      FILE-HANDLE      < 1
                   DISPLAY          MESSAGE   BOX
                                    "No files found!"
                                    TITLE     FILE-SELECTION
                   GO               TO MAIN-100.

           MOVE    1                TO FIND-STATUS.
    PERFORM UNTIL            IS-FALSE
                   PERFORM          DISPLAY-RESULT
            PERFORM          INITIALIZE-REC
                   CALL             "FindNextFileA" USING
                                    BY VALUE FILE-HANDLE
                                    BY REFERENCE FILE-FIND-DATA
                                    RETURNING FIND-STATUS
                                    ON EXCEPTION GO TO MAIN-810
                                    END-CALL
                  END-PERFORM.
    ACCEPT OMITTED.

       MAIN-100.

           PERFORM UNLOAD-DLL.
           GO      TO               MAIN-900.

       MAIN-800.

           DISPLAY MESSAGE          BOX
                   "Couldn't call FindFirstFileA!"
                   TITLE            "Fatal error".
           GO      TO               MAIN-900.

       MAIN-810.

           DISPLAY MESSAGE          BOX
                   "Couldn't call FindNextFileA!"
                   TITLE            "Fatal error".
           GO      TO               MAIN-900.

       MAIN-900.
       MAIN-EXIT.
           GOBACK.

       DISPLAY-RESULT SECTION.
       DISPLAY-RESULT-001.

           ADD     1                TO WS-COUNT.
           DISPLAY FILE-INFO.

           IF      WS-COUNT         =  24
                   DISPLAY          "(Press ENTER)..." AT 2501
                                    WITH NO ADVANCING
                   ACCEPT           OMITTED WITH NO ADVANCING
                   DISPLAY          OMITTED AT 0101 ERASE SCREEN
                   INITIALIZE       WS-COUNT.

       DISPLAY-RESULT-900.
       DISPLAY-RESULT-EXIT.
           EXIT.

       INITIALIZE-FILE-VARS SECTION.
       INITIALIZE-FILE-VARS-001.

           INITIALIZE               FILE-SELECTION
                                    FILE-HANDLE
                                    FILE-ATTR
                                    WS-PATH.

       INITIALIZE-FILE-VARS-900.
       INITIALIZE-FILE-VARS-EXIT.
           EXIT.

       INITIALIZE-REC SECTION.
       INITIALIZE-REC-001.

           INITIALIZE               WIN-FILE-NAME
                                    DOS-FILE-NAME
                                    CREAT-LOW
                                    CREAT-HIGH
                                    ACCES-LOW
                                    ACCES-HIGH
                                    WRITE-LOW
                                    WRITE-HIGH.

       INITIALIZE-REC-900.
       INITIALIZE-REC-EXIT.
           EXIT.

       UNLOAD-DLL SECTION.
       UNLOAD-DLL-001.

           CANCEL  "KERNEL32.DLL".

       UNLOAD-DLL-900.
       UNLOAD-DLL-EXIT.
           EXIT.

       LOAD-DLL SECTION.
       LOAD-DLL-001.

           SET     ENVIRONMENT      "DLL-CONVENTION" TO 1.
           CALL    "KERNEL32.DLL"   ON EXCEPTION GO TO LOAD-DLL-800.
           GO      TO               LOAD-DLL-900.

       LOAD-DLL-800.

           DISPLAY MESSAGE          BOX
                   "Couldn't LOAD Kernel32!"
                   TITLE            "Fatal error".
    GOBACK.

       LOAD-DLL-900.
       LOAD-DLL-EXIT.
           EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FIND FIRST, FIND NEXT

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7uliip$cf5$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <380E0334.BFABA3C7@home.com>...
>So what's special about Mr. Donald Tees - I asked the same question
>about a week ago and nobody commented <G>

Sorry about that one Jimmy, I really must have missed that message unless
you were talking about a different platform. As you point out yourself; This
sample does not support non windows platforms.

The problem with this task, is that it is almost impossible to make platform
independent unless your cobol vendor supports it and themselves has created
a host independent wrapper. You could of course do a host independent
wrapper yourself.

But, this is all help I have to offer on the subject :-(

I do however believe it should be able to use pretty much the same approach
for OS/2 as Win32 and OS/2 are pretty equal in this subject.

Unix? no clue at all.

Cheesle
```

> Cheesle

Thanks for reply. I think you've probably already guessed where I'm
going with this one - ability to access public domain Open Source with
routines which take you to a particular platform(filename-routines),
based on a conditional switch.

From the public domain you extract the routines applicable and compile
under your O/S. The highly desirable, but probably very unattainable in
the short term, is some generic link agreed to by COBOL compiler vendors
where pre-compiled source sits in the public domain to do this.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <380E5EA9.B63FBFA5@home.com>...

>Thanks for reply. I think you've probably already guessed where I'm

Well, I don't know what time that might solve your problem, but there
actually is a relatively platform independent method avail:

you could call the shell through CALL SYSTEM and have a user configurable
string holding the list command, be it 'ls' on Unix or 'dir' on Windows,
redirect the whole stuff to a file, read in the file and display.

But, I guess you may already have thought of that?

Cheesle
```

From previous posts on the group I recall reading that Fujitsu provide

doesn't exist you could try going directly to the Win32 APIs ie
FindFirstFile, FindNextFile and FindClose.

donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7uka42$bkk$1@news.igs.net...
> Does anybody have a Win95 Cobol subroutine (I am using Fujitsu) to
find
> files in a directory using wildcards?
>
The CBL routines come close (CALL "CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST"  USING...).

You have to include a special FJ-supplied library at link time.
Documentation
on the CBL ROUTINE USER'S GUIDE can be found at www.adtools.com.
```

Unfortunately, for generic usage, not very useful.

<a id="t-ab57ffc26a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view — 13 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uidkq$37$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Apologies to those who receive this note more than once - or for which this
is a duplicate of a previous request to "preview" this.)

If you do not like (or more accurately do not particularly want to
"tolerate") my usual BLUNT comments on COBOL and/or the COBOL Standard,
please feel free to delete this note NOW.

   ***

For the last year or so, I have been meaning to create some sort of
documentation of what I *personally* like and don't like in the (evolving)
draft COBOL Standard.  The opportunity has come up for me to share my
opinions with a private group in the near future, so I have created a
PowerPoint presentation on this topic.  I do not know what impact this might
have on others, but I did think that it would be worthwhile to share this
with others who might be interested.

Therefore, the slides of this presentation can now be viewed at:

 http://pw2.netcom.com/~wmklein/goodbad.pps

To view this, you do need to have a PowerPoint viewer.  If you use a current
Windows operating system, a free copy of the viewer can be downloaded (if you
don't have it already) from:


http://officeupdate.microsoft.com/downloadDetails/Ppview97.htm?s=/downloadCat
alog/dldPowerPoint.htm

(I do not know how/if those with OS/2 or UNIX systems can use PowerPoint.
You might want to check out one of the Microsoft WebPages for this.)

These slides are INTENDED to be accompanied by my personal "talk" - so some
of them may not be entirely self-sufficient, but I think that, in general,
they are understandable "as is."

  ***

Let me stress a couple of the bullet-items that do appear in the slides:

1) There is *NOTHING* "new" in this presentation.  Every "negative" comment
that I include has already been presented to and discussed (and rejected) by
J4 and WG4. (Some on multiple occasions and some for things that have changed
back-and-forth multiple times in the draft.)  This doesn't mean that they
won't change their mind <again>(with enough reasoning/input) in the future,
but does indicate that a "group - or two - of experts" does explicitly
disagree with me (either in principle or practice).

2) I do *NOT* claim to be representing anyone other than myself. I don't
claim to be representing the "average" user or implementor - much less any
group or national body. I think that even those who agree with many of my
general points, would disagree with some of my rankings or individual
evaluations.  Even I would probably change them from day-to-day depending on
what was frustrating me most at the time.

3) Although (as you will see in the conclusions) there are some serious
problems (IMHO) that would cause me to "vote against" making the draft
"official" if it were put to a vote today, there are lots and LOTS of really
good things in the draft (again in my opinion). I think that it is worth
"salvaging" and still hope that it can/will be.

4) I have marked this presentation as "copyrighted."  Therefore, please feel
free to pass on my URL (and/or download the presentation) - but please do NOT
copy it or modify it for your own use (or at least not without checking with
me first).

  ***

"Bureaucracy"

 - I have placed this presentation in my web-space.  Mindspring (Netcom,
Earthlink, or whatever) has a monthly "bandwidth" restriction.  Therefore,
either this presentation (or my COBOL FAQ) may make it unavailable some time
between now and the end of the month.  I don't think so, but it is possible.

 - I will be out of town (and out of the country) from the afternoon of Oct
20th thru Nov 3. Therefore, if you want to give me feedback (PRO or CON;
flame or otherwise), you will need to do so before this Wednesday noon or in
a couple of weeks.

 - Should anyone (particularly those of you in the UK) actually want to talk
to me about it, I will be based in London for the next two weeks.  Staying
at:

   Commodore Hotel
 50 Lancaster Gate, Hyde Park, London W2 3NA.

  Tel: +44 0171 402 5291
  Fax: +44 0171 262 1088

(as far as I know, I will NOT have any email access during this time)

  ***

For anyone who has read this far, thank you for your patience and I hope you
find the slides interesting and thought-provoking - if nothing else.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view

- **From:** jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net (Jeff Farkas)
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12768ef16f4a3974989686@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7uidkq$37$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <7uidkq$37$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>, 
wmklein@nospam.netcom.com says...

  Thanks for the information Bill. The presentation was well done and the 
information was very useful. 

    It seems a shame that COBOL is headed in the same direction that a 
lot of newer languages are headed today and to make matters worse the 
folks who use it on a daily basis have no input to the direction it 
should take. Funny thing is that the market should be driven to supply 
products that the consumer wants and needs but in this case it's the 
other way around. The vendors decide what they can supply and what they 
want to supply and the users simply have to accept it and make do with 
what they get. 

  While it is unlikely that I would take the time to read the new 
standards it seems odd that only the vendors are allowed to review them. 
I realize it might be a bit if trouble to post all of that information 
and allow everyone to read it but it would make for some interesting 
discussion as to the future of COBOL. It seems that the vendors are 
running the show and the last thing they want is any input from the 
users.. yes that's us. The folks who use COBOL every day and have to make 
it work and figure out what is going on. But, there job is not to make 
our lives easy.. it's to make money and the greater the differences 
between implementations the more we have to spend on various complies and 
tools to debug the code.. and the more money the vendor makes. 

  But, what else in new?? 

Jeff Farkas
jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view(3)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uimpq$ldg$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7uidkq$37$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.12768ef16f4a3974989686@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Jeff, (and everyone else),

 The draft Standard most definitely *IS* available for everyone to view
(and/or review). If you go the J4 "external" web-page (which I often point
to), it has a direct link to the down-loadable version.

It is also important that everyone know that there are several NON-vendors on
the J4 and WG4 committees.  There is absolutely no restriction on what you do
as to being active in the development and review process.

For those who don't have it, let me repost the J4 web-page - so anyone who
wants it can get a copy of the current draft.

 http://merant.microfocus.com/Standards/

NOTE WELL: this same page tells you how you can get on the list to be
notified whenever there is a public review period for "official"
input/comments.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL (draft) Standard - a VERY personal perspective/view

```
While not a direct comment on what you have posted, I hope that you and the
others find it of interest.

As some have noted, a possible way to open up (and perhaps speed up) the process
of designing the new COBOL standard, and open source approach might work.  There
is currently a gnu effort under way (see signature below for links) to develop a
COBOL compiler by creating a COBOL to C translator.

I'm sure the effort would welcome your, and other ng reader's, assistance.

Jerry P wrote:
>
>Or you could enable Windows passwords.

And they can boot with a floppy.  Set the BIOS password; much more
secure than any software protection.  Takes disassembling the PC to
override.  If they can do that, they can remove the disk drive and
put it in another computer. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stop others from using your computer!!! 6733

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PRqP3.15444$Fj2.135027@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<GOaN3.14509$a23.3320@news.rdc1.il.home.com> <NIaP3.385$7X2.31797@typhoon01.swbell.net> <sMjP3.14453$Fj2.126738@news1.mia> <7ul338$810$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Simon R Hart wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>Jerry P wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>secure Windows platform.(if there was ever one)

No, not better yet.  Unless disabled from the BIOS, most modern PCs
will boot from a floppy disk or a CD-ROM, way, way before Windows
gets a chance to do anything.  TO be really secure, you must stop the
boot process before any program can be loaded.  Only the BIOS can do
that, because it is the first thing executed by system reset.
```

Albert Ratzlaff R. <albert@conexion.com.py

> What happened to those removeable hard disks? Just like a car
radio -
> take it with you. You can't get much more secure than that.

They got 'em now as caddies into which you install whatever IDE
drive you want. The "Nest" uses a floppy drive bay, the caddy has
a handle and key lock. Superb not only for security but you can
drop the sucker in a fire-proof safe. Cost? Less than $20.

As for security in general, we are super-pleased over SCRAMDISK,
a free, extremely secure, encrypted virtual disk system that runs
invisibly on Windows machines. Available at

www.scramdisk.clara.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Printer Spacing Sheets

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uj0jk$psp$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7ugv04$5q1$1@news.ndsu.nodak.edu> <DfE0BCAyLJD4Ewx2@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Hey,
I have a (true) story about that

In the early eighties I designed a system for life-insurance. CICS, Cobol,
DL/I. We spent many hours with the customer to design screens, and we did
that with spacing charts
(24 lines and 80 columns). The customer was unhappy about one of the
screens - he needed more information and we couldn't sqeeze one single byte
extra on that awful screen.

We told him that he had to accept to split the information into 2 screens -
no deal. He told us to come back next day. He would consult his
brother-in-law (a self-made guru).

When we came bach the next day, he stood at the end of the table saying:
'Look here, this is what I want - it shouldn't be that difficult to
understand for proffs like you'!!!!!

We found out that his brother-in-law worked at a TV station. The screen
layout form he had given to our customer was 60 lines and 100 columns. We
had a hell of a time to declare the design impossible - 'Look here - it's a
screen - isn't it'.

Fun with all that memories.

Regards
Ib
Alistair Maclean skrev i meddelelsen ...
>I threw my last ones out a few months ago because they were too small
>(The users always wanted more than the 133 characters allowed).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Hi My name is Mike and I am looking for a place where i can buy some
printer
>>spacing charts for COBOL... If you know some where io can buy them or get
>>them free it would help...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>People are like teabags - you have to put them in to hot water before you
find
>out how strong they are.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Printer Spacing Sheets

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380C0BFC.6A28F728@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ugv04$5q1$1@news.ndsu.nodak.edu>`

```


Michael Volochenko wrote:
> 
> Hi My name is Mike and I am looking for a place where i can buy some printer
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Think they might be difficult to find. Unless you need them specifically
for designing a layout with a pre-printed form, suggest following. Use
any good source editor to lay out your lines as you want them to appear,
then take each line and turn it into a COBOL print line, e.g. :-

*..........Number : xxxx Name : xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

01 Line1.
   05 			pic x(10).
   05 			pic x(09) value "Number : ".
   05 L-AccountNumber 	pic x(04).
   05 			pic x(08) value " Name : ".
   05 L-AccountName     pic x(20).

01 Line 2 etc.....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Printer Spacing Sheets

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380de57e.13782987@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ugv04$5q1$1@news.ndsu.nodak.edu> <380d0adf.79131190@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>If you have Excel I have some .XLS (Someone else created) COBOL coding
>sheets.
>
>E-mail me for a copy!

Thane:

The only problem with coding forms is that they're kind of short if
you want to use them as a printer spacing template.

The simplest approach I can think of...assuming that the printed
output is going to be fixed pitch fonts at 10 characters per
inch......

Just create a Word document first adjusting the margin to create an 8"
by 10' printable area, then create a table of 80 columns by 60 rows.
Darken the borders and you should have your template!


>On Tue, 19 Oct 1999 00:18:52 -0500, "Michael Volochenko"
><dante_15@hotmail.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

RoadRaat

Try www.datajunction.com or www.siber.com

I believe that Siber Systems has a data conversion tool, but if you
don't find anything on the web site, you can also send an e-mail

two steps backward, but these days people seem to opt for anything
with a Microsoft label on it...even if it means slower, buggier and
less efficient....just my opinion.




Bob Wolfe, flexus

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Cobol data files

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380CF6CB.8CE8F911@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.127437a4fc1711389896a0@news> <380AB9C3.84FAAD8C@home.com> <7ug515$3a624@SGI3651ef0.iddeo.es> <380c9162.10911459@news.enter.net> <7uig6f$q6r$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Simon R Hart wrote:
> 
> Bob Wolfe wrote in message <380c9162.10911459@news.enter.net>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >less efficient....just my opinion.

Indeed, we have a tool called data2flat that converts MF (or FSC)
data files to CVS format (comma-separated values) that can be easily
imported into Aceess or any other database. 

More information on this tool is available at
http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

We also have a different variation of these tools that
imports data from Cobol data files into Crystal Reports
which is really nice GUI-driven report generator by 
Seagate Software.

We also have a converter from Cobol data files to 
DBF files (DBase IV format).

And finally, we have a general DataAccess library 
that is used to build all these converters and
it can be used by our customers to build their own
Cobol data readers/converters to their liking.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

agent.  It seems to me that RM COBOL has a high degree of source code

<a id="t-338890c023"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@nospam.bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x1%O3.3486$E4.4569@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com> writes: 
> Robin, your are wrong and John is right - in every respect.
> 
> CMPAT(V1) produced V1 compatible object modules.
> CMPAT(V2) meant that routines that arrays, areas and aggregates shared with
> V1 (or CMPAT(V1)) modules were no longer binary compatible.

Object code was incompatible with earlier version.  In particular,
the enquiry functions LBOUND, HBOUND, DIM were functions directly
affected, because they returned 31 bit results, whereas the earlier
versions produced only halfword (16 bit) results.  As John Kennedy
pointed out, it was on account of changes to the descriptors that
were associated with arrays etc that made the versions incompatible.

It's surprising that it went along as long as it did, with only
16 bit results.

> Further information can be found in the Programming Guide, which is
> available on-line.
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ujkvc$aak$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<x1%O3.3486$E4.4569@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Robin, repeating an incorrect statement does not make it correct. You should
review the cited documentation, esp. the section "Differences between
compiling with CMPAT(V1) and CMPAT(V2) - in particular, before starting an
argument.

Here is a quote from that doc:
"Built-in functions DIM, HBOUND, LBOUND and ALLOCATION return fullword
values when compiled with CMPAT(V2) and return halfword values when compiled
with CMPAT(V1)".

John is correct. If you want object code compatibility, you can have it
through a compiler option.

robin <robin_v@nospam.bigpond.com> wrote in message
news:x1%O3.3486$E4.4569@newsfeeds.bigpond.com...

with
> > V1 (or CMPAT(V1)) modules were no longer binary compatible.

wrote:

> > > > > "John W. Kennedy" <rri0189@attglobal.net> writes: > robin wrote:

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

were
> > > > > changed to return FIXED BINARY (31) results (previously FIXED
BINARY

<a id="t-02f94b0fa9"></a>
## Thread: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** robin <robin_v@nospam.bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5pRO3.2948$E4.4893@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
"John W. Kennedy" <rri0189@attglobal.net> writes: > "R. A. Vowels" wrote:
> > 
> > "John W. Kennedy" <rri0189@attglobal.net> writes: > robin wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Another XA Change.

No, a change compiler and a change of compiled code.  The change
was to allow arrays to have more than 32767 elements.

> Indeed, it would be nearer the mark to say that descriptors were
> changed; the xBOUND and DIM changes were merely a resultant.

Indeed.

> 
> -- 
> -John W. Kennedy
> -rri0189@ibm.net
```

#### ↳ Re: In search of a PL/I - COBOL for OS/390 comparison

- **From:** "Mark Yudkin" <myudkin@csi.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.pl1,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uh0i4$5t7$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<5pRO3.2948$E4.4893@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Robin, your are wrong and John is right - in every respect.

CMPAT(V1) produced V1 compatible object modules.
CMPAT(V2) meant that routines that arrays, areas and aggregates shared with
V1 (or CMPAT(V1)) modules were no longer binary compatible.

Further information can be found in the Programming Guide, which is
available on-line.
```

<a id="t-4e0fb4c5fc"></a>
## Thread: OT: (Humor/Humour) - Signs That You've Had Too Much Of The 90's: — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: OT: (Humor/Humour) - Signs That You've Had Too Much Of The 90's:

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380C09FF.BAA3EA76@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ub187$6t1$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I received the following humo<u>rous post from one of my UK contacts.  "US
> translations are in "<>" marks:

This one doesn't need translating and it is true. Knew I could fit it in
sometime. Michael M and Pete Dashwood know it from Compuserve M/F Forum
days :-

Over 30 years ago I worked for Unigate (milk and dairy product
distributor) in UK. At the time Green Giant didn't have their own
distribution set-up and used us. Having initially designed the invoicing
system I was responsible for allocating new product codes, (plus
check-digits), until the system got off the ground.

One day I got a phone call from our Liverpool depot, from my very good
friend the O & M Manager,  "Could you please allocate a new product code
?". "Sure, what's the description ?". Bowen Morgan replied,
"Miscellaneous Green Giant in sacks". After the pregnant pause from my
end of the phone he roared with laughter, then explained.

One of the barges transporting Green Giant from the merchant ship in the
harbour to the shore had sunk. They were left with a mess of soggy
cardboard boxes and the labels came off the cans. 

With the new product code they sold the sacks for what they could get. 
You can be damn sure the folks at the Jolly Green Giant HQ wouldn't be
too thrilled with this story ! Ho! Ho! Ho!

 Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

---

<a id="t-30a89624be"></a>
## Thread: RM Cobol (AcuCobol) Lock Table Overflow — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: RM Cobol (AcuCobol) Lock Table Overflow

- **From:** "bart higdon" <develop@intouchcomputers.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QJZO3.17230$E_1.1000852@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<yGnK3.16513$t%3.1241603@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
try bumping up the

MAX-FILES 75
MAX-LOCKS 75
LOCKED-RECORD-DELAY 400
LOCKING-RETRIES 500
V-LOCK-METHOD 1

statements in the config file. if thi doesn't work up the numbers and try
again. but this should take care of the problem.
```

<a id="t-66358213d5"></a>
## Thread: Perform statement question — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### RE: Perform statement question

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E47B36D9946CD311995000A0C9EA3E924E6631@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
Point:

Are all "maintenance programmers" that bad?

Don't you look before you "add" code?

What kind of "test" can prove that a fix worked but not show that it didn't?

Does that last "oops" reflect that you think all programmers that use
internal sorts are in league with the devil?

-----Original Message-----
From: john_mindock@my-deja.com [mailto:john_mindock@my-deja.com]
Sent: Friday, October 15, 1999 11:05 AM
To: comp.lang.cobol@list.deja.com
Subject: Re: Perform statement question

snip

Some years later, in the middle of the night, a maintenance programmer
discovers that a record with 'ERROR' in it has abended. He needs
to handle that in a special way, moving all 9's to that field.
Since the record-handling is done in PARAs that start with 'A',
he adds the error-handling PARA-A99 in its seemingly-proper place.
He tests his changes and sure enough it works nicely when the record
has 'ERROR' in it.
The rerun goes fine - no more abend. So he goes home, happy to have
solved the problem so quickly.

The next morning he is stunned to find out that all
records have gone thru PARA-A99 - his 'ERROR' record has gone thru it
twice.

Personally I never use SECTIONs - they once were required for infernal
(oops internal) sorts but not anymore.
John



 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-d14c322953"></a>
## Thread: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs — 1 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-09](1999-09.md#t-d14c322953)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: When does MF Cobol release Called Programs

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ugp8d$m9v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<026faffc.a596e592@usw-ex0102-015.remarq.com> <7r17k5$7j8@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
:> I suggested that they may be
:> happening because none of the programs have Cancel verbs
:> used and that MF may not be releasing them from memory.  If
:> cancels are not used, then when does Cobol free them? When
:> the calling program executes an "exit program"?  Or does it
:> not happen until a stop run is encountered??

Without a Cancel the program will remain in memory until the
STOP RUN.  It is quite common (in my systems) to have some
callable programs that, say, look after system control
functionality, or file access.  These are CALLed and never
CANCELed until end of run because they have files open or
hold data items and this would be lost if they were 
cancelled or otherwise removed.

Some run times are able to discard code segments when memory
gets tight because these can be reloaded from the file - the
code is not modified.  Data segments, however, must remain
resident (at least in virtual memory) because they must 
retain their 'last used' state unless they are cancelled.
```

Sorry for this very basic question, but my lessons are very old

Is it necessary to initialise variables in a Cobol program or are these
variables have a default value ?


Thanks

Patrick

##### ↳ ↳ R: initialising variables

- **From:** "igor" <igor@euro-soft.it>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7un8ab$35k$1@nslave1.tin.it>`

```
if you want to initilialize all the variable in a record you can use the
initialize verb

for example
01 my-rec.
    02   my-data-num    pic 9(9).
    02   my-data-alp      pic x(10).

initialize my-rec.




Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net> wrote in message
38112beb.179822651@news2.ibm.net...
> On Wed, 20 Oct 1999 15:04:07 +0200, "Patrick Dubois"
<Patrick.Dubois@RdAm.Nestle.Com>

#### ↳ Re: initialising variables

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ukghn$khq$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch>`

```
It is always BEST to initialize (initialise) them in your program.  This can
be done via the INITIALIZE verb (if you do this several times in your
program) or via the VALUE clause if you only need it done once - at the
beginning.

You do NOT need to initialize fields that are first used as "receiving"
fields - but I have found it (usually) a good practice to do so - just in
case some maintenance comes along that expects them to be "tested" before
receiving anything.

Many (most?) compilers do have methods of setting up "defaults" for
initialization - but these differ from compiler to compiler.  For example,
look at the Micro Focus DEFAULT-BYTE directive or the IBM mainframe WSCLEAR
and STORAGE options.  However, as implied earlier, I wouldn't rely on these
as a "good programming technique".
```

#### ↳ Re: initialising variables

- **From:** Garry Milks <milksg@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38153D08.4367481A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch>`

```
Ephatically yes. Initialize constants using the VALUE clause, initialize
variables in the program logic (i.e. PROCEDURE DIVISION). All variables
used in COMPUTE statements should be initialized, keep in mind that the
receiving variable in a COMPUTE statement is initialized by the value
returned by the COMPUTE, its the other variables in the COMPUTE statement
that you really have to worry about so make sure they are properly
initialized.

It is good programming practise to initialize your variables and believe me
its a real progarmming time saver (in the long run).
Later
g

Patrick Dubois wrote:

> Sorry for this very basic question, but my lessons are very old
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Patrick
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: initialising variables

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch> <38153D08.4367481A@home.com>`

```


Garry Milks wrote:
> 
> Ephatically yes. Initialize constants using the VALUE clause, initialize etc...
> It is good programming practise to initialize your variables and believe me
> its a real progarmming time saver (in the long run).

Patrick,

Suggestion - one way you can achieve this is to make your variables
Level 05's, subs of 01 MyVariables - then INITIALIZE MyVariables. With
things like counters, you can get away without initializing e.g.:-
 
	perform varying n from 2 by 2 until n > NaxNumber
		........

	BUT - if the above had been written :-

	perform varying n from MyKounter by 2 until n > Maxnumber
		.............
 	  and you haven't set an initial value for MyKounter ??????

Be careful - you can get some horrible results if you don't INITIALIZE.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: initialising variables

- **From:** "Hilde Reitan" <hilde.reitan@c2i.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uNnR3.393$ek.14903@juliett.dax.net>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch> <38153D08.4367481A@home.com> <3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan skrev i meldingen <3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com>...

>Be careful - you can get some horrible results if you don't INITIALIZE.



But be aware of the fact that INITIALIZE does not affect FILLERs. That is,
the value in a FILLER remains the same, initialize or not.

Hilde
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: initialising variables

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v4oq5$1vi4$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch> <38153D08.4367481A@home.com> <3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com> <uNnR3.393$ek.14903@juliett.dax.net>`

```

Hilde Reitan wrote in message ...
>
>James J. Gavan skrev i meldingen <3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com>...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the value in a FILLER remains the same, initialize or not.

But be aware that the current draft of the COBOL standard
does have a provision for initializing FILLER items.

INITIALIZE identifier-1 WITH FILLER

will initialize elementary items including elementary FILLER
items.

This feature could appear in any new implementation; even
before the draft is approved.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: initialising variables

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-27T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7v61lc$8ub$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
    To take a stab at the original question, binary items (comp, etc) do
have a valid default value.  Just what this default is depends on contents
of data name and size of data name.  The default for a one byte data name
would be 32.  a 2 byte item defaults to 8224 (assuming ascii spaces).

    Non binary data names normally default to non numeric.

    So far as initializing fillers, I suggest a move spaces before the
initialize.  A move spaces is trvial in code size and time of execution.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<38174AD4.F0E9542A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I don't often use the INITIALIZE command.  I always use the free (in
run-time costs) VALUE clause.  Occasionally the INITIALIZE is useful,
but until maintenance programmers are comfortable with the way it works,
I find it clearer to initialize the fields I care for individually.
(after moving spaces, or a template record to the record).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: initialising variables

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v8ka7$4pp$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<003FEFE709F7D111A9D300A0C99D8052063353D4@vevdmz01.nestec.ch> <38153D08.4367481A@home.com> <3815EA66.AC34B3E@home.com> <7v61lc$8ub$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3816ee6b.216288863@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
    The point is that binary items can not be non numeric, ie if you fail to
initialize them in some manner, they will not trigger a run time error as a
usage display or packed item might, but instead will behave in an unexpected
manner.  Depending of course on what uninitialized working storage is set
to.  Using a default fill value of low values would solve problems with
binary numeric items, but might cause problems elsewhere, as with pic X
items that seem to be blank, but are not = spaces.

```
I have to agree; I always initialize variables in w-s.  Except for edit 
(conversion or chip-chop) fields where you always move something to it first
in the procedure division.  The initialize verb makes the code less clear.
By intializing in w-s with VALUE, you can see the initial values as you scan
down the printout of the program.

Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Phil Brady <demo3@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F97D3.F22F11C5@bigfoot.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
Alan

You are really asking the wrong question.

The "runtime efficiency" of a language has absolutely NOTHING to do with the
language itself.  It is the result of the vendor's implementation of the
compiler for that language.  If a specific vendor does an especially good job
writing compilers for several languages, the odds are that there will be NO
APPRECIABLE DIFFERENCES in the runtime efficiency from one language to the next.

Alan Martin wrote:

> Nice one Volker, attack the validity of the question rather than attempting
> to answer it.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> start piling up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3810CC80.E09FEDDC@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F97D3.F22F11C5@bigfoot.com>`

```
I think you are overstating your point here. Languages can and do
constrain the ability of the compiler writer to generate
efficient code. 

A case in point is my earlier example about the mandatory decimal
truncation in cobol. Another is the stricter aliasing rules in
Fortran which allows stronger assumptions to be made by the
optimiser - generating significantly better code in many cases
than C. Runtime type and array checking in Java as another
example. The rule that packed data items must be stored in a
constrained manner in COBOL.

The language can have another more indirect effect. If there is
less competition, then there is less pressure to produce better
code.

Tim Josling

Phil Brady wrote:
>

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Exactly - and that is what benchmarks are for, to determine the
strengths and weaknesses of various products. It's hard for one
product or language to do everything.

Of course benchmarks can produce surprising results. I know of
onew instance where a cobol program was replaced by C thereby
speeding up the IO severalfold.

Tim Josling

> across the country, instead of comparing our acceleration with Ferraris.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed Stevens <ed.stevens@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
So you got the I/O sped up by a factor 10.  Was this ONLY because the
cobol program got replaced by a C program?  I've seen cobol programs
sped up by that much and more just by tweaking the JCL, eliminating
inefficient blocking and buffering.  Perhaps if the original cobol
program in your example were given to a good programmer for some
tuning . . . 

You could easily see the same performance improvements going from a C
to a Cobol program.  All you're really doing is going from a poorly
written program to a well written one.  The language itself has
nothing to do with it.

On Fri, 22 Oct 1999 06:20:38 +1000, Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
wrote:

>Exactly - and that is what benchmarks are for, to determine the
>strengths and weaknesses of various products. It's hard for one
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> across the country, instead of comparing our acceleration with Ferraris.

Ed Stevens/TN
```

- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> 
> So you got the I/O sped up by a factor 10.  Was this ONLY because the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

How about a non-standard benchmark.
I wrote both systems, one with RM/COBOL and the other with Oracle Forms
4.5. Both on a SCO Unix server - the Oracle one with double the
processor speed and RAM of the RM/COBOL one. Identical functionality.
Processing 50,000 records (data conversion, lot of updates) with Oracle
allows me to read a whole chapter of a book (instead of just staring at
the screen). With COBOL I can't read half as much.
Ok, ok, there are a lot of other important factors, I know. But IMHO
COBOL wins over databases in raw efficiency. (Which sadly won't prevent
me from saying goodbye to COBOL in a not too distant future.)

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7upukp$od0$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote in message ...
>So you got the I/O sped up by a factor 10.  Was this ONLY because the

Sounds like a non representative Cobol application. After all, most Cobol
applications handle business logic, which is mostly user input / output and
disk IO.

I have no problem accepting that a C appliction would perform 10 times
faster than a Cobol application would. On the other side, what is the
practical benefit of doing a user interaction in 1 hundreths of a second
rather than 1 tenth of a second when the user probably will be sitting next
to that screen for at least a minute, likely more anyway?

Considering generic hardware I/O like printing and disk access, I really
doubt that there will be any improvement at all, not by any chance at a
factor of 10.

On the other side, if there was a Cobol application calculating the weather
forecast for the next week a replacement would make sense, but who uses
Cobol to calculate the weather forecast?

The comparision is without foundation in the real world.

Cheesle
```

- **From:** "James S. Rogers" <jimmaureenrogers@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7utt0b$35r$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com> <7upukp$od0$1@news.cerf.net> <%_lQ3.23695$Fj2.202760@news1.mia>`

```
This is a joke, right?

*Real* number crunching is done in a wide range of languages, including
but not limited to Fortran.

The basic thrust of the comment is, however, correct. Each language is
designed to fullfill a particular expressive mission. Each language will
excel in one or more algorithmic domaines. COBOL will excel in record
based, I/O intensive applications.

If the "simple" program used in the benchmark is massively concurrent
embedded system, then Ada will excel.

Jim Rogers
Colorado Springs, Colorado

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3810C9D8.F3E2F136@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com> <7upukp$od0$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
This *was* a *real* business application. The performance
difference was the difference between getting the daily batch job
run in 8 hours vs 36 hours - I'm sure you would agree this is a
worthwhile objective.

My point is twofold

1) Outside the mainframe COBOL is not that well supported
2) Benchmarks have a way of confounding your prejudices

Tim Josling

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38125F8E.63458833@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com> <7upukp$od0$1@news.cerf.net> <3810C9D8.F3E2F136@melbpc.org.au> <3811730A.C9F18426@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Tim Josling wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch :-)

I worked in a major bank in Aus for a while and the in house plastics
ran for 36 hours.  But note without changing one line of code it was
reduced to 10 hours.  Running parts in parallel and streaming off
duplicate runs made a huge difference.

Rewriting a system (in whatever language) without changing the
functionality will always make it run faster.  I just took a benchmark
on something I wrote (was in C and still is) took it from just under 2
minutes to 6 seconds.  I changed the way it worked by understanding
the data not listening to the problem definition.

Algorithms count, language rarely affects it that much,
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>

Ken Foskey wrote in message

>Algorithms count, language rarely affects it that much,


That has also been my experience.  If the data structuring is well thought
out, and the methodology well designed, then the speed of execution is
pretty well determined.  The language tends to be a very minor part compared
to those two criteria.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3814624C.A4CAE8A0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com> <7upukp$od0$1@news.cerf.net> <3810C9D8.F3E2F136@melbpc.org.au> <3811730A.C9F18426@att.net> <38125F8E.63458833@zip.com.au>`

```
> Rewriting a system (in whatever language) without changing the
> functionality will always make it run faster.  I just took a benchmark
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

Usually, when I have seen this type of improvement, the improvement was
from understanding the data and data structure better.  One example was
with a hierarchical database without a prior pointer (nowadays nobody
leaves out prior pointers in hierarchical or network databases - it's
cheaper to have an unused pointer than to change things later).  This
was ok with small chains when they rarely needed to move backwards, but
was terribly inefficient when the database no longer fit the original
assumptions.
```

>difference was the difference between getting the daily batch job
>run in 8 hours vs 36 hours - I'm sure you would agree this is a

Cobol development must have been a lousy job.

reads through an indexed file of 100 Mb or so, redo that application in C or
what ever and prove the above statement.

I doubt you will succeed, as previously stated in this thread, one may
achieve a significant improvement just by tuning the Cobol application as
well.


Cheesle.

- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com>`

Amazing how much you know about the problem without actually
knowing anything about the problem.

This was on Unix. The language vendor acknowledged the
performance problem with their cobol implementation but declined
to provide a fix. The IO module was replaced by a C module which
fixed the problem.

Tim Josling

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y2mQ3.23701$Fj2.202797@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <EqcPOM1OypkZyLbe9ixWKs++0x6K@4ax.com> <3810C8E0.32FE336F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
>
>This was on Unix. The language vendor acknowledged the
>performance problem with their cobol implementation but declined
>to provide a fix. The IO module was replaced by a C module which
>fixed the problem.

I write a number of utility programs using QuickBasic, Microsoft C
and Micro Focus COBOL, based on which language seems best suited to
the task.  When it comes to serial I/O, C edges out BASIC when I take
the time to boost the buffers.  But MF COBOL literally *trounces* C
and BASIC on serial I/O.  The result is not even close, several times
faster.  This is because MF COBOL has particularly efficient serial
I/O capability.  As others have said, it is the implementation, not
the language, that makes the difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gA8QOERqJhV3AGqNj7omxnVLgFr8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au>`

```
The venerable Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Fri, 22 Oct 1999 06:20:38 +1000 these words:

>Of course benchmarks can produce surprising results. I know of
>onew instance where a cobol program was replaced by C thereby
>speeding up the IO severalfold.

This occured twenty years ago at Host International.  I wasn't there,
but I heard the same stroy from two diferent people.  They had a
program which had to run every day, where the daily run time was
exceeding 24 hours.  The job was set up to work properly with one
days's run overlaping the next, but this program was consuming a lot
of resources.  

A programmer was asked to look into the program and see if he could
make it more efficient.  He discovered the program was using the wrong
statement to read a database; instead of "read next record" it was
"read the fisrt record with a key greater than the last key".  The
programmer changed one line and reduced the run time to 10 minutes.

We may therefore conclude that the right code is 100 times faster than
the wrong code, so?

I could write a Cobol program which is fully dependant on Cobol
truncation rules and editing PICtures and such.  It would take a lot
of programming time to make a C version run correctly, much less
efficiently.  What is being proved?

The analogy has already been made that C is like a sports car and
Cobol is like a truck.  The sports car may look prettier, and may move
faster and it may corner better, but when it comes to moving lots of
freight the truck is still the way to go.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@open.org>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381084EF.136F@open.org>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <gA8QOERqJhV3AGqNj7omxnVLgFr8@4ax.com>`

```
In all the mainframe shop applications I work with where PERFORMANCE is
key, we usually write in Assembler.  Otherwise we choose COBOL or
EasytrievePlus so it will be easier to maintain.

I would have to assume that performance IS the key for the person that
started this thread.

My recomendation (which may have been already said, because I have not
read the entire thread) is that you must do your own bench marks on your
own system in order for them to mean anything to your shop.  Otherwise
you are comparing apples-to-oranges.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381093D9.7A632CE9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <7ujl09$f1p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381029e3.179304456@news2.ibm.net> <7umjip$1aa$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380F10E9.8D52C8C@NOSPAMhome.com> <380F7596.8AB2AA4F@melbpc.org.au> <gA8QOERqJhV3AGqNj7omxnVLgFr8@4ax.com> <381084EF.136F@open.org>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:

> My recomendation (which may have been already said, because I have not
> read the entire thread) is that you must do your own bench marks on your
> own system in order for them to mean anything to your shop.  Otherwise
> you are comparing apples-to-oranges.

But before doing that, make sure what you're measuring is what matters.

At one time, most shops spent lots of effort to reduce expensive CPU
time, and relatively little effort in reducing cheap programmers time
(or business opportunity costs).  

Now, programmer time, down time, and opportunity costs are much more
important relative to CPU time, and other hardware costs.  I don't see
this trend reversing.

So maybe a good benchmark test should be to have existing applications
and then introduce new requirements - using typical programmers, time
them in implementing the changes and documentation in a trustworthy
manner.

This could be done with various flavors of COBOL as well, giving the
programmers a couple of days to learn the various tools of a particular
COBOL vendor.  (if the tools are too complicated, the average programmer
will find more urgent needs than to learn the tools).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

Normally you would expect COBOL to be faster for batch file
processing, which I why I said it was 'surprising'. But outside
the mainframe this is not always so. 

By the way this was not an aprocryphal story, nor sloppy coding.
See my earlier post for more details.

Tim Josling

> Nice one Volker, attack the validity of the question rather than
attempting
> to answer it.
>
> In your mind your saying we cant compare Voodoo3 using Glide against
TNT2
> using OpenGL. Everyone does it.

While it may be valid to compare, say, video cards, it is
not sensible to compare 'PC plug in cards' where they do
different jobs and their only similarity is that they all
plug into a PC.  Your question was similar (IMHO) to asking
"which is faster a Video PCI card or a Network PCI card ?".

Diferent vendor version of C will have different performance
and different versions of Cobol have different performance,
just as brands of Video cards and brands of network cards may vary.
To expect to be able to say whether a video card is better
that another or is better than a network card depends on
more than whether it is 'faster' at doing a simple (simplistic)
single thing.

languages then

nanoseconds would
> start piling up.

an 'equivalent' C program, or vice versa.

(Thane Hubbell) writes:

>
>If the differences are that small, the exercise is futile.  
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
