This seems to point back to an earlier mention of someone hosting(or not
hosting)
 a site that compared the performance of the various COBOL vendor products.
If there were such a site, the benchmarks would have to be done across a broad
spectrum of major application functions to highlight both strength and
weaknesses
of each product for a given application.
Such a site would need to provide source for the programs used in the
benchmarks
for others to be able to perform their own evaluations and demonstrate any
'vendor specific' code that would provide a significant advantage over other
products.

This site like an auto company site showing the specs on the various models 
available.   Each language would be representative of another company.

OR arrange it differently , showing each vendor as the company and the various
languages as the different models available.

Thane, you are right about needing to 'flag' interpreted vs optimized native
code.
As each individual is looking for something different from the various
development 
environments.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h3jP3.51$zO.3355@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
The source code language has nothing to do with performance.

Performance differences are found in:

1. The code created by the specific (brand) compiler in response to specific
source code.
2. The efficiency of the source code used by the programmer, both in design
and the exploitation of the specific compiler's assets.

It's not the paintbrush, it's the artist.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uk6op$rln$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <h3jP3.51$zO.3355@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>The source code language has nothing to do with performance.
[...]
Although it is generally true that the language used has little
or no effect on the overall performance of an application, there
are some cases where there may be dramatic differences.

In some languages, numeric values are held as strings of
digits rather than as binary values in native form. A math
intensive application in such a language will be much slower.

Compare Tcl versus C. The standard recommendation for
Tcl is to write math intensive routines in C and call them
from Tcl.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nUtP3.1021$zO.30928@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <h3jP3.51$zO.3355@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7uk6op$rln$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
(I am not picking on Mr Smith here but his message was up when I "clicked.")

You know, I see this question all the time here and even more in the BASIC
newsgroups - "Which  BASIC|COBOL compiler is faster, and which language is
faster?"

Someone pointed out that BCD math is slower than binary math. SO WHAT? Then
DON'T USE BCD MATH ON THAT PLATFORM!  COBOL supports binary math. (Also
floating point math). Programmer's decision.

So, some languages hold numerics in strings rather than binary or BCD.(I
never heard of this 'feature' before today).
THEN DON'T USE THAT LANGUAGE PRODUCT FOR MATH-INTENSIVE APPLICATIONS.
Programmer's decision.

It was also pointed out how COBOL truncation may affect performance. WELL,
USE THE RIGHT <EXPLETIVE> PICTURE CLAUSE and TRUNCATION is NO LONGER A
<EXPLETIVE> PROBLEM. Programmer's decision.

Damn, all these "programmer decisions" might lead one to believe that
perhaps the programmer's style and ability is an important factor in program
performance; that is, maybe, just maybe, "It's not the paintbrush, it's the
artist!"

MCM
L'artiste



Rick Smith wrote in message <7uk6op$rln$1@news.hitter.net>...

>Although it is generally true that the language used has little
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7un9mm$2a6$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <h3jP3.51$zO.3355@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7uk6op$rln$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Precisely why you use one language for one thing, and another language for
another thing.  Language comparisons based on speed *have* to have several
sections that isolate particular types of data processing to be meaningful.
To compare a word processor written in Cobol VS. C is one comparison, but
comparing a large client/server database application written in the same two
languages is a completely different thing.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380E2A9C.7D0D687A@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
Most compilers have licence conditions that prevent the
publication of benchmark results. I can tell you that 

1. COBOL compilers tend to be much (several times) slower than C
compilers for equivalent programs. This becomes a serious
problems for > 2000 line programs, depending on the speed of your
machine. This seems to be simply a result of a less competitive
market.

2. Code generated tend to be somewhat slower for COBOL than C,
due to language rules. For example, if you have a pic 9(8) comp
item, the compiler will truncate any results to 8 digits, whereas
in C, the equivalent 'int' will not require the extra code.

3. Packed decimal arithmetic is 20 times slower than binary
except on the mainframe where it is about as fast as binary.

4. Some cobols are interpretive, or use 'byte codes' like Java.
Acucobol for example. I would not advise intense numeric
calculations in a such a case.

Tim Josling


Alan Martin wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know any sites with speed comparisons of COBOL vs other
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> Microfocus COBOL and FJ cobol versions I looked at both had
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That being the case they, and any other vendors doing it, are very dumb,
dumb, dumb. 

MicroFocus about 2 years ago used to have a forum on Compuserve, open to
anybody, and that's how I first 'met' Michael Mattias and Pete Dashwood.
It had topic areas and when M/F introduced Visoc (precursor to
NetExpress), they were inundated with queries from people using beta
versions - how do I do this, it doesn't appear to let me do this etc....
(Would have helped if every GUI was covered with sample source code -
familiar refrain ?) They didn't like what they were getting back - and
one senior man, I don't know his position, was quite candid with me; he
didn't like the forum being used for Visoc and, I believe, may have been
instrumental in having it shut down.

The Forum is now replaced by Answerlab for which I pay a fee - I don't
get the logic - it is restricted to N.America, and I believe
Spanish-speaking users - so that shuts out poor Jose Capucho from
Portugal. You finish up in Answerlab asking specific technical questions
related to M/F modules that you are using - so no input from other N/E
users in Europe and it doesn't have the general interest flavour that
this NG does.

This whole 'Net thing has opened up an entirely new world. I am very new
to it and don't Net-surf, restricting myself to this NG. But it has been
a real eye-opener seeing problems people encounter with different
compilers, machines, communicating with DBs and other languages etc...
Albeit, reluctantly, the vendors have to open up, because we are all
going to be talking a lot more to each other, and from the exchanged
information making evaluations - and as the end-users, at Bill Klein's
prompting, we should be making comments on things such as standards -
not here, but to our local Standards representative or direct to J4,
whatever.

As I said to somebody just recently, (but the message got returned
undelivered - bugger it), "The Standards have bitten off one gargantuan
chunk, and I doubt they or compiler vendors can digest it in one meal".
And as Bill comments in his write-up, he's very dubious there will be
too many of us using the major part of the enhancements - even if
COBOL-XXXX ever happens - Meeting in Amsterdam Spring 2000; deliberation
takes us through to 2001; maybe agreement then - now allow vendors to
implement just part of the changes - we're looking at 2003-2004 before a
fully standard-compliant compiler becomes available. These dates don't
even touch on when OO will truly become available, (ignoring that
IBM/Fujitsu/Merant are partially there already - using a draft standard)

Tim - your own concerns should be passed on to the Standards Committees.
I'm already penning mine on User Defined Functions and OO.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote in message
news:380E2A9C.7D0D687A@melbpc.org.au...
> Most compilers have licence conditions that prevent the
> publication of benchmark results. I can tell you that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> market.

But this may be (almost) irrelevant. My old Realia 4 compiler
compiled at better than 50,000 lines of source code per second.
If a C compiler is twice as fast, well, who cares?

>
> 2. Code generated tend to be somewhat slower for COBOL than C,
> due to language rules. For example, if you have a pic 9(8) comp
> item, the compiler will truncate any results to 8 digits, whereas
> in C, the equivalent 'int' will not require the extra code.

The truncation rules for COBOL assume the programmer
knows what is required for the application, as opposed to, say,
C, where the compiler assumes it knows better.

If a field is defined in COBOL as 9(8), presumably that
exact precision is required for the application and any other
rendition is, by definition, wrong. For example, a paycheck for
$1234.0175622451 hardly makes sense.

(Although I did once write a check for $0.00 to a hospital
as my small protest to zero-balance statements ("Please
pay this amount:    $0.00). When the check did not clear,
I began calling the hospital asking if they wanted me to
send another, etc.)

>
> 3. Packed decimal arithmetic is 20 times slower than binary
> except on the mainframe where it is about as fast as binary.

That's because, of course, mainframes have special hardware
to deal with packed numbers. One would think that a comparative
benchmark would shy away from obvious hardware-related
advantages or disadvantages.


What would be even more interesting, for this NG, is a benchmark
of various COBOL compilers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F7988.1A2EB383@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380E2A9C.7D0D687A@melbpc.org.au> <ahDP3.457$VZ5.33944@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
See comments in body of text
Tim Josling

Jerry P wrote:
> But this may be (almost) irrelevant. My old Realia 4 compiler
> compiled at better than 50,000 lines of source code per second.
> If a C compiler is twice as fast, well, who cares?
Unless you have a 1,000,000 line project, no difference. But the
COBOL compilers I tested were a lot slower than this. I don't
want to wait 6 seconds to compile 10,000 lines. 

OK some people would not get 6 second turnaround in a month of
Sundays eg on an overloaded mainframe. But 0.2 seconds is better
than 6 seconds by a large margin.

> If a field is defined in COBOL as 9(8), presumably that
> exact precision is required for the application and any other
> rendition is, by definition, wrong. 
> 
The issue I have with the decimal truncation is that you have to
have it - they assume you want decimal truncation, not binary
truncation. In cobol 2000 they have a pure binary data item to
get over this 'bug' in the standard.

The decimal arithmetic is good if you want it, but otherwise it
is a real pain. It is one of COBOL's most useful features but you
should be able to do binary when you want IMHO - and the
standards committee agrees.
> >
> > 3. Packed decimal arithmetic is 20 times slower than binary
> > except on the mainframe where it is about as fast as binary.
> That's because, of course, mainframes have special hardware
> to deal with packed numbers.
Yes for example 390 has AP, MP, SP, PACK, UNPACK etc. So if
writing new code, you would probably want to avoid packed on
non-mainframes. However, often you are porting existing mainframe
code, and it is difficult to change. 
> What would be even more interesting, for this NG, is a benchmark
> of various COBOL compilers.

<replay for="those who weren't paying attention">
Most compiler manufacturers prohibit publishing benchmarks of
their products, in the licence agreements.
</replay>

The languages have merits other than speed. As well as the
decimal arithmetic, COBOL has a lot of things in the language
that are OS dependent in other languages. Some would argue that
the pseudo English syntax makes programs easier to follow. There
is some evidence that COBOL is easier to learn that some other
languages because the syntax seems more friendly and so is less
intimidating (vs say LISP or even C).

The new standard is so huge with OO extensions etc I am afraid
this may no longer be true.

Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Interfaces in the Draft Standard (was: Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7us77d$2qcp$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380E2A9C.7D0D687A@melbpc.org.au> <ahDP3.457$VZ5.33944@typhoon01.swbell.net> <380F7988.1A2EB383@melbpc.org.au> <380ffa48.271522005@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Posted and mailed.

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <380ffa48.271522005@news1.attglobal.net>...
>Posted and mailed.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>differ from a "Class"?

What the heck, I'll give it try!

Interfaces are used to check the conformance of method
implementations. The use of interfaces provides for error
checking at compile time rather than run time.

An interface is similar to a class in that an interface may inherit
from other interfaces and a class may inherit from other classes.

It is different from class in that it only defines method interfaces
while the class defines factory and object data in addition to
complete methods.

One writes an interface by including one or more empty methods
in an interface program; that is, the method-name, linkage data,
and using phase.

One uses the interface in a class by including the interface-name
in the repository paragraph; then, after the factory or object
paragraph header, one references the interface-name in an
implements clause.

Finally, one writes the code that implements the methods that
were defined in the interface.

If there is any difference between the implementation and the
definition, the compiler will report the error. If no interface is used,
the error MAY be reported at run time.

Those who do high-reliability programming will use interfaces
and program and function prototypes extensively, as will those
who work with very large systems.

This, at least, is my understanding from reading the draft with
a few opinions added! ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```


Rick Smith wrote:
>  Thane Hubbell wrote :-
> >Speaking of which - what the heck is INTERFACE for, and how does it
> >differ from a "Class"?

> and Rick replied :-
> What the heck, I'll give it try!
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> a few opinions added! ;-)

I was getting there but I think you've done a pretty good job.
Background, Thane queried me privately and I queried Simon Hart who was
also using NetExpress; we were stumped - other than I found a few
general references in Arranga/Coyle and Topper. The most enlightening
was the Fujitsu manual.

So where else did you look 'a few opinions added'..., as well as the
cyrptic/legalese of the Standards ? Thane, if you have access :-

  Handbook of Programming Languages Vol I : OO Programming Languages
ISBN 1-57870-009-4. - General interest chapters on the various OO
languages, but the closest to current topic is the section on Java.

Although Fujitsu and Merant come closest, NetExpress doesn't as yet have
the INTERFACE/CONFORMANCE/PROTOTYPING features - 'Let's see what the
Standards people come up with' - so that's what threw Simon and myself.

To CONFORM or not to CONFORM. As you indicated Rick, high reliability
programming or large systems. I can see this might become a religious
topic in due course. Certainly the concept of preparing a model for
future use and the desirable features of parameter checking, plus
confirming availability of methods is great - but looking at the Fujitsu
introductory pages - I get the impression it can become quite complex.
Examples, examples, i.e. source code - we need models on which to base
our designs and to test.

Ken Foskey, I now know is into OO by using C++ (I was wondering why he
was such a clever bugger <G>), so perhaps he'll join in too. Want to
tackle this one Rick : -

Multiple Inheritance (Not currently part of Merant OO).

	class - Dad			class - Mum
	method "I'm-your-dad"		method "I'm-your-mum"
		.				.
		.................................
				.
			Class : Jimmy

Real dead easy, biologically - Jimmy inherits from Dad and Mum. Same
applies in OO, but when we invoke Jimmy for "I'm-your-mum", it's going
to search class Jimmy first, not finding it, search Class Dad, again not
finding it, searches Class Mum - Bingo! Extend my family tree upwards to
about the year 1600 - then we've got problems to search for a class
containing method "I'm-your-great-great-great-great...............dad".
(I haven't seen anything written which suggests a limit on levels of
inheritance). 

Does CONFORMANCE provide a short-cut to do this, or do any of the
existing OO languages contain a hint on how to do it ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methodolgy : Was: Interfaces in the Draft Standard

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v2d48$fmm$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <380E2A9C.7D0D687A@melbpc.org.au> <ahDP3.457$VZ5.33944@typhoon01.swbell.net> <380F7988.1A2EB383@melbpc.org.au> <380ffa48.271522005@news1.attglobal.net> <7us77d$2qcp$1@news.hitter.net> <3813C686.AA2A8D6B@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan wrote in message <3813C686.AA2A8D6B@home.com>...
[...]
>I was getting there but I think you've done a pretty good job.
>Background, Thane queried me privately and I queried Simon Hart who was
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>cyrptic/legalese of the Standards ? Thane, if you have access :-

The documentation supplied with compilers and books about
OO COBOL are a snapshot taken a one point in time. The draft
of the next COBOL standard is more dynamic. Interfaces may
have been added after the choices for features to include in the
compiler and features to discuss in the books were made.

I used only the draft standard and my general knowledge of
modular programming to describe and opine.
[...]
>Ken Foskey, I now know is into OO by using C++ (I was wondering why he
>was such a clever bugger <G>), so perhaps he'll join in too. Want to
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>containing method "I'm-your-great-great-great-great...............dad".

For the example given, I would consider defining a Class : Person
with properties Name, Dad, and Mum where Dad and Mum are
object references to a Class : Person. Then I might define a method,
Relationship, with parameters Level and Sex to provide the information
necessary to describe the relationship. Finally a subprogram or
function could be used to build and display the string that describes
the relationship.

As I interactively navigate the family tree (using a recursive
subprogram with a Person object, Level, and Sex as a parameters),
the program would display the name and relationship relative to the
root of the tree. With an event and method to AddParent and events
and subprograms to SaveTree and LoadTree, a simple FamilyTree
application is born. (There is more to it than described; but why should
I have all the fun!)

>(I haven't seen anything written which suggests a limit on levels of
>inheritance).

There are practical limits. For COBOL, two or three levels might
be common. Considering that the classification of the animal
kingdom consists of about eight(?) levels, eight levels seems
an excessive upper limit. There are, however, some differences
in the way languages are used where higher numbers might
actually make sense.

When you are ready to discuss parameterized classes and
interfaces, count me out! ;-)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7umtoi$bai@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <01bf1b5e$0cb668e0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
All this discussion on performance may seem interesting, but I think it
misses the point.  As Gunnar points out, there are other factors which are
just as important such as the skills of the programmer.  Others have pointed
out that the choice of the programmer to use or not use particular features
of a specific language also have a major impact.  When I was doing my
doctoral dissertation (see my home page for a link to it) on how we learn
what are sometimes the wrong ways to do things in COBOL, one performance
issue I specifically investigated was the maxim that one should use blank
comment lines (* in column 7) instead of blank lines because the compiler
uses less CPU time when it can stop scanning after column 7.  I ran some
tests of the same program with 10,000 blank lines vs. 10,000 blank comment
lines (easy enough to do with a good editor).  Well, the maxim is true, the
program with blank comment lines cost $.05 less to compile.  But then one
asks the question, "how much time does it take to tab over to column 7 and
type an asterisk 10,000 times?"  If it takes only 1 second, then the
programmer spends nearly 3 hours doing this task.  Even presuming that he
never makes a mistake and accidently types the asterisk in column 6 or 8,
then at $50/hour (including fringes), these asterisks cost $150.00.  Unless
we plan on compiling the program more than 3000 times during its life (an
average of once every workday for 12 years), we will never make up for the
few nanoseconds of time that the compiler runs quicker/cheaper.

Why are we so concerned about nanoseconds?  The true speed of a program is
usually not limited by CPU speed, rather we should concentrate our efforts
where it matters on such things as (1) readability of programs, since they
are usually read more often than they are written (an area where COBOL can
excel if done properly), (2) efficiency of I/O, using indexes instead of
sequential searches,etc., (3) ease of use, saving the time of both learning
and using by the user of the program, etc.  Nanoseconds might make sense in
calculation intensive applications like nuclear physics.  And COBOL might
not be the appropriate language to use in these problem domains either.

My rules of thumb are (1) choose a language that has features appropriate to
the problem domain, and (2) make efficient and effective use of the most
costly resources.  If I do those things right, then the nanoseconds usually
take care of themselves.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

Gunnar Opheim wrote in message <01bf1b5e$0cb668e0$0100007f@vaagshaugen>...
>
>Alan Martin <amartin@student.cowan.edu.au> wrote in article
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Gunnar.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380FDF11.8AF1EF0C@home.com>`

```


Alan Martin wrote:
> 
> Does anyone know any sites with speed comparisons of COBOL vs other
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

Alan,

Just re-read your original message. You got info but it was a bit
off-topic wasn't it. If I read you right, you were asking about
application performance.

Well, from the feedback you've had so far :-

Short-answer to your original question - are there bench-mark tests -
doesn't look like there are. Just so happens I think there should be.
But even if you confront them with hard statistics, the Doubting
Thomas'es still wont believe you - any more than the Vatican bought into
Galileo's idea that the World was round.

- Compiler performance - it doesn't have a hoot to do with application
performance. Obviously, as developers, we like the compiler tool to be
as FAST if not FASTER than the next, regardless of language. 'Twas a
time with Radio Shack and old RM/COBOL I would spend half an hour
compiling one program; get one typo, I had to go through the cycle
again. That was the technology some 20 years ago. With my current
compiler on a PC I can completely re-compile the same application, (i.e.
the WHOLE application), 2 to 3 times, in the same time period. 

Considering the Radio Shack's diskettes were two 8.5" platters, very
prone to CRC errors through continual re-use, the application performed
very well.

What really matters is the application and the language :-

	"My object so sublime,
	I shall achieve in time,
	To make the punishment fit the crime,
	The punishment fit the crime...."

	G & S - 'The Mikado'

The language must fit the application - NOT the other way around. Take
NASA - it is a real stretch of the imagination to suggest that COBOL is
used to trigger off some action through space aimed at a shuttle or
satellite. Maybe it is done with the newer languages, but it wouldn't
surprise me if they have invented a bunch of 'NASA-languages' to fit a
specific need/instrument.

Does that leave COBOL out in the cold ? No, it's quite possible their
administrative systems, payroll, catering, supplies, accounting etc.
could be in COBOL which is ideally suited to 'traditional' commercial
applications dealing with money and administration - and some of the
other languages can be a bad or poor fit to do this. (Historically,
COBOL was first used by the Pentagon for admin. - they had large input
to the original standards)

Sincerely hope you feel you've got some feedback to help you with your
thoughts.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

Assuming some new language replaces COBOL at some future date, there are
sure going to be a lot of busy people translating for the next thirty
years or so - see quote :-

------------------------------------------------------------------------
"Currently there are over 3 million COBOL programmers in the world.
According to the Gartner Group, COBOL programmers represent 80% of all
programmers in the world. COBOL is taught in many business information
systems departments, thus continuing to produce COBOL programmers 

( +++ As we know from this NewsGroup - lots of institutions are giving
up on COBOL - they want to teach the new fads - and as we already know
Alan, the same bloody nonsense is going on Down Under - continuation of
quote +++).

A recent study reports that COBOL programming continues to be a highly
demanded job skill - higher than any other language except 'C'. A
different survey indicated that over 70% of all entry-level programming
positions value knowledge of COBOL.

COBOL is the dominant programming language of the corporate world, with
over 80% of ALL code written in COBOL. For the existing base of
software, estimates range from 70 billion lines of COBOL code to over
200 billion. Although the estimates vary, it is fair to say that there
are billions of lines of COBOL code."

 E.Reed Doke & Bill C. Hardgrave "An Introduction to Object COBOL" 1998 

------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3811125C.28955E54@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
Look at the syntax between two languages.  Some things are just a
pain in the rear with a particular language.  What brought this to
mind is that I needed to test a display field for only alphas and
numerals.  (IBM VS/COBOL II R4.0 compiler).

The IBM hardware con do this in one instruction.

COBOL has a class test, but you can only check for ALPHABETIC
(A-Z), or NUMERIC (0-9 plus '-').  No ALPHANUMERIC class.  After
much through and discussion on coffee break, the best our group
of programmers could come up with is
    MOVE TEST-FIELD TO HOLD_FIELD
    INSPECT HOLD-FIELD
      CONVERTING 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789'
      TO HIGH-VALUES
    IF HOLD-FIELD IS EQUAL TO HIGH-VALUES THEN
        ....
    END-IF

In PL/I there is a ALPHANUMERIC class.
In C, there is strspn()
In BASIC, there is VERIFY$
In Rexx, there is DATATYPE() or VERIFY()
In Fortran, you must perform a character loop scan
IN S/390 Assembler, use the TRT instruction.

Now the question again: Which language will perform better?

S/390 Assembler and PL/I tie with 1 instruction.
C, in an optimal environment, takes 3 S/390 instructions
COBOL II, in an optimal environment, takes 3 instructions, but
those three will take longer to execute than those that C will
use.

Moral?  It's not just the platform, or environment, but also the
language structure/syntax.
```

> Look at the syntax between two languages.  Some things are just a
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

*****for NLS - but we're back to the miserable saga of how up-to-date
***** your COBOL compiler is; 68, 74, 85, 89, 97

> 
> In PL/I there is a ALPHANUMERIC class.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jared Thomas

  Now the question again - as put by the person who originated this
thread :-  

Alan Martin wrote:

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

As regards the original question you are way off topic, (don't worry, I
do it myself all the time <G>). So given your multiple-choice list
above, exactly which language are you proposing to use for Application
"X" ?

> language structure/syntax.

Answer -  it aint none of the above. It's the language which is the best
fit for the application overall, regardless of platform, environment,
structure/syntax. (Engineering - sure, use FORTRAN - my engineering
application has been running in COBOL for 20 years and hasn't tripped up
yet with missing 'FORTRAN-like' features).	

Jimmy, Calgary AB

    SPECIAL-NAMES.

then

    IF TEST-FIELD IS LETTERS-AND-NUMBERS

previous discussions in this group, I believe this will generate
a one instruction test.

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
>
>SPECIAL-NAMES.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>     DISPLAY 'Yes it is'.

Good post!  Tiny quibble - on EBCDIC machines you would need

CLASS ALNUM IS 'A' THRU 'I', 'J' THRU 'R', 'S' THRU 'Z', '0' THRU '9'.

Perhaps better to list the letters individually, should only affect
the compile time, not run time.  Most CPUs I am familiar with support
character scan instructions for these things.
```

```
On Fri, 22 Oct 1999 20:41:48 -0500, Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
wrote:

>The IBM hardware con do this in one instruction.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>



>In PL/I there is a ALPHANUMERIC class.
>In C, there is strspn()
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>IN S/390 Assembler, use the TRT instruction.


Jared.  Are your coffee drinking COBOL friends aware that they can set
up their own class?  

Here is an example! (Took me 1 cup of coffee to get this! <G>)

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  TESTZZ.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           CLASS ALPHA-NUMERIC IS "A" THRU "Z"
                                 "0" THRU "9"
                                 "a" THRU "z"
                                 SPACE.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FIRST-FIELD PIC X(4) VALUE "abCD".
       01  SECOND-FIELD PIC X(4) VALUE "$#CD".
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       START-OF-PROGRAM.
           IF FIRST-FIELD ALPHA-NUMERIC
              DISPLAY "First Alphanumeric"
           ELSE
              DISPLAY "First NOT Alphanumeric"
           END-IF
           IF SECOND-FIELD ALPHA-NUMERIC
              DISPLAY "Second Alphanumeric"
           ELSE
              DISPLAY "Second NOT Alphanumeric"
           END-IF
           STOP RUN
           .

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ut4p0$fqe$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au> <3811125C.28955E54@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com> wrote:

: COBOL has a class test, but you can only check for ALPHABETIC
: (A-Z), or NUMERIC (0-9 plus '-').  No ALPHANUMERIC class.  After
: much through and discussion on coffee break, the best our group
: of programmers could come up with is
:     MOVE TEST-FIELD TO HOLD_FIELD
:     INSPECT HOLD-FIELD
:       CONVERTING 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789'

iThat is a very insular view as to what constitutes alphanumeric.
Many countries have a few more than that.

:       TO HIGH-VALUES
:     IF HOLD-FIELD IS EQUAL TO HIGH-VALUES THEN
:         ....
:     END-IF

Let me see now:

        SPECIAL-NAMES.
            CLASS MYALPHANUMERIC IS " "
                                     "0" THRU "9"
                                     "A" THRU "Z" *> different is EBCIDIC
                                      etc

             IF ( Test-Field MYALPHANUMERIC )
                   ...

: Now the question again: Which language will perform better?

It is usual to do these sort of checks when data is initially
input.  So after waiting several seconds for the user to
key it in it tends not to be relevant that it may take
a few more or a few less nanoseconds to tell the user the
input is right or wrong.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs other languages: Performance Comparisons - anyone ??????

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1cf3$936a2160$0100007f@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7ujcm5$1vj$1@news.cowan.edu.au>`

```
How about -- when helping a student with a coaxial cyl. transmission line
problem, we came up with formulae which required evaluation of
various Bessel functions of complex arguments.  

She initially wrote up the formulae in Microsoft Excel (which supported
complex numbers by holding them in text strings, and converting to numeric
form just prior to calculation, then immediately converting back).  Once
the formulae were worked out and tested, I recoded them in Fortran.
Using the old MS Fortran (16 bit) for DOS to compile, the EXE was run
under SoftPC on her Macintosh. 
The compiled Fortran being run under simulation beat the native, 
but not optimized for complex number calculation, Excel 
by about 3 to 1.  

What does that prove?           ABSOLUTELY NOTHING.  

So why are you interested in meaningless comparisons?
```

```

> 
> How about -- when helping a student with a coaxial cyl. transmission line
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> So why are you interested in meaningless comparisons?

It demonstrated that between the two of you, you felt that Excel was
appropriate for designing the solution for the problem, and Fortran
could run the job more quickly.  I have no idea which platform would be
easier to maintain and modify in your environment.

In my environment, maintenance costs are paramount.
```

## Thread: Give a graduate a chance!!! — 9 msgs

### Re: Give a graduate a chance!!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XdjP3.14384$Fj2.125987@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<380BEB36.85E39E4B@yahoo.com> <DB8P3.817$L42.33749@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <380D3E53.60C549E2@yahoo.com>`

```
The question is not "Should we be able to have fun?", it is "When and
where is it appropriate to do so?"  Asking for a job programming COBOL
business applications is a serious thing.  COBOL programs handle the
most critical functions within businesses.  Businesses often succeed
or fail on the performance of their MIS departments, with millions or
billions of dollars at stake.  If you aren't able to be serious when
asking for a job, when your own interestests are very much at stake,
why would your prospective employer think you would take his business
interests any more seriously, when it is his money at stake? :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Give a graduate a chance!!!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

```


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> The question is not "Should we be able to have fun?", it is "When and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> interests any more seriously, when it is his money at stake? :-)

Come on Judson, lighten-up. Some magnificent words from your favourite
Book :-

	"A time to love, a time to die
	 A time to laugh, a time to cry...."


When I first got into this 'racket' I read that Ford, Dagenham (UK) were
employing a matron(nurse) to tend to the stresses of their computer
people - back when dress vogue was sandals and beards. Thought, 'What
the hell am I getting myself into..... ?'.

I would say that the most successful computer people I've enjoyed
working with, had a highly developed sense of humour as well as
analytical minds. They knew when to 'laugh' and when to 'cry'.

Unless the gentleman we are talking about is mentally-challenged - he
knows when he gets to the interview he has to put on a serious face, not
an idiot's grin.

Stay cool Joey.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Of course, there is a time for everything.  That's exactly my point, there
is a time to be funny and a time to be serious.  Would *you* send out a
resume for a COBOL position with a crayon smiley face on it?  Or sign it
"joeycool?" ;-)  Remember, the post we are talking about was an attempted
first contact with prospective employers, a sort of 'mini-resume'.

>working with, had a highly developed sense of humour as well as
>analytical minds. They knew when to 'laugh' and when to 'cry'.

I agree, of course.  I just don't think joking in a resume for a COBOL
position is a wise thing.  I suspect that even resumes for clowns are
serious. :)

>Unless the gentleman we are talking about is mentally-challenged - he
>knows when he gets to the interview he has to put on a serious face, not
>an idiot's grin.

Yes, but if the idiot's grin is the first thing the prospective employer
sees, do you really think he will get that interview? ;-)

### Renumbering - how do I

- **From:** Star Straf <star@prairienet.org>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380DC5E9.BAE73114@prairienet.org>`

```
Is there a simple command or utility to renumber the
line numbers for a cobol program in either UNIX or
windows 95.  I have a program that I inherited that
needs massive changes and I would like to 
renumber/resequence the line numbers.  I haven't
done any COBOL in a long time and I thought I remeber
there begin a simple resequence command but our 
sysop here dosen't know of such a thing

Thanks
  Star Straf   KU    star@prairienet.org
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZIjP3.14446$Fj2.126667@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<380DC5E9.BAE73114@prairienet.org>`

```
Star Straf wrote:
>Is there a simple command or utility to renumber the
>line numbers for a cobol program in either UNIX or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>sysop here dosen't know of such a thing

I have one in C.  E-mail me and I'll be happy to send you the source
and DOS EXE file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Renumbering - how do I

- **From:** Steven Lalewicz <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F1584.CF9CDEB6@mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<380DC5E9.BAE73114@prairienet.org>`

```
Star Straf wrote:

> Is there a simple command or utility to renumber the
> line numbers for a cobol program in either UNIX or
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> � Star Straf�� KU��� star@prairienet.org

Hi,

If you are using any Mico Focus compiler you can use the RESEQ
directive. This causes the compiler to produce line numbers in the
listing file (.lst) which you can then copy back to .cbl.

UNIX:
cob demo.cbl -C reseq -C rawlist -P� # rawlist� remove page headers
PC:
cobol demo.cbl reseq rawlist list();

The COPYLIST directive controls inclusion of the copy book in the
listing. The sequence numbers produced counts the contents of the copy
book too. If you wish to retain individual copy books (and don't want to
create them by cutting them out the listing), not sure what to suggest.
Maybe my answer is getting too complex.

Hope I've helped somewhere......

Regards,
Steve.
�
```

### problems recording time

- **From:** "Emulov" <e@mulov.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```
Hi.

In a COBOL program, I need to know how long the user used the program, when
he quits. So I get the time when it starts and ends, but this doesn't work
if for example the program is opened at 8 in the evening, and closed at 2
the next morning.

I am using CA-Realia II v3, on a 24 hour MS Windows 98 PC.

Thanx for any useful help!
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()

- **References:** `<7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

DD

#### ↳ Re: problems recording time

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380E2ACC.DC5EE784@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```


Emulov wrote:
> 
> Hi.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If you can get a copy of Thane Hubbell's "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
hours" it will give you date/time examples.

Failing that you can use ACCEPT Date or Time, but preferably :-

    move function current-date to ws-DateStart *> 21 alpaha/numeric 
    move function current-date to ws-DateFinis *> 21 alpaha/numeric     

If you don't have 'move function' then use 'compute function' on a
numeric Date21N.
	
When starting/finishing you get the 21 character date/time/GMT format -
check your manuals/on-line help. Taking ws-DateStart and ws-DateFinis
you need to bring them down to the lowest denominator, subtract and then
convert back to previous units - to get your time usage. 

	1 hour   = 	xxxxx hundreths
	1 minute =	xxxxx hundreths
	1 second =	xxxxx hundreths

       01 ws-Date21.
          03 ws-yyyymmdd.
             05 ws-Year.
                10 ws-cc          pic 9(02).
                10 ws-yy          pic 9(02).
             05 ws-Month          pic 9(02). *> range 1 - 12
             05 ws-Day            pic 9(02). *> range 1 - 31
          03 ws-yyyymmddN
             redefines ws-yyyymmdd pic 9(08).
          03 ws-Time.
             04 ws-HoursMinsSecs.
             05 ws-HoursMins.
                06 ws-Hours   pic 9(02). *> range 0 - 23 past midnight
                06 ws-Minutes pic 9(02). *> range 0 - 59 past hour
             05 ws-Seconds    pic 9(02). *> range 0 - 59 past minute
             05 ws-Hundredths pic 9(02). *> range 0 - 99 past second
          03 ws-GMT.
             05 ws-PlusMinus      pic x.           *> value 0, + or -
             05 ws-GMThoursMins.
                06 ws-GMThours    pic 9(02). *> range 0 - 12/13
                06 ws-GMTminutes  pic 9(02). *> range 0  - 59

       *>  ws-hundredths = "00" if system cannot generate fractions
       *>  of seconds

       *> Greenwich Mean Time :

       *> if the system cannot provide a local time differential
       *> a value of "00000" is returned in the last five positions

       *> ws-PlusMinus refers to GMT : either '0' above or '+' or '-'

       *> ws-GMThours     range '-' 0 - 12; '+' 0 - 13
       *> ws-GMTminutes   range '-' 0 - 59; '+' 0 - 59

       *> GMT minus '-' - indicates hours/mins behind   GMT
       *> GMT plus  '+' -     ..        ..     ahead of GMT

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exactly the same horrible problem we had with pounds shillings and pence
and fractions of pennies in UK before decimalisation :-

#21. 10s. 3 3/4d.

#(UK pound)  21.   10.(shiilings)    3 3/4d (3 pence 3 farthings) :-

        21 * 960 =	20,160		pound    = 960 farthings
	10 *  48 =	   480		shilling =  48 	"
	 3 *   4 =	    12		penny    =   4	"
	 3       =	     3			 =   3  "
	-----------------------
		= 	20,655 farthings

PS: Hope somebody's not going to say I goofed on my arithmetic <G>
This particular 'Einstein' breathed a sigh of relief when we went
decimal.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Thank goodness Bill Klein is relaxing on a trans-Atlantic flight <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: problems recording time

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380e8dc4.178191681@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
You didn't goof originally.  Function Current-date  IS allowed in a
MOVE, it does NOT return a numeric result.  The results (full 21
bytes) are alphanumeric.

wrote:

>
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

#### ↳ Re: problems recording time

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20qP3.22783$E_1.1234042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```
> In a COBOL program, I need to know how long the user used the program,
when
> he quits. So I get the time when it starts and ends, but this doesn't work
> if for example the program is opened at 8 in the evening, and closed at 2
> the next morning.

Convert both times to seconds.  If the opening and closing dates don't
match, run both of them through an integer-of-date function, multiply the
difference between them by the number of seconds in a day and add it to the
closing seconds.  Subtract the opening seconds from it and convert the
result into days, hours, minutes, and seconds.  HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: problems recording time

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7unf3u$qgo$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>`

```
Or at 8 p.m. until 9 p.m next day. Obviously you need a date and a time. You
can then calculate the time correct. If you need to get a sum of sessions
for each user, then calculate seconds in session, add them all together and
display them at end as hours,minutes and seconds.

Good luck
Ib Tanding

Emulov skrev i meddelelsen <7ul262$qq4$1@xenon.inbe.net>...

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

show you how to convert the result of Function Current-Date to
absolute seconds (time is returned too), and then back to a time.  You
could use this to find a duration.  (I use it to show how to convert
GMT to local time).

## Thread: COBOL For MVS - Is There A Format Function? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL For MVS - Is There A Format Function?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>`

```
Is there a function in COBOL for MVS to apply editing rules to a variable (NOT
using MOVEs to Working-Storage!)?

Similar to FORMAT in VB or PUT STRING EDIT in PL/I,,,

TIA!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL For MVS - Is There A Format Function?

- **From:** "John Smith" <cord@getout.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7um60k$j5v@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>`

```
Not sure exactly what editing you need to do.
You might consider using a redefine or level 88 under the variable.

Example:

05  ARRIVAL-AS-PROMISED     PIC X(1).
       88   ON-TIME    VALUE 'Y'.
       88   LATE          VALUE 'L'.
       88   LOST          VALUE 'Z'.

05  PHONE-NUMBER     PIC X(10).
        10  PHONE-AREA-CODE       PIC X(3).
        10  PHONE-PREFIX                 PIC X(3).
        10  PHONE-LAST4                   PIC X(4).
05  PHONE-IN-COMPANY REDEFINES PHONE-NUMBER
        10  FILLER                                 PIC X(5).
        10  COMPANY-PHONE           PIC X(5).

Gene


Theo DP wrote in message <19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>...
>Is there a function in COBOL for MVS to apply editing rules to a variable
(NOT
>using MOVEs to Working-Storage!)?
>
>Similar to FORMAT in VB or PUT STRING EDIT in PL/I,,,
>
>TIA!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL For MVS - Is There A Format Function?

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O1kDLxDH$GA.302@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>`

```
Not sure what you want, but if it helps, you can de-edit by moving from a
numeric edited picture to a regular numeric one. There are also functions
to parse the numeric value from even somewhat fancy numeric edited fields.
As far as formatting I think you still have to use Move's.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL For MVS - Is There A Format Function?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lcEP3.151$SS6.3834@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>`

```
Theo DP wrote in message <19991020221054.13914.00000293@ng-fp1.aol.com>...
>Is there a function in COBOL for MVS to apply editing rules to a variable
(NOT
>using MOVEs to Working-Storage!)?
>
>Similar to FORMAT in VB or PUT STRING EDIT in PL/I,,,
>


You can enforce various PICTURE clauses if defined in a SCREEN SECTION.
(e.g, PIC 999,999 will force the user to enter digit,
digit,digit,comma,digit,digit,digit).

Other than that, I think all you can force COBOL to handle is class
condition (e.g., IF X IS NUMERIC|ALPHABETIC..)

MCM
```

### Paid WEB Site study for COBOL Programmers in Los Angeles/Sunnyvale

- **From:** mwardeninfoco@aol.com (MWardenInFoCo)
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991020140140.10165.00000109@ng-cb1.aol.com>`

```
We are conducting a focus group on Web Site development issues in Sunnyvale on
October 27th at 8 p, and in Marina Del Rey on October 28th at 8pm. 

 Each participant will be paid $150.

We are seeking companies that use COBOL as one of their programming languages.
The participants will be programmers with 2+ years experience using COBOL,
writing 50% or more of code in COBOL in the last 30 days, and who either
develop new code or maintain existing code.

Companies manufacturing or distributing  programming utilities or software
development tools as their sole industry are not qualified participants. 
	 
Please reply or you may call me at my office at:  888.286.0448.  Feel free to
forward this information electronically to whomever you feel is appropriate for
this group.  

Have a great day!

Sincerely,


Michelle Warden
InFoCo Research Consultant
```

## Thread: emptying a table <- What about this? — 1 msgs

### Re: emptying a table <- What about this?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yD0OOKiiLzFtPEy1LDb4JSpbaIbN@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7u4ic4$544$1@news0.skynet.be> <LbkN3.2214$LW6.30493@news2.mia> <7u52mo$mcr@dfw-ixnews16.ix.netcom.com> <WroN3.43201$Ev4.39379@news3.mia> <7u8q08$19o@journal.concentric.net> <3807F4BD.AF9D5CFA@home.com> <7u9kaa$lf2$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
The venerable "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Sat, 16 Oct 1999 06:06:31 -0400 these words:

>    Some compilers now complain about forward overlapping moves.  I assume
>that it impacts the algorythm used when copying from one area to another.
>Some compilers would drop back to byte at a time instead of 16 or 32 bits at
>a time.

It's not just a compiler issue, it's a hardware/microcode issue on
some systems.  This is where the compiler might produce code with
differing results on theoretically compatible computers.  The CPU
manuals always warn you of circumstances where overlapping moves are
undefined.
```

## Thread: MF-COBOL 3.2 with newer libraries — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF-COBOL 3.2 with newer libraries

- **From:** darius_cooper6054@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uktet$el8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have executables on AIX which were compiled with MF-COBOL v3.2 and
use the older run-time libraries (OS/X Libraries V11.06B).

Is there some way to get these to run with the new v4.1 (Patch 13.10)
run-time libraries or do I *have* to recompile.

I want to avoid a recompile if at all possible, but I understand that
the older libraries are not Y2K compliant. So, a temporary (first few
months of Y-2000) solution woiuld be to have the programs run with the
newer libraries.

Thanks for any info/suggestions.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

### ESDS help -student

- **From:** Dave Jakubowsky <jakubowsky.dave@mcleod.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380FA8E5.9977E5E1@mcleod.net>`

```
Looking for the best way to access an ESDS transaction file randomly and
then read sequentially.
I can do this either batch or CICS.
Should I treat this as a relative file and access by RBA?
Other ideas welcome.

Thank you for your time.
Dave
```

#### ↳ Re: ESDS help -student

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380ffe0b.272485141@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<380FA8E5.9977E5E1@mcleod.net>`

```
You are on the right track.  We do that with ASM here batch, but in
CICS you can do it with EXEC CICS commands.  RBA is the only way to
go.


On Thu, 21 Oct 1999 18:59:34 -0500, Dave Jakubowsky
<jakubowsky.dave@mcleod.net> wrote:

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ESDS help -student

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uqomu$gsu@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<380FA8E5.9977E5E1@mcleod.net> <380ffe0b.272485141@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
>You are on the right track.  We do that with ASM here batch, but in
>CICS you can do it with EXEC CICS commands.  RBA is the only way to
>go.

What about having an upgrade alternate index for the ESDS? You could
then read it randomly by key. The AIX points to the RBA's in the base
cluster. Not sure if that would work in CICS but I think so.  You
can't do it in COBOL batch though, you'd have to have an AL program.
```

#### ↳ Re: ESDS help -student

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38127ff7.3596431@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<380FA8E5.9977E5E1@mcleod.net>`

```
On Thu, 21 Oct 1999 18:59:34 -0500, Dave Jakubowsky <jakubowsky.dave@mcleod.net> wrote:

>Looking for the best way to access an ESDS transaction file randomly and
>then read sequentially.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You can process an ESDS
	sequentially
	direct via RBA (but you need to know the RBA, of course)
	direct via a key (You need to create an alternate index on top of the ESDS)
All of this either in batch or CICS

You guys seem to have this all figured out.  I haven't done CICS yet, and I

processing being described, couldn't it be done simply by opening the file for
dynamic access?

RoadRaat

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ESDS help -student

- **From:** Dave Jakubowsky <jakubowsky.dave@mcleod.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381A685F.F48C06F4@mcleod.net>`
- **References:** `<38127ff7.3596431@news2.ibm.net> <19991029232554.14540.00000326@ng-fm1.aol.com>`

```
An ESDS has to be defined as
    Organization is Sequential
    Access is sequential

So there really isn't any way to open it for dynamic access.
still waiting on instructor to put into FCT

FYI - RBA = Relative Byte Address
It is an option on a CICS read

Dave

<a id="t-885c329e9e"></a>
## Thread: EBCDIC to ASCII — 13 msgs

### EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** "pacman" <mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Can anyone solve this problem,

I am working for a company that migrated cobol from VME to unix and
migrate flat files to ORACLE.

problem is though, as EBCDIC numberics come after ASCII numberics
when a START is done on a migrated file or a  sort routine is run, the
sort order is wrong, anyone have any idea to solve this,

ie the numbers are being found before the alphas, the
opposite to EBCDIC and as 999 is eof marker the records eof marker
is brought back before the alphanumeric data in the file....

we write our own file drivers to replace the file access

HELP???
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991021184837.05216.00000119@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In article <7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>, "pacman"
<mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk> writes:

>
>problem is though, as EBCDIC numberics come after ASCII numberics
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am not too familiar with the use of this, but could you do something 
with COLLATING-SEQUENCE to alter the priority of the character set?
I'm sure the syntax is still valid in the COBOL Syntax.   I REALLY don't know
how this would affect an internal sort, and I have not heard of anything 
that allows overriding the collating sequence of an external sort on the PC.
There are SortControl Parms on the mainframe that permit altering the
weighted value of every character in the character set.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380ffd41.272282945@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk> <19991021184837.05216.00000119@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
The collating sequence never impacts the order of returned records in
indexed file or external database (as is this case).  Further analysis
is required to determine a proper solution.  I would use some SQL to
eliminate the 999 records totally and rely on normal EOF processing,
then live with the ASCII collating sequence for the data.  Either that
or change the 999 records to ZZZ records.  

On 21 Oct 1999 22:48:37 GMT, sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:

>In article <7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>, "pacman"
><mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk> writes:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991022153629.06831.00000032@ngol02.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<380ffd41.272282945@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
In article <380ffd41.272282945@news1.attglobal.net>, thaneH@softwaresimple.com
(Thane Hubbell) writes:

>
>The collating sequence never impacts the order of returned records in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I guess that I missed the part about the indexed file or database.
I have a problem with the prospect of eliminating the 999 records,
as in some applications the 999 record is not only for EOF but also 
for trailier records that contain control totals/hash totals/etc.  
These systems usually have a 000 record for a header for tracking 
last updated/software versions/client specific control parameters.

I know nothing of what the original poster is using , just throwing out some
ideas.


header
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ut54i$fqe$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<380ffd41.272282945@news1.attglobal.net> <19991022153629.06831.00000032@ngol02.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote:

: I guess that I missed the part about the indexed file or database.
: I have a problem with the prospect of eliminating the 999 records,
: as in some applications the 999 record is not only for EOF but also 
: for trailier records that contain control totals/hash totals/etc.  
: These systems usually have a 000 record for a header for tracking 
: last updated/software versions/client specific control parameters.

Won't the '000' records be after the alpha one in EBCIDC too ?
Low-values and high-values should be valid for using in
record keys.  Headers should have low-values and trailers 
high-values.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991024135103.01235.00000362@ngol03.aol.com>`

In article <7ut54i$fqe$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>, Richard Plinston
<riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> writes:

>
>Won't the '000' records be after the alpha one in EBCIDC too ?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You're right,  I was thinking in terms of numeric 'key' fields that were 
used in the banking industry.  Nearly everything in the systems were 
based on a Bank/Account number order.  Some systems included
Branch as part of the 'key' which made for a major pain when a client 
moved from one branch to another.
```

These systems usually have a 000 record for a header for tracking 
last updated/software versions/client specific control parameters.

I know nothing of what the original poster is using , just throwing out some
ideas.


header
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** "pacman" <mike@thecolberts.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v01n4$ef2$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk> <oeuQ3.15308$y61.140086@news3.mia>`

```
Thank you to the above people that have responded,

but James King, I am not a lazy student,
I work a company in Manchester, England which have downsized several large
VME systems.......

Unfortunately in the past we have written SQL to cope with this problem
but some files are left as flat files from the VME system, depending on
what the client wants, this causes a problem, because all our file
drivers ie STDM, Line Sequential, Indexed etc. have to be changed also.
The other problems are MF cobol SORT which uses it's own internal
driver (the SORT code is from the original system.) and when we have
written replacement DML software for an IDMS database the sort fails
there.....

The only ways around this problem as I can see so far  :
One is to do two selects on the database one starting from after HEX (9) and
then one from LOW-VALUES to HEX(9). But this would have to
be done for every character in the key as mixed alpha's and numerics
would cause a problem. The problem with this is that is creates a huge
load on the system........

The other would to add a hex value to the numerics when loading to the
database to put them the other side of the alphas, converting them
to and from the program, this would work but it would leave a unmanagable
database.......

****Any body think of anything else******

Thanks Phil.....

By the way what has spelling numerics got to do with anything??????


James King wrote in message ...
>I really can't take this seriously as you can't spell numerics, AND any
site
>that can write replacement
>file access drivers should have the talent to solve problem.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
pacman wrote:

> Thanks Phil.....
> 
> By the way what has spelling numerics got to do with anything??????

I wish I had more experience in conversions to ASCII so that I could
help you.  But to answer your question - the writer assumed you were
lazy because you didn't take the time  and effort to proofread your
message (not much is required).  With as many students asking us to do
their homework for them, not to mention trolls, sometimes people look
too hard at questions, trying to guess whether we should take the
questioner seriously.  "pacman" doesn't sound like a name assigned to a
business user.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EBCDIC to ASCII

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ndZQ3.41$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7unldq$7li$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk> <oeuQ3.15308$y61.140086@news3.mia> <7v01ni$efb$1@news8.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
What we have here is a variation of "the Y2K problem."

Just as everyone knew two-digit years would come back to haunt "someone else
in a couple of years", someone "knew" that using using '000' and '999' as
end-of file markers could come back to haunt someone else in a couple of
years.

You've been haunted.
```

---

<a id="t-f9f2c61b1e"></a>
## Thread: Reading Cobol Files — 2 msgs

### Reading Cobol Files

- **From:** "Alluyn Peter" <pa@schaubroeck.be>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf1bae$b5092c80$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be>`

```
Hello,

we develop our client-server software in NetExpress 3.0 using C-ISAM files
on a unix-server.  Some of our devellopers are using Smalltalk and Java. 
Is it possible to access these c-isam files from Smalltalk our Java ?

Thanks Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading Cobol Files

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3814C4F9.46F028A3@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<01bf1bae$b5092c80$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be>`

```
Alluyn Peter wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks Peter

Siber Systems sells a library that you can use to access
Cobol data files produced by MicroFocus compilers.
You can read more about it at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

The library interface is in C++, but I understand that
accessing C++ libraries from Java or SmallTalk is a lot
easier than reading the Cobol data directly.

You can download some of our Cobol-data-file to everything-else
converters from our site to see if they can read your data.
If they can, then the library will read them too, because
these converters are based on this DataAceess library.
And if they cannot, then we want to see your data
to make sure we can read it.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-b90efbade2"></a>
## Thread: Record Locking - Who has locked it — 7 msgs

### Record Locking - Who has locked it

- **From:** spanna2000@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We're using Microfocus COBOL under SCO Unix. When a file is record is
locked it would be useful to know which user has the record locked. We
have a problem whereby an operator in a multiuser system can lock a
record out whilst they change the record. They forget what they're
doing go off to lunch and leave the record locked. If this is a
commonly used record it can cause havoc.

Does anyone have a way to tell which user account has the record
locked, either via MF's EXTFH or via the operating system's lock
tables?

Regards

Mark


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Record Locking - Who has locked it

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F5A5A.87D749D7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


spanna2000@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> We're using Microfocus COBOL under SCO Unix. When a file is record is
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Mark,

What you are doing is as NO-NO. Not the answer to your question but
check out the previous thread "Record Locking while validating records".
As you will see - your situation occurred with a system in Australia !

I think Randall Bart made the best point - put a time-stamp on records
and perhaps use that same-time stamp to clear/unlock  the record if the
user is not responding in a suitable timescale. If you have diffculty
picking up the thread check it out on deja.com.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking - Who has locked it

- **From:** spanna2000@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7updom$kn3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380F5A5A.87D749D7@home.com>`

```
Ok. I take the point of that thread i.e. by application design there
are other ways to track multiple users working with the same but...

This system is getting into it's 20th year and doesn't have the benefit
of CICS or any other transaction processing environment. We're not
going to rewrite it - it mostly does the job - very well in fact, it's
fast reliable and _usually_ easy to maintain.

However, operator X (out of a possible 9999) has locked the customer
account record. The system won't allow any further access to that
record. Operator X has gone walkabout.

The operating system or EXTFH knows which operator has the lock, if we
could just find out who has the lock we could send somebody round to
unlock the process.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking - Who has locked it

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VTYP3.38$pD5.2793@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380F5A5A.87D749D7@home.com> <7updom$kn3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We recently had a loooong thread on this "locking a record during lunch
break" issue, which might be worth reviewing.

What was not covered was using a logical lock. If the application used a
logical lock (i.e., wrote a marker into the file indicating an "in use"
status) there's no reason you could not store the user-ID who currently has
locked the record, too.

Solves many problems at once.

My $0.02
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking - Who has locked it

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uqh18$99n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380F5A5A.87D749D7@home.com> <7updom$kn3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
spanna2000@my-deja.com wrote:

: The operating system or EXTFH knows which operator has the lock, if we
: could just find out who has the lock we could send somebody round to
: unlock the process.

You are making an unwarranted assumption that 'the operating
system or EXTFH' _knows_ which operator has the lock.

Certainly they know what is locked and when an unlock is
requested they probably can confirm that it is the same
process that requested the lock in the first place, but that
does _not_ mean that they store the process id let alone
the operator.

It may be, for example, that when a lock is obtained the
system (eg EXTFH) returns a token that is used in the
rewrite or unlock.  It is unlikely, but this is a viable
mechanism that aoids knowing the actual process.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Record Locking - Who has locked it

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uvt22$35r$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<7ungfs$9sj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <380F5A5A.87D749D7@home.com> <7updom$kn3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7uqh18$99n$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Somebody (or something) somewhere knows because RM has a call to get the PID
for locks.

>I think Randall Bart made the best point - put a time-stamp on records
>and perhaps use that same-time stamp to clear/unlock  the record if the
>user is not responding in a suitable timescale. If you have diffculty
>picking up the thread check it out on deja.com.

happens in other nesgroups and on the job as well.  Once at work a
co-worker asked a question, I replied that I was ignorant and proceded
to tell him several points I was ignorant about.  The next day he
said, "You were exactly right."

point":

other people's wisdom, too.

## Thread: [OT] Medical Referral Info, Please — 8 msgs

### [OT] Medical Referral Info, Please

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AruP3.26$g5.1688@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Good day... this is strictly personal; those who wish to preserve
bandwidth might do so by replying to me by email.

Might anyone have a recommendation to, or method of finding other than The
Usual, a pulmonary oncologist in the New York City (preferably Manhattan)
area?  (adult, not paediatric)  I've been asked to employ all methods at
my disposal in helping to find one so that a second opinion might be
obtained.


Thanks much.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Medical Referral Info, Please

- **From:** David Ramsey <dramsey@voicecommander.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380F5064.979908F6@voicecommander.com>`
- **References:** `<AruP3.26$g5.1688@dfw-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> Good day... this is strictly personal; those who wish to preserve
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks much.

Doc, must it be New York? I would *strongly* urge someone with a serious
cancer problem that local doctors view pessimistically to contact M.D.
Anderson Cancer Center in Houston, Texas. They are among the very, very
best at what they do.

See their general URL: http://www.mdanderson.org/
and the thoracic oncology department:
http://www.mdanderson.org:80/centers/thoracic/specialists.htm

These people saved my life when national survival rates for my cancer
(mediastinal germ cell tumor) were in the range of 20%. Their treatment
program for a mediastinal germ cell tumor demonstrates a 90+% survival
rate. Other cancers often have similar survival rates at MD Anderson.

If the person for whom you are doing this search is in need, then I
*strongly* urge them to contact MD Anderson.

Good luck to them and I will say a prayer for this unknown (to me)
person.

> 
> DD
```

David Ramsey <dramsey@voicecommander.com

> Doc, must it be New York? I would *strongly* urge someone with a

<a id="t-d0a27fd5bc"></a>
## Thread: ACCEPT: How do I remove the underline? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### ACCEPT: How do I remove the underline?

- **From:** "Chiew Heng Wah" <heng7@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>`

```
Hi. I am new to this newsgroup.

Is there a clause associated with ACCEPT such that every time you accept the
field would be displayed as underlined?

(I tried "ACCEPT ..... WITH NO UNDERLINE" but that doesn't work.)

By the way, where is the FAQ for this NG located?

Thanks

Heng Wah.
```

#### ↳ Re: ACCEPT: How do I remove the underline?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7unddm$o61$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>`

```
What compiler??
Micro Focus has Accep/display rules, you can modify. The point is that the
entry field is marked to show you its position and size. You will not get
the underscores into your program.

Rgards
Ib
Chiew Heng Wah skrev i meddelelsen <380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>...
>Hi. I am new to this newsgroup.
>
>Is there a clause associated with ACCEPT such that every time you accept
the
>field would be displayed as underlined?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: ACCEPT: How do I remove the underline?

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7upd6b$3oj$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>`

```

Chiew Heng Wah wrote in message <380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>...
>Hi. I am new to this newsgroup.
>
>Is there a clause associated with ACCEPT such that every time you accept
the
>field would be displayed as underlined?
>


If the compiler is from Micro Focus, if you do an ACCEPT with attributes (eg
UNDERLINE) followed by an ACCEPT or DISPLAY without attributes, the run-time
just updates the text so the previous attributes remain. A simple way to
force the attributes to be updated is by specifying a colour eg
FOREGROUND-COLOR 7 (white).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ACCEPT: How do I remove the underline?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uru05$j74$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my> <7upd6b$3oj$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
    Do the underlines go away when you enter data (even spaces)?  If yes, it
might be the prompt clause.


Gael Wilson <gael.wilson@merant.com> wrote in message
news:7upd6b$3oj$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk...
>
> Chiew Heng Wah wrote in message <380f2ec2.0@news.tm.net.my>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> If the compiler is from Micro Focus, if you do an ACCEPT with attributes
(eg
> UNDERLINE) followed by an ACCEPT or DISPLAY without attributes, the
run-time
> just updates the text so the previous attributes remain. A simple way to
> force the attributes to be updated is by specifying a colour eg
> FOREGROUND-COLOR 7 (white).
>

_(thread depth: 4)_
