- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3810EA62.664B20CA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3808AA52.3C895929@netflow.es> <qw3O3.3294$8M3.32768@news3.mia> <7un30o$odu$1@wanadoo.fr> <zEKP3.8215$lK.141211@news2.mia> <380F9883.39F47321@home.com> <3810d192.31953130@news.enter.net>`

```


Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> Actually, I couldn't.  It took every milligram of energy in my being
> in order to send you enough subliminal messages to have the desired
> effect on your superior intellect!
> 
> It exhausted me and I have not been able to send any more since!

Roberto,

Assuming it wasn't totally said in jest "your superior intellect" - I
have to decline. I am a very ordinary being, maybe a little analytical
which attracts me to computing. A little philosophical in thought
perhaps, but I'm not going to come up with some new concept which will
stun the world. And in the sciences I rate a big ZERO !

I owe a huge tribute to my teachers, (those same Christian Brothers
who took off their shoes and socks and used to stomp the grapes in the
Napa Valley - leading to Christian Brothers wines). They brought my
favourite topics to life - History and English Literature. That coupled
with encouragement from a young friend of my mother who introduced me to
classics like Thackery's "Henry Esmond" or Rolf Boldrewood's "Robbery
Under Arms" - I've never been Down Under but I can visualize it so
clearly, (at least as it was), from Boldrewood's book.

And perhaps the only person on this planet - I didn't like the movie
"The English Patient", and the book even less. I think there is far more
likelihood that I will take a shot at using SP2 before I pick up another
book penned by the same Canadian/Sri-Lankan author, (Booker prize or
not) <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

                                       2

ibis

======================================================
i.bis              V      6 1 FUT  ACTIVE  IND 2 S X
eo, ire, ivi(ii), itus
go, walk; march, advance; pass; flow; pass (time); 
ride; sail;

======================================================
redi.bis           V      6 1 FUT  ACTIVE  IND 2 S X
redeo, redire, redivi(ii), reditus
return, go back, give back; fall back on, revert to; 
respond, pay back;
======================================================
numquam            ADV    POS
numquam
never;
======================================================
peri.bis           V      6 1 FUT  ACTIVE  IND 2 S X
pereo, perire, perivi(ii), peritus
die, pass away; be ruined, be destroyed; go to waste;
======================================================

igor@euro-soft.it

7umijl$8rr$2@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it...

#### ↳ Re: VB e Cobol

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-10-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7un262$ofv$1@duke.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<7umijl$8rr$2@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
I hope you can understand (my brand of) English.
You should state what compiler/runtime generated the files you are trying to
write with VB.
As far as I know, almost every compiler manufacturer has it's own
proprietary filesystem.

There are two possibilites:

A) Your filesystem has some kind of ODBC driver, therefore you can treat
your Cobol files as if they were a (Sql) Database (some limitations apply).

B) You develop a module in Cobol to perform file i-o and call it from VB (if
possible).

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft

ACI Leasing S.p.A. <leasing@lucca.aci.it> wrote in message
news:7umijl$8rr$2@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it...

>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

```

<a id="t-2f2b3b212d"></a>
## Thread: Input File (READ) — 6 this month / 7 total

### Input File (READ)

- **From:** ladybug44@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi... I am a beginner at writing Cobol, and for my class, we need to
input a file.  The file consists of many records, that look like this:
xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx xx xx xx xx xx xx
xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx xx xx
xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx xx xx xx xx
xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx
(alphanumeric)
Now, the bunch of x(2)'s at the end need to be read into a table, but i
will not know how many x(2)'s will be in the table. It will change for
each record.
So far, I have code written that goes something like this (in general
terms):
FD  INPUT-FILE.
01  INPUT-RECORD.
    05  FILLER                 PIC X(9).
    05  FILLER                 PIC X(4).
    05  WHAT-I-NEED OCCURS 5 TIMES
        INDEXED BY NEED-INDEX.
        10  IR-NUMBER          PIC X(2).
        10  IR-ANOTHER-NUM     PIC X(2).
This is where i get confused:  There can be as little as nothing past
the FILLERs, or there can be up to 5 sets of WHAT-I-NEED.  However, it
does not read the input right.  Is there something I am doing wrong?
When I check what is in the first FILLER, it keeps shifting over 2, each
record.  The first record will read it in right, but from then on, all
of it is messed up.  Should I do this instead(it doesn't seem to fix
the problem):
     05  WHAT-I-NEED OCCURS 5 TIMES
         DEPENDING ON SOME-NUMBER
         INDEXED BY NEED-INDEX.
And somehow keep tract of how many iterations of WHAT-I-NEED is input in
each record?
I am having a hard time explaining this, and I am not trying to get
anyone to do my homework for me, but if someone could point me in the
right direction, I would be extremely grateful.
Thank you,
Elizabeth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Input File (READ)

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DC8Q3.479$Lm1.28301@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Assumption: Each 'Record' in your file is terminated by
something the program (internally) recognizes as a
delimiter (usually a carriage-return and/or line-feed).
Otherwise, you have streaming input, like a teletype
machine (which COBOL is loathe to handle) OR you
have variable length records.

Rule: When an input record is shorter than the maximum
defined record length, the program fills the remainder of
record definition with spaces.

Assuming live data cannot
be spaces, you could loop through all five (or more)
occurrences looking for a data element of space and,
having found it, reduce the index by one to get the last
"live" data item.

<ladybug44@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi... I am a beginner at writing Cobol, and for my class, we need to
> input a file.  The file consists of many records, that look like
this:
> xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx xx xx xx xx xx xx
> xxxxxxxxx xxxx xx xx xx xx
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now, the bunch of x(2)'s at the end need to be read into a table,
but i
> will not know how many x(2)'s will be in the table. It will change
for
> each record.
> So far, I have code written that goes something like this (in
general
> terms):
> FD  INPUT-FILE.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> This is where i get confused:  There can be as little as nothing
past
> the FILLERs, or there can be up to 5 sets of WHAT-I-NEED.  However,
it
> does not read the input right.  Is there something I am doing wrong?
> When I check what is in the first FILLER, it keeps shifting over 2,
each
> record.  The first record will read it in right, but from then on,
all
> of it is messed up.  Should I do this instead(it doesn't seem to fix
> the problem):
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And somehow keep tract of how many iterations of WHAT-I-NEED is
input in
> each record?

You could do this, but I assumed the file layout was provided. If you
create the file, you can certainly add a field indicating to
subsequent
programs how many data elements follow within the record. .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Input File (READ)

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7us5bt$b68$1@duke.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <DC8Q3.479$Lm1.28301@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```

Jerry P <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:DC8Q3.479$Lm1.28301@typhoon01.swbell.net...
[snip...]
> Rule: When an input record is shorter than the maximum
> defined record length, the program fills the remainder of
> record definition with spaces.
>

Im my experience, when reading a line sequential file with a record
definition of (say) 1 to 510 chars, all I get is the actual data until the
end-of-record indicator (LF or CR+LF). The ramaining bytes stay untouched,
presumably with the result of the previsous read. Therefore I consider a
good practice to initialize the bigger record definition prior to read the
file.

Runtime/Compiler : R/M Cobol 6.09

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft
```

#### ↳ Re: Input File (READ)

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ushsf$nhs$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,
If your input file is line sequential, you should make some minor changes to
your program.

You will never know in advance, how many bytes you get (the length is the
number bytes from start to x'0D' x'0A' ).

Keep things simple. Read first and decide what to do with the data after
that. Her is my suggestion:

FD  INPUT-FILE.
01  INPUT-RECORD                   PIC X(255).

In Working-Storage:

01  Ws-Input.
       05  Filler                               Pic x(15).
       05  Data-I-Might-Need.
             07 Need-element          Occurs 80 times.
                    09 IR-Number         Pic X(2).
                    09 Filler                   Pic X.

The idea behind this is that records read can be examined (validated) as you
like.
Some compilers will not initialize Input-Record. If that should cause
problems,  move all spaces to Input-Record before read.

After read, move Input-Record to WS-Input and decide what to do.

The record size should be adjusted according to the actual max. record size.

Later on, you can try read into WS-input (Some Cobol-dialects will not allow
you to define fillers in the Input-Record).

Regards
Ib



ladybug44@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi... I am a beginner at writing Cobol, and for my class, we need to
>input a file.  The file consists of many records, that look like this:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

Thank you everyone for responding... I finally figured out all I needed
was a filler for the spaces at the end of each record, and that was
making all of my input-record shift by two.  Thanks again!
E


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

#### ↳ Re: Input File (READ)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7usipd$433$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 If the file is line sequential, which would be the norm for this type of
data, then Cobol will fill the rest of the line with space. In a case like
that, you can simply read the lines and check to see if you got data or
spaces.

You may be running into a problem because of your record being too short. If
your description is longer than the physical record, then Cobol will blank
fill on a line sequential file. If it is too short, however, the unread
portion of the record can carry over into the next read.  That may be your
problem.  I would try sticking a filler at the end.

The code, compared to the "XX"s that you drew, is also wrong. You have no
space between the x(2) fields.  IE:

    05  WHAT-I-NEED OCCURS 5 TIMES.
        10  IR-NUMBER          PIC X(2).
         10 filler                        picture x.
    05 maybe-a-filler            picture x(a bunch).

   The "Indexed by" clause just obscures the issue, and is not needed.  You
can reference the data with a simple subscript.


ladybug44@my-deja.com wrote in message <7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi... I am a beginner at writing Cobol, and for my class, we need to
>input a file.  The file consists of many records, that look like this:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```

Steve Kalemkiewicz wrote in message ...
>Can the Declaratives after the Procedure Division Header handle any other
>errors besides file errors?

Not in the current COBOL standard.

The draft of the next COBOL standard does allow
Declaratives for numerous other errors and exceptions.
------------------
Rick Smith

[INPUT | I-O | OUTPUT | [Named Files] ]

END-DECLARATIVES.

#### ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net>`

```
Oh, another thing....  Are the declaritives really necessary?  In other
words, if an application is coded without any bugs (at least file related),
how often would it really have to execute them?  So does it pay to code them
at all?

-SPK
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fHZQ3.105$fQ1.6253@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net> <huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`

```
Steve Kalemkiewicz wrote in message ...
>Oh, another thing....  Are the [DECLARATIVES] really necessary?  In other
>words, if an application is coded without any bugs (at least file related),
>how often would it really have to execute them?  So does it pay to code
them
>at all?
>


Mostly you use them to handle, under program control, situations which the
operating system normally comandeers and a COBOL program would not be coded
to handle on a file-by-file basis.

Example: hard parity errors, physical boundary violations (in some OSs, the
max size is determined when the file is created and it cannot be expanded
without re-creating the entire file), insufficient disk space, or other
situations where you do not care to code some handler for a non-success FILE
STATUS.

For example, if you work with sequential files, you probably interrogate
file status for 'at end' (status = '10'); with indexed files you might
interrogate 'invalid key' (status='23') . But what if the status is some
weird hardware error?   Depending on your program, it may be possible to
recover from that error with operator intervention. The DECLARATIVES can
give your program a 'hook' to prompt an operator after all the 'reasonable'
file errors have been handled in your program.

Example: insufficient disk space. If you are writing a report file and you
get this error, you could use the DECLARATIVES to:

1. Prompt the operator to remove some files from the disk to make space, (or
even for a "new disk ID")
2. Returning to your program, resume work with the increased space or on the
new pack.

You can use this technique, coded ONCE in the DECLARATIVES, and it will work
for each file in your program specified in the DECLARATIVES paragraph.

MCM




MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38146892.780A66C9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net> <huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`

```
Steve Kalemkiewicz wrote:
> 
> Oh, another thing....  Are the declaritives really necessary?  In other
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> -SPK

I have used them maybe 5 times.  Of course, I generally am more
concerned with databases than files, but standard file handling
techniques work just fine most of the time.

Besides, I LOVE not having sections in my programs, not that sorts don't
require them, the only PROCEDURE DIVISION requirement for sections is
the declaratives.

But put in more error handling conditions that it can handle, and I will
gladly use declaratives.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v28tk$m23$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net> <huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`

```
Steve Kalemkiewicz <senseisk@hotmail.com> wrote:
: Oh, another thing....  Are the declaritives really necessary?  In other
: words, if an application is coded without any bugs (at least file related),
: how often would it really have to execute them?  So does it pay to code them
: at all?

Declaratives are not necessary at all.  One can do checking
after each file action in the body of the program.  Just
make sure that FILE-STATUS is specified for each file and
check this after every OPEN, READ, WRITE, etc.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38150f38.93595852@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net> <huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`

```
On Mon, 25 Oct 1999 01:36:13 GMT, "Steve Kalemkiewicz"
<senseisk@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Oh, another thing....  Are the declaritives really necessary?  In other
>words, if an application is coded without any bugs (at least file related),
>how often would it really have to execute them?  So does it pay to code them
>at all?

I'm of mixed opinion.

When I started with COBOL we coded dummy declaratives - because you
HAD to code either "Invalid Key" clauses or declaratives.  The '85
standard still requires this, although most compilers do not, and the
next standard does not - detecting file errors is entirely up to you.


I do pretty well with file status values.  However, the code at work
uses declaratives extensively - and it works very well as a catch all
for file errors that might otherwise go undetected.  They seem
tedious, but I think they can be a good thing.

On the other side of the coin - when I write a brand new program - I
don't use them.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Declaritives

- **From:** bgwillia@vcu.edu (Boyce G. Williams, Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381a0b7a.20783808@usenet.acw.vcu.edu>`
- **References:** `<yCIQ3.5205$pJ3.342305@news.flash.net> <huOQ3.14$21.1971@news.flash.net>`

```
On Mon, 25 Oct 1999 01:36:13 GMT, "Steve Kalemkiewicz"
<senseisk@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Oh, another thing....  Are the declaritives really necessary?  In other
>words, if an application is coded without any bugs (at least file related),
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>-SPK

I use my declaritives to replace the old "ready trace" of IBM OS/VS
fame.  In production mode, I leave my code in place like this:

007800*
007900 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
008000*
008100*
008200 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
008300*
008400*SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-370 WITH DEBUGGING MODE.
008500 SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-370.

   .
   . 
   .

044800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
044900D
045000D
045100DDECLARATIVES.
045200D
045300DAA-DEBUG SECTION.
045400D    USE FOR DEBUGGING ALL PROCEDURES.
045500D
045600DAA-DEBUG-LINE.
045700D    DISPLAY DEBUG-ITEM.
045800D
045900DEND DECLARATIVES.
046000*
046100*
046200 000-START-PROGRAM-RUN.
046300*

If I need to do a "ready trace", all I have to do is swap the "*"
between my "SOURCE-COMPUTER" lines and recompile.  When finished, swap
it back.
```

<a id="t-529283e1ec"></a>
## Thread: Ah, youth! — 24 msgs

### Ah, youth!

- **From:** Ed Stevens <ed.stevens@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com>`

```
We have some young (early to mid-twenties) consultants working on
helping us develop some in-house, browser based apps for our company
intranet, using the MS Interdev tools.  Most of the data is in Oracle
databases on the servers and the rest is in a DB2 database on the
mainframe.  I was musing out loud that perhaps, since we were
increasingly going to the DB2 databases, perhaps we should just scrap
the Oracle and put all the data back on DB2.  The young expert looked
at me in amazement and said (in all seriousness)  "do you think the
mainframe could handle the load?"  (BTW, the total load from these
apps probably won't go more than a few hundred - certainly well below
a thousand -- per minute.)  I tried to explain to him that we could
drive our servers (the fattest of which contains 4 x 400mhz Pentium
Xeons) to their knees well before the OS-390 even registered a blip.


I don't think he believed me ....
Ed Stevens/TN
```

I gotta ask... how can someone in that age group be labelled a 'consultant?'

Contractor, specialist, temporary, sure; call me old-fashioned, but I still
think that the appellation 'consultant' should be reserved for those who can
_apply_ something (in this case, information technology) to "real" business
situations.
```

Back in the 60's I was being sold as a consultant after a year or so of
assembler programming, (FAS, PLAN, BAL).

Mind you we were good in those days.:-)

Rick

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T6aR3.2097$CS3.82036@viper>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
>I gotta ask... how can someone in that age group be labelled a
'consultant?'


That depends on who you are working for. When I was 26...a mere 5 years
ago...I got my first job as a "software consultant". Then I didn't know my
ass from a gO tO statement. But because I came from a small town and a small
college with little to no job experience to offer, I was hired as a
consultant. That is honestly what my company called it. When I acted as a
consultant, making suggestions as to how to clean up such evil things as GO
TO statements and the spaghetti code they inevitably cause, I was nearly
fired. Several times. I never gave up on that one but somehow I kept my job,
although I was moved around quite a bit. Due to that I have more expereience
than most at my age, at least with mainframes.

Age has nothing to do with a title. Title is just a GOTO label manufatured
out of the ether by managers to label us and limit our salary.

Professionals recognise experience and treat those with it as such. No title
will ever give you experience. But in most cases experience earns you a
title.

Besides if we didn't have small minded programmers we wouldn't be making so
much money off this Y2K "bug".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LGhR3.1941$Uw.21268@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <T6aR3.2097$CS3.82036@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
>
>Besides if we didn't have small minded programmers we wouldn't be
>making so much money off this Y2K "bug".

Duh!  You just revealed your ignorance of the programming conditions
20 to 30 years ago. ;-)  Before the early to mid 80's, it *was* the
correct thing to do to use 2 digit years in most situations.  Anyone
born since then are like those of us who did not live through the
Great Depression.  It is simply impossible for you to understand how
severely limited computer space was in those days.  The error was
that management did not start spending the money to correct it after
storage became available to make that practice obsolete.  Programs
written since the mid to later 80's should have been written Y2K
compliant.  Unfortunately, most new programming in business is to
enhance and extend already existent applications, when field format
compatibility is an issue.  Again, management failed to allocate the
necessary resources.  Remember, programmers write the code, but it
is management that sets the priorities. :-)

And one other point, the computer industry has been a fast changing
field ever since it was born.  Anyone who tells you that in 1970
they had evidence to suggest that source code written then would
still be operational in 30 years is less than honest.  And for all
languages except COBOL, there is virtually none.  Even the language
C is less than 30 years old, and has changed enormously.  Visual
Basic is only 8 years old, and has changed enormously.  Do you
think Visual Basic and Java will even be around in 30 years?  I
doubt it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37KR3.23$iS.1594@viper>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <T6aR3.2097$CS3.82036@viper> <LGhR3.1941$Uw.21268@news3.mia>`

```
>Duh!  You just revealed your ignorance of the programming conditions
>20 to 30 years ago. ;-)  Before the early to mid 80's, it *was* the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>severely limited computer space was in those days.  The error was


I agree with you. At the time the programs were written it was the correct
thing to do. But many years have passed. Programmers have a responsibility
to try to convince business to increase storage and fix such problems. 30
years is an extremely long time to let such a problem exist and continue to
cause problems. Unfortunately the way most of the businesses I have worked
at are structured to continue this misconception. The misconception is that
changing the code will be too expensive. And that is where they left it for
some 10-20 years. Little did they know that spending a few hundred grand 20
years ago would have saved millions in consultant charges and soon to be
lawsuits. I have seen time and again people not spending the time or money
to fix problems they know for a fact will cause much more severe problems in
the future. Simply doing what you are told by the business people is not
enough. A great deal of effort is needed by all of us to prevent these
problems from occurring in the future.

Unlike stacking boxes in a warehouse, programming has many areas where
benefits can not be easily translated into profit. We must keep trying to
convince the business people of the error of their ways.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XKYR3.7223$l22.55770@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <T6aR3.2097$CS3.82036@viper> <LGhR3.1941$Uw.21268@news3.mia> <37KR3.23$iS.1594@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
>
>I agree with you. At the time the programs were written it was the correct
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>cause problems.

I agree with you that programmers should have been raising the alarm
to management.

Actually, the storage situation only began to improve much about 15
years ago.  Remember, the IBM AT was released with only a 20MB hard
drive in 1984, and it was considered a state of the art Mercedes Benz
class PC.  I immediately bought one (my first hard drive), but knew
from the start that I couldn't load all my stuff on it, because of
space limitations.  I now easily load all of my stuff on my hard
drive, with gigabytes to burn. :-)  Mainframe hardware is limited by
the same technology constraints as PCs, just at a higher level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3816a770.4914737@news2.ibm.net>`

```
On Wed, 27 Oct 1999 08:12:25 GMT, vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
wrote:

>On Mon, 25 Oct 1999 13:49:08 GMT, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>And no, they can't apply any knowledge to anything

In their defense - if I were one of them, and offered such a position,
I might take it.  I would be honest about my knowledge on the job, but
my goodness, companies are HAPPILY paying for this.  But tell them you
have 15+ years of experience, and they don't even WAIT to hear your
price, the automatic assumption is that you will either be too jaded
or to expensive to be valuable.  

As an example, Jalepeno pepper companies would not make them if no one
bought them.  Same is true of $2500.00 per day consultants.  

>         The difference between Genius and Stupidity is that Genius has it's limits

So many great quotes here.  Can I borrow that one sometime?

One of my co-workers once used a modified famous quote when someone
suggested we move everything to one common language and database.

"Conformity is the hobgoblin of the small mind".

---

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wFDR3.566$it.6533@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <odZQ3.42$fQ1.5113@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3816a770.4914737@news2.ibm.net> <b1DR3.87$lG6.5315@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:b1DR3.87$lG6.5315@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
>
> Volker Bandke wrote in message <3816a770.4914737@news2.ibm.net>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >May I call you back to the real world now, please?  I know of
"consultants"
> from a large
> >company ... and their company charges 2500 Dollars per day for them!
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am reminded of a story that I heard round about the time I was a member of
'that age group'...

Henry Ford was having problems with one of the electrical generators that
was required to keep his assembly line running. The local serviceman was
unable to resolve the problem so Ford, feeling a little stressed, because
his company was losing thousands of dollars a day, got on the phone to
George Westinghouse and told him to come and fix his <expletive deleted>
generator.

Some time later George showed up and asked a few questions. Five minutes
later he pulled out a piece of chalk and draw a big 'X' on one panel. He
said to the local serviceman, "Replace the coil behind this panel". He left
immediately, being a rather busy person himself.

A few days later an invoice from Westinghouse arrived on Henry Ford's desk
for $1000 to cover consulting costs. $1000 was quite a lot of money in those
days. Ford was outraged. He got on the phone to George again and demanded an
itemized accounting for the $1000.

The next day (mail was fast in those days) an itemized invoice arrived on
Henry Ford's desk:

    Chalk mark    $1
    Knowing where to put chalk mark    $999

Ford paid the invoice.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Jason Bailey" <jayandtonya@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vr2R3.351$ET3.11298@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com>`

```
I am 26 and learned COBOL as my first language (not including BASIC at age
12 on a Tandy home PC).  I also know C, Java, VB, VBA, and SQL.  My
experience in COBOL has vastly improved my ability to apply software
solutions to business.

I am one of the fiercest defenders of COBOL in a group of programmers who'd
rather be writing OpenGL code than business logic.  Many of my colleagues
(who are older than me, I shoud add) believe COBOL should be dumped in place
of VB or Java.  Can you imagine?!  Our employer is an insurance company with
an enormous investment in terminal-based COBOL.  When it came time to
upgrade our current system, most of my cohorts were espousing the virtues of
COM-based distributed apps and that we should go in that direction
(completely abandoning years of development and refining of our current
system).  I was fairly alone in supporting GUI-based COBOL like NetExpress
or AcuCOBOL.  In the end, however, it was my side's demonstration of lower
TCO and flatter development curve that one the day.  We are now developing a
new system using our old COBOL business logic with a fresh, Windows
front-end.

Don't paint with a broad brush when labelling the young.  There are some of
us who have our "heads on straight" and can see past the "Oooo, ain't it
cool?!" mentality of the new technologies to focus on what can get the job
done the most efficiently, effectively, and with the fastest ROI.

Jason
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v2dnh$63t$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <vr2R3.351$ET3.11298@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
When I said that I wouldn't start something between generations was it
because I know some dedicated young men and women. What you are saying is
very similar to what I could expect from my young friends.

But - you have met the 'youngsters' I wrote about. People who know
everything better and preach their 'wisdom' to the world. I still beleive
that the real wisdom comes through expierience - by allowing yourself to be
wrong from time to time.

My mother once said to me - when I was young  - if you need to laugh, then
look in the mirror. Many young people look in the mirror and admire the view
instead.

Please don't bother the negative remarks in this thread. Obviously, you are
more a part of our mature team.

Best regards
Ib

a

#### ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v01hs$f1q$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com>`

```
We must forgive the young generation. They will learn, but only when they
need to. I do not invite to a long discussion between generations, but the
huge pile of work (and money) in the IT-business has done something to us
all. We do not need to learn to earn our living, and I think that the young
generation will be surprised in a couple of years. Technology and language
standards are changing rapidly, and I expect to educate myself further
because the decades in this business have shown that the big money are where
the new stuff is.

Most of the youngsters are highly speciallized, effective and competent - no
doubt about that, but aren't they just a little bit narrow-minded. Views and
ways different from their own well defined (MS- or Unix-) world 'sucks'. I
feel a little sorry, and I hope that I am wrong.

Regards
Ib


Ed Stevens skrev i meddelelsen ...
>We have some young (early to mid-twenties) consultants working on
>helping us develop some in-house, browser based apps for our company
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ed Stevens/TN
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3814598A.C2C8B595@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <7v01hs$f1q$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:

> Most of the youngsters are highly speciallized, effective and competent - no
> doubt about that, but aren't they just a little bit narrow-minded. Views and
> ways different from their own well defined (MS- or Unix-) world 'sucks'. I
> feel a little sorry, and I hope that I am wrong.

Pick a generation - that generalization made about youth has always had
a great deal of validity.  But for some of us, wisdom has come with
maturation, and it will come with the current youth as well.

My parents were more aware of my generation's singers than I am of the
current generation's though.  I find the current generation's clothing
to be at least as ugly as my grandparents found my clothing. 
Admittedly, the current youth who want to shock have to go to much
greater extremes than my generation did.

But without these extremes - even comparing the hard working youth of
today with the hard working youth who were my peers - we see similar
trends and tendencies (within the changing environments).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Ah, youth!

- **From:** "Albert Ratzlaff R." <albert@conexion.com.py>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38143657.C53DF023@conexion.com.py>`
- **References:** `<UWATOAKtQAOT8VSZX3xTPN6EmnzN@4ax.com> <7v01hs$f1q$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> We must forgive the young generation. They will learn, but only when they
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A lot of them (youngsters) feel great when they can output a pie chart
with data from your COBOL system. See! you can't do that in COBOL!
Actually, I could if I wanted to. But I am busier taking care that the
data that gets into the system is correct, consistent, and up-to-date.
That involves a lot more than just using the already available and
validated data for fancy reports.
But to be honest, most of these young guys do change there mind after
gaining a bit of experience in real world applications, and slowly but
surely come to respect what has been done before them.

However, that is not an excuse for us not to learn the new stuff.

Regards
Albert Ratzlaff
```

## Thread: Q: Error Handling — 9 msgs

### Q: Error Handling

- **From:** "Desmond Johnson" <crud-mucosa@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Greetings,

  I am a mid-level COBOL programmer still trying to learn this language. I
am working on a project where I need to have my program return an error code
number so that the JCL that runs it can selectively execute other programs
associated with it. My question: How do I get COBOL to return a programmer
defined error code number?

  Thanks,
 Crud
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: Error Handling

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991024135911.06363.00000209@ngol07.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>, "Desmond Johnson"
<crud-mucosa@worldnet.att.net> writes:

> I am a mid-level COBOL programmer still trying to learn this language. I
>am working on a project where I need to have my program return an error code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the IBM MainFrame environment there is a 'special register/reserved field'
RETURN-CODE that you can move a number to which is passed to the 
JCL as the closing COND CODE.   The interla definition of the field S9(4) Comp.

Would this be what you are looking for?
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: Error Handling

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381360BB.5A3D9325@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
If you are using an IBM COBOL on MVS, OS390 or VM, MOVE value to
RETURN-CODE where RETURN-CODE is an IBM extension defined as S9(4) COMP
(or BINARY in COBOL II and beyond).  Value can be either a constant or a
numeric field.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services  

Desmond Johnson wrote:
> 
> Greetings,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Crud
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: Error Handling

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38136905.56984985@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Sun, 24 Oct 1999 12:29:42 -0400, "Desmond Johnson"
<crud-mucosa@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>Greetings,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Just move a number to the reserved word Return-Code.

For example, in some jobs I have :  Move 12 to Return-Code

This sets the condition code to 12 for the program execution step.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Error Handling

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v022a$g4c$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Before you go into a great number of different return-codes, check the
installation standards. Cond codes can be used to control the batch-flow,
and each installation can be different in their use of CC's.

I know of installations, where CC= 4 is a warning (the program handled the
error) while CC=4 in other installations simply stops the flow.

regards
Ib

#### ↳ Re: Q: Error Handling

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gk181sgp3p3r8uj9cn27qcfplgc0jmpp0o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net>`

```
On Sun, 24 Oct 1999 12:29:42 -0400 "Desmond Johnson"
<crud-mucosa@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

:>  I am a mid-level COBOL programmer still trying to learn this language. I
:>am working on a project where I need to have my program return an error code
:>number so that the JCL that runs it can selectively execute other programs
:>associated with it. My question: How do I get COBOL to return a programmer
:>defined error code number?

From: Language Reference COBOL for OS/390 & VM (SC26-9046-00)

1.1.3.5 RETURN-CODE
 
The RETURN-CODE special register can be used to pass information between
separately-compiled programs.
 
You can set the RETURN-CODE special register to pass a return code to the
program or the system before executing a STOP RUN statement.  It has the
implicit definition:
 
  01  RETURN-CODE GLOBAL PICTURE S9(4) USAGE BINARY VALUE ZERO
 
The following are examples of how to set the RETURN-CODE special register:
 
  COMPUTE RETURN-CODE = 8
 
or
 
  MOVE 8 to RETURN-CODE.
 
When control returns to the operating system, the special register contents
are returned as a user return code.  When used in nested programs, this
special register is implicitly defined in the outermost program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Q: Error Handling

- **From:** "Desmond Johnson" <crud-mucosa@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v2jm3$juc$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7uvc4c$1c6$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <gk181sgp3p3r8uj9cn27qcfplgc0jmpp0o@4ax.com>`

```
I got a good number of responses to this question.  Thanks to all who
took time to respond to me, you helped me find what I was looking for.

Thanks again,
--Crud
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:gk181sgp3p3r8uj9cn27qcfplgc0jmpp0o@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 24 Oct 1999 12:29:42 -0400 "Desmond Johnson"

programmer

>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> When control returns to the operating system, the special register
contents
> are returned as a user return code.  When used in nested programs, this
> special register is implicitly defined in the outermost program.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Director, Dissen Software, Bar & Grill - Israel

## Thread: COBOL and COM — 2 this month / 3 total

### COBOL and COM

- **From:** "David Lundeen" <dlund@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LY7R3.753$hP2.12538@news4.mia>`

```
Hi,

I need to call some Realia Cobol programs from VB and C++.  I'd like to do
it using COM.  If anyone has any experience doing this, or any information
on how (or if) this can be done, I'd appreciate the information.

Thanks.

David Lundeen
dlund@bellsouth.net
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL and COM

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vafmd$rfd$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<LY7R3.753$hP2.12538@news4.mia>`

```

There are companies that provide ActiveX controls that basically act an an
interface between COBOL and ActiveX compliant tools such as VB and C++.

The 2 I know of are Softis with a product called Louis (www.softis.is) and
Transoft (www.transoft.com).  I don't know if either of these work with
Realia though.

good luck
Gerald Moull

news:LY7R3.753$hP2.12538@news4.mia...

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: What���s the Compiler Runtime for?

- **From:** petwir@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v2enh$mcd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3814062e_3@news2.prserv.net>`

```
In article <3814062e_3@news2.prserv.net>,
  "Robert Schenk" <schenk@online-service.de> wrote:
> Sorry, for this question. I may be ot.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The "run time" components of any software language are subroutines used
by your program at the time that it executes.  e.g. a COBOL COMPUTE
statement may actually call upon run time subroutines to perform the
mathamatics.

Pete


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: What���s the Compiler Runtime for?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nnaR3.2103$CS3.82230@viper>`
- **References:** `<3814062e_3@news2.prserv.net>`

```
>Today the manufacture of a programm we use told us the Cobol Compiler
>Runtime is not supported for Y2K. What is the module exactly for?


Anytime you hear so many BUZZ words strung out together you have to look at
the title of the person making the statement. If their is BUSINESS anywhere
in the title you can almost be sure they do not know what they are talking
about.
VB has a RUNTIME MODULE which is called be the VB pseudo code it generates.
All standard VB compilers produce non functional code which to be functional
must call a RUNTIME module which performas all the basic functions of the
language.
COBOL produces functional and independant executable code which does not
require any runtime module to run. With possibly a few exceptions.

To be Y2K compliant, whether it be VB or COBOL all executable programs or
runtime modules called must use 4 digits to represent the year. They also
must never convert that date back to 2 didgits except to output it to a
report, which may still cause problems.

Earlier versions of COBOL are not Y2K compliant simply because they return a
2 digit year from their SYSTEM date. There was no date computations built
into earlier versions of COBOL other than returning the date from the system
clock.

Regardless of what language is used, if the date computations are not using
4 didgits for the year, or an equivalent representation, none of the code
can be considerred Y2K compliant unless it does not use dates in any manner.

What the manufaturer is trying to say is that,"We are not willing to make
our code Y2K compliant. You will have to find a way around our unwillingness
to fix our code. We will however continue to charge you for licensing and
support of our NON-Y2K compliant code."

He also could be saying that the code you guys are responsible for is not
Y2K compliant and they can't be held responsible for Y2K damages until you
guys fix your code.

I have never heard any COBOL executable code referred to as a COBOL COMPILER
RUNTIME. He also could be referring to your COBOL compiler. Whic as I
previously stated may not be Y2K compliant. However this can always call a
Y2K compliant application to retrieve a proper 4 digit year. Then as long as
the 4 digits are mantained throughout the computations and data storage
everything comes up roses.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: What���s the Compiler Runtime for?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NjhR3.35$4B.2601@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3814062e_3@news2.prserv.net> <nnaR3.2103$CS3.82230@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote in message ...
>
>VB has a RUNTIME MODULE which is called be the VB pseudo code it generates.
>All standard VB compilers produce non functional code which to be
functional
>must call a RUNTIME module which performas all the basic functions of the
>language.
>COBOL produces functional and independant executable code which does not
>require any runtime module to run. With possibly a few exceptions.
>

A few exceptions: Microfocus on PCs and Unix (if compiled that way); IBM
Mainframe COBOL.

Hint: these 'exceptions' are a LOT of the installed COBOL base!

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: What���s the Compiler Runtime for?

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qtpa1sob8cfjuusrd7f3nja3v09vt6fbib@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3814062e_3@news2.prserv.net>`

```
On Mon, 25 Oct 1999 10:17:54 +0200 "Robert Schenk" <schenk@online-service.de>
wrote:

:>Today the manufacture of a programm we use told us the Cobol Compiler
:>Runtime is not supported for Y2K. What is the module exactly for?

:>Thanks for a short discription.

Program written in high level languages are insulated from a lot of the
operating system communication required to have a program run, i.e. the stuff
an assembler programmer has to explicitly code.

The cobol run time will typically:

   Allocate storage and buffers
   Provide error handling
   Assist with more complicated verbs
   etc.

Now will this cause you trouble? Hard to say.

1. Are you using any of the assists to get date info? 

If so, what format is a Y2000+ date returned, and can you code around it?

2. Do any of the assists require correct date info?

In this case you might be in deep ^@%$^$%$ and you may have no choice but to
go to a different compiler. 
```

## Thread: Micro-Focus COBOL/2 Under Windows 95/98 — 5 msgs

### Micro-Focus COBOL/2 Under Windows 95/98

- **From:** "Mike A. Colledge" <mcolledg@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3814CE65.D6F2CFC7@ix.netcom.com>`

```

I recently inherited a system written in Micro-Focus COBOL/2 around
1994.  They have been running the programs with no problems in DOS and
Windows 3.1, but now they want to convert their computers to Windows 98.

I have not been able to make the old Micro-Focus programs run under
Windows 98.  I try to run them in the DOS window and I get the following
error message -
     LOAD FAILURE (157) ON FILE C:XXXXXXXX.EXE
This error says there is not enough program memory, so I tried running
them using the XM utility program.  Then I get the error message -
     Encountered unrecoverable system interrupt 0D error B8000 cs 042F
ip 0050

Is there any way to make these old programs run under Windows 98?  I've
tried looking at Merant's web site but it is useless.  Have they ever
thought of making their knowledgebase accessible?  I also called their
technical support people, but they wouldn't help unless I paid several
hundred dollars for a support contract.

We do plan to convert these programs (probably to Delphi 5) in the
future, but would like to be able to run them on Windows 98 in the
interim.  Any assistance anyone can offer would be gratly appreciated.


Mike A. Colledge
The Colledge Group, Ltd.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro-Focus COBOL/2 Under Windows 95/98

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991025185913.09005.00000632@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3814CE65.D6F2CFC7@ix.netcom.com>`

```
In article <3814CE65.D6F2CFC7@ix.netcom.com>, "Mike A. Colledge"
<mcolledg@ix.netcom.com> writes:

>
>I recently inherited a system written in Micro-Focus COBOL/2 around
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't know if this will help or not.  Assuming that the error message is
correct
about memory availability,  I would suggest doing a CHKDSK or MEM on the 
old DOS/Win3.1 system to see what you have for available memory.  Then do 
the same in the DosPrompt under Win98.  In all likelihood, you will have more
than 595K in the base 640K available under Win98.   SOME old programs don't 
like having more than 590K available in the base 640K.  you can get around this

by creating a copy of the DOSPROMPT.PIF and reducing the requested memory
amount to less than 600K and see how that works.

If the old program was making use of 'system calls', you may be out of luck as
several 'system level' calls have been removed or replaced with Win32 specific 
calls.

Hope that this helps narrow the focus on what may be your particular problems.
```

#### ↳ Re: Micro-Focus COBOL/2 Under Windows 95/98

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v3j6b$1rb$2@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3814CE65.D6F2CFC7@ix.netcom.com>`

```
    Which runtime are you using?  If it is reasonably recent (3.1, 3.2, 3.3,
3.4) you could try getting the "current" runtime (3.4) from MF.  This also
includes a more recent XM.   If it was compiled with a compiler much older
than ver 3 it might not work with newest runtime.

    Also consider the other XM switches, such as JKQ and R in addition to
"P" switches.


    If you want to run without XM, try optimizing the dos window.  You
should be able to get > 600K free memory when you execute the MEM command.

    Do the usual - himem, emm386, dos=high,umb.  Be conservative with
EMM386.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381511f1.94292388@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3814CE65.D6F2CFC7@ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Mon, 25 Oct 1999 14:40:54 -0700, "Mike A. Colledge"
<mcolledg@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>This error says there is not enough program memory, so I tried running
>them using the XM utility program.  Then I get the error message -
>     Encountered unrecoverable system interrupt 0D error B8000 cs 042F
>ip 0050
>

I think XM is the right way to go.  Try the XM switch settings.

IE:  SET XM=/P1 

Then /P2, etc through /P6

There are a myriad of other switches that XM understands, but this is
the most common one I have seen used to fix the 0D error under 32 bit
Windows.

<a id="t-f615392acb"></a>
## Thread: Mainframe Express - Redirecting TSO output — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframe Express - Redirecting TSO output

- **From:** Thomas Kleymann <thomas.kleymann@sdm.de>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ubt9nhe5f.fsf@sdm.de>`

```
Using Mainframe Express 2.0, I would like to redirect the TSO output
(Application Output - Mainframe Express TSO Emulation) to a file
(without modifying the Cobol sources). 

Would this be possible? How?

--Thomas Kleymann
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframe Express - Redirecting TSO output

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38158018.E23A0384@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<ubt9nhe5f.fsf@sdm.de>`

```
Hi Thomas.

I forwarded your question to a resident expert. His reply follows:

---- 8< --- Cut Here ------------------------------------------------
It depends what you mean by TSO output. 

1) If this means output from a program (i.e. via DISPLAY statements)
running under TSO, then you do exactly the same as what you would do on
the mainframe by issuing the following commands:

ALLOC F(SYSOUT)DA('USERID.MY.DATA') SHR REU
CALL MYPROG

The ALLOC command has the effect of associating the ddname SYSOUT with
the dataset 'USERID.MY.DATA'. By default under TSO, SYSOUT is associated
with the screen.

2) If this means capturing output from TSO commands, then, as you could
do on the mainframe, you could write a REXX EXEC that utilises the
OUTTRAP external function to capture command output into variables, and
then write these to a dataset via EXECIO.

3) If you just want a snapshot of the current contents of the screen,
you can right-click on the Application Output window and use the Edit
function to mark and copy the text to the clipboard. you would then open
up a new file in the editor and paste the contents of the clipboard into
it.
---- 8< --- Cut Here ------------------------------------------------

I hope this helps.
Cheers, Kev.

Thomas Kleymann wrote:
> 
> Using Mainframe Express 2.0, I would like to redirect the TSO output
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tel +49 89 63812-285,  Fax -150
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking in Fujitsu : Newbie Question

- **From:** "Nilo Paim" <npaim@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v32cd$lvu$1@srv4-poa.zaz.com.br>`
- **References:** `<7v20nj$qju$1@fraggle.esatclear.ie>`

```

John <liamNOSPAMsean@hotmail.com> escreveu nas not�cias de
mensagem:7v20nj$qju$1@fraggle.esatclear.ie...
> Hi!
> I'm just starting to learn Cobol and after I compile my code i get an
error
> when  linking.
> The message in DOS tells me...
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi,

If you are using command line, add option -M during compilation.

If you are using P-Staff, in compiler options specify option "MAIN".

The reason of the problem is that you must have one and only one main entry
point and you are not informing the compiler where is it.

HTH

Nilo npaim@zaz.com.br
```

<a id="t-e800b38754"></a>
## Thread: sct-3-6-1: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: new features — 1 msgs

### sct-3-6-1: CobolTransformer Data File Readers: new features

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3814D53A.B3133D6F@siber.com>`

```
Siber Systems is pleased to announce release 3-6-1
of our DataReader technology. Using this technoliogy,
you or your application can read Cobol data files 
directly, without writing a Cobol program to do it.

For more info look at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

The new features are:

* Date and Time recognition in record items.

For every data item you can now specify special date-time 
picture string that will tell DataAccess library that 
this data item should be interpreted as date-time and 
it will specify the format of this date-time data item.

Once data item is interpreted as date-time, you will be
able to reformat it to your liking using data output
facilities such as Crystal Reports generator.

Example: if you have numeric-edited data item that
stores date time as "98/12/31 23:59:59", it is 
automatically turned into numeric data item 981231235959.
You use date-time picture string YYMMDDHHNNSS
to cause this field to be interpreted as date-time.


* Data2Dbf converter.

This converter converts Cobol data files to dBaseIV DBF format.
While dBaseIV is not used much these days, this format
can be used as an intermediary format for importing Cobol data
into spreadsheets such as Excel and other databases such as Oracle.
Data2Dbf read FSC, MF and other Cobol data files.


* EBCDIC <-> ASCII translation.

Cobol Data File reader will be able to perform
EBCDIC <-> ASCII translation, so that mainframe
Cobol data files can be read on PC.


* DataAccess library API and manual released.

We finally formalized the API for DataAccess library.
Now you can license the DataAccess library from us
when you need to read data from Cobol files directly
into your C++ or C application. All read data is 
converted to C/C++-friendly data formats.


We also recap some of the important news from 
the previous DataReader technology releases:

* data2cr: Crystal Reports Reader for Cobol Data Files.
After you install the data2cr software (CR Data Source), 
your Seagate Software Crystal Reports report generator
will be able to read Fujitsu, Micro Focus and other
Cobol data files directly.

Just imagine: you will be generating elegant and informative 
reports from your Cobol data files directly, using 
powerful graphical user interface of Crystal Reports, 
instead of tediously programming report generation
directly in Cobol language.


* data2flat: Convert your Micro Focus, Fujitsu and other 
Cobol data files to comma-separated-values (CVS) text file.
This makes examining contents of you data files easy 
and it also helps a lot when importing Cobol data files
into other spreadsheets such as MS Excel or into databases
such as Oracle.

Best regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-7f4d4b7c00"></a>
## Thread: reading Unix Process ID (not PPID!) under MF-Cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### reading Unix Process ID (not PPID!) under MF-Cobol

- **From:** bernd@bigtimes.com (Bernd Lohner)
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3813cb89.4447581@news.panix.com>`

```
Hi,

does anyone know of a way how to read the Unix process ID (PID) NOT
THE PARENT ID PPID under MF-Cobol (either OCDS or Server Express)?

I need that ID to generate unique Files (or reuse a file if I am the
same process but a different Program) on a multi-processor machine.

I can read the PPID from the environment but that gives me trouble if
the same parent spawns off more than one child....

Ideas? Thoughts?

Please email me at bernd@bigtimes.com


-BL
```

#### ↳ Re: reading Unix Process ID (not PPID!) under MF-Cobol

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38140CAB.DC7CD9E5@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<3813cb89.4447581@news.panix.com>`

```
How about....

        working-storage section.
        01 mypid pic s9(9) comp-5.
        01 my pic x.
        procedure division.
                call "getpid" returning mypid
                display "MYPID : " mypid
                accept my
```

## Thread: looking for constructive criticisms — 28 msgs

### Re: looking for constructive criticisms

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
In article <SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>, sheps@bigpond.com 
says...
> I have attached (as a Word File) a program that I have just finished writing
> for an assignment...Tonight a few of my fellow students and I got together
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


 Hi Judy,

  One comment, I think your assumption of getting 32 records each and 
every time you execute this program is not very "safe". If you get more, 
less or none you will encounter some problems.

  You can set up a separate paragraph to read the input and then read the 
first record when you open the file. From there read all subsequent 
records from the bottom of paragraph B-LOAD-MASTER and test for 32 or end 
of file. 

 I bet there are other things that need to be "fixed" but it looks like 
your headed in the right direction. Keep up the good work!

Jeff
jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net
http://home.earthlink.net/~jeffreyfarkas/
```

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

- **Message-ID:** `<7v757m$fg3$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FIrR3.3011$hP2.33387@news4.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>You very quickly succeeded in provoking a number of irritated responses
>from some very responsible and helpful people who frequent this newsgroup.
>Enough so to raise my eyebrows.  Of course, you are probably right and
>all of them wrong.  ;-)

I thought I should avoid to enter this thread, but I have to...

To some extent I understand Judy.

Generally speaking;

That one does a mistake does not provide everyone else with a general
amnesty to do/say/behave in any way they want. Such response is the reason
we got wars on our little planet, without further comparison.

Sometimes it makes sense to be rough, like to the fellow that used a rather
rude language in his way of asking. I guess he did it because he believed it
to be the right culture.

When a question is asked in a polite manner without offense, it is my
opinion that it deserves a polite answer as well, no matter how "stupid" the
question might be.

I guess many of us (including myself) too easily forget that we our selves
once were newbies. That did not know the culture, expected behavior etc,
etc.

Disclaimer:
I am not saying this to pretend like I am the most polite and kind person of
the world, I have been using rough attitude without a cause too. Just
expressing my general view.

Cheesle
```

Cheesle wrote:
>
>When a question is asked in a polite manner without offense, it is my
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>etc.

I did not pay particularly close attention to this thread, so I just reread
the messages the messages, to make sure I hadn't missed something.  It only
confirmed my original impression, which is this.  Judy posted a request for
help, and attached a binary Word document of the source.  Richard Plinston
made what I thought was a very reasonable response, pointing out the problems
with that.  I repeat his comments here, verbatim:

Richard Plinston wrote:
> Why would anyone ever put source code as a 'Word File' ?  It might
> make it look pretty, but its functionality is completely lost.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> program ?

The only thing in there that could have been provocative was the way
Richard phrased the first sentence "Why would anyone...".  However,
he carefully explained his point, clearly tried to be helpful, and
even included a 'wink' to show his post was in good humor.  But Judy
responded with a vitriolic, practically bitter, response that was,
in my opinion, very much unjustified.  I repeat her comments, verbatim:

Judy Shepherd wrote:
> Not exactly the constructive criticisms that I was looking for.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> stand corrected.

From that point on, Judy began to take heat *for her attitude*.  I
could not find even one other critical message about her attaching a
binary file in a text newsgroup, but a number of kind, helpful posts.
If I simply missed them, please correct me.  In my opinion, Judy took
a hostile and arrogant attitude toward what was at worst a very mild
(and correct) criticism.  This was what generated all the heat, and
why I made my posts to this thread, in an attempt to help her see. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k6ZR3.7263$l22.56140@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FIrR3.3011$hP2.33387@news4.mia> <7v757m$fg3$1@news.cerf.net> <4qFR3.5124$qy6.49393@news2.mia> <7v81u1$r8s$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
>
>I still mean that we that are veterans could be better to show indulgence
>with newbies. Note that I am saying: we.

I agree.  I often wish we (collectively) were a bit less hasty to dump
on some of the naive questions we get, particularly from students.  I
don't care if somebody wants an answer to a COBOL related question
because they need to write a COBOL program for work or school, or if
they simply need the information for a research project of some kind,
or or simply curious.  All are learning.

In my first programming class, I was the very last person to 'see the
light'.  For several weeks I went through the motions, but I didn't
understand what I was doing, or why.  Finally, it dawned on me that I
was making it much too complex, and that the computer was much dumber
than I had thought.  I had seen too many 'computers' in movies and on
TV, I suppose. :-)  But once the light dawned, from then on, it was
like eating candy.

Some of the beginner questions we get remind me of myself at first.
They have been given a lot of information, but they have not yet been
able to assimilate it into a coherent 'vision' of what it is a program
actually does, and how the programming process works, or perhaps just
some aspect of the language.  That leap of perception may be a tiny
one for most, but for me it was a big one.  But no one since has ever
suggested that I was a less capable programmer because of it.  I would
like to see us be more willing to respond to those 'clueless' beginner
questions.  I think some of those 'do your own homework' questions are
because the person really doesn't know where to begin. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7va0bv$38n$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FIrR3.3011$hP2.33387@news4.mia> <7v757m$fg3$1@news.cerf.net> <4qFR3.5124$qy6.49393@news2.mia> <7v81u1$r8s$1@news.cerf.net> <k6ZR3.7263$l22.56140@news1.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote in message ...
>was making it much too complex, and that the computer was much dumber

O boy isn't that true! Still today, I experience stuff that I consider
complicated really is so basic when I finally understand it. This is however
not related to programming only, but to any science in my opinion.

>able to assimilate it into a coherent 'vision' of what it is a program

This ability takes years to catch. I don't know precisely when, but there
were one moment in time, I "woke up", and started thinking like a computer.
This may sound hilarious, however I believe it is true. To become a
professional (and then I mean really professional) programmer, at some level
you will have to start thinking like a computer.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fz7S3.8415$l22.68065@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FIrR3.3011$hP2.33387@news4.mia> <7v757m$fg3$1@news.cerf.net> <4qFR3.5124$qy6.49393@news2.mia> <7v81u1$r8s$1@news.cerf.net> <k6ZR3.7263$l22.56140@news1.mia> <7va0bv$38n$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
>
>This ability takes years to catch. I don't know precisely when, but there
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>you will have to start thinking like a computer.

I agree.  I think this applies to many fields of intellectual effort.
When I was young, I became deeply involved in chess for a time.  Even
when relaxing or having a conversation, I was constantly visualizing
chess positions and moves in my head.  To speak a language well, you
must learn to think in that language.  Somehow, you develop a model
of the computer function in your mind.  Then you can 'turn the crank',
and as you say, you begin to 'think like a computer'.  I started a
related thread recently about how programmers think through the
programming process.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3819A16E.9C87B11D@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <FIrR3.3011$hP2.33387@news4.mia> <7v757m$fg3$1@news.cerf.net> <4qFR3.5124$qy6.49393@news2.mia> <7v81u1$r8s$1@news.cerf.net> <k6ZR3.7263$l22.56140@news1.mia> <7va0bv$38n$1@news.cerf.net> <fz7S3.8415$l22.68065@news1.mia>`

```
It's amazing how easy programming is once you "get it".  I haven't quite
"got it" for OO programming - it's still work, but time and again, what
was once hard coding turned quickly into easy.  (not necessarily soon,
but when it turned, it turned quickly).

Structured COBOL just flows, without half thinking, for instance.

Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Cheesle wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming process.
```

Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> saying that you are finding that OO makes difficult coding easier?

I am still speaking OO the way I speak Spanish - stopping to think about
every sentence.  It hasn't turned the corner of being natural yet. 
Unfortunately, I am lazy enough that when I am not spending
$1500/quarter to force me to work, I don't get the practice I need to
turn the corner.  And I have never had an opportunity to explore OO for
Cobol.

Fortunately for me, the demand for mainframe cobol is virtually all
structured and not OO.
```

```
Well.  I am a woman, and I am in the beginning stages of learning how to
program in Cobol, yet the few times that I have posted, I have never
encountered anything that you have described.  I have only received
helpful hints and encouragement via the newsgroup and email.

However, I have followed this thread, and I do think that most of the
negative comments were merely responses from your own negative attitude.

~e

In article <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>,
  "Judy Shepherd" <sheps@bigpond.com> wrote:
> Absolutely dealing with others in the most civil way possible....by
not
> being condescending, not presuming they are stupid because they are
not as
> educated, by not compounding the image of the IT industry as a "boys"
> domain.....by making helpful suggestions....rather than sarcastic
comments
> ....sometimes the impersonal nature of the internet allows people to
suspend
> good manners.....I hope that I am never accused of that...
>
> Jude.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judy Shepherd" <sheps@bigpond.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jmNR3.5174$we.8356@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7v7kgj$e5p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Please be assured that Richard has received a very contrite apology from
myself....

I have been humbled, educated and frankly given a damned good
spanking....feeling severly brusied and chastened I respectfully ask that I
now be allowed to remove the scarlet "B" (for bitch) that has apparently
been indelibly stamped on my forehead...

a wiser and much more respectful.

Judy Shepherd.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7v9jgg$qb9$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7v7kgj$e5p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jmNR3.5174$we.8356@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
There were a few comments about "women's lib" that may well have contributed
to the antagonism that crept into the thread.

I am going to make a suggestion here (which I have done myself in this very
echo).

Each man should post a request for help under a female name, then under a
male name a few days later, and note the differences in responses. Then a
few should alter their posting habits.

It is quite an education.

Donald.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: No longer looking ....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38189717.EAAB3D7A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<SDYQ3.3505$we.5409@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <MPG.127f74f8f581806c98969c@news.earthlink.net> <eNiR3.4319$we.6470@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <4mjR3.2135$Uw.22938@news3.mia> <6yrR3.4458$we.6887@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7v7kgj$e5p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <jmNR3.5174$we.8356@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <7va0ff$38v$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> Judy Shepherd wrote in message ...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Remember; "Short is man's mind"

Judy, 

Enough with the sackcloth and ashes - Lent is only supposed to be 40
days a year - not a lifetime. Want a tip, think of the recipient when
asking a question, what will they interpret if you paraphrase ?

I sometimes (paraphrase), put things here and back comes "What do you
mean ?", "You conclude .....". I find that a bit snotty. It's a two-way
street and we can all learn without some answering in a combative style.
I just mused about the Screen Section and back came some really
interesting info.

Didn't realize you were an Ausie until the knight in shining white
armour from Sydney jumped in to your defence. Want some guidelines -
follow Ken Foskey's messages - he is always extremely helpful, and even
if he disagrees with you, has a nice way of putting it.

> >now be allowed to remove the scarlet "B" (for bitch) that has apparently
> >been indelibly stamped on my forehead...

Not to my mind. Erase that "B" for bitch. I just perceived a feisty
young lady; believe me, you are 'young' when you stack your time-sheet
up against mine.

And as regards communication - having done a very nice job producing a
program, some nut is going to ask you to change it, and you will say to
yourself, "Now why didn't that jerk tell me (communicate) what he really
wanted in the first place ?"

> I wish you good luck further in your studies.

Seconded.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
