## Thread: COBOL Sequential files — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL Sequential files

- **From:** Matthew Courtois <mcourtoi@san.rr.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38190406.956F6460@san.rr.com>`

```
I'm having to perform transformations on variable length cobol records
contained in a sequential file.  I am not a cobol programmer.  I was
given the copy books for the records, which made perfect sense.  I could
determine the byte offset for the occurs variable to determine the
variable length portions of the records.

When I started parsing the input file I was surprised to find that each
record is prefixed and suffixed with the length of the record (4 byte
little-endian integer).  This made it much easier to read each record.
But, I'd like to know if this a standard way for creating a sequential
cobol file, or if it implementor defined.  The compiler that was used is
Fujitsu for Win95 version 3.  Can I expect the same say for MF Cobol and
Visual Age Cobol?  If so, can I expect the length prefix/suffix to be
big-endian on big-endian platforms such as AIX and Sun?

Thanks....
--matt
```

- **References:** `<38190406.956F6460@san.rr.com>`

```

> wrote in message news:38190406.956F6460@san.rr.com...

It's operating system / implementer contingent. Don't count

- **References:** `<38190406.956F6460@san.rr.com>`

```

I do not know if that goes for Visual Age Cobol, but I think so. They have

<a id="t-a8fd5a4d67"></a>
## Thread: ANSI Standards Test Suite? — 1 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### ANSI Standards Test Suite?

- **From:** Alistair Moir <alistairmoir@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vape5$np3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

## Thread: MF COBOL 3.2x and DOS — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL 3.2x and DOS

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991029214236.18690.00000124@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

I have an application that is setup as follows:

PROGRAMA.GNT
     PROGRAMB.GNT    PROGRAMC.GNT     PROGRAMD.GNT
     PROGRAMB.G01    PROGRAMC.G01      PROGRAMD.G01
     PROGRAMB.G02    PROGRAMC.G02      PROGRAMD.G02
                                   PROGRAMC.G03      PROGRAMD.G03

I package the files together in a LBR call PROGRAMA.LBR

The system will then "Chain" from back and forth between the programs depending
on the function the user is doing. 

I use the "Section" statement to force the generation of the various "G0?"
files.

The problem that I am running into is the program will lock up at random times
during the running of the program.  It does not happen that often, but enough
to be a headache.

I am using the following compilier directives:

NOSMALLDD
01SHUFFLE
NODATALIT

From reading the documentation I believe this is correct, but would appreciate
any insight on the exact purpose of these directives and if I should be using
them or any others ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.2x and DOS

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vfiv8$cfu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<19991029214236.18690.00000124@ng-co1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote:
: Good Afternoon,

Well, it's morning here, and spring, and probably tomorrow
for you, but never mind.


: PROGRAMA.GNT
:      PROGRAMB.GNT    PROGRAMC.GNT     PROGRAMD.GNT
:      PROGRAMB.G01    PROGRAMC.G01      PROGRAMD.G01
:      PROGRAMB.G02    PROGRAMC.G02      PROGRAMD.G02
:                                    PROGRAMC.G03      PROGRAMD.G03

The G01, G02 etc are overlays caused by the SEG command, that is
the memory will hold ProgramB.GNT sections plus _either_
the .G01 sections or the .G02 sections, they will overwrite
each other.  If they are completely unrelated (such as G01
being initialisation and G02 being processing) then this is OK
otherwise, if say it alternates between a G01 routine and a
G02 routine, it may become somewaht slow.

Are these really large programs that require to be overlayed to
fit ?

: I package the files together in a LBR call PROGRAMA.LBR

: The system will then "Chain" from back and forth between the programs depending
: on the function the user is doing. 

I dislike the Chaining of programs.  Though I have not had problems
with it in the past I prefer having a small 'control' program
that CALL/CANCELs as required.  This usually holds an Interface
block in working storage that is passed to each called program.
There is a menu program that is the default 'next program' so
if a program exits without having set the next program to go to
then the menu is displayed.

Something like:

            WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
            01  Interface.
                03  Program-Name             PIC X(8).
                03  Next-Program             PIC X(8).
                03  Menu-Program             PIC X(8)).
                03  Company-Parameters
                    ......
                    common data as required

             PROCEDURE DIVISION.
                ...

                MOVE "SYSLOGON"       TO Program-Name
                MOVE "SYSMENUA"       TO Next-Program
                MOVE "SYSMENUA"       TO Menu-Program

                PERFORM Call-Cancel
                    UNTIL Next-Program = "FINISH"

                STOP RUN.

             Call-Cancel.

                 CALL Program-Name USING Interface
                 CANCEL Program-Name

                 MOVE Next-Program   TO Program-Name
                 MOVE Menu-Program   TO Next-Program
                 .

Each program can then just EXIT PROGRAM and the menu will
display or can set Next-Program if required.

Under DOS you also have to worry about memory fragmentation
when programs are called and cancelled or file opens cause
buffer blocks to become lodged.  The RTS tries to resolve
this but it is best to avoid the problem by loading all
common routines first.

My start-ups (as above) also open an indexed file to load
EXTFH and do some displays to load ADIS and also do dummy
CALLs on system routines (such as your SCREEN.GNT) to make
them load into lower memory.  This then allows maximum
contiguous memory to CALL/CANCEL the changing programs.

If you call programa which then calls screen, screen will
be loaded above programa.  f screen is not cancelled then
it will break memory into two parts (though the RTS may be
able to resolve this - I don't use the RTS I use dynamic
run-time COBLIB).


: I use the "Section" statement to force the generation of the various "G0?"
: files.

Hopefully in some logical and structured manner.


: The problem that I am running into is the program will lock up at random times
: during the running of the program.  It does not happen that often, but enough
: to be a headache.

When I did look after a system that chained I recall this happening
when it ran out of memory and the RTS couldn't resolve it.  
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.2x and DOS (segments)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vfnam$cg8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19991029214236.18690.00000124@ng-co1.aol.com> <7vfiv8$cfu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
    Some cobol's can REALLY get confused if you misuse overlays, ie jump
from segment 51 directly to segment 52
without going thru a fixed segment first.  MF does not seem to mind.  Yet
another way to write non portable code.

    I have not tried this using the exe or dll model, only the runtime with
INT/GNT's.


Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7vfiv8$cfu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

> : Good Afternoon,
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> G02 routine, it may become somewaht slow.

    snip
```

<a id="t-23e222ec99"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL 3.2 Compilier Directives - NESTCALL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL 3.2 Compilier Directives - NESTCALL

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991030115618.15745.00000326@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I am confused as to when I should use this compilier directive.  I have a LBR
of programs that "CHAIN" back and forth to different programs.  However, inside
of these programs I "CALL" a program (GNT) to handle the screen I/O.

Could someone please comment ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.2 Compilier Directives - NESTCALL

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vfak1$t09$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19991030115618.15745.00000326@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 wrote in message <19991030115618.15745.00000326@ng-fq1.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>I am confused as to when I should use this compilier directive.  I have a
LBR
>of programs that "CHAIN" back and forth to different programs.  However,
inside
>of these programs I "CALL" a program (GNT) to handle the screen I/O.
>
>Could someone please comment ?

Hi Bob,

The NESTCALL directive is only needed if your program has the following
structure:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG-A.
DATA DIVISION.
....
PROCEDURE DIVISION
....

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG-B.
DATA DIVISION.
....
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
....
END-PROGRAM. PROG-B.
END-PROGRAM. PROG-A.

This is a nested program because there is a program (PROG-B) inside another
(PROG-A).

The reason the compiler needs the directive is because of the way the
compiler has been written.

Regards,
Steve.

>
>Thanks,
>Bob Hennessey
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL 3.2 Compilier Directives - NESTCALL

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7veuof$2acd$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<19991030115618.15745.00000326@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Use NESTCALL when you are using "nested" programs.
Do not use it otherwise.

If each source file, after processing COPY statements,
contains only one program, you are not using nested programs.

If the source file contains multiple
    END PROGRAM program-name
statements, the program may be nested.

In the source for a nested program, there will always
be at least one occurence where two end program
statements appear in sequence, such as

....
end program inner.
end program outer.

In this case, the program 'inner' is nested within
the program 'outer'.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Bob7536 wrote in message <19991030115618.15745.00000326@ng-fq1.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>I am confused as to when I should use this compilier directive.  I have a
LBR
>of programs that "CHAIN" back and forth to different programs.  However,
inside
>of these programs I "CALL" a program (GNT) to handle the screen I/O.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Hello

We are a software house and we released on the market DataExchanger Pro for
any databases.

replication, synchronisation features without programming.
DataExchanger Pro the Exchange Server includes a powerfull Visual Scripting

chris.lawson@wanadoo.fr

........................................................... Article from
Infoworld about DataExchanger
...............................................
By Mario Apicella, InfoWorld test Center


One of the challenges that IT professionals face is getting diversified
components to work together. This is especially difficult when it comes to
compatibility among database systems because, despite the unifying promises
of ODBC and SQL, transparency among databases is still a dream. Any
administrator knows that replicating a table among databases works smoothly
only if the source and target data structures are from the same vendor. For
heterogeneous replications, you will likely need additional software and
effort.
A recently released product from Cross DataBase Technology promises to make
those reluctant products cooperate by providing an ODBC-based common
management point for all your databases, regardless of vendor and platform.
I found DataExchanger Pro 3.2 to be a reliable master key that opens a free
flow of data among DBMSes.

Similar products, such as Data Transformation Server from Data-Mirror,
provide separate products for different platforms; these require greater
commitments in purchasing, maintenance, and training.

DataExchanger Pro includes three components, all Windows-based scripting
tools: DataExchanger (DEX); DataExchanger Watch, a debugging tool for the
scripts created with DEX; and Data Connector, a workbench for interactively
testing an ODBC connection to a database. Data Connector can simplify data
transfer among databases without the need to type any code, and you won't
have to remember the differences between one database and another.

Administrators will find Data Connector interesting because it can save the
time and cost of manually converting SQL scripts, and it removes the
headaches of recalling any number of minute differences among databases.

Data Connector is a fine tool, but the strongest aspect of DEX is its
capability to create scripts without having to type a single character. This
is not only easy on the hands, but it opens up a wealth of possibilities to
make your script as complex as your objective requires.

For example, you can effortlessly create routine database actions, such as
connecting or disconnecting, making commit or rollback changes, copying
tables, or starting a replication. DEX also allows you to insert in your
script flow-control blocks -- such as goto, gosub, finish, or abort -- as
well as calls to other programs -- such as invoking a file transfer via FTP
or executing a DOS or Windows program. DEX can also provide the interface to
create an e-mail message from your script; so that if a critical script gets
stuck, you will be immediately informed.

If you are starting to think that this is not an ordinary script-maker, then
you're catching on. The powerful features of this product are impressive.
DataExchanger gives you the tools to build a sophisticated application from
which you can use the resources of your environment to ensure that your data
transfer among databases, no matter how complex, will work smoothly.

However impressed you may be, the functionality of DEX goes further still.
The product is a multithreaded application that can split the execution of
each script over 16 concurrent threads. Having a script execute in parallel,
of course, makes sense only if you run DEX on a Windows NT Server with
sufficient machine resources. With its flexible system of timers and locks,
DEX allows you to prepare scripts that can safely run unattended,
simplifying your daily operations.

I encourage you to consider DataExchanger Pro 3.2: It is an excellent
product that can erase the gap among your discordant databases. If you need
to move tables across different databases and platforms, you will love this
product.

Mario Apicella, a Technology Analyst for the Test Center, is a former
programmer and database administrator.

You can reach him at mario_apicella@infoworld.com.
```

<a id="t-f763e0d81d"></a>
## Thread: Initialize verb & variable arrays — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Initialize verb & variable arrays

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-10-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vfm3e$bva$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
    On the compilers that I have tried (MF 3.1 & 3.4 runtime, Netexpress 2.0
& 3.0) the Initialize verb initalizes the entire array, as if the occurs
depending on data name contained the maximum legal value.  Even the odoslide
compiler option does not seem to prevent this.

    When the array is really in WS, this seems to be harmless, except
possibly for the odoslide bit.  But when the array is in allocated memory
only just large enough to hold the occurs depending length of the array, it
blows up.  Badly in 16 bit products.

    Is this behaviour defined in the J4 specs, or is it implementor defined?
Will it change in the future?

    As it is, I have to manually initialize, although move space or
low-values to the group name works well enough.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize verb & variable arrays

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wjZS3.7462$9H3.76856@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vfm3e$bva$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
>    On the compilers that I have tried (MF 3.1 & 3.4 runtime, Netexpress 2.0
>& 3.0) the Initialize verb initalizes the entire array, as if the occurs
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Will it change in the future?

We had a long thread on this only recently.  COBOL 85 standard says in
paragraph 6.17.3 paragraph (4), that the data item referenced in the
INITIALIZ statement, or any data item subordinate to it, may not contain
an OCCURS DEPENDING clause.  Sounds like the compiler failed to flag the
syntax error.
```

<a id="t-0190b1ae85"></a>
## Thread: The artist not the paintbrush — 7 this month / 8 total

### The artist not the paintbrush

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381BA8AF.54A1929D@home.com>`

```

Michael, I couldn't resist <G>

The blurb is about OO :-

" Craftism - the combination of pragmatist and empiricist practices in
software engineering. Those who practice this combination in software
engineering are Craftites. The term Craftite is apropos because most of
the adherents of pragmatism and empiricism in software engineering see
themselves and software engineering in a way similar to how most of the
guild craftsman of the Middle Ages saw themselves and their work
activities. Latter day Craftites, just as the craftsman of yore, see the
work of their profession primarily AS AN ART CARRIED OUT BY THE GIFTED.
While this may have been true for the craftsman of the Middle Ages
this does not characterize the nature and organization of present-day
software engineering activities."

I was browsing OO books this afternoon in Chapters, mainly Perl and
Java, JavaScript etc. but found one titled "Fuzzy System Design".
Thought, ahh that's the book for me - only to find the author had put an
entirely different meaning on the word 'fuzzy'. But his book did start
with one truism, quoting his old school teacher, "Learning is doing".

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: The artist not the paintbrush

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cwZS3.7485$9H3.77341@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<381BA8AF.54A1929D@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>" Craftism - the combination of pragmatist and empiricist practices in
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>software engineering activities."

That's an interesting quote. :-)  Strikes me that the writer is trying
to promote a philosophy that I see quite a bit these days.  It is the
philosophy that it is education, only education, that makes for ability.
People who believe that are offended when someone with less education
blows their doors off. ;-)  In any profession that requires a lot of
intellectual ability, such as programming, talent is generally more
important than education, so long as a certain amount of knowledge is
there.  I have seen many well educated dummies who were completely
incompetent.  Education is certainly important, and necessary for many
things.  But education is an enabler.  It will never be a substitute
for talent, ever. :-)
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00

Jimmy

And quotes like "Learning is doing" gives me some alergic reactions. I know
that it is true within some limits, but I would never spend money on a book
with quotes like this.

It's 4 a.m. here and I might need som sleep, but to me the quote i purely
naive nonsense.

Imagine a specialist on call:  Hey, learning is doing! Just play around on
the keyboard and see how clever you can be over night.

Or when you hear your daughter screeming from the kitchen: Ther' you go -
Learning is doing - a frying pan with boiling oil is very hot. Or - You see,
a food processor can easily take 2-3 fingers.

Learning starts with hard work, getting hold on basics. After that, some
people might get expierience by doing things over and over again, but
unfortunately we see people doing the same stupid things over and over
again, so "Learning is doing" - no I am tired and go to sleep. I am
beginning to talk rubbish myself - good night.

regards
Ib






James J. Gavan skrev i meddelelsen <381BA8AF.54A1929D@home.com>...
>
>Michael, I couldn't resist <G>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The artist not the paintbrush

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381CAD92.A39DB6A8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<381BA8AF.54A1929D@home.com> <7vgbn3$d91$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```


Ib Tanding wrote:
> 
> And quotes like "Learning is doing" gives me some alergic reactions. I know
> that it is true within some limits, but I would never spend money on a book
> with quotes like this.

Ib,

It started out as a joke to rib Michael. But I see the 'learning is
doing' touched a raw nerve for you. Back in 1963 there were no courses, 
no nothing (or rather anything). First project I was on as an analyst
went from mid '63 to Summer '66 before it went live. We were learning as
we progressed. In to-day's terms an horrendous timeframe for something
as simple as printing invoices ! But when it went live, it went live
without a single major cock-up.

There is nothing wrong with formal training, as it became available -
but I still insist analysis is somewhat of an inherent skill, just like
those who have skills for sports, or the various branches of the arts
and sciences - and no amount of reading tomes is going to turn you into
a good analyst, you just have to think, think, think ..... and keep
re-thinking. You can take all the courses you want - it still finishes
up with applying the book learning to doing it, and sometimes getting
burned in the process.

Exactly the same applies with programming. Certainly an understanding of
how instruction sets etc. work can be a beneficial background. But even
with learning and doing you could still finish up as a 5 on a scale from
1 to 10. When I first got my Radio Shack and started learning RM/COBOL I
was working with a programmer who had his MSc in Computer Science. (His
basement was full of example listings which he had squirreled away over
the years). He insisted that he should write the first program to serve
as a model. I didn't object, made perfect sense. Some six months later
he told me I wrote better COBOL programs than he did - I still don't
know what he was referring to. 

Until such time as all our COBOL compilers have an OO module we are left
in the doldrums of treating OO as an abstract topic. As and when OO
COBOL becomes universally obtainable - it will get down to the doing to
learn, is OO viable/relevant and  what are the best approaches,
regardless of all the theory that has been written.

> .... but I would never spend money on a book with quotes like this.

I didn't <G>

PS: Haven't got the source, (picked it up through comp.obj), but from
memory the author of the 'Craftite' quote is an Earl Cox - has three
papers on his website.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

I just cannot stay away from 'empty' quotations. I have had the plesure to

more learning and less doing.

You are not completely right about no courses. I went to several courses
from  '63 to '67
starting with punch-card machines 077 Collator, 519 Reproducer, 548
Interpreter, 448 Tabulator. 602 calculator, 082 Sorter, moving to IBM 360 in
'64. Assembler, RPG and so on. The great adventure came in '68 - Cobol.

Keep on joking. Have fun.

## Thread: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page? — 11 this month / 33 total

### Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** dc606@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
How do I code to get the following:

1.  Page numbers on each page
2.  Column headings on each page
3.  Write only 5 entries on each page

Thank you


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RZ5T3.1904$iS.87617@viper>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>1.  Page numbers on each page
>2.  Column headings on each page
>3.  Write only 5 entries on each page


Dont forget to use the often ignored functionality of DFSort or SyncSort.
The control card would look something like:

SORT FIELDS=(001,008)
*            KEY TO SORT FILE STARTS AT 1 AND GOES FOR 8 CHARS
OUTFIL FILES=01,
*            CORRESPONDS TO FILE ASSOCIATED WITH DD SORTOF01
LINES=007,
*            DISPLAYS ONLY 7 LINES PER PAGE, 2 FOR PAGE HEADER AND 5 FOR 5
ENTRIES PER PAGE
OUTREC=(C'ENTRY:',001,080),
*            DEFINES DETAIL LINE TO BE SIMPLY THE CURRENT RECORD PREFIXED BY
"ENTRY:
HEADER1=('PAGE NUMBER:',&PAGE,
                       /,
*            DEFINES A LINE BREAK
                      001:'COLUMN 1 HEADER',
*            DISPLAYES "COLUMN 1 HEADER" AT POSITION 001
                      020:'COLUMN 2 HEADER',
*            DISPLAYES "COLUMN 2 HEADER" AT POSITION 020
                      /)

Uncommented it would look like:
SORT FIELDS=(001,008)
OUTFIL FILES=01,
LINES=007,
OUTREC=(C'ENTRY:',001,080),
HEADER1=('PAGE NUMBER:',&PAGE,
                       /,
                      001:'COLUMN 1 HEADER',
                      020:'COLUMN 2 HEADER',
                      /)
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi,
Normally I would say: "Read the Manual" or something like that. However, I
am in a good mood, and your problem is very general to many new students.
Your question can be answered in many ways, but here is a simple model,
everyone can use for writing reports:

You define a line-counter and a page-counter. Initial value of line-counter
is GT max. no of lines per page and initial value for page-counter is zero.

You define the headings including Disp-Page-Count (pic zzz9.).

Whenever the line-count is GT max. no of lines per page , perform your
heading (write after advancing 1 page). The first statement in your heading
routine should be 'Add 1 to Page-Count'. After that, 'Move Page-Count to
Disp-Page-Count. The last statement in your heading should be 'Move Zero to
Line-Count'

Whenever the line-count is LT max. no of lines per page + 1 (you can put an
'Else' upon GT  above) you should write a detail line - remember to 'Add 1
to Line-Count'. Write after advancing 1 line.

It is very easy to play the violin, when you know how.

Regards
Ib
dc606@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>How do I code to get the following:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
Ib Tanding <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:

: Whenever the line-count is GT max. no of lines per page , perform your
: heading (write after advancing 1 page). 

I have separate code for throwing a page and printing headings.
The code that looks after all this is buried in copy books so
I never have to worry about it, but the first headings are
printed without throwing a page and a page throw is done at
the end of the program - even if the report is interrupted
and abandoned.

This always leaves the printer with the last printed page 
available to be torn off and pages are not wasted.

If the page is not thrown at the end of a report then the
user has to wind it through to make it available and then
the next report will throw a blank page.  Having the user
adjust the paper in _any_ way genearlly leads to lineup
problems.

With back fed or sheet feed (laser) printers it is possible
to design paper handling to avoid waste and avoid user
interaction.  I always set up the printers using micro-feed
adjustments so that the user has to just press the 'load'
button and the paper is perfectly aligned.

In one case there was wastage of invoices occurring when
cash sales were made because one form was wasted after
each print-run.  With cash sales a print run was 1 form.
(I didn't write the system originally). By replacing the
printer with a back-fed one and changing the code 
slightly the savings in stationary paid for the new
printer in 6 months.


A
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
dc606@my-deja.com wrote:
>How do I code to get the following:
>
>1.  Page numbers on each page
>2.  Column headings on each page
>3.  Write only 5 entries on each page

The following example uses a header with three lines.  Use
as many header lines as is appropriate for your application.
You define the maximum number of lines per page in Working
Storage so it is easy to modify later.  If your compiler
has syntax for constants (e.g. 78 level in MF COBOL), that
is even better.

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
    77  PAGE-COUNT          PIC  9(04).
    77  LINE-COUNT          PIC  9(02) VALUE 99.
    77  MAX-LINES           PIC  9(02) VALUE 5.
...
    01  PAGE-HEADER-1.
        ...
        03  PH1-PAGE-COUNT  PIC  Z(04).
        ...
    01  PAGE-HEADER-2.
        ...
    01  PAGE-HEADER-3.
        ...

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
    PERFORM PRINT-ENTRY <until no more entries>.
...

PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.

    <move data to print record>

    WRITE PRINT-RECORD BEFORE ? .
    MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-RECORD.
    ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT.
...

PAGE-HEADING.
    IF (LINE-COUNT < 99)
        WRITE PRINT-RECORD BEFORE PAGE
    END-IF.
    ADD 1 TO PAGE-COUNT.
    MOVE PAGE-COUNT TO PH1-PAGE-COUNT.
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-1 BEFORE ? .
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-2 BEFORE ? .
    WRITE PRINT-RECORD FROM PAGE-HEADER-3 BEFORE ? .
    MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-RECORD.
    MOVE 0 TO LINE-COUNT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia>`

```
Hi,

I, too, am at this point in my COBOL course.  I have a question regarding
the following instruction that Judson so kindly gave to us poor students
(same as in my text):

PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.

The question I've had since last year - in my first few courses - is this:
do I need to say 'not less than' or can I say 'equal to' or 'less than or
equal to'????  Or could double spacing maybe mess us the 'equal to'?

Hope I'm making sense,
Thanks,
Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
>The question I've had since last year - in my first few courses - is this:
>do I need to say 'not less than' or can I say 'equal to' or 'less than or
>equal to'????  Or could double spacing maybe mess us the 'equal to'?


As with most programming languages there are a couple different ways to do
this.

My preferred method would be to use a conditional:

05 LINE-COUNT PIC X(003) VALUE ZERO USAGE BINARY.
    88 IN-BODY-OF-PAGE VALUES 1 THROUGH 40.
*************************************************** VALUE 40 TO ALLOW FOR
BUFFER AT BOTTOM OF PAGE
    88 PAGE-BREAK VALUES 41 THROUGH 999.

Then the logic would be :

PRINT-DETAIL-LINE.
    IF PAGE-BREAK
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF
    WRITE REPORT-RECORD FROM DETAIL LINE
        AFTER ADVANCING LINE-SPACING LINES
    .


Lack of periods is intentional. Single period on a line by itself at end of
paragraph.

THEN intentionally left out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n2ZS3.7432$9H3.76191@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Pat wrote:
>
>I, too, am at this point in my COBOL course.  I have a question regarding
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>equal to'????  Or could double spacing maybe mess us the 'equal to'?

You are correct.  I avoid ending a loop by testing the loop variable
being equal to some value, for two reasons.  First, as you point out,
you can 'miss' the value if the loop variable is incremented in an
unexpected way by some subsequent change to the program.  Second,
to my mind it is usually clearer, and expresses the essence of the
termination condition better, to use a relative test.  You stop when
you *pass* the boundary conditions.  Using equal does not express that
as clearly.  Always try to use a test that is clearest and makes the
most sense.  For example, if you are looping through values 1-5, it
makes more sense to test for > 5 than for not < 6, since 6 is outside
the range.  If you test against the highest or lowest values in the
range, then it is easier to find the references later.  Even better,
use a constant so you can change it in one place.

Of course, it is perfectly permissible to end a loop by testing for
equal, when you are looking for a specific condition other than the
value of a loop variable.  For example, when looping through a table
looking for some value (e.g. exiting when Table-Entry(sub) = <value>
is perfectly fine). :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381C6159.52E54F35@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Pat wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pat

I generally say IS GREATER THAN.  It avoids the "not", and it also
allows for you incrementing the counter by more than 1 at a time. 
(instead of counting 5 detail lines, I generally count actual physical
lines, including blank lines, headers, and footers).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OzZS3.7490$9H3.77432@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <381C6159.52E54F35@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Pat wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>lines, including blank lines, headers, and footers).

I agree and do the same.  But in the above example, I believe the
'not' is less objectionable than either testing for greater than 6,
when 5 is the limit, or in keeping the line count 1 greater than the
number of lines actually written.  Both violate the Rule of Least
Astonishment. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381C60B5.DA8B87D3@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
dc606@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> How do I code to get the following:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you

This is very basic stuff, easily accomplished either with or without
report writer.  Looks like homework - if you show us your current work,
we can give you clues for the part you got stuck in.

If you're not using report writer, create a line counter with a value of
888.  When you're ready to print, then check this value.  If it is
greater than 5 then perform your heading routine (write title & headings
& move zero to this counter).

Then increment this counter and write your detail line.   The detail
print should be within your I/O loop.

I told you much more than I should have.
```

Hi all.

  Last week I had a problem with an intialize statement . It looked like
this.

01 WK-WORKING.

     05 WK-REC-1.
           10  WK-DATA-1      PIC X(5).
           10  WK-DATA-2      PIC S9(2)  COMP-3.
           10  WK-DATA-3   OCCURS  3
                                             PIC X(2).

           10    FILLER              PIC X(10).



   Before doing any read in my program I'm doing an intialize statement
instead of the usual perform statement with spaces for a alphanumeric an
zeroes for a numeric to save time.

  INITIALIZE WK-REC-1 REPLACING ALPHNUMERIC BY SPACES

NUMERIC BY ZEROES

 I noticed after some testing, that the array was not intialize at all.
Leaving me with no choice then using the old fashion way of
initializing  variables.


Any hints on this one.

Thanks

jean-ren.duvalxxx@sympatico.ca

#### ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZH5T3.1894$iS.87409@viper>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca>`

```
Just a guess....but is your group under an FD? Then the field would, and
cannot, be initialized until the file associated with the FD has been
openned.

Did you check your informational and warning messages from the compile?
Typically the INITIALIZE and MOVE CORRESPONDING statements generate warnings
for inappropriate fields which get dropped from their implied functionality.
Try PROCESS FLAG(I,I) in the source code to ensure the messages are being
output to the listing.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W9ZS3.7447$9H3.75550@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca>`

```
The FILLER will not be initialized.  If the other fields are not being
initialized, there is a problem with your initialize statement (I don't
see one), or your compiler.  Note that some compilers support options
to initialize FILLER fields.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381C637D.933C8BC6@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca>`

```
The "old fashoned" and still most efficient way of initializing
variables is via the VALUE statement.  Not always possible with occurs
and redefines, but certainly possible here.

- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <tmccOIO+f54Bzr4nDaMBhEYvrGly@4ax.com>`

```
On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 16:07:11 GMT, wbblakemore@no.pobox.spam.com
(William B. Blakemore) wrote:

>On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 07:08:55 -0600, "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com> wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>">", rather than "=". 

Uh, Not < and > are not  the same thing.

Not < is the same as >=, so when equality is important, Not < can be
clearer that > or =.  Some compilers don't accept >= anway, so not <
is the only solution that gives the proper action.

## Thread: executable icons? Fujitsu 3 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### executable icons? Fujitsu 3

- **From:** "Barvin" <barvin@mwis.net>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IYPS3.1367$UG4.14008@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
I've been seeing some Fujitsu users out here so if you're one I'd like to
know if you have created the executable to have your own icon (.ico) rather
than the globe icon of Fujitsu's ?
I've experimented with @iconDLL as well as @iconNAME with no success, though
I'm not clear on their purpose anyway as they are runtime variables, whereas
the program icon is created with the executable.
What am I missing?
```

#### ↳ Re: executable icons? Fujitsu 3

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381c7285.342163555@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<IYPS3.1367$UG4.14008@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 00:01:40 -0500, "Barvin" <barvin@mwis.net> wrote:

>I've been seeing some Fujitsu users out here so if you're one I'd like to
>know if you have created the executable to have your own icon (.ico) rather
>than the globe icon of Fujitsu's ?

Well, it's a bit of a Funky way to do it, but I do this:

Make an .rc file that contains the following:

#define ID_DEF               100

Icon ICON icon.ico


Put it in the same directory as your icon.ico file.

Run RC filename.rc

Then replace project.res with your new .res, naming it project.res.  

You will get your new icon.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

<a id="t-cd03b81518"></a>
## Thread: Hey Grumpy Old Men — 2 this month / 22 total

### Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
If the subject offended you, remember:
"The shortest way between 2 hearts is a smile. "

If you aren't a grumpy old man - why continue? You might be offended, but on
the other hand - it would be nice to see your reaction.

I feel that this newsgroup is for discussing matters related to the fine
computer language: COBOL.  Though it hurts me that students often are
treated like dirt and that is the reason for the title.

If you think about what you do, when you have a problem. You will consult
friends and collegues. No problem in the earth is more important than yours.
They can die from hunger in Africa as long as you get the answer to your
problem. Yours - and mine - network is huge compared to a students network,
so I think that it is important to let them in.

It is important because we owe it to the language. We owe it to the next
generation. What is expierience if nobody can benefit from it?

I think we need a 'greenhouse'-newsgroup if we cannot treat students
properly in this newsgroup. We could go there to help all the young students
with their homework - not by giving programs away, but by giving hints where
to look and what to do. Help them to be able to solve their own problems.

Think about it and let us see your opinion.

I am 53 years old and I am very fond og Cobol. I have been in this business
since 1963 but the next 2 days I'll be away for education (they are still
hoping, aren't they). When I am back, I will look into this thread and count
all the 'Grumpy old Men'.

Trust me: I will respect all views equally.

Regards
Ib
```

#### ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-10-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<325778775638E8A3.E033AFADDFCAFB30.E1006852BE6EA718@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 12:50:32 +0100, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
enlightened us:

>If the subject offended you, remember:
>"The shortest way between 2 hearts is a smile. "
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ib,

It's not that we are "grumpy old men" (although my wife does accuse me
of being that sometimes), it is just that students need to at least
make a stab at something before asking for help.  If you've noticed in
the past whenever a student posts some code that shows they've at
least tried, they get more help than they probably need.  But when
there is a post like the one you are referring to where it appears the
student wants us "professionals" to do their work for them, we would
be doing them a great disservice by complying with their wishes.  No
grumpiness intended.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```
